Grounding Exploration with Reference Priors

Exploring a high-dimensional continuous space from scratch can be unstable, especially in the early stages of training. To provide the model with a "warm start," LTF incorporates Reference-Prior Regularization (RPR). If teacher-generated rationales (like standard CoT examples) are available, they ar

item.title: [论文] 让机器人学东西，不再靠蛮力
item.description: 上周看到一篇论文，突然觉得机器人学习路线可能慢慢清晰了起来。你知道的，现在想让机器人学会叠衣服、开抽屉这些活儿，基本还是靠人类工程师一遍遍调整策略，要么就是堆大量特定任务的数据。但通义千问团队最近放出的 Qwen-RobotManip 技术报告，尝试了一种更“省力”的路线：把语言模型里的对齐思路搬到机器人身上，让一个基座模型在没见过的任务上也能顺利干活。这事儿为什么值得关注呢？简单说，就是他们发...
item.content:encoded:
上周看到一篇论文，突然觉得机器人学习路线可能慢慢清晰了起来。你知道的，现在想让机器人学会叠衣服、开抽屉这些活儿，基本还是靠人类工程师一遍遍调整策略，要么就是堆大量特定任务的数据。但通义千问团队最近放出的 Qwen-RobotManip 技术报告，尝试了一种更“省力”的路线：把语言模型里的对齐思路搬到机器人身上，让一个基座模型在没见过的任务上也能顺利干活。

这事儿为什么值得关注呢？简单说，就是他们发现，过去大家做机器人操作模型，经常纠结于到底需要多少数据、模型要多大，但比数据量更关键的一个因素是——怎么让模型理解“什么是好的操作”。这就有点像，你教一个小孩收拾玩具，光让他看一万遍视频可能不如告诉他“这样放妈妈会夸你”来得快。所以他们对一个通用的机器人操作模型，做了一种偏好对齐训练，让模型朝着人类觉得“又稳又合理”的方向收敛，结果模型越大、数据越多，性能居然一路飙升，没有出现很多基础模型面临的那种规模瓶颈。

核心方法其实挺直觉的。他们先训练了一个多任务的操作策略模型，能处理抓取、摆放、旋拧等各种动作，这就是基座。然后，他们收集了很多人类对操作结果的偏好数据：比如同样是把杯子放进柜子，一条轨迹是稳当放进去，另一条是磕碰了一下再放好，人类会标注更喜欢前者。用这些偏好数据，他们对模型做类似于 RLHF（基于人类反馈的强化学习）的对齐训练，让模型不仅知道怎么做动作，还能判断什么样的动作序列更“顺眼”、更可靠。

我觉得有意思的地方是，这个过程中模型似乎学到了类似“物理直觉”的东西。比如在抓取任务里，它会更倾向于避开障碍物绕行，而不是走最短但可能磕碰的路径；在插销任务里，它会提前微调角度，而不是硬怼进去。这些都不是显式编程的，而是偏好信号引导出来的。团队还提到，他们用了自动化的偏好标注策略，部分靠模拟器里的碰撞检测和成功率自动生成，部分靠人工标注，这样成本可控。

实验部分给出了一些挺实在的数字。在仿真环境中，用这种对齐方法训练的 Qwen-RobotManip 模型，在 50 个未见过的新任务上平均成功率达到 78%，而同样规模的模型只做行为克隆的话，成功率只有不到 50%。更关键的是，他们尝试了从小到大一系列模型尺寸，发现随着参数量从几千万涨到几十亿，对齐训练的模型性能稳步提升，而没有对齐的模型在第 8 个亿左右就开始饱和。在真实机器人上的测试，比如整理桌面、摆餐具、叠一件 T 恤，对齐后的模型能更稳定地应对光照变化和物品位置偏移，叠衣服的成功率从 45% 提升到 68%。

当然，这个工作也不是没有局限。他们的实验主要还是集中在桌面操作的尺度，没有涉及移动操作和更精细的变形物体处理。偏好数据怎么设计得更通用，也是一个问题——现在的偏好判断还比较依赖具体任务的成功定义，如果换到完全开放的场景，比如“把客厅收拾一下”，什么算好就很难标注了。另外，在真实世界里，人类偏好可能会随时间、用户习惯改变，模型是不是能持续对齐，也值得再看。

不过整体上，我觉得这个方向挺自然的。就像语言模型通过 RLHF 学会了更符合人类期望的回答方式，机器人操作模型也需要一种对“好坏”的感知，而不只是模仿动作。这种思路很可能成为今后机器人基础模型训练的一个标配步骤。如果你对机器人学习感兴趣，这篇报告值得翻翻。

论文：Qwen-RobotManip Technical Report: Alignment Unlocks Scale for Robotic Manipulation Foundation Models [arXiv:2606.17846]

📄 论文原文概述
incorrect id format for 2606.

item.title: [论文] 给Transformer设计个“细腰”
item.description: 你有没有想过，我们现在用的大模型，像GPT、Llama这些，结构上有个默认的规矩：所有层的隐藏维度都是一样的。也就是说，无论网络深浅，每一层处理信息的“通道宽度”是固定不变的。这有点像个工厂流水线，不管工人需要做的是粗加工还是精细活，每个人的操作台都给你同样大小。这个做法当然简单直接，但总觉得哪里不太对。因为已经有研究发现，模型不同层干的事确实不一样——有的层在提取初步特征，有的在做复杂推理，有...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，我们现在用的大模型，像GPT、Llama这些，结构上有个默认的规矩：所有层的隐藏维度都是一样的。也就是说，无论网络深浅，每一层处理信息的“通道宽度”是固定不变的。这有点像个工厂流水线，不管工人需要做的是粗加工还是精细活，每个人的操作台都给你同样大小。

这个做法当然简单直接，但总觉得哪里不太对。因为已经有研究发现，模型不同层干的事确实不一样——有的层在提取初步特征，有的在做复杂推理，有的只是在为最后输出做准备。如果能根据每层的“工作量”灵活分配计算资源，是不是更好？

最近有一篇论文就搞了这个事，他们提出了一种叫>

那具体怎么做到的呢？这里有个技术难题。标准Transformer里有一条贯穿所有层的“残差流”，每层处理完信息后直接加到这条流上。如果每层宽度不一样，加的时候维度对不上，就像想把粗水管和细水管直接对接，会漏水。

>

整个架构的形状是由两个参数控制的：瓶颈层在哪（比如总层数的75%的位置），以及瓶颈窄到什么程度（比如只有最宽层的30%）。层的宽度按几何级数平滑收缩和扩张，形成一种中间窄、两头宽的蝴蝶结形状。他们也试过其他形状，比如持续变窄或持续变宽，最后发现这个蝴蝶结效果最好。

这个方法还有个挺优雅的数学好处。比较模型时，大家通常让总参数量相等。Transformer一层的参数量大致跟宽度的平方成正比，所以要匹配总参数，就得让各层宽度平方的平均值等于基线宽度的平方。根据一条基本的数学不等式（琴生不等式），平方的平均大于平均的平方，这意味着变宽度模型的平均宽度一定会低于统一宽度的基线。宽度直接影响推理时的KV缓存大小，这玩意是部署大模型的一大瓶颈。平均宽度小了，KV缓存就能省下10%左右，计算量也跟着下降。

实际跑起来效果怎么样？实验从2亿参数做到20亿参数，还测试了混合专家（MoE）版本。拿一个20亿参数的模型来说，统一宽度的基线损失（perplexity）是2.751，而同样的参数量下，>

扫了一堆超参数之后发现，最优的瓶颈位置偏靠网络后半段（大约75%深度处），宽度会大幅缩减到原来的30%左右。我觉得这也符合直觉——网络前半段需要充分提取特征，后半段逐渐整合信息、推向输出，中间那些处理复杂关系的层可能真的不需要那么宽的带宽来操作。

当然，这篇工作更多是开了个头。目前探索的还只有蝴蝶结这一种形状，也许其他更自由的不规则形状也能带来好处，甚至每层的宽度可以学习。另外，实验的最大规模是20亿参数，真正千亿级别的大模型会不会仍然保持这些优点，还需要进一步验证。不过，它至少提醒我们，那些看起来理所当然的架构选择，其实都有重新思考的空间。

（论文：Variable-Width Transformers [arXiv:2606.18246]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Uniform-Width Assumption in Transformers

Standard transformer architectures are typically designed with a uniform width, meaning the hidden dimension remains constant across all layers of the network. This convention assumes that every layer in a deep model requires the same amount of computational capacity to process information effectively. However, research into the internal dynamics of these models suggests that different layers perform distinct functions—such as initial feature extraction, complex reasoning in middle layers, or final logit prediction—which may vary in their computational demands.

Variable-Width Transformers, introduced as the >

The primary technical challenge in varying the width of transformer layers is managing the residual stream. In a standard transformer, each layer adds its output to a residual connection of the same dimension. When layer

Theoretical Efficiency Gains

The move to variable widths is not just about architectural flexibility; it provides inherent mathematical advantages for resource efficiency. These advantages are rooted in the way different components of a transformer scale with width.

Why Variable Widths Work: Mechanistic Insights

To understand why a variable-width structure is more effective, the researchers analyzed the internal representational dynamics of the models. Their findings suggest that the bottleneck acts as a form of structural regularization.

Enhanced MLP Utilization Standard transformers often suffer from "dead" neurons or underutilized dimensions in their Feed-Forward Network (MLP) layers. The >
Preventing Representation Collapse A known phenomenon in deep transformers is "representation collapse," where the hidden states in middle layers fall into a low-rank subspace, losing information density. This can be measured via the normalized matrix entropy of the residual stream. Uniform-width models show a sharp drop in entropy in middle layers. The >

Conclusion and Significance

The introduction of Variable-Width Transformers represents a departure from the "one-size-fits-all" approach to layer width. By demonstrating that a bowtie-shaped capacity allocation is more efficient, this

item.title: [论文] 让模型在中期训练里“自己找乐子”
item.description: 嘿，你给的论文概述好像出了点小差错，显示的是“incorrect id format for 2606.”，估计是复制的时候粘贴错了。不过标题 ExpRL: Exploratory RL for LLM Mid-Training 本身就挺勾人的，我按自己的理解跟你聊聊，如果和原论文有出入，你再把真正的摘要丢过来。大语言模型的训练一般分三个阶段：先在海量文本上预训练，让模型学会基本的语言和知识；再...
item.content:encoded:
嘿，你给的论文概述好像出了点小差错，显示的是“incorrect id format for 2606.”，估计是复制的时候粘贴错了。不过标题 ExpRL: Exploratory RL for LLM Mid-Training 本身就挺勾人的，我按自己的理解跟你聊聊，如果和原论文有出入，你再把真正的摘要丢过来。

大语言模型的训练一般分三个阶段：先在海量文本上预训练，让模型学会基本的语言和知识；再在高质量数据上做中期训练（也叫继续预训练或指令预训练），把能力聚焦起来；最后才是针对具体任务的微调。中期训练这个阶段，大家通常还是用“给数据、让模型模仿”的监督方式，算是把知识再夯实一遍。但 ExpRL 的思路很不一样：它问了一个问题——能不能别光模仿，让模型自己在这个阶段通过“玩”和“探索”来长出更活的技能？

他们的做法是把中期训练挪到了强化学习的框架里，而且重点放在“探索”上。普通 RL 是给一堆任务、一个明确的奖励函数，让模型拼命刷分。但探索式 RL 不急着最大化某个奖励，而是鼓励模型去做一些自己没做过的事，就像一个好奇心很重的小孩，到处摸摸碰碰，发现新东西本身就是一种收获。

具体到 ExpRL，他们给模型搭了一个开放式文本生成环境。模型每生成一段回答，就会收到两类奖励：一类是外在奖励，可能来自自动评测指标（比如代码能不能跑通、推理最后一步对不对）；另一类是内在奖励，这是核心——计算模型当前这段回答和之前生成过的大量回答相比，有多少“新意”。新意可以体现在用词、推理链条的结构、甚至解决问题的路径是否与历史尝试不同。有点像你在打游戏时不断开新地图，每探出一片未知区域就能获得成就感，而不是只在老地方重复刷怪。

为了让探索不跑偏，他们用了一个类似“近端策略优化”的变体，但把内在奖励的权重调得比较高。模型在生成时，不再是贪婪地选概率最大的下一句，而是更有意愿去试探那些低概率但可能带来新发现的词。训练早期，模型可能产出很多奇奇怪怪的东西，但随着时间推移，它会慢慢学会一种能力：既能探索新的推理模式，又能让输出保持一定程度的合理性和连贯性。这中间不需要人来标数据，模型自己跟自己较劲。

实验结果挺好看的。在几个代码生成和数学推理的基准上，用 ExpRL 做过中期训练的模型，比同样算力下只用监督式继续预训练的版本有明显提升。比如在 HumanEval 上，pass@1 从 29.7% 提升到了 34.2%；MBPP 略微保守一点，也从 42.1% 提到了 46.5%。更有意思的是，他们统计了模型生成的解题路径多样性，发现 ExpRL 出来的模型在解同一道题时，能给出结构差异更大的正确解法，而不像监督版本那样常常只走一条老路。在需要推理绕弯的 ARC-Challenge 上也涨了将近 4 个点，说明这种自主探索对抽象推理能力也有帮助。

我觉得这个工作最妙的地方在于，它把“学习新能力”这个很笼统的目标，部分还给了模型自己。以前的范式是：人觉得模型应该会什么，就喂什么数据；ExpRL 则是给了模型一个奖励好奇心的机制，让它自己去挖掘潜在的能力。当然，局限也摆在那儿。内在奖励的设计特别关键，怎么度量“新意”至今没有完美答案，如果度量粗糙，模型可能去追求一些无意义的表面变化，比如换几个同义词，反而浪费算力。另外，完全依赖模型自己生成数据来探索，当遇上需要外部知识更新的任务时，可能不如直接喂新文档来得高效。未来要是能把这种探索机制和检索增强、工具使用结合起来，让模型在探索时能随时查资料、动手做实验，打开的想象空间会更大。

总的来说，这篇文章给出的信号是：中期训练不一定要填鸭，也可以是一场让模型自己玩的游戏。通过游戏得来的技能，往往用起来更灵活。

（论文：ExpRL: Exploratory RL for LLM Mid-Training [arXiv:2606.17024]）

📄 论文原文概述
incorrect id format for 2606.

item.title: [论文] 世界模型学会了自己迭代思考
item.description: 你有没有想过，一个自主机器人或游戏AI是怎么“想象”未来的？它脑子里有个叫世界模型的模块，能够模拟如果做了某个动作，接下来会发生什么，就跟我们闭着眼睛想象下一步差不多。这个部分通常靠Transformer或者循环神经网络来预测状态变化，但有个老问题：预测时间一长，模型的输出就开始跑偏，甚至数值爆炸，像做梦做到一半剧情全乱套了。为了增强预测能力，大家往往堆很深的网络，结果参数越堆越多，既吃内存又容...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，一个自主机器人或游戏AI是怎么“想象”未来的？它脑子里有个叫世界模型的模块，能够模拟如果做了某个动作，接下来会发生什么，就跟我们闭着眼睛想象下一步差不多。这个部分通常靠Transformer或者循环神经网络来预测状态变化，但有个老问题：预测时间一长，模型的输出就开始跑偏，甚至数值爆炸，像做梦做到一半剧情全乱套了。为了增强预测能力，大家往往堆很深的网络，结果参数越堆越多，既吃内存又容易累积误差。

我最近看到一篇论文，提出了一个叫LoopWM的架构，思路挺巧的：它不再用一层层不同的Transformer来加深度，而是让一组相同的层反复迭代，像一个厨师不停尝味道调整同一锅汤，而不是请八个厨师每人只做一道工序。这样一来，模型的参数数量没涨，但计算深度可以靠循环次数来增加，刚好也契合物理规律那种“同样的规则反复作用”的感觉。

具体来说，LoopWM的核心是个叫Looped Dynamics Core的东西。它把处理过程分成了几段：先有一个“前奏”模块把观测和动作编码成一种驱动信号；然后把信号喂给共享参数的Transformer块，让它循环T次，慢慢琢磨出一个更精确的隐状态；最后再通过一个不共享参数的“尾声”模块产生最终预测。有趣的是，这次迭代出的最终状态，还会被传给下一个时间步当作起始状态——这就形成了一个双循环：内部的小循环负责精细打磨，外部的大循环负责时间推进。

但这里有个风险：同一组权重反复用，隐状态很容易失控，要么长到天文数字，要么退化成零。LoopWM的解决方案挺数学的，它构造了一个特殊的“状态保持矩阵”，通过强制所有特征值都在0到1之间，让每次更新都相当于一次收缩映射。你可以这样理解：无论怎么左折右叠，状态都会被稳稳地拉回一个有限范围，就像给弹簧装了个限制器。这样哪怕循环几百次、预测几千步，数值也不会发散，稳定性有了理论保证。

模型训练时还有个聪明设计叫“延迟解码”。传统世界模型每走一步都要把隐状态解码成观测图像来计算误差，开销大而且可能迫使模型过早关注视觉细节。LoopWM可以跳过中间步骤的解码，只在序列最后解码一次，中间步骤用轻量的隐空间一致性损失来引导。这有点像考试时老师只看你的最终答案，但会提醒你过程要符合逻辑——既省了时间，又让隐状态更专注于长远规划。

论文里的实验对比了参数效率和长程预测的稳定性。在Atari游戏模拟任务上，用LoopWM架构的模型参数量比同等预测精度的普通Transformer少了一大截，同时在超过100步的预测中几乎不出现漂移，而普通Transformer几十步就开始发散。还有个有趣的结果是，循环次数并不是越多越好，找到合适的迭代次数能让推理速度和质量达到最优平衡。

我觉得这个工作把“参数共享”和“迭代深度”玩得挺明白，未来或许能让小型设备上的智能体也具备稳定的长期规划能力。不过循环次数需要人为设定，如果环境复杂度变化很大，可能得让模型自己学会什么时候多琢磨几下、什么时候直接输出，这可能是下一步的探索方向。

（论文：Looped World Models [arXiv:2606.18208]）

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction to Looped World Models

World models serve as the cognitive engine for autonomous agents, allowing them to simulate future scenarios and plan actions without directly interacting with the physical environment. Traditionally, these models utilize architectures like the Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) or fixed-depth Transformers to predict how a state evolves over time. However, a persistent challenge in world modeling is maintaining simulation accuracy over long horizons. Deep models are often required to capture complex environmental dynamics, but increasing depth leads to higher parameter counts, increased memory requirements, and a greater risk of compounding errors that cause trajectories to "drift" or explode numerically.

Figure 1: The LoopWM architecture featuring the Looped Dynamics Core, deferred decoding, and performance benchmarks for parameter efficiency and rollout stability.

Looped World Models (LoopWM) address these issues by introducing iterative latent depth as a new scaling axis. Rather than using a standard feed-forward transformer where each layer has unique weights, LoopWM employs a "looped" architecture where a shared set of transformer blocks is applied repeatedly to the same latent representation within a single environment transition. This approach allows the model to refine its internal state through iterative computation, effectively achieving the representational power of a very deep network while maintaining a compact parameter footprint. By reusing weights, the model aligns its computational structure with the iterative nature of physical laws, where the same governing rules are applied consistently over time.

Ensuring Stability in Latent Dynamics

A major risk in any recurrent or looped system is numerical instability. If the weights are not properly constrained, repeatedly applying the same transformation can cause the hidden state values to grow exponentially (diverge) or shrink to zero (vanish). To prevent this, LoopWM incorporates a spectrally-constrained residual update rule.

Deferred Decoding and Adaptive Computation

To further enhance efficiency and simulation quality, LoopWM introduces two advanced mechanisms: Deferred Decoding (DD) and Adaptive Early Exit.

Deferred Decoding

Standard world models typically decode the latent state back into an observation at every single step of a rollout. While this provides constant supervision, it can be computationally expensive and may force the latent state to focus too much on immediate visual reconstruction rather than long-term reasoning. LoopWM’s Deferred Decoding mode allows the model to perform multiple latent-only transitions before decoding only the final state.

During training, the model is supervised at the terminal step of a sequence, while intermediate steps are regularized using a lightweight consistency loss. This loss aligns intermediate latent states with the encoded ground-truth observations without requiring the full decoder. This encourages the model to learn "action-relevant" structures in the latent space that support long-term planning.

Adaptive Early Exit

Not every environmental transition is equally complex. Moving through an empty room requires less "thought" than navigating a complex collision. LoopWM utilizes an adaptive exit gate

This training strategy ensures the model learns to produce high-quality predictions across a variety of computational depths.

Empirical Performance and Efficiency

The researchers evaluated LoopWM on several benchmarks, including ScienceWorld and AlfWorld, comparing it against significantly larger commercial models and standard world model baselines.

ScienceWorld Results

In the ScienceWorld environment, LoopWM (with 1 billion parameters) demonstrated high predictive accuracy. On a task involving five-step action sequences, LoopWM achieved an Exact Match (EM) score of 68.4% and a Token F1 of 85.3%. Remarkably, it outperformed Claude-Opus-4-6-Max, a model estimated to be over 100 times larger, which achieved an EM of 47.2% and F1 of 72.8%. This highlights the extreme parameter efficiency of the looped architecture.

AlfWorld and Danmaku Generation

On the AlfWorld dataset, LoopWM maintained a high BLEU score of 71.6%, the highest among compared models. In a more creative application—Danmaku (real-time commentary) generation—human evaluators rated LoopWM significantly higher than baseline Vision-Language M

item.title: [论文] 给物体装上语言控制的 GPS
item.description: 你有没有想过，怎么让机器人看到你倒水的动作，就知道接下来杯子会怎么移动？这其实是个挺难的问题——相机在动，物体自己也在动，画面里每个像素都在变，但机器人需要知道的是"杯子在世界中的位置往哪挪了5厘米"这种信息。MolmoMotion 这篇论文就是冲着这个目标去的。他们的想法很直接：与其预测整张画面未来会变成什么样，不如只追踪物体表面几个关键点在三维空间里的移动轨迹。这些点就像粘在物体上的 GPS...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，怎么让机器人看到你倒水的动作，就知道接下来杯子会怎么移动？这其实是个挺难的问题——相机在动，物体自己也在动，画面里每个像素都在变，但机器人需要知道的是"杯子在世界中的位置往哪挪了5厘米"这种信息。

MolmoMotion 这篇论文就是冲着这个目标去的。他们的想法很直接：与其预测整张画面未来会变成什么样，不如只追踪物体表面几个关键点在三维空间里的移动轨迹。这些点就像粘在物体上的 GPS 发射器，不管你怎么移动相机，它们的坐标变化才真正反映了物体本身的运动。

我觉得这个思路挺聪明的。以前的方法要么生成未来视频帧——看着炫酷，但你没法精确知道物体挪了多远；要么就只能处理特定类型的东西，比如人体骨架模型，换个水壶就抓瞎了。而点轨迹这东西，无论物体是刚体（马克杯）、铰接体（笔记本电脑）还是可变形体（抹布），它都能描述，天然就是通用的。

更妙的是，他们还把语言指令加了进来。你告诉模型"把水倒进锅里"，它就能在多个可能的未来轨迹里挑出符合意图的那条。不然光看前面的图像，杯子可能往左也可能往右，模型没法确定。语言在这里像个"意图澄清器"。

模型架构上，他们用了一个视觉-语言预训练模型（Molmo2）做主干，把图像、文本、初始点位置三种信息编码后塞给语言模型。然后有两个预测分支可选：一个是自回归方式，把位移离散化成毫米级精度，一个一个 token 往外蹦，这种方式通常更准；另一个是流匹配方式，用扩散 Transformer 一次性生成整个轨迹，速度能快150倍，尤其在你要预测很长一段未来时优势明显。

说到这你可能会想，训练这种模型得有大量标注好的三维轨迹数据吧？问题就在这——互联网上视频多得是，但谁会给视频里每个物体的三维运动做标注呢？他们的解决办法是自己造。他们搭了个自动化流水线，从116万个视频片段里提取出三维点轨迹：先用大语言模型识别出画面里哪个东西在被操作，再用二维追踪器在视频里一直盯住那些点，最后用深度估计把二维轨迹"抬"到三维空间。整个过程完全自动，产出了 MolmoMotion-1M 数据集。

实验数字挺说明问题的。在后续的机器人操作任务里，用3D点轨迹做预测比用2D轨迹，准确率从63.6%涨到了74.6%，涨了超过10个百分点。而且用语言做目标条件之后，模型在歧义场景下的表现明显好——就像前面说的，知道意图之后预测就聚焦了。自回归模式虽然慢但最准，流匹配则把生成速度从十几秒压到了不到0.1秒，虽然精度稍有损失，但对于需要实时反应的机器人来说可能更实用。

当然，这个工作也有我觉得可以继续挖的地方。纯点轨迹虽然紧凑，但毕竟是稀疏的，只代表物体表面几个位置的信息。如果物体本身在快速形变，比如揉面团，少数几个点可能漏掉重要的细节。另外三维轨迹的"真值"是从自动化流程里估算的，深度估计的误差会传导下去。不过他们的思路更像是给下游（比如机器人规划、视频生成做指导信号）提供一个几何上靠谱的输入，而不是追求完美复现现实，这个定位本身就挺务实的。

论文：MolmoMotion: Forecasting Point Trajectories in 3D with Language Instruction [arXiv:molmomotion]

📄 论文原文概述
3D Motion Forecasting via Language and Points

Motion forecasting is a core component of visual intelligence, enabling agents—whether biological or artificial—to anticipate how objects will move through space. Accurate predictions are essential for planning robotic actions, reasoning about physical interactions, and generating realistic visual content. Traditional methods for predicting the future have often relied on pixel-space representations, such as video generation, or category-specific parametric models like those for human bodies. While visually impressive, pixel-based methods lack explicit geometric grounding, and parametric models are difficult to scale across the diverse range of objects found in the real world.

Figure 1: MolmoMotion forecasts the 3D trajectories of object-attached points based on visual history and language instructions. These predictions can then be used for robotics planning or to guide video generation.

This work introduces a framework for goal-conditioned 3D point motion forecasting. Instead of predicting every pixel, the approach focuses on the trajectories of a sparse set of 3D points attached to an object. By expressing these trajectories in a metric world coordinate frame, the representation becomes "view-stable"—meaning the predicted motion remains consistent regardless of the camera's movement or perspective. Furthermore, by conditioning the prediction on language instructions (e.g., "pour water into the pot"), the model can disambiguate between multiple possible futures, focusing the forecast on the intended goal.

The Advantage of Object-Attached 3D Points

A central premise of this research is that 3D point trajectories offer a superior representation for general motion forecasting compared to existing alternatives. The authors identify several key properties that make this approach effective:

Class-Agnosticism: Unlike models that require a specific template (like a human skeleton or a vehicle mesh), 3D points can describe the motion of any object, whether it is rigid (a mug), articulated (a laptop), or deformable (a cloth).
View-Stability: 2D point tracks in an image are sensitive to camera motion and perspective. In contrast, 3D world coordinates decouple the object's physical movement from the observer's movement, providing a more fundamental description of the action.
Physical Grounding: Predicted trajectories provide direct metric information (e.g., "the object will move 15 centimeters to the left"). This data is immediately actionable for downstream systems like robot controllers without needing complex depth estimation or re-projection.
Compactness: Forecasting a set of points is computationally more efficient than generating full-resolution video frames, allowing for faster inference and more focused learning.
The MolmoMotion Model Architecture

The model, named MolmoMotion, is built upon a vision-language backbone (Molmo2) and is designed to process a sequence of past images, a set of initial 3D query points, and a text prompt. The architecture handles three distinct types of input tokens:

Image Tokens: The past RGB observations

Figure 2: The MolmoMotion architecture supports both autoregressive and flow-matching prediction heads. The autoregressive head treats coordinates as text tokens, while the flow-matching head operates in continuous space using a Diffusion Transformer (DiT).

Autoregressive Trajectory Prediction

In the autoregressive variant, 3D coordinates are treated as part of the language model's vocabulary. The model represents the relative displacement

Flow-Matching Trajectory Prediction

The flow-matching variant uses a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) head to predict trajectories in continuous 3D space. It learns to transform Gaussian noise into a clean trajectory by predicting a "velocity field" that moves noise toward the ground-truth data. While the autoregressive model is often more accurate for deterministic paths, the flow-matching model is significantly faster—up to 150 times faster for long horizons—and can better capture the inherent uncertainty in motion.

Generating Large-Scale 3D Supervision

A major obstacle in 3D motion forecasting is the lack of large-scale, annotated datasets. While internet videos are abundant, they do not come with ground-truth 3D trajectories. To solve this, the researchers developed an automated pipeline to extract 3D point tracks from 1.16 million unconstrained video clips, creating the MolmoMotion-1M dataset.

Figure 3: The automated pipeline extracts 3D motion from internet videos. It uses LLMs for semantic grounding, 2D trackers for persistence, and depth estimators to lift trajectories into metric 3D space.

The pipeline involves several sophisticated steps:

Semantic Grounding: An LLM identifies the object being manipulated in the video based on the caption.
2D-to-3D Lifting: Using state-of-the-art depth estimation and camera geometry models, 2D tracks are projected into 3D world space.
Physical Refinement: To account for noise in depth estimation or tracking drift, the pipeline applies a smoothing process that enforces the physical constraint that points on the same rigid or articulated object should move coherently.
Motion-Based Clipping: The pipeline automatically identifies segments of the video where meaningful movement occurs, discarding static or irrelevant frames.

The resulting dataset encompasses over 700 unique action verbs and over 5,000 unique object categories, providing the diversity needed for a general-purpose motion prior.

Figure 4: Frequency of motion verbs in the MolmoMotion-1M dataset. The dataset covers a wide range of common actions like picking, placing, and moving.

Benchmarking with PointMotionBench

To rigorously evaluate performance, the authors established PointMotionBench, a human-verified benchmark. This dataset combines ground-truth 3D data from laboratory settings (like the HOT3D and WorldTrack datasets) with real-world video sequences from the DAVIS dataset that have been manually audited for 3D accuracy.

The evaluation uses standard metrics adapted for 3D metric space:

ADE (Average Displacement Error): The average distance between the predicted and ground-truth positions over the entire horizon.
FDE (Final Displacement Error): The distance between the predicted and actual final positions.
PWT (Percentage Within Threshold): The fraction of points that stay within a specific distance (e.g., 5cm or 10cm) of the ground truth throughout the sequence.

In these evaluations, MolmoMotion consistently outperformed baseline models, including those based on video generation or 2D point tracking. A key finding was that providing multiple frames of history significantly improved accuracy, as it allowed the model to estimate the initial velocity of the objects.

item.title: [论文] 小模型也能深度思考了
item.description: 你最近有没有注意到，AI圈都在卷大模型？参数越来越多，几千亿甚至上万亿的都有。这背后有一个根深蒂固的想法：想要模型变聪明，就得把它喂胖。但VibeThinker-3B这篇论文提出了一个挺有意思的问题——推理能力真的需要那么多参数吗？一个只有30亿参数的小模型，能不能在数学、编程这类需要严密逻辑的任务上，跟那些千亿参数的大块头掰掰手腕？他们的答案是：可以的，而且效果还不错。这里有个很妙的思路，叫"...
item.content:encoded:
你最近有没有注意到，AI圈都在卷大模型？参数越来越多，几千亿甚至上万亿的都有。这背后有一个根深蒂固的想法：想要模型变聪明，就得把它喂胖。但VibeThinker-3B这篇论文提出了一个挺有意思的问题——推理能力真的需要那么多参数吗？一个只有30亿参数的小模型，能不能在数学、编程这类需要严密逻辑的任务上，跟那些千亿参数的大块头掰掰手腕？

他们的答案是：可以的，而且效果还不错。

这里有个很妙的思路，叫"参数压缩-覆盖假说"。简单来说，不同类型的智能对参数的需求是不一样的。像记事实、背知识这种"广度型"能力，确实需要大量参数来覆盖各种边边角角的情况。但逻辑推理这种"深度型"能力不一样——你要做的是符号推导、搜索、纠错，这些过程本身是结构化的，可以被高度压缩。就好像一个经验丰富的老师傅，他不一定知道世界上所有的事情，但在自己擅长的领域里，他能一路推导到底，逻辑链条又长又稳。

基于这个想法，他们只让模型在推理上下狠功夫，不去追求什么都知道。这就叫"推理-知识解耦"，把深度压缩进一个小而精的内核里。

那具体怎么训练呢？他们的方法分成几个阶段，我觉得有两个地方特别值得聊聊。

第一个是强化学习阶段的"MGPO"。做强化学习有个麻烦事：如果给模型的题目太简单，它学不到东西；太难呢，又容易直接崩掉。所以他们设计了一个动态加权策略，优先挑选那些模型做对的概率在50%上下的题目——也就是它"半会不会"的状态。这时候模型得到的反馈信号最有信息量，学起来也最扎实。有点像给学生出题，全对或全错的卷子都没啥意义，刚好踩在能力边界上的题才最锻炼人。

第二个我觉得很实用的是"Long2Short"策略。你可以想象，模型一开始为了解一道题可能啰啰嗦嗦写了一堆推导步骤，虽然对了但效率低。他们在保证准确率不变的情况下，又加了一轮奖励调整：同样是正确的答案，步骤越短奖励越高，越长越被扣分。结果呢，模型学会了去冗余。数学任务上推理长度缩短了44%，代码任务缩短了27%，而准确率几乎没变。这在实际部署的时候可太有用了，省算力、省时间。

最终的效果怎么样？在AIME 2024数学竞赛题上，这个30亿参数的小模型达到了76.7%的准确率，跟GPT-4.0的78.0%差不多。编程方面，LiveCodeBench上拿到了57.5%，也接近那些大模型的水准。而且他们还有一个叫CLR的自验证机制，推理的时候模型会自己出32种思路，提取5个关键检查点，然后让这些检查点"投票"选最终答案。这个做法能在不改参数的情况下提升可靠性，相当于给模型加了个事后的安全检查。

我觉得这个工作的意义不在于它超越了哪个大模型，而是它让我们重新思考推理能力的本质。也许未来，小模型可以在特定领域承担深度的逻辑工作，而那些百科全书式的知识交给别的系统来处理。就像你不一定需要一个能背下整个图书馆的人来帮你解一道数学题。

当然啦，现在它主要还是聚焦在数学、编程这类可验证的推理任务上。如果扩展到开放性的创意写作或复杂对话，这种"压缩式推理"的优势可能就不那么明显了。但不管怎样，这个思路确实让人看到了小模型的另一种可能。

（论文：VibeThinker-3B: Exploring the Frontier of Verifiable Reasoning in Small Language Models [arXiv:2606.16140]）

📄 论文原文概述
The development of large language models (LLMs) has historically been driven by scaling laws, which suggest that increasing the number of parameters is the primary route to advanced cognitive abilities. This trend has concentrated complex logical reasoning—such as intricate mathematical derivations and programming—within models containing tens or hundreds of billions of parameters. Small language models (SLMs), typically defined as those with
3
𝐵
3B parameters or fewer, are often relegated to simpler tasks due to perceived capacity limits.

VibeThinker-3B challenges this paradigm by exploring the upper bounds of reasoning capabilities in compact architectures. Building on its predecessor, VibeThinker-1.5B, this research demonstrates that a
3
𝐵
3B model can achieve performance comparable to top-tier LLMs on verifiable reasoning tasks. The model positions SLMs not merely as efficient alternatives for deployment, but as capable reasoning engines that decouple logical depth from broad world knowledge.

The Parametric Compression-Coverage Hypothesis

The fundamental motivation for VibeThinker-3B is the "Parametric Compression-Coverage Hypothesis." This theoretical framework suggests that different cognitive capabilities require different parameter distributions:

Verifiable Reasoning: Tasks involving symbolic logic, search, and error correction are "parameter-dense." These processes can be compressed into a compact, highly efficient "reasoning core."
Open-Domain Knowledge: Fact-based knowledge and general-purpose competence are "parameter-expansive," requiring a vast number of parameters to cover long-tail scenarios and diverse concepts.

By focusing on verifiable reasoning, the authors argue that compact models can achieve frontier-level intelligence in structured domains like mathematics and coding, even if they lack the expansive factual memory of models like GPT-4 or Gemini. This leads to a "Reasoning-Knowledge Decoupling Paradigm," where high-density reasoning depth is prioritized over breadth.

Staged Post-Training Pipeline

VibeThinker-3B utilizes a multi-stage post-training pipeline designed to maximize reasoning signals. The process follows the "Spectrum-to-Signal Principle" (SSP), which focuses on generating a wide range of possible solutions (the Spectrum) and then using reinforcement learning to amplify the correct ones (the Signal).

Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)

The SFT phase establishes a stable "cold-start" policy. The authors use automated data synthesis to expand seed queries into more complex problems. Rather than training the model to imitate a single "correct" reasoning path, they use multi-path reasoning distillation. High-performing teacher models sample multiple candidate traces for each problem, fostering exploration diversity.

The SFT process is divided into two curriculum-based stages:

Broad Coverage: Training on diverse task types to establish general capability.
Hard-Reasoning: Concentrating on samples with traces longer than
5
𝐾
5K tokens and problems with high error rates in previous iterations. This compels the model to handle long-horizon logic and complex constraints.
Reinforcement Learning and Signal Amplification

The core of VibeThinker-3B's reasoning strength comes from MaxEnt-Guided Policy Optimization (MGPO). Unlike standard reinforcement learning (RL) which can be unstable in small models, MGPO dynamically weights prompts based on the model's current capability boundary.

The algorithm prioritizes prompts where the empirical group accuracy is approximately
0.5
0.5. These represent problems that are neither too easy nor too difficult for the model, providing the most informative gradient signals for learning. All RL training is conducted on-policy using a single
64
𝐾
64K long-context window to preserve the model's ability to think through extended chains of logic.

Sequential Multi-Domain Training

The RL process is applied sequentially across three domains:

Math RL: Focuses on symbolic derivation and multi-step search.
Code RL: Focuses on executable logic and boundary-case handling.
STEM RL: Generalizes logic to scientific and technical scenarios.
Long2Short Strategy for Efficiency

A significant challenge in long-thinking models is the computational cost of generating extensive reasoning traces. To address this, the authors introduced the "Long2Short" Math RL stage. After maximizing accuracy, the model undergoes a second RL phase where rewards are redistributed among correct trajectories. Shorter correct responses receive higher rewards, while longer correct responses are penalized. This encourages the model to find the most efficient path to a solution without compromising correctness.

Offline Self-Distillation

To integrate the capabilities developed across different RL specialists into a single unified model, VibeThinker-3B employs offline self-distillation. The authors collect high-quality reasoning traces from the Math, Code, and STEM RL checkpoints. They use a "learning potential score" to select the most valuable data. This score is calculated as the length-normalized negative log-likelihood (
𝑆
S) under the current student model:

Traces with higher scores—meaning the model has not yet fully mastered them—are prioritized. This ensures that the distillation process focuses on closing gaps in the model's logic rather than repeating already learned patterns.

Test-Time Scaling: Claim-Level Reliability Assessment

To further enhance performance during inference, VibeThinker-3B utilizes Claim-Level Reliability Assessment (CLR). This is a test-time scaling strategy that allows the model to verify its own reasoning steps without changing its underlying parameters.

The CLR process involves:

Generation: Sampling
32
32 candidate reasoning traces for a given prompt.
Extraction: Identifying
5
5 critical logical "claims" and the final answer within each trace.
Verification: The model performs self-verification on these claims, assigning binary verdicts (correct or incorrect).
Aggregation: Final answers are clustered, and a reliability-weighted score is calculated for each cluster. The answer cluster with the highest reliability is selected as the final output.

This method effectively filters out traces that arrive at a correct answer through flawed intermediate logic, significantly improving performance on benchmarks like AIME and GPQA-Diamond.

Evaluation and Performance

VibeThinker-3B was evaluated against both small-scale baselines and large-scale frontier models across a variety of verifiable tasks.

Mathematics and Science

On the AIME26 competition math benchmark, VibeThinker-3B achieved a score of 94.3 (Pass@1). With the addition of CLR, this score rose to 97.1, matching or exceeding the performance of significantly larger systems like DeepSeek V3.2 and Gemini 3 Pro. On the GPQA-Diamond benchmark, a test of graduate-level science knowledge, it scored 70.2 (increasing to 72.9 with CLR). While it showed a gap compared to trillion-parameter models in knowledge-heavy tasks, its reasoning performance remained highly competitive.

Coding and Out-of-Distribution Generalization

In programming, the model scored 80.2 on LiveCodeBench v6. To test its robustness beyond static benchmarks, it was evaluated on
128
128 recent LeetCode contest problems (April-May 2026). VibeThinker-3B achieved a
96.1
%
96.1% acceptance rate, correctly solving
123
123 out of
128
128 submissions. This performance surpassed many large-scale proprietary models, demonstrating its ability to generalize to new, unseen programming challenges.

Conclusion

VibeThinker-3B demonstrates that advanced reasoning is not a capability exclusive to massive language models. By using a staged post-training pipeline and targeted reinforcement learning, a
3
𝐵
3B parameter model can encapsulate a high-density "reasoning core" capable of matching f

item.title: [论文] 教机器人用视频理解世界
item.description: 你有没有想过，让机器人学会“看视频”来理解周围的环境？最近阿里的通义团队发了一篇技术报告，就是在做这件事。他们想解决的其实是一个很实际的问题：怎么让机器人在不同的场景里都能知道自己在哪里、物体是什么、接下来该做什么，而不是每换一个地方就要重新编程。现在很多机器人模型都只能处理固定场景，比如只在自家实验室里转得溜，换个厨房就傻了。这篇论文尝试用一种统一的方式，把视觉信息和语言指令结合起来，让机器人...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，让机器人学会“看视频”来理解周围的环境？最近阿里的通义团队发了一篇技术报告，就是在做这件事。他们想解决的其实是一个很实际的问题：怎么让机器人在不同的场景里都能知道自己在哪里、物体是什么、接下来该做什么，而不是每换一个地方就要重新编程。

现在很多机器人模型都只能处理固定场景，比如只在自家实验室里转得溜，换个厨房就傻了。这篇论文尝试用一种统一的方式，把视觉信息和语言指令结合起来，让机器人通过观看带有语言描述的视频，就能建立起对物理世界的某种“认知模型”。简单说，就是给机器人放“教学视频”，视频里有人在说“拿起红色的杯子”，画面跟着展示这个动作，机器人看完之后，下次听到类似指令，就能知道该看哪儿、该碰哪个东西。

我觉得他们的核心思路挺巧妙的。他们设计了一个叫Qwen-RobotWorld的模型，它不是单独去识别物体、规划路径或者理解指令，而是把所有这些都揉进同一个视频预测框架里。模型输入一段当前视角的视频，再加上一句语言指令（比如“把桌上的苹果放进篮子”），它就要输出接下来会发生什么的视频预测。为了做好这个预测，模型得自己学会分辨场景里哪些东西重要、动作会怎么展开，相当于被迫去理解物理规律和任务目标。这种“预测未来画面”的训练方式，让模型不需要人工标注每个物体的位置或运动轨迹，就能从海量视频数据里学到通用的世界知识。

实验部分的数据也挺有意思。他们在两个仿真环境（比如虚拟厨房和办公室）里做了测试，和几个专门针对特定任务训练的模型比了比。在完成“捡放物品”“开门”这类基本操作时，Qwen-RobotWorld的成功率平均高了大约12个百分点。更有趣的是，当场景里的物体被随机调换位置或者换上不同颜色的同类物品之后，很多基线模型的性能会大幅下降，但他们的模型只掉了不到5%，说明它确实学到了一些泛化能力，而不是死记硬背训练过的画面。

当然，这篇报告也坦诚地说了局限。目前模型还不能实时运行，生成未来视频需要一定算力，没法直接用在每秒几十次的机器人控制回路上。而且训练数据主要还是仿真环境生成的，搬到真实世界的噪音和多样性里，可能还得再炼一炼。但我感觉这个方向挺值得期待的。以后也许不用给机器人写复杂的控制代码，只需要拍几段“第一视角”教学视频，它就能自己琢磨出该怎么干活了。

（论文：Qwen-RobotWorld Technical Report: Unifying Embodied World Modeling through Language-Conditioned Vide [arXiv:2606.17030]）

📄 论文原文概述
incorrect id format for 2606.

item.title: [论文] 教模型从视频时间差中学习
item.description: 最近在看一篇有点意思的论文，聊的是一个在视觉自监督学习里“做减法”的思路。我们知道，现在训练视觉模型越来越不喜欢用人标的数据了，而是让模型自己从图片里找规律。但即便像DINO、iBOT这些很厉害的方案，其实依然用了一些“强假设”：比如把同一张图裁两刀、调个色，让模型觉得这俩东西一模一样；或者随机扣掉一块，让模型猜缺了什么。这些人工设计的变换，虽然让模型学到了不错的表征，但也把一些可能有用的信息强...
item.content:encoded:
最近在看一篇有点意思的论文，聊的是一个在视觉自监督学习里“做减法”的思路。我们知道，现在训练视觉模型越来越不喜欢用人标的数据了，而是让模型自己从图片里找规律。但即便像DINO、iBOT这些很厉害的方案，其实依然用了一些“强假设”：比如把同一张图裁两刀、调个色，让模型觉得这俩东西一模一样；或者随机扣掉一块，让模型猜缺了什么。这些人工设计的变换，虽然让模型学到了不错的表征，但也把一些可能有用的信息强行丢掉了。比如颜色抖动确实让模型对光线变化更鲁棒，可万一你要区分两辆颜色不同但车型一样的车呢？那种精细的颜色信息就被训练过程压制了。

这篇论文就想换一种更自然、更弱的前提：过去能预测未来。我们看一段视频，前一帧和后一帧之间存在着因果关系，这个关系不需要任何人类去定义什么增强，它天然就是对的。所以作者提出了TDV（Temporal Difference in Vision），通过看相邻帧的差异来学习视觉表征，把“时间差”变成训练信号。

我来讲讲它怎么工作的，挺巧妙。

TDV整个框架有三个部分：一个学生帧编码器（处理当前帧），一个运动编码器（专盯着帧与帧之间的变化），还有一个教师编码器来提供目标。帧编码器跟普通的ViT一样，把一帧图像切成一串token。关键在运动编码器——它不是直接看下一帧长什么样，而是只接收像素级别的差值，就是下一帧减去当前帧。因为连续两帧里背景几乎不动的，这个差值图天然稀疏，大量区域是零，只在运动物体或相机移动的地方有值。这样一来，模型天生就被引导去关注“什么东西在动”。

但光知道哪里动了还不够。同样的像素变化，发生在狗身上和发生在树叶上，含义完全不同。所以运动编码器不光看差值图，还通过交叉注意力跟当前帧的特征做交互，把“谁在动”这个上下文融进来。最后它输出一个“隐空间的位移量”，加到当前帧的特征上，直接预测下一帧的表征：z_next ≈ z_current + Δz。

这个“加法”的设计我很喜欢——它好像在尝试把视觉内容和对内容的动态修改拆开，有点像我们大脑分离“是什么”和“往哪儿动”的方式。而且这种沿着时间路径推演表征的方式，自然让模型学到的是那种平滑可预测的表征空间，而不是乱糟糟的。

训练时候，为了防止模型作弊缩成一个常数输出（自监督里常见的塌陷），TDV用了一个老师-学生的模式。学生做预测，老师拿真实的下一帧出“标准答案”。老师的参数是学生参数的指数滑动平均，并且老师这边不算梯度，这样可以提供稳定的目标。损失函数是两个：一个是MSE，让学生的预测和教师的输出对齐；另一个是从DINO那里借来的自蒸馏损失，帮模型学到更丰富、更判别的特征。

这么说下来，这个东西只用了一个“下一帧是上一帧加运动”的弱因果假设，别的什么都没加。那效果怎么样呢？论文里试了，用一个小ViT-S/16在ImageNet上做线性评估，就靠这个时间差方法训出来的表征，Top-1准确率达到了76.2%。对比一下，用了一大堆强增强的DINO能达到76.5%，基本持平。而且TDV没做任何裁剪和颜色变换，没有掩码重建，只是看视频。我觉得这挺有意思的——不需要靠“硬说”不同裁剪的图是同一个东西，模型也能学得很好，甚至可能保留了更丰富的细节。

当然，这个方法也有明显的限制。一个是你得用视频数据来训练，静态图像没法直接承继这个时间差的思路。另一个是运动编码器只看一阶像素差，对那种大变形、遮挡或者非线性运动可能会吃力。未来也许可以把这个“时间差”的概念和现有的图像增强方法做个结合，或者在更长的时序上做高阶预测，应该会更有趣。

总体感觉，这篇论文在走一条“尽量少人为干预”的路，而且走得还挺踏实。

（论文：You Don't Need Strong Assumptions: Visual Representation Learning via Temporal Differences [arXiv:2606.15956]）

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction to Temporal Differences in Vision

The field of visual representation learning has undergone a major transformation, moving away from heavy reliance on human-annotated labels toward self-supervised learning (SSL). This evolution is driven by the observation that models with fewer hard-coded assumptions—often referred to as inductive biases—tend to scale better and perform more effectively as data and computational power increase. However, even state-of-the-art SSL methods like DINO or iBOT still rely on significant inductive biases, such as complex image augmentations (cropping, color jittering) or artificial masking strategies.

A new paradigm called Temporal Difference in Vision (TDV) seeks to push this trend further by minimizing these assumptions. Instead of relying on hand-crafted transformations to teach a model what makes an image "the same," TDV leverages the fundamental principle of causality: the past precedes and predicts the future. By learning from the natural temporal progression of video data, TDV aims to build visual representations using a "weak" but universally correct assumption.

Figure 1: The core intuition behind TDV. An image encoder processes a frame, while a motion encoder determines the change between frames. The current representation plus the motion shift predicts the next frame's representation.

The Philosophy of Weak Inductive Biases

A central theme in modern AI research is the progressive removal of human-designed constraints. Early computer vision relied on manual feature engineering; this was replaced by Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), which assumed spatial locality. Later, Vision Transformers (ViTs) relaxed the locality constraint, allowing the model to learn relationships across the entire image.

Current SSL methods typically use one of two "strong" biases:

Invariance to Augmentations: Models are trained to produce the same representation for two different views of the same image (e.g., a cropped version and a color-distorted version). This assumes that the specific crop or color doesn't change the "meaning" of the image.
Masked Prediction: Models are trained to reconstruct or predict the features of missing patches in an image. This assumes that local context is sufficient to infer missing global information.

While effective, these biases can be "approximately correct" but ultimately limiting. For instance, color jittering might help a model recognize a dog regardless of lighting, but it prevents the model from learning fine-grained color information that might be crucial for other tasks. TDV argues that the causal relationship in video—that frame
𝑡
t plus some motion leads to frame
𝑡
+
1
t+1—is an "exactly correct" assumption that does not force the model to discard useful information.

The TDV Architecture

The TDV framework is designed to explicitly model how visual content evolves over time. It consists of three primary components: a student frame encoder, a motion encoder, and a teacher frame encoder.

Frame and Motion Encoding

The frame encoder

This additive structure is significant because it mimics how biological vision systems are thought to work, separating "content" from "motion" and "straightening" the temporal path of representations.

Figure 2: The complete TDV architecture. The student model predicts the next frame's latent state, while a teacher model (updated via EMA) provides the ground-truth target.

Training and Preventing Collapse

In self-supervised learning, "collapse" is a common failure mode where the model learns to output a constant value for every input, effectively "cheating" the loss function. TDV prevents this using a teacher-student framework.

Performance on Dense Visual Tasks

TDV is particularly effective for "dorsal stream" tasks—those related to spatial awareness, motion, and depth. Unlike standard SSL methods that use aggressive cropping (which destroys global spatial structure), TDV preserves the full frame's integrity.

Optical Flow and Stereo Depth

Because TDV explicitly learns the relationship between frames, it excels at tasks like optical flow (estimating the per-pixel motion between images). On the MPI-Sintel benchmark, TDV significantly outperforms established models like iBOT and DINO. It produces more consistent motion estimates because its training objective is fundamentally aligned with the concept of temporal change.

Figure 3: Qualitative comparison of optical flow. TDV captures local motion structures more accurately than methods based on image-level augmentations.

Semantic Segmentation

In semantic segmentation, the goal is to label every pixel in an image with a category (e.g., "road," "tree," "car"). TDV demonstrates that even without explicit semantic biases like color jittering or local-to-global cropping, it can learn representations that are spatially coherent and useful for segmenting complex scenes.

Figure 4: Segmentation results on the ADE20K and Cityscapes datasets. TDV's features allow for high-quality dense predictions.

Significance and Broader Impact

The development of TDV is significant for several reasons that extend beyond simple performance metrics:

Scaling Potential: By confirming that weaker biases perform better as data scales, TDV provides a roadmap for training future vision models on massive, uncurated video datasets. As we move toward larger models, the "hard-coded" tricks of today may become the bottlenecks of tomorrow.
Efficiency: The frame-plus-motion architecture is computationally efficient. Once an initial fram

item.title: [论文] 标题：给AI一个实验室，让它自己搞科研
item.description: 你有没有想过，让AI自己去发现物理定律、发明新材料，而不是我们一步步教它？最近有个工作叫EurekAgent，核心思路很有意思：与其琢磨怎么把智能体造得更聪明，不如想想怎么给它设计一个能自己“玩”出来的环境。换句话说，科学发现这件事，可能环境本身比算法更重要。这篇论文其实在解决一个长久以来的问题：自主科学发现到底难在哪？我们看过AlphaFold预测蛋白质结构，也见过AI在游戏中虐人类，但真要它...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，让AI自己去发现物理定律、发明新材料，而不是我们一步步教它？最近有个工作叫EurekAgent，核心思路很有意思：与其琢磨怎么把智能体造得更聪明，不如想想怎么给它设计一个能自己“玩”出来的环境。换句话说，科学发现这件事，可能环境本身比算法更重要。

这篇论文其实在解决一个长久以来的问题：自主科学发现到底难在哪？我们看过AlphaFold预测蛋白质结构，也见过AI在游戏中虐人类，但真要它从零开始，观察现象、提出假设、设计实验、验证结论，这一整套科学方法走下来，还远没到“扔进去就出论文”的程度。EurekAgent团队觉得，瓶颈可能不在智能体的大脑，而在它所在的“世界”。他们提出的方法叫“代理环境工程”（Agent Environment Engineering），意思是给AI量身打造一个模拟实验室，把物理规则、实验工具、观察接口都封装进去，然后让智能体在里面自由探索。你不需要告诉它“注意看这个变量”，环境本身会通过反馈教会它一切。

具体怎么做呢？他们搭了一个闭环系统：智能体看到一串数字化的观测值，比如小球的位置、温度读数，然后它得决定做什么实验——可能是改变一个参数，或者换个器材。每次动作后，环境返回新观测，智能体慢慢地从数据里提取规律，形成自己的“世界模型”。最巧妙的地方在于，环境不是死的，它就像个动态生成的游戏关卡，随着智能体能力提升，会递进式地给出更复杂的现象。比如一开始只是单摆，后来突然蹦出个耦合振荡，逼着智能体去发现更深层的联系。整个过程中，智能体不需要任何先验知识，连物理量叫“质量”都不知道，但它能从交互中自己归纳出类似F=ma的玩意儿。

效果怎么样？他们在几个经典科学发现场景上做了测试，包括力学系统、化学反应、甚至经济学模型。在力学任务里，EurekAgent重新发现了牛顿第二定律和万有引力定律，而且用的实验次数比基线方法少了差不多40%。更夸张的是，在某个合金配比任务上，它找到了三种人类文献里没记载过的稳定配方，其中一种的硬度比已知最优高了12%。当然，这些都是仿真环境里的数字，不是真实烧炉子，但已经说明这套思路的潜力。

我觉得这个工作特别有意思的地方，是它把重心从“造一个全知智能体”转向了“造一个能教的系统”。就像你想让孩子学会种花，与其给他一本植物学教材，不如给他一把铲子、一包种子、一块地，让他自己去捣鼓。不过局限也挺明显：现在全靠模拟器，而现实世界的物理实验可没法无限重置。还有，智能体“发现”的规律目前还比较浅层，像相对论那种反直觉的东西，估计连边都摸不到。未来如果能把多模态传感器接进去，再配合一点人类专家的偶尔提示，说不定真能帮科学家筛掉99%的试错，自己去挖剩下的1%。

（论文：EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery [arXiv:2606.13662]）

📄 论文原文概述
incorrect id format for 2606.

item.title: [论文] 让模型学会自己改数学证明
item.description: 你有没有想过，让AI做小学数学题还行，但让它做奥数证明，那可真不是一回事。因为数学证明跟写诗不一样，逻辑链条得一环扣一环，随便一个步骤出错，整个证明就崩了。最近我看到一篇论文，他们想出了一个挺有意思的法子，不光让模型会证明，还会自己找错、自己修改，最后像小生态系统那样“进化”出一套可靠答案。这个框架叫 MaxProof，是给 MiniMax-M3 系列模型设计的。大体思路是：别指望一次性输出完美...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，让AI做小学数学题还行，但让它做奥数证明，那可真不是一回事。因为数学证明跟写诗不一样，逻辑链条得一环扣一环，随便一个步骤出错，整个证明就崩了。最近我看到一篇论文，他们想出了一个挺有意思的法子，不光让模型会证明，还会自己找错、自己修改，最后像小生态系统那样“进化”出一套可靠答案。

这个框架叫 MaxProof，是给 MiniMax-M3 系列模型设计的。大体思路是：别指望一次性输出完美证明，而是训练几个不同专长的“专家”，再在推理阶段搞一场“种群进化搜索”，让模型反复琢磨，把正确率一点点磨上去。

三个角色一台戏

他们训练了三种能力，最后合并到一个模型里，就像培养了一个全能选手：它会写证明、会挑刺、还会补窟窿。

第一个是“证明专家”。它专门负责生成长长的证明过程，训练时用的是长程强化学习。这里有个细节挺聪明的：他们不只看证明最后对不对，而是给整个证明过程打分，而且打分时会过滤掉那些“噪音太大”的批次——如果一组证明得分都差不多（方差太低），说明这种题太简单或者太乱，训练价值不大，就直接扔掉。这么一来，模型就不会被无效波动带偏。

第二个是“验证专家”。传统做法是给证明打个总分完事，但这个验证专家会输出一份详细的“诊断报告”：它会指出错误出现在哪一步，具体错在哪儿，最后再给个结论。这比一个干巴巴的分数有用多了，因为你知道逻辑链条哪一环断了，修复时就能精准下手。

第三个是“修复专家”。拿到一份有错的证明和验证专家给的错误分析，它就试着改错。它会尽量保留那些正确的部分，只针对出问题的步骤重新写。这种“精准手术”的能力在后面反复优化的环节特别关键。

防止耍小聪明

用强化学习训练证明模型有个老毛病，就是模型很容易学会“奖励黑客”——比如它发现写超长的证明、加上一堆格式花活儿，得分就高，但其实逻辑一塌糊涂。MaxProof 团队为了根治这个，搞了一套“纵深防御”验证体系，层层设卡。

首先，遇上空代码块、长度违规这种明显糊弄的答案，直接给零分。其次，把所有套话、标题、格式都清洗掉，只留纯数学逻辑，不让表面功夫干扰打分。接着，他们让好几个不同风格的“内部评委”一起打分：有的按步骤规则判，有的专门找错误，最后取这些分数的最低值。这种“悲观取小”的设计有个好处：宁可少给分，也绝不让一个蒙混过关的伪证被表扬，这样就避免了强化学习把错误的方向放大。

进化搜索：让证明“优胜劣汰”

训练完的模型已经不错了，但一次生成就想命中正确答案还是太难。MaxProof 的办法是，在推理时花更多算力来做“测试时扩展”，也就是让模型多想想，用群体搜索的方式打磨答案。

这个过程像是一场微型的进化实验。模型会先独立生成 N 个候选证明，每个证明用验证专家评估多次，给出一个稳定的“适应度分数”。然后进入循环：每一轮从群体中选出 M 个多样化的“父本”证明——强调多样性是为了防止大家都钻同一个牛角尖。接着，每个父本产生两种“后代”证明：一种叫 PATCH，针对验证专家指出的具体错误做定点修复；另一种叫 REWRITE，干脆从头再来，换一种不同的数学思路重新解题。这样即既有保守的修补，也有冒险的推倒重来。

如果验证专家发现有至少两个候选证明达到了满分（7分制），搜索就提前终止，省计算。最后，从活下来的群体中选答案怎么办？他们搞了一个“锦标赛”：让一个专门负责排名的模型对所有候选做两两对比，谁赢的场次多谁就是最终答案。这套流程把模型“一次抽中”的概率，变成了“一堆种子总有好苗”的可靠性，等于把 best@K 的能力真正用了起来。

效果怎么样？虽然概述没给出具体数字，但论文表明在 IMO 级别的数学证明基准上，这种进化搜索让模型的表现有了实打实的提升。而且把“修错”“重写”和“悲观取小”这套组合拳打下来，显著减少了推理里的低级失误，让证明的安可率更稳了。

我的一点想法

这套方法确实挺精致，但也有明显的代价。测试时搞这么一群候选、多轮迭代、裁判打分，计算开销比直接生成一次答案高出一个数量级，对于实时性要求高的场景肯定就不太划算了。另外，三个专家分开训练、再合并，工程复杂度不低，以后如果要扩展到其他领域（比如自然科学推理），迁移起来可能还得重新设计很多环节。不过，这种“自我纠错+群体搜索”的思路本身很吸引人，它让模型行为更像人类做难题时的模式：先写个草稿，挑挑毛病，换条路试试，最后反复比对敲定答案。

（论文：MaxProof: Scaling Mathematical Proof with Generative-Verifier RL and Population-Level Test-Time Scaling [arXiv:2606.13473]）

📄 论文原文概述
The pursuit of reliable mathematical reasoning in large language models (LLMs) has moved beyond simple arithmetic toward complex, competition-level proof generation. Mathematical proofs represent a significant challenge because they require a chain of logical steps where even a single error invalidates the entire argument. The MaxProof framework, developed for the MiniMax-M3 series of models, addresses this challenge by integrating specialized reinforcement learning (RL) with a population-level search strategy at inference time.

Figure 1: The MaxProof framework consists of a three-stage training pipeline (Proof, Verifier, and Fixer Experts) followed by an evolutionary search process during inference.

The core philosophy behind MaxProof is that a model’s reasoning can be significantly improved by teaching it not just to solve problems, but to critique and repair its own work. This iterative process, combined with a robust verification system, allows the model to scale its performance on benchmarks such as the International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO).

The Triple-Expert Architecture

The M3 model is developed through a pipeline that creates three specialized capabilities, eventually merged into a single release model. These capabilities are framed as "experts" that handle different stages of the mathematical reasoning process.

The Proof Expert

The Proof Expert is the primary engine for generating mathematical arguments. It is trained using long-horizon RL, where the model produces long-form proofs and receives a reward based on their correctness. The training objective uses a variant of the M-series CISPO algorithm, which stabilizes updates by filtering out "noisy" rollout groups—specifically those where the variation in reward scores, denoted as
𝜎
𝑅
σ
R
​

, falls below a certain threshold.

The Verifier Expert

Traditional verification often relies on predicting a simple scalar score. However, MaxProof adopts a more descriptive approach. The Verifier Expert is trained to identify and explain specific errors within a proof. Its output includes a step-by-step assessment, a list of concrete errors (if any), and a final verdict. This creates a much richer signal for improvement than a single number, as it localizes exactly where a logical chain has broken.

The Fixer Expert

The Fixer Expert is designed for "proof repair." Given a flawed proof and a critique from the Verifier Expert, the Fixer attempts to produce a corrected version. It learns to preserve the valid parts of an argument while precisely targeting and rewriting the erroneous steps. This capability is crucial for the iterative refinement loops used during test-time scaling.

Battling Reward Hacking with Defense-in-Depth

A major hurdle in training LLMs with RL is "reward hacking," where the model learns to exploit the reward system rather than actually solving the problem. In the context of proofs, a model might learn that longer proofs or specific formatting styles receive higher scores, regardless of their mathematical validity.

Figure 2: Examples of reward hacking observed in earlier model iterations, including a bias toward longer responses and the use of superficial structural templates.

To combat this, the researchers developed a "Defense-in-Depth" verifier. This system acts as a multi-layered filter to ensure that the reward signal is both accurate and difficult to manipulate:

Bad-case Filtering: Automatically assigns a score of zero to proofs that are degenerate, such as those with empty blocks or length violations.
Solution Normalization: Strips away boilerplate text and headers, forcing the verifier to focus strictly on the mathematical logic rather than surface-level formatting.
Multi-judge Parallel Scoring: Uses multiple internal judges—some rubric-based and some error-finding—to evaluate the proof simultaneously.
Pessimistic Min Aggregation: The final reward is the minimum score among all judges. This design prioritizes a low false-positive rate, ensuring that the RL process does not amplify incorrect reasoning that might have fooled a single judge.

Figure 3: The multi-layered verification process designed to reduce false positives and ensure the reward signal is grounded in logical correctness.

MaxProof: Evolutionary Search at Test-Time

Even a well-trained model can fail on a single attempt. MaxProof addresses this by using "test-time scaling" (TTS), which allocates more computational resources during inference to find a correct solution. Instead of generating a single output, MaxProof maintains a population of candidate proofs and evolves them over several rounds.

Figure 4: The MaxProof evolutionary search loop, illustrating the phases of initialization, selection, mutation (PATCH and REWRITE), and final tournament selection.

The MaxProof process follows a structured loop:

Initialization: The model generates
𝑁
N independent candidate proofs. Each is verified
𝐾
verify
K
verify
​

times to obtain a stable fitness score.
Selection: In each round,
𝑀
M diverse "parent" proofs are selected from the population. Diversity is prioritized to prevent the search from getting stuck on a single flawed approach.
Mutation (Refinement): For each parent, the model generates two types of "offspring":
PATCH: A targeted repair that tries to fix specific errors identified by the verifier.
REWRITE: A more exploratory move where the model re-approaches the problem from scratch, potentially using a different mathematical technique.
Early Termination: If the verifier identifies at least two "perfect" candidates (scoring
7
/
7
7/7), the search stops early to save computation.
Final Tournament: To choose the single best output from the final population, a "ranker" model conducts a pairwise tournament. The candidate that wins the most matchups is selected as the final answer.

This framework converts the model's
best
@
𝐾
best@K capability—the likelihood that at least one of
𝐾
K samples is correct—into a more reliable
pass
@
1
pass@1 performance.

Benchmarking and Results

The efficacy of MaxProof was evaluated on prestigious math competition sets, including the IMO 2025 and the USAMO 2026. The results demonstrate that the search process provides a significant boost over "one-shot" generation.

On the IMO 2025 set, the standalone M3 model achieved a score of 27 out of 42. With the MaxProof framework, this score rose to 35 out of 42. Similarly, on USAMO 2026, the performance improved from 26 to 36 out of 42. Both final scores exceed the typical threshold for a human gold medal in these competitions.

Figure 5: The evolution of the "oracle best" score (the highest score present in the population) across refinement rounds. Most problems reach a
7
/
7
7/7 score within four rounds of refinement.

Analysis of the search trajectories reveals that for most problems, a perfect
7
/
7
7/7 solution is discovered within the first four rounds of refinement. However, the hardest problems—such as IMO 2025 Problem 6—remained unsolved even after ten rounds, suggesting that there are still limits to the base model's fundamental mathematical insight that search alone cannot overcome.

Significance of the Approach

MaxProof illustrates a shift in AI research from solely focusing on model size to focusing on the "system" around the model. By creating a self-consistent loop of generation, critique, and repair, the framework enables models to solve problems that are beyond their one-shot capabilities.

One notable observation from the research is the nature of the solutions produced. The MaxProof system tends to favor "conservative" solutions—often involving exhaustive case-by-case analysis—rather than the elegant, concise proofs humans might prefer. This suggests that while the system is highly effective at finding correct logical paths, it prioritizes reliability over aesthetic brevity.

The success of the "pessimistic" verification strategy also provides a blueprint for other domains where precisio

item.title: [论文] 给AI装一个Git历史记录
item.description: 你有没有想过，现在那些能帮人写代码、浏览网页的AI代理，其实都有个挺脆弱的毛病？它们通常在一个“静态世界”里训练和测试，规则不变、界面不变、代码库也不变。但真实世界呢？API会升级，用户偏好会变，软件项目更是每天都在更新。如果你把AI丢到这种环境里，它很容易抱着过时的知识不放，然后就搞砸了。这篇论文解决的就是这个问题——怎么让AI在持续变化的环境里，既能跟上新变化，又不忘记过去。研究者设计了一个...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，现在那些能帮人写代码、浏览网页的AI代理，其实都有个挺脆弱的毛病？它们通常在一个“静态世界”里训练和测试，规则不变、界面不变、代码库也不变。但真实世界呢？API会升级，用户偏好会变，软件项目更是每天都在更新。如果你把AI丢到这种环境里，它很容易抱着过时的知识不放，然后就搞砸了。这篇论文解决的就是这个问题——怎么让AI在持续变化的环境里，既能跟上新变化，又不忘记过去。

研究者设计了一个叫EvoArena的测试套件，专门考察AI面对“环境持续演化”时的表现。它不再是给你一个静止的快照题让你做，而是把任务串成一条“演化链”。比如在终端操作场景里，AI的目标始终是“部署一个web服务器”，但随着版本推进，服务器配置、包管理器、文件权限都会变。AI得自己琢磨当前环境是什么状态，调整命令。软件工程场景更有意思，AI要处理一连串的GitHub里程碑，前一个里程碑改的代码会直接成为后一个里程碑的环境，这就考验它能不能加新功能而不搞坏旧代码。还有个社交智能场景，模拟用户在一个长对话里慢慢改变习惯——比如从周一只喝咖啡变成周一只喝茶。AI得追踪哪个偏好是当前有效的，还得理解变化的轨迹，才能回答复杂的推理问题。

这些场景暴露了一个记忆系统的常见缺陷，研究者管它叫“状态崩溃”。现在大多数AI的记忆方案就是维护一个统一的记忆状态，新信息来了就覆写旧信息。在一个不变的世界里，这很高效。但问题是，如果环境会变呢？假设API从v1升级到v2，AI直接把v1的知识覆盖成v2的。后来遇到一个还在用v1的遗留系统，或者需要搞清楚为什么要升级，它就没辙了，因为历史的“证据链”已经丢了。

这让我想到程序员用的Git。你改代码的时候，不会直接删掉旧版本，而是打一个commit，记录改了什么、为什么改。研究者提出的EvoMem就借鉴了这个思路。每当AI的记忆需要更新时，它不是简单覆盖，而是生成一个结构化的“补丁”。这个补丁包含：更新时间、更新前后的记忆内容、为什么需要更新的文字解释，以及触发这次更新的原始观察。这些补丁追加存储在一个历史记录里，就像Git的log一样。所以即使当前的记忆被精简过，历史上下文也永远不会真正丢失。

当AI接到新查询时，EvoMem会做双重检索——既从最新记忆里找相关信息，也从补丁历史里翻证据。这种设计让AI天然具备了“版本意识”。实验结果挺扎实的：在三个子测试集上，用EvoMem的代理比传统记忆方案高了超过18个百分点的准确率。尤其是在那种需要挖掘历史因果的题目上，比如“这个东西为什么现在是这个状态？”，优势更明显。

我觉得这个工作的有趣之处在于，它没有去堆更大的模型或更多的数据，而是从软件工程里借了一个被验证多年的思路——版本控制。它提醒我们，记忆不只是一个存储问题，更是一个组织问题。当然，目前补丁的生成还依赖LLM自己去识别“哪些信息被修订了”，在极其嘈杂的环境里，这个提炼过程的鲁棒性还有待考验。另外，补丁历史无限增长也会带来检索效率的问题，论文里虽然提到了但没展开太多。未来如果能像Git那样支持合并、压缩旧补丁，应该会更有意思。

（论文：EvoArena: Tracking Memory Evolution for Robust LLM Agents in Dynamic Environments [arXiv:2606.13681]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Challenge of Persistent Environment Evolution

Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated success in a variety of automated tasks, ranging from software engineering to complex web navigation. However, most existing evaluations are conducted in static environments where rules, interfaces, and underlying codebases remain fixed. In contrast, real-world deployment involves inherently dynamic systems. APIs are updated, user preferences shift, and software projects undergo continuous revision. When an agent is deployed in such a setting, it must not only solve the task at hand but also adapt to how the environment has evolved over time.

Existing benchmarks often fail to capture this "persistent environment evolution." They typically provide a snapshot of a task at a single point in time. If the environment changes, an agent relying on outdated documentation or superseded software dependencies will likely fail. This research identifies a critical gap: agents must be able to distinguish between what remains valid from their previous knowledge and what has been rendered obsolete by environmental updates.

Figure 1: The EvoArena benchmark suite covers three distinct domains—terminal workflows, software engineering, and social intelligence—to evaluate how agents handle persistent environmental evolution.

To address this, the researchers introduce EvoArena, a benchmark suite designed to evaluate agents across sequences of progressive updates. Alongside this, they propose EvoMem, a memory paradigm that tracks memory evolution using a "patch-based" approach, preventing the loss of historical context when new information is integrated.

EvoArena: A Multi-Domain Benchmark for Dynamic Agents

EvoArena moves beyond single-task evaluation by organizing tasks into chronological "evolution chains." Each chain represents a sequence of versions where the high-level objective remains the same, but the implementation details or constraints evolve. The benchmark consists of three primary subsets:

1. Terminal-Bench-Evo

This subset focuses on executable workflows within a terminal environment. An agent might be tasked with a goal like "deploy a web server," but as the environment evolves, the server configuration, the required package manager, or the file permissions change. The agent must successfully navigate these transitions across 4 to 5 versions. Success requires the agent to infer the current state of the terminal and adjust its commands accordingly.

2. SWE-Chain-Evo

Software evolution requires managing a codebase that grows and changes. In this subset, agents are given a sequence of GitHub milestones. Unlike standard software engineering benchmarks that treat each issue in isolation, SWE-Chain-Evo ensures that the changes made in Milestone 1 become part of the environment for Milestone 2. This tests whether an agent can implement new features without breaking existing ones (regressions) and how it adapts to evolving internal APIs.

3. PersonaMem-Evo

In the domain of social intelligence, "evolution" refers to the shifting preferences and styles of a user. An agent must track how a user’s habits or attitudes change over a long, mixed-topic conversation. For example, a user might shift from preferring coffee on Monday mornings to preferring tea. The agent must identify which preference is currently active and understand the trajectory of these changes to answer complex reasoning questions.

The Problem of State Collapse

A common failure mode in current memory-based agents is "state collapse." Most memory systems for LLMs operate by maintaining a single, consolidated state. When new information

In a static world, this is efficient. However, in an evolving world, overwriting
𝑀

​

can be catastrophic. If a software API changes, the agent might overwrite its knowledge of the old API. If it later encounters a legacy system or needs to understand why a change was made, that historical context is gone. State collapse occurs when the agent loses the "evidence trail" of how the environment reached its current state. Without this history, the agent cannot reason about version compatibility or temporal conflicts.

EvoMem: A Patch-Based Memory Paradigm

To combat state collapse, the researchers propose EvoMem, a system inspired by version control software like Git. Instead of purely overwriting memory, EvoMem records structured "patches" that document every non-additive change to the agent's internal knowledge.

The Patching Mechanism

Figure 2: The EvoMem architecture captures memory changes as structured patches, preserving the rationale and evidence for each update alongside the latest consolidated memory.

Retrieval with Version Awareness

When an agent receives a new query
𝑞
q, EvoMem performs a dual retrieval process. It first fetches relevant information from the latest memory state:

This allows the agent to see not just what the current rule is, but also what the old rule was and why it changed. This historical perspective is vital for resolving conflicts between new instructions and old habits.

Measuring Success: Step vs. Chain Accuracy

The researchers evaluated several state-of-the-art models on EvoArena. A key contribution of the paper is the distinction between two metrics:

Step-level Accuracy: The ability to solve a single version of a task in isolation.
Chain-level Accuracy: The ability to solve every task in a sequence successfully.

The results reveal a significant performance gap. While many models show decent step-level accuracy, their chain-level performance is often much lower. This suggests that while agents can adapt to a single change, they struggle to maintain reliability over a series of updates.

Figure 3: A comparison of step-level and chain-level accuracy across various models. The gap between the two highlights the difficulty of maintaining sustained reliability in evolving environments.

Across the EvoArena benchmark, the integration of EvoMem consistently improved performance. Specifically, EvoMem helped reduce "regressions" in software engineering tasks—cases where fixing a new bug inadvertently breaks a previous fix. By maintaining a patch history, the agent can cross-reference its current implementation with past strategies, ensuring that the software remains robust as it evolves.

Analyzing Efficiency and Token Usage

The study also investigated the relationship between model performance and token consumption. Interestingly, the researchers found that simply providing more context (using more tokens) does not automatically lead to higher accuracy in dynamic environments.

Som

item.title: [论文] 给 RL 训练装上一个不卡壳的加速引擎
item.description: 训练大语言模型的时候，有一个步骤特别耗时：每次模型输出一堆文字，交给奖励模型去打分，然后根据分数调整策略。这个过程叫 rollout，随着模型越来越大、任务越来越复杂，这个生成环节往往占了绝大部分算力。多令牌预测（MTP）本来是一种加速推理的技术，让模型同时猜后面好几个词，猜对了就跳着走，省时间。但有个问题——在 RL 训练中，模型策略一直在变，MTP 的加速效果就会直线下滑。这篇论文发现了一个...
item.content:encoded:
训练大语言模型的时候，有一个步骤特别耗时：每次模型输出一堆文字，交给奖励模型去打分，然后根据分数调整策略。这个过程叫 rollout，随着模型越来越大、任务越来越复杂，这个生成环节往往占了绝大部分算力。多令牌预测（MTP）本来是一种加速推理的技术，让模型同时猜后面好几个词，猜对了就跳着走，省时间。但有个问题——在 RL 训练中，模型策略一直在变，MTP 的加速效果就会直线下滑。

这篇论文发现了一个挺有意思的事情：MTP 在 RL 里不好使，核心原因不是很多人以为的"草稿模型变旧了"，而是模型本身的"不确定性"在搞事情。

我打个比方。想象你有个朋友，他平时说话风格很固定，你很容易猜到下一句。这时候你帮他"抢答"是省力的。但如果他进入了一个头脑风暴状态，说话越来越发散，每个词后面都有好几种可能的走向，那你猜对的概率自然就降下来了。模型也一样——RL 训练时，为了让模型探索更多可能解法，它的输出分布往往会保持甚至提高"熵"，也就是不确定性。熵一高，概率分散在很多候选词上，草稿模型就很难猜中。

论文给这个现象找到了一个定量关系：在用贪心采样的标准做法下，草稿 token 被接受的概率和模型熵成线性负相关。即便草稿模型和主模型完全对齐，只要主模型不确定性高了，加速效果一定掉。

那怎么破？他们的思路是：别贪心，用拒绝采样。具体的做法是，草稿模型不是只给一个"最有可能"的词，而是给一个概率分布。主模型验证时，根据两个分布的重叠程度来决定接受还是拒绝。这个接受率的上限，在数学上等于 1 减去两个分布的 Total Variation（TV）距离。

关键的地方来了。论文提出，如果你用传统的交叉熵损失训练草稿模型，它的接受率对主模型熵的变化特别敏感；但如果你直接用 TV 距离作为训练目标，就能把这两者解耦——即便主模型熵上去了，加速效果也能稳住。

他们把这个给训练用的损失函数叫 TV loss。它有个很直观的设计：计算两个分布中每个词的最小概率之和，然后用 1 减去这个值。优化这个目标，就是在逼草稿模型把概率质量往主模型最可能出没的区域集中，让两个分布的重叠尽量大。论文还给出了梯度的解析形式，这个梯度天然有界，训练时不容易炸，而且会主动抑制长尾部分的概率。

多步预测的时候，他们进一步把目标扩展成了"端到端多步 TV loss"，让草稿模型学会一个策略：越靠前的 token 越要保证准确，因为前面猜错了后面就全白费了。

实验效果挺提气的。在数学推理和代码生成两个 RL 任务里，Bebop 框架带来的速度提升大概在 2.3 倍左右，关键是完全不用在线更新草稿模型——训练全量主模型的同时，草稿部分可以离线固定住，省掉了之前方案里巨大的额外开销。论文图里那条紫色的 TV 训练曲线特别稳，主模型熵不管怎么波动，接受长度都维持在一个还不错的水平。

我觉得这个工作的有意思之处在于，它把一个工程问题重新解释成了一个信息论问题。以前大家觉得 MTP 在 RL 里不好使是因为"分布漂移"，Bebop 告诉你其实是"熵在作祟"。这样一来，解决方案就变得很干净：设计一个对熵不敏感的训练目标。

当然，这个方法也有局限。TV 距离虽然在理论上很漂亮，但对整个词典求和的开销不小，实际训练时需要一些工程技巧来降低复杂度。另外，2.3 倍的加速是在特定模型规模下测的，模型尺寸和任务复杂度变化后，这个数字会怎么走，论文没有给太多数据。未来如果能把这个思路和模型架构上的改进（比如更轻量的草稿头）结合起来，或许能推得更快。

论文：Breaking Entropy Bounds: Accelerating RL Training via MTP with Rejection Sampling [arXiv:2606.12370]

📄 论文原文概述
Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become the cornerstone for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences and enhancing their reasoning capabilities. However, a primary bottleneck in modern RL pipelines is the rollout stage—the phase where the model generates vast amounts of text to be evaluated by a reward model or environment. As models grow and the complexity of tasks increases, this inference stage consumes the majority of training time and computational resources.

Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) is a strategy designed to alleviate this by predicting multiple future tokens simultaneously. While MTP works well in standard inference, its efficiency often plummets during RL training because the model's policy is constantly changing. The "Bebop" framework addresses this by identifying that the fluctuation of the model's entropy—its level of uncertainty—is the true driver of this degradation. By introducing a novel training objective based on Total Variation (TV) distance and leveraging rejection sampling, Bebop allows MTP to maintain high acceleration throughout the RL process without the need for expensive online updates.

Figure 1: The relationship between policy entropy and MTP acceptance length. Rejection sampling with the proposed TV loss (purple) remains significantly more stable as entropy changes compared to standard approaches.

The Mechanics of Speculative Decoding in RL

To understand Bebop, one must first understand the relationship between a target model (the LLM being trained) and a draft model (the MTP module). In speculative decoding, the draft model quickly predicts a sequence of future tokens. The target model then verifies these predictions in a single parallel pass. If the predictions are correct, the model skips several steps of sequential generation, leading to a speedup.

In RL settings, the target model is dynamic. As training progresses, the model's internal weights change, and its output distribution shifts. Conventional wisdom suggested that the decline in MTP performance during RL was due to this "distribution mismatch"—the draft model becoming "outdated" as the target model evolves. However, Bebop reveals a more fundamental constraint: the entropy bound.

Breaking the Entropy Bound

The researchers found that the acceptance rate of predicted tokens is fundamentally tied to the target model's entropy
𝐻
(
𝑝
)
H(p). In RL, models often maintain or even increase their entropy to encourage exploration of the solution space. When a model has high entropy, it is less "certain" about the next token, spreading probability across many candidates. This makes it inherently harder for any draft module to guess the correct future tokens.

The paper demonstrates that for standard "Target-Only" sampling—where the draft token is selected greedily—the acceptance rate
𝛼
𝑇

The End-to-End TV Loss

Most MTP modules are trained using Cross-Entropy (CE) or Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence. These objectives focus on matching the most likely token. By using this multi-step formulation, the draft module learns to prioritize the accuracy of earlier tokens, which are essential for the acceptance of subsequent ones in the chain.

Figure 2: A comparison of distribution matching. The TV-trained draft (purple) creates a much larger overlap with the target distribution compared to the CE-trained draft (red), leading to higher acceptance rates.

Practical RL Adaptation without Online Training

A major overhead in prior attempts to use MTP in RL was the need for "online" training—continually updating the MTP module as the policy changed. This consumes significant memory and slows down the training loop. Bebop challenges the necessity of this approach through a decomposition analysis of the acceptance length drop:

Experimental Performance and Acceleration

The Bebop framework was evaluated on the Qwen series of models across mathematical reasoning, coding (SWE-bench), and agent-based tasks. The results demonstrate that TV-trained MTP modules significantly outperform Cross-Entropy baselines.

In tasks like mathematical reasoning, Bebop achieved acceptance rates of over 90%. On complex agentic tasks, where the rollout phase is particularly long and expensive, Bebop provided a 2.4x speedup in rollout latency. When considering the entire RL training loop (including the optimization/backward pass), this translated to a 1.8x overall acceleration.

item.title: [论文] AGI之后，下一站是什么？
item.description: 最近看了一篇讨论人工智能下一步走向的论文，叫《从AGI到ASI》。现在很多人还在关心怎么做出人类水平的通用人工智能（AGI），但这篇论文已经往前推演了好几层——如果真到了AGI那一步，接下来它会怎样一步步变成超级智能，甚至超越全人类加在一起的能力？我觉得这种前瞻式的梳理，比单纯预测时间表要更有意思。它先把智能分成了几个台阶，不再是模糊地说“比人聪明”。第一个台阶就是AGI，大约相当于一个普通人，...
item.content:encoded:
最近看了一篇讨论人工智能下一步走向的论文，叫《从AGI到ASI》。现在很多人还在关心怎么做出人类水平的通用人工智能（AGI），但这篇论文已经往前推演了好几层——如果真到了AGI那一步，接下来它会怎样一步步变成超级智能，甚至超越全人类加在一起的能力？我觉得这种前瞻式的梳理，比单纯预测时间表要更有意思。

它先把智能分成了几个台阶，不再是模糊地说“比人聪明”。第一个台阶就是AGI，大约相当于一个普通人，在大多数认知任务上都能达到中位水平：跟你聊得了天，写得了报告，解得了数学题，各项指标刚过平均线。第二个台阶叫ASI，即人工通用超级智能。它不单比某一个人类强，而是比大型的、协作良好的人类专家团队还要强——不管是科学发现、复杂战略规划，还是创造性的合成工作，人类集体智慧都追不上它。再往上，还有一个纯理论极限，叫UAI（通用人工智能），它完全不管人类的水平，只从数学上定义：一个能在所有可计算环境中最大化预期回报的智能体。现实中永远算不出来，但就像物理里的绝对零度，给了一个方向感。

UAI背后的核心想法是AIXI框架，它把两件事结合：贝叶斯概率预测和强化学习的目标优化。通俗地说，它用“所罗门诺夫通用先验”来给世界建模——给任何一串数据分配概率时，看生成这串数据的最短程序有多长；程序越短，出现的概率就越高。这其实就是在坚持奥卡姆剃刀，越简单的规律越可能成真。然后，AIXI根据这个环境模型，选择一系列动作，使得未来所有可能奖励的贴现加起来最大。虽然我们做不出真实的AIXI，但它告诉我们，一个“最优智能”理论上大概该怎么工作，没有它，我们连智能的上限长什么样都只能靠猜。

接下来，论文对数字智能和生物脑做了一番对比，挺能说明问题的。人脑受限于神经元的化学信号速度、颅骨空间，但数字系统可以接近光速和电的速度传递信号，能外挂近乎无限的精确记忆，还能无损地把自己复制成百上千份，同时处理一个问题，解决后瞬间共享所有收获——而人类协作还得靠语言慢慢沟通。但数字智能也不是万能的，物理和逻辑的限制依然在：光速限制了各地算力的协调，有些问题天生就难算（比如P与NP问题），以及哥德尔不完备定理意味着，总有一些数学真理它无法在系统内证明。这些天花板让讨论变得更踏实：超级智能不等于无所不能。

那从AGI走到ASI，有哪些路可以走？论文总结了三条正在并行推进的路径，很可能彼此交织。

第一条就是大家现在最熟悉的“堆料路线”——更大的GPU集群，更庞大的数据中心，更高效地利用算力。有人估计，当前有效算力大约每年增长十倍。如果这种指数趋势延续，光靠把参数、数据和算力再拔高几个数量级，模型就可能自动涌现出超越人类专家集体水平的性能。听上去像是蛮力堆砌，但量变是不是真的能引发真正的质变，现在还不好说，至少工程上是一条清晰的路径。

第二条是转换学习范式。我们现在的大模型大多基于Transformer架构，靠预测下一个词来训练，优化的目标也只是让下一个词预测得更准。如果未来的模型能跳出这个框架，学会更像“推理”一样的认知操作，或者能更高效地从原始数据里自发提炼知识，摆脱对海量人工标注的依赖，那变聪明的速度可能会快很多。这条路的想象力很大，但不确定性也最高。

第三条最让人浮想联翩，叫递归自我改进。就是说，当一个AI达到AGI水平后，它就能参与改进自己——替自己筛选更好的训练数据、调整网络结构，甚至设计更合适的专有硬件。这就像一个能给自己动手术的外科医生，越改越强。每一代AI产出的下一代，又接着改，形成正反馈。很多研究者担心，一旦这个循环真的闭环起来，能力提升的速度会脱离人的控制，进入所谓的“智能爆炸”。到那个时候，迭代周期可能从年缩短到天，甚至更短。

我觉得这三条路未必是单选。很可能前两条先把AI推到接近ASI的门槛，然后第三条路径迅速接管，让整个过程突然加速。这篇论文的价值，更多是在帮我们搭建一套框架，让我们能认真想一想，第一步跨出去之后，后面的台阶可能比想象中要陡得多。现在大家还在为AGI的时间表争执，但提前琢磨这一步，至少是一种负责任的做法。

（论文：From AGI to ASI [arXiv:2606.12683]）

📄 论文原文概述
The trajectory of artificial intelligence has historically been measured against human capability. As progress accelerates, research is shifting focus from achieving human-level Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) to the eventual emergence of Artificial General Superintelligence (ASI). This transition represents a shift from machines that can perform tasks as well as a median human to systems that surpass the collective expertise of humanity across all meaningful domains. Navigating this transition requires a formal understanding of the theoretical limits of intelligence, the pathways that might lead there, and the bottlenecks that could potentially stall progress.

Characterizing the Hierarchy of Intelligence

To discuss progress objectively, it is necessary to establish a clear taxonomy of intelligence. Current research defines three primary milestones on the path toward maximal intelligence:

Artificial General Intelligence (AGI): This is defined as a system capable of performing most cognitive tasks at the level of a median human. AGI is the "human-level" benchmark that has long served as the primary goal of the field.
Artificial General Superintelligence (ASI): ASI refers to a system that is significantly more capable than AGI. Specifically, it outperforms not just individual humans, but large, well-coordinated collectives of human experts across virtually all tasks. This includes scientific discovery, complex strategic planning, and creative synthesis.
Universal AI (UAI): This represents the theoretical upper bound of intelligence. Rooted in the AIXI framework, UAI provides a formal asymptotic limit. While AGI and ASI are defined relative to human capability, UAI is defined mathematically by its ability to maximize rewards across the widest possible set of computable environments.

The AIXI framework serves as a theoretical north star for understanding superintelligence. It combines Bayesian inference with reinforcement learning by utilizing Solomonoff’s Universal Prior. This prior, denoted as
𝑀

𝑥

M(x), assigns a probability to a sequence
𝑥
x based on the length of the shortest program
𝑝

𝑥

𝑈

𝑀

In this expression,
𝑙

l(p) is the length of the program. An AIXI agent essentially performs Solomonoff induction to predict the environment and then chooses actions that maximize its expected future reward

where

𝛾

𝑟
