​

is the reward at each step. While AIXI itself is incomputable, it provides a rigorous foundation for what an "optimal" general intelligence would look like, independent of human biological constraints.

The Biological-Digital Divide

A central premise of the transition from AGI to ASI is that digital intelligence possesses fundamental architectural advantages over biological brains. While human intelligence is constrained by the slow chemical signaling of neurons and the physical volume of the skull, digital systems benefit from:

Processing Speed: Digital substrates operate at the speed of light and electricity, orders of magnitude faster than the millisecond-scale firing of biological neurons.
Memory and Input/Output: Digital systems can access vast external databases with near-perfect recall and ingest information at bandwidths far exceeding human reading or sensory speeds.
Lossless Replication and Scaling: A digital agent can be perfectly copied. This allows for massive parallelization where thousands of instances of an agent can work on a single problem simultaneously, sharing their findings instantly—a feat impossible for human collaborators who must communicate via language.

However, even an ASI is not omnipotent. It remains subject to the laws of physics and the constraints of logic. The speed of light limits the coordination of geographically distributed compute nodes. Furthermore, complexity-theoretic bounds, such as the
𝑃
≠
𝑁
𝑃
P

=NP question, suggest that some computational problems are fundamentally "hard" regardless of the intelligence of the solver. There are also logical limits, such as Gödel’s Incompleteness Theorems, which imply that an ASI cannot prove all true statements within a formal system. Understanding these upper bounds is critical for realistic forecasting of what a superintelligence can and cannot achieve.

Technological Pathways to Superintelligence

Research identifies four primary, non-exclusive pathways that could drive the evolution from AGI toward ASI.

Scaling Compute and Data

The most visible pathway is the continuation of current trends in "effective compute." This is the product of hardware improvements (Moore's Law), increased financial investment in data centers, and gains in algorithmic efficiency. Current estimates suggest an effective compute growth rate of approximately 10x per year. If these scaling laws continue to hold, simply increasing the parameters, data, and compute of existing models might yield capabilities that naturally surpass human expert collectives.

Algorithmic Paradigm Shifts

While current models rely heavily on the transformer architecture and the next-token prediction objective, the path to ASI may involve fundamental shifts in how AI learns. This could include moving beyond "log-loss" minimization to more sophisticated forms of reasoning, or developing architectures that can learn more efficiently from raw data without the need for massive human-labeled datasets.

Recursive Self-Improvement

This pathway describes a "closed-loop" scenario where AI systems begin to contribute to their own development. An AGI could automate the process of curating higher-quality training data, optimizing neural architectures, or even designing more efficient hardware. This creates a feedback loop where each iteration of the AI is more capable than the last, potentially leading to an "intelligence explosion" where progress accelerates at a hyperbolic rate. This mirrors the transition from genetic evolution (slow) to cultural evolution (fast) seen in human history.

Group Agent Formation

ASI might not emerge as a single "super-brain," but rather as a highly coordinated collective of AGI agents. Just as human civilization achieves more than any single human could, a virtual economy of specialized AGI agents—interacting with high-bandwidth communication and perfect cooperation—could exhibit superhuman collective intelligence. This "group agent" approach leverages the scalability of digital substrates to solve problems through extreme division of labor.

Frictions and Potential Bottlenecks

Despite the potential for rapid growth, several "frictions" could impede the progress toward ASI. These bottlenecks represent the primary uncertainties in future AI forecasting.

The Data Wall: AI models have largely been trained on the vast but finite corpus of human-generated text and media. If this data is exhausted, scaling may stall. While synthetic (AI-generated) data is a potential solution, it carries the risk of "model collapse" where the AI begins to learn from its own errors, leading to a degradation in quality.

Resource Demands: The energy and material requirements for the next generation of supercomputers are immense. Continued scaling requires massive investments in power grids and the procurement of rare-earth materials for chips. Economic or environmental constraints could force a plateau in compute availability.

The Abstraction Barrier: This is a theoretical bottleneck suggesting that AI systems trained primarily on human language and concepts may be limited by the "human window" of understanding. If an AI cannot discover fundamentally new abstractions—concepts that humans do not have words for—it may remain capped at a high level of human competence rather than achieving true superintelligence.

Deliber

item.title: [论文] 让注意力学会“挑重点”看
item.description: 你如果用过那些能处理几十万字长文本的AI，可能会好奇：它们怎么在眨眼间找到答案，而不是被海量信息淹死？最近我看到一篇论文，就在解决这个问题，思路挺巧的。大语言模型处理长上下文时有个头疼的地方——标准的注意力机制复杂度是平方级的。想象你要读完一整本《三体》，每读一句话都得把前面所有内容回忆一遍，读到后面成本就爆炸了。随着代码库级分析、复杂任务流程和持久记忆系统的兴起，序列长度动不动就冲到几十万甚至...
item.content:encoded:
你如果用过那些能处理几十万字长文本的AI，可能会好奇：它们怎么在眨眼间找到答案，而不是被海量信息淹死？最近我看到一篇论文，就在解决这个问题，思路挺巧的。

大语言模型处理长上下文时有个头疼的地方——标准的注意力机制复杂度是平方级的。想象你要读完一整本《三体》，每读一句话都得把前面所有内容回忆一遍，读到后面成本就爆炸了。随着代码库级分析、复杂任务流程和持久记忆系统的兴起，序列长度动不动就冲到几十万甚至上百万token，这时全量注意力的计算和内存开销就成了训练和推理的主要瓶颈。

这篇工作的核心方法是叫MiniMax Sparse Attention（简称MSA），它给注意力装上了一个“索引器”。做法不是在计算时无脑扫全部历史，而是先快速筛选出与当前查询可能相关的少数几个段落，再用精细注意力去读这些段落。这样每步的计算量就稳定在常数级别，整体算下来几乎和序列长度成线性关系。

具体架构很有意思，分成两条分支。索引分支很轻量，它把输入投影到低维空间，然后算出每个查询token跟所有历史key token的初步相似度。这些token级别的分数再按“块”聚合（比如每128个token一块），取块内最高分作为该块的得分。最后选得分最高的前k个块，顺便把当前查询所在的那个块也强制保留，避免训练不稳定。主分支就只在这些被选中的块里做标准的高精度注意力运算。

这听起来像搜索引擎的初筛+精排流程，但难点在于：选择操作是离散的，不可导，没法直接用梯度训练。他们的处理办法是加了一个KL散度辅助损失——让索引分支去模仿主分支的注意力分布，主分支当“老师”。为了防止这个辅助任务把模型带偏，梯度被截断，不会反向传播到主分支或隐藏状态，只更新索引器那一点点参数。另外训练早期（比如前400亿token）先用全注意力，等索引器学得差不多了，再切换成稀疏注意力。

但算法效率不能只看纸上FLOPs。因为稀疏访问在GPU上可能很碎片化，理论节省未必变成实际加速。他们专门设计了一套CUDA kernel来配合。比如做top-k选择时只关心相对顺序，就跳过昂贵的指数运算；又因为选出的块数量少，迭代顺序改成以键值块为外层循环，减少读取开销。最终效果是：对于百万token的序列，预填充速度最高提升了14.2倍。

我觉得这个方法的价值在于，它不是事后诸葛亮式的附加加速，而是从预训练开始就原生集成的。也就是说，模型从头就学会了“挑重点看”的能力，而不是事后裁枝剪叶。他们也在论文中展示了可以把已有的密集检查点无缝转换成MSA模型。

当然局限性也有。比如这种架构对训练初期的设计依赖较高，索引器需要预热，而且超长序列的推理虽然预填充快了，但在自回归解码阶段KV cache的管理可能依然是个挑战。另外，索引器只有单头，在面对极端多头注意力的场景时，是否还能精准筛选，可能需要进一步探索。

总体来说，这个工作展示了一条通过算法-硬件协同设计让超长上下文推理真正落地的路径。感兴趣的话可以读原论文：MiniMax Sparse Attention [arXiv:2606.13392]。

📄 论文原文概述
The computational demand of Large Language Models (LLMs) has increased as applications shift toward processing ultra-long contexts, such as repository-scale code analysis, complex agentic workflows, and persistent memory systems. While standard Transformer architectures rely on the softmax attention mechanism, this component presents a significant scaling challenge: its computational and memory requirements grow quadratically with the sequence length. As the number of tokens increases to hundreds of thousands or millions, the cost of full attention becomes a primary bottleneck for both training and deployment.

To address this, researchers have introduced MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention mechanism designed to enable efficient ultra-long context processing. MSA is built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA), a variant where multiple query heads share a single key-value (KV) head to reduce memory overhead. Unlike fixed-pattern sparse attention methods that use static rules—such as attending only to a local window or specific "sink" tokens—MSA employs an adaptive, content-dependent selection mechanism. This allows the model to dynamically decide which parts of the past context are most relevant to the current query.

The significance of MSA lies in its ability to be "natively trained," meaning the selection mechanism is optimized during the initial pretraining of the model or through a seamless conversion process from existing pretrained checkpoints. By co-designing the algorithm with specialized GPU kernels, MSA translates theoretical reduction in operations into tangible wall-clock speedups, achieving up to a 14.2x increase in prefilling speed for sequences of one million tokens.

The Architecture of MiniMax Sparse Attention

The core innovation of MSA is its two-branch structure: the Index Branch and the Main Branch. Standard attention computes a similarity score for every query-key pair across the entire sequence. In contrast, MSA uses the Index Branch to identify a small subset of important blocks, and the Main Branch performs high-precision attention only on those selected blocks.

The Index Branch

The Index Branch is a lightweight component designed to predict which "blocks" of keys and values are necessary for the current query. To keep overhead low, it introduces one index query head per GQA group and a single index key head shared across all groups. The process follows these steps:

Projection: The input hidden states are projected into low-dimensional index queries
𝑄
𝑖
𝑑

​

and index keys
𝐾

​

.
Token-level Scoring: For every query token, it calculates a score against all previous key tokens.
Block-level Aggregation: Individual token scores are aggregated into block-level scores. If the sequence is divided into blocks of size
𝐵

​

, the score for a block
𝑗

Top-k Selection: For each GQA group, the system selects the
𝑘
k blocks with the highest scores. To ensure stability during training, the "local block"—the one containing the current query token—is always included in the selection.
The Main Branch

Once the top
𝑘
k blocks are identified, the Main Branch performs standard scaled dot-product attention. However, instead of looking at the entire history of length
𝑁
N, it only considers the
𝑘
×

​

tokens within the selected blocks. This design fixes the computational cost per query token to
𝑂

​

), effectively making the attention complexity linear with respect to sequence length after the initial indexing step.

Training a Differentiable Indexer

A significant challenge in sparse attention is that the "Top-k" selection operation is non-differentiable; you cannot pass a gradient through a choice of discrete indices. To train the Index Branch effectively, MSA utilizes an auxiliary learning signal called the KL Divergence Loss.

The Index Branch is trained to mimic the attention distribution of the Main Branch. Specifically, the model calculates the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the indexer's predicted scores and the actual attention weights produced by the Main Branch (averaged across heads within a group). The Main Branch acts as a "teacher," guiding the Index Branch to focus on the same areas that the full attention mechanism would prioritize.

To ensure this auxiliary loss does not interfere with the general learning of the model's backbone, several safeguards are implemented:

Gradient Detachment: Gradients from the KL loss are prevented from flowing back into the Main Branch or the primary hidden states. This ensures that the KL loss only updates the specific linear projections (
𝑊
𝑖
𝑑
𝑥
W
idx
​

) used for indexing.
Indexer Warmup: During the start of training (e.g., the first 40 billion tokens), the model uses full attention while the Index Branch learns to predict the distribution. Once the indexer reaches a stable state, the model switches to sparse attention for the remainder of the training process.

GPU Optimization and Efficiency

Theoretical efficiency in terms of Floating Point Operations (FLOPs) does not always lead to faster real-world performance because sparse memory accesses can be inefficient on modern hardware like GPUs. MSA addresses this through hardware-aware kernel design.

Specialized Kernels

The researchers developed custom CUDA kernels to handle the unique requirements of MSA:

Exp-free Top-k: Since the Top-k selection only needs the relative order of blocks, the kernels skip the expensive exponential operations typically used in softmax, saving significant compute time.
KV-Outer Iteration: Standard attention kernels often iterate over queries. MSA uses a "KV-outer" iteration order, which is more efficient when the number of selected blocks is small relative to the total sequence. This maximizes "arithmetic intensity"—the ratio of computation to memory access.
Load Balancing: Long-context tasks often involve "hotspots" where many queries attend to the same key blocks. MSA includes a scheduler that dynamically splits these busy blocks into smaller chunks, pre-assigning them to different processing units (CTAs) on the GPU to prevent bottlenecks.
Performance Gains

The impact of these optimizations is evident at massive scales. When processing a sequence of one million tokens on NVIDIA H800 GPUs:

Attention FLOPs: Reduced by 28.4x compared to standard GQA.
Prefill Latency: MSA achieves a 14.2x wall-clock speedup.
Decoding Latency: MSA achieves a 7.6x speedup.

Unlike standard attention, where the cost of adding a new token grows as the "history" gets longer, MSA maintains a nearly constant cost per token during decoding, provided the selection budget
𝑘
k remains fixed.

Experimental Results

MSA was validated using a 109-billion parameter Mixture of Experts (MoE) model trained on three trillion tokens. The researchers tested two scenarios: MSA-PT (training the sparse model from scratch) and MSA-CPT (converting a pretrained dense model into a sparse one via continued pretraining).

Capability Preservation

A common risk with sparse attention is the loss of general reasoning or "world knowledge" stored in the model. However, evaluations across a wide array of benchmarks showed that MSA models perform competitively with full-attention baselines.

Benchmark Full Attention (GQA) MSA-PT (Sparse)
MMLU (Knowledge) 75.3 75.8
GSM8K (Math) 83.3 84.1
HumanEval (Code) 68.3 70.1
AI2D (Multimodal) 73.1 74.3

The results indicate that the Index Branch successfully learns to retrieve the necessary information to maintain performance in reasoning, coding, and multimodal tasks.

Long-Context Performance

To test the model's ability to actually use long contexts, the researchers used the RULER and HELMET benchmarks, which require the model to find specific info

item.title: [论文] 一个能看完一部长电影还记住细节的AI模型
item.description: 你有没有想过，让AI看完一部长达一小时的视频，然后问它“那个戴红帽子的人第一次出现是在什么时候”，它真的能答上来？这个问题听起来简单，但技术上其实挺棘手的，因为处理的视频一长，模型要处理的信息量就会爆炸式增长，算力根本撑不住。今天聊的这篇论文，就是快手Keye团队搞的一个模型Kwai Keye-VL-2.0，他们专门针对这个长视频理解问题做了一系列优化，让模型能“看”得久、“记”得住。这个模型用...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，让AI看完一部长达一小时的视频，然后问它“那个戴红帽子的人第一次出现是在什么时候”，它真的能答上来？这个问题听起来简单，但技术上其实挺棘手的，因为处理的视频一长，模型要处理的信息量就会爆炸式增长，算力根本撑不住。今天聊的这篇论文，就是快手Keye团队搞的一个模型Kwai Keye-VL-2.0，他们专门针对这个长视频理解问题做了一系列优化，让模型能“看”得久、“记”得住。

这个模型用了一个叫Mixture-of-Experts（MoE）的架构，总共有300亿参数，但每次推理只激活30亿。你可以理解成团队里有十个人，每次干活只叫醒最擅长的那个人，既省力气效率又高。它的核心创新在于，把DeepSeek Sparse Attention这种稀疏注意力机制，适配到了一个混合专家模型里。

传统的注意力机制有个毛病，就是当输入变长时，计算量会按平方级别增长——如果视频长度翻一倍，计算量不是翻一倍，而是翻平方，这谁受得了。Keye-VL-2.0的做法是，用一个叫“Lightning Indexer”的东西，在处理长序列时只挑出最相关的一小部分内容来关注，而不是把所有内容都算一遍。这样一来，计算复杂度就从O(L²)降到了O(Lk)，k是一个固定的小数字，L远大于k。打个比方，就像你看一小时视频，不需要同时记住每一帧都在干什么，而是脑子里有个索引，需要用的时候能快速定位到相关镜头。这个优化让模型能无损处理长达256K token的上下文，大概是几小时视频的量。

训练过程也挺有意思，分了四个阶段，很像上学。最开始只训练最基础的视觉-语言对齐器（就像学会把看到的和说出来的对应上），然后慢慢加长上下文、增加任务难度，从图像理解到15秒短视频，再到GUI操作、复杂的视觉推理题，最后才开始处理一小时级别的长视频。这种渐进式学习，让模型在学新东西的同时不容易“忘本”。

说到“忘本”，这也是多模态模型的一个普遍问题。当你给模型教新技能（比如写代码、用工具），它原有的数学推理能力或者语言能力有时候会退化，这就是所谓的“灾难性遗忘”。Keye-VL-2.0的解决方案挺巧妙的：他们用了强化学习加一种叫“多教师在策略蒸馏”（MOPD）的方法——用13个在不同领域（代码、空间推理、OCR等）训练得特别好的“老师模型”，来给统一的“学生模型”做指引，让它在吸收各种专长技能的同时，基础的通用能力也不掉队。

实际效果怎么样？在长视频理解的测试集LongVideoBench上，这个300亿参数的模型拿到了74.1分，比参数多好几倍的Qwen3-VL-235B（2350亿参数）表现还好。更重要的是，它不光能理解整段视频讲了什么，还能做“时间锚定”——就是你问它“这人在视频第几分几秒说了这句话”，它能比较准确地给你找出来。这种能力在动作定位的测试里，也拿了同体量模型里的最好成绩。

不过话说回来，这篇技术报告没有提训练这样规模的模型到底花了多少算力和时间，这对于想要复现的人来说是个遗憾。另外，256K的上下文虽然已经很长了，但如果未来想要处理更长的实时流媒体内容，可能还需要新的结构创新。我觉得这项工作比较亮眼的地方在于，它把稀疏注意力和MoE给结合起来了，而且真的做出了可用的开源模型，这对于想在自己项目里尝试长视频理解的人，算是个挺好的参考。

论文：Kwai Keye-VL-2.0 Technical Report [arXiv:2606.10651]

📄 论文原文概述
Advancing Long-Context Multimodal Intelligence

The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) has recently shifted toward multimodal integration, where models process not just text, but images and videos. While short-video comprehension has seen significant progress, extending these capabilities to ultra-long contexts—such as hour-long videos—remains a major technical challenge. The Kwai Keye-VL-2.0-30B-A3B model, developed by the Keye Team at Kuaishou Group, addresses these hurdles by introducing a scalable architecture designed for long-horizon reasoning and complex agentic tasks.

Figure 1: The Keye-VL-2.0 model represents a step forward in integrating long-video understanding with agentic reasoning capabilities.

Keye-VL-2.0 is an open-source Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) multimodal foundation model featuring
30
B
30B total parameters, with
3
B
3B parameters activated during any single inference step. The project focuses on two primary goals: overcoming the infrastructural bottlenecks of extreme context scaling and resolving the algorithmic dilemma of "catastrophic forgetting" that often occurs when specialized capabilities are added to general-purpose models.

Architecture and the Sparse Attention Breakthrough

At the core of Keye-VL-2.0 is a heterogeneous architecture consisting of a Vision Encoder (ViT), a Language Decoder (LLM), and a specialized MLP Projector. The LLM backbone is built upon Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507, providing the fundamental reasoning and knowledge base.

A significant contribution of this work is the adaptation of DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA) into a Group Query Attention (GQA) based multimodal framework. Traditional dense attention mechanisms suffer from quadratic computational complexity relative to the input sequence length
𝐿
L. In ultra-long contexts, this complexity, denoted as
𝑂
(
𝐿
2
)
O(L
2
), makes processing hour-long videos computationally prohibitive and memory-intensive.

By integrating DSA, the model utilizes a "Lightning Indexer" that identifies a Top-k sparse index set for attention. This shift transforms the attention complexity from
𝑂
(
𝐿
2
)
O(L
2
) to:

𝑂
(
𝐿
𝑘
)
O(Lk)

where
𝑘
k is a fixed number of tokens retrieved, and
𝐿
≫
𝑘
L≫k. This optimization allows for the lossless processing of contexts up to
256
K
256K tokens, enabling the model to "watch" and reason over long videos without the aggressive frame subsampling that typically leads to information loss.

A Four-Stage Pre-training Curriculum

To build a model capable of both broad perception and deep reasoning, the researchers employed a phased training curriculum. This structured approach ensures that the model gradually acquires foundational visual-language alignment before tackling more complex long-range dependencies.

Figure 2: The four-stage training process, scaling from initial projector alignment to
256
K
256K long-context extension.

Stage 0: Projector Initialization: The ViT and LLM are kept frozen while the MLP projector is trained on
8
K
8K sequences to establish the initial mapping between visual features and language representations.
Stage 1: General Multimodal Pre-training: The model is trained on
1
T
1T tokens with a
32
K
32K context length. This stage emphasizes general image perception, OCR, and short-video (15-second) understanding.
Stage 2: Multi-Task Capability Injection: Training continues on
2
T
2T tokens with the context window expanded to
64
K
64K. Here, the model is introduced to specialized tasks like GUI interaction, grounding, counting, and complex visual STEM problems.
Stage 3: Long-Context Extension: The final stage extends the sequence length to
256
K
256K, training on
500
B
500B tokens. This stage is critical for handling hour-long videos and massive document sets, using a
1
:
1
1:1 mix of long and short samples to maintain general performance.
Post-Training and the Multimodal Alignment Dilemma

A recurring issue in AI development is the "Multimodal Alignment Dilemma," where training a model on new, specialized tasks (like coding or tool-use) causes its performance on foundational tasks (like mathematics or linguistics) to degrade. Keye-VL-2.0 tackles this through a sophisticated post-training pipeline.

Reinforcement Learning (RL)

The researchers utilized Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) across several domains. This includes:

Video RL: Optimizing temporal event tracking and long-range information aggregation.
Agentic RL: Improving multi-step environment interactions for search and tool-use.
General RL: Enhancing reasoning through verifiable ground-truth rewards in STEM and math.
Cross-Modal Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD)

To consolidate the diverse skills learned during RL without losing foundational knowledge, the team introduced MOPD. This method uses
13
13 specialized RL-trained "teachers" (experts in specific domains like code, spatial reasoning, or OCR) to provide token-level feedback to the student model. By routing specific queries to the most relevant teacher, the unified MoE backbone can absorb specialized intelligence while remaining a robust generalist.

Comprehensive Video Understanding

The results of this architectural and training strategy are most evident in the model's performance on video benchmarks. Keye-VL-2.0-30B-A3B shows strong capabilities in aggregating information over long durations.

Figure 3: Comparative performance of Keye-VL-2.0 against other leading models on long-video and temporal grounding benchmarks.

On LongVideoBench, the model achieved a score of 74.1, outperforming significantly larger models like Qwen3-VL-235B. Its performance on Video-MME-v2 (42.4 accuracy) further highlights its ability to process 512 sampled frames effectively.

Beyond general understanding, the model excels at "Temporal Grounding"—the ability to locate specific moments in a video based on a text prompt. Utilizing the TimeLens framework, Keye-VL-2.0 achieved leading mIoU scores across ActivityNet, QVHighlights, and Charades subsets, indicating high precision in fine-grained temporal localization.

Figure 4: An example of dense video captioning and scene segmentation, demonstrating the model's ability to track events over time.

Agentic Intelligence and Real-World Reasoning

Keye-VL-2.0 is designed not just to see, but to act. This "agentic" capability involves using tools, writing code, and interacting with environments to solve problems.

Coding: In LiveCodeBench v6, it achieved a score of 64.2, demonstrating strong algorithmic reasoning and the ability to self-correct based on execution feedback.
Tool Use: The model ranked first on the τ2-Bench (82.6) and VitaBench (33.1), showing proficiency in selecting tools, tracking states across multi-turn conversations, and recovering from errors.

The model also maintains high performance in traditional vision-language tasks. It exhibits strong spatial reasoning, as seen in complex room layout tasks, and high accuracy in visual mathematical problems.

Figure 5: Example of the model's spatial reasoning capabilities in identifying and locating objects within a complex 3D environment.

Figure 6: Visual reasoning example where the model analyzes a medical diagram to explain biological processes.

Implementation and Deployment Efficiency

Beyond accuracy, the Keye Team focused on the practicalities of training and inference. Deploying
30
B
30B parameter models with
256
K
256K context requires extreme optimization.

ViT-LM Heterogeneous Parallelism: The training system separates the processing of the Vision Encoder and Language Model onto different GPU groups, balancing the load to ensure maximum hardware utilization.
Chunk ViT: In inference, video frames are processed in chunks to manage memory.
DSA Optimization: The custom kernels for sparse attention significantly reduce the "prefill" (initial processing) and "decode" (generating the response) costs for long sequences. At a
128
K
128K token position, the prefill

item.title: [论文] 用软令牌给长上下文“瘦身”
item.description: 你有没有遇到过这样的情况：让大模型读一本小说、分析一堆文档，结果它半天不吭声，GPU内存也被占得满满的？问题就出在KV缓存上——模型在阅读时，会把每个词的注意力信息都存下来，上下文越长，缓存就跟着线性膨胀，内存和延迟都吃不消。最近看到一篇论文，没有去删减那些缓存条目，也没有粗暴地摘掉输入里的字词，而是给模型配了个“浓缩器”，把一整段文本直接压成几个密集的数值向量，解码器再直接从这些向量里读取信息...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有遇到过这样的情况：让大模型读一本小说、分析一堆文档，结果它半天不吭声，GPU内存也被占得满满的？问题就出在KV缓存上——模型在阅读时，会把每个词的注意力信息都存下来，上下文越长，缓存就跟着线性膨胀，内存和延迟都吃不消。最近看到一篇论文，没有去删减那些缓存条目，也没有粗暴地摘掉输入里的字词，而是给模型配了个“浓缩器”，把一整段文本直接压成几个密集的数值向量，解码器再直接从这些向量里读取信息。我第一反应是：这不就是给上下文找了个信息蒸馏工吗？

他们把这个框架叫做“潜在上下文语言模型”（LCLM）。思路不算全新，但之前类似做法往往在压缩率和信息保真度之间踩不好平衡：要么压得太多导致模型忘掉关键细节，要么压得太轻，该省的内存和延迟没省下。这篇工作的一大贡献是摸索出了一套好用的架构配置和训练流程，让这种软令牌压缩能在实际长文任务上跑出竞争力。

具体怎么做呢？整个流水线分成编码器、池化、适配器、解码器四步。编码器先把输入切成一个个窗口，每个窗口处理后得到一堆隐状态向量。接着池化算子把它们聚合成更少的“潜令牌”，比如说把连续的多个隐状态做平均，得到一个代表向量——他们试了好几种池化方式，发现均值池化在16倍压缩比这种高压缩场景下既简单又可靠。然后适配器（一个不起眼的两层MLP）负责把编码器出来的维度映射到解码器要用的维度。最后，解码器（一个正常的预训练大模型）就直接读这些潜令牌，好像它们本来就是嵌入向量一样。

我个人觉得比较巧妙的是训练策略。直接端到端训练整个系统，很容易把基座模型的语言能力带偏。他们搞了个多阶段方案：先冻结编码器和解码器，只让适配器学会对齐两边空间；然后逐渐解冻编码器，让整个前端学习如何产生更富信息量的潜令牌；最后再加入解码器的微调，小心翼翼地在保持原始能力的同时适应浓缩语境。另外编码器的初始化也很有讲究，用预训练的嵌入模型（比如某个现成的文本向量模型）比用普通的因果语言模型好得多，生产出来的潜表示质量更高。

看结果的话，他们对比了各种KV缓存压缩方法和硬令牌删除方法。在RULER和LongBench这类长上下文基准上，LCLM画出了一条新的帕累托前沿：同样的首次令牌生成时间（TTFT），它的准确率明显更高；而达到类似准确率时，TTFT能压到几十分之一。比如在16倍压缩比下，内存占用和预填充延迟直接砍掉九成以上，但性能只比不压缩的模型低一点点。

我觉得这项工作的现实意义在于，它为大规模落地的长上下文推理提供了一条“软缓存”路径。因为生成的都是统一形状的潜令牌，可以无缝接入vLLM这类推理引擎，不用像某些KV缓存压缩方法那样因为非均匀结构而难以加速。不过它也不是没有局限。目前的训练流程还是需要针对特定基座模型和压缩比从头训练一个压缩模块，如果未来能做成即插即用、动态调节压缩率的版本，会更灵活。另外潜令牌虽然信息密度高，但也失去了原本符号语言的可解释性——你没法像看删减后的提示那样，直观检查哪些信息被保留了。这可能在一些需要审计或调试的场景里是个短板。

总的来说，我觉得这个方向挺值得关注：它不跟已有的缓存精简策略卷“哪些该丢”，而是换了个角度，让模型学会“如何高效地记”。就像我们给人读一份长报告之前，先请另一个同事把它浓缩成一份要点向量，既省时间，又不丢关键判断。

（论文：End-to-End Context Compression at Scale [arXiv:2606.09659]）

📄 论文原文概述
The scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) to handle increasingly long contexts has become a central focus of artificial intelligence research. As these models are deployed for complex tasks such as multi-document analysis, long-form code generation, and multi-turn agentic interactions, the computational overhead of managing millions of tokens becomes a significant bottleneck. Specifically, the Key-Value (KV) cache, which stores the intermediate activations of the attention mechanism, grows linearly with sequence length. This growth leads to prohibitive memory requirements and increased inference latency, particularly during the "prefill" stage where the model processes the initial prompt.

To address these challenges, researchers have developed various context compression techniques. However, existing methods often face a trade-off between efficiency and fidelity. High-compression methods often degrade the model's performance on information-dense tasks, while high-fidelity methods may not offer substantial wall-clock speedups or memory savings in production environments.

This paper introduces Latent Context Language Models (LCLMs), a framework for end-to-end context compression at scale. LCLMs utilize an encoder-decoder architecture to compress raw text into a sequence of "soft tokens" or latent representations. By optimizing the architecture and training recipe, the authors demonstrate that LCLMs can achieve significant reductions in memory and latency while maintaining performance comparable to uncompressed models across diverse benchmarks.

Figure 1: Comparison of LCLMs against various KV cache compression baselines. LCLMs establish a new Pareto frontier, achieving higher accuracy at significantly lower Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) across long-context benchmarks like RULER and LongBench.

The Context Compression Landscape

Current approaches to managing long contexts generally fall into two categories: KV cache compression and hard-token compression.

KV cache compression methods, such as SnapKV or KVzip, aim to reduce the memory footprint by evicting less important entries from the cache after the full context has been processed. While these can reduce the memory required for the decoding stage, they often require the full context to be processed first, which means the initial prefill latency remains high. Furthermore, many of these methods are query-dependent, meaning the compressed cache is optimized for a specific question and may not be reusable for different queries within the same context. Integration with optimized inference engines like vLLM is also challenging due to the non-uniform nature of these compressed caches.

Hard-token compression involves deleting or rewriting tokens in the input prompt before it reaches the model. While this reduces both prefill and decoding costs, it is inherently lossy. Fine-grained details, such as specific variable names in code or precise numbers in a technical document, can be lost during the summarization or pruning process.

LCLMs take a different path by using "soft-token" compression. Instead of selecting or deleting discrete tokens, an encoder transforms blocks of text into continuous latent vectors. These vectors are designed to retain the maximum amount of relevant information for the decoder. This approach allows for parallelizable compression, as the encoder can process different parts of the context simultaneously, and it generates a uniform cache that is fully compatible with standard inference engines.

Architecture of Latent Context Language Models

The LCLM architecture consists of four primary components: an encoder, a pooling operator, an adapter, and a decoder. Given an input sequence
𝑥
1
:
𝑇
x
1:T
​

and a desired compression ratio
𝑁
N, the goal is to produce a significantly shorter sequence of latent tokens that the decoder can use for generation.

The process begins with the encoder, which processes the input in windows of size
𝑊
W. For each window
𝑤
𝑖
w
i
​

, the encoder produces a set of hidden states
ℎ
(
𝑖
)
h
(i)
. The authors found that using a pre-trained embedding model for the encoder initialization significantly improves the quality of the learned representations compared to using a standard causal language model.

Next, a pooling operator aggregates these hidden states into a smaller number of latent tokens
𝑧
(
𝑖
)
z
(i)
. The study explored several pooling strategies, including mean pooling (averaging states), concatenation (joining sequential states), and token-based pooling (using special tokens like EOS). Through extensive searching, mean pooling was identified as a robust default that balances efficiency and performance, particularly at higher compression ratios like
16
×
16×.

The adapter then maps these latent tokens from the encoder's dimensionality
𝑑
enc
d
enc
​

to the decoder's dimensionality
𝑑
dec
d
dec
​

. The resulting projected tokens
𝑠
1
:
𝑀
s
1:M
​

serve as the compressed context. While more complex attention-based adapters were tested, a simple two-layer MLP was found to be more compute-efficient while achieving lower training loss.

Finally, the decoder—a standard pre-trained LLM—is conditioned on these latent tokens. Because the latents appear to the decoder as a sequence of embeddings, the decoder can process them using its standard attention mechanism.

Figure 2: The LCLM pipeline (left) involves encoding, pooling, and adapting input tokens into latent representations. The training loss curves (right) illustrate the impact of various architectural choices, such as window size and masking types, on model performance.

A Multi-Stage Training Recipe

Training a soft-token compressor that does not degrade the base model's capabilities is notoriously difficult. To overcome this, the authors developed a multi-stage training recipe:

Stage 0: Adapter Warmup: The encoder and decoder are frozen. Only the MLP adapter is trained to align the encoder's output space with the decoder's input space.
Stage 1: Encoder Training: The decoder remains frozen while the encoder and adapter are updated. This focuses the learning on the compression task without risking "catastrophic forgetting" in the large decoder.
Stage 2: End-to-End Joint Training: All components are trained together. The decoder is unfrozen with a lower learning rate to allow it to adapt to the latent inputs without losing its general language capabilities.
Stage 3: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): The model is fine-tuned on a curated mixture of instruction-following, reasoning, and long-context data.

A critical component of this training is the use of interleaved compression. During pre-training, sequences are divided into segments where some are compressed and others are left as raw text. The model is trained to predict the next token in the uncompressed segments by conditioning on the preceding compressed segments. Additionally, a "reconstruction" objective is used, where the model must reproduce the original text from its compressed latent form, encouraging the encoder to preserve fine-grained details.

Empirical Performance and Efficiency

The LCLM models were evaluated against state-of-the-art KV cache compression methods across a variety of tasks and context lengths.

Efficiency Gains

LCLMs provide dramatic improvements in both Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) and peak memory usage. Because the encoder can process the context in parallel batches, the prefill time is significantly reduced. In benchmarks, LCLMs were up to 8.8 times faster than baseline methods on a 4k context and 5.2 times faster on a 64k context at
16
×
16× compression.

Regarding memory, LCLMs allow the peak GPU memory to remain relatively stable even as the context grows. While standard models or KV-eviction methods might run out of memory at 512k or 1M tokens, LCLMs with high compression ratios can handle these lengths on a single GPU by keeping the logical sequence length seen by the decoder small.

Figure 3: Sc

item.title: [论文] 用一棵“想法树”做研究
item.description: 你有没有想过，现在的 AI 编程助手其实很像一个执行力超强的实习生——你让它改代码、修 bug，它做得很快。但如果让它自己主导一个研究项目，不断试错、调整方向，最后提出一个真正有效的改进，就没那么容易了。因为真正的研究从来不是一条直线，而是充满了分支、回头路和意外发现。最近看到一篇论文，就专门在解决这个问题。他们设计了一个叫 Arbor 的框架，核心思路是：让 AI 像科学家一样，用一棵“假设树...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，现在的 AI 编程助手其实很像一个执行力超强的实习生——你让它改代码、修 bug，它做得很快。但如果让它自己主导一个研究项目，不断试错、调整方向，最后提出一个真正有效的改进，就没那么容易了。因为真正的研究从来不是一条直线，而是充满了分支、回头路和意外发现。

最近看到一篇论文，就专门在解决这个问题。他们设计了一个叫 Arbor 的框架，核心思路是：让 AI 像科学家一样，用一棵“假设树”来组织和推进整个研究过程，而不是埋头做一堆零散的实验。

问题出在哪？现在的 AI 做研究，通常是给定一个初始代码库和“想办法把这个性能指标搞上去”这样的目标，然后反复生成候选方案、跑测试、看结果。但这种方式很容易迷失。比如改了一堆参数，有的有效、有的无效，AI 很难把各个实验的教训串起来，也不知道什么时候该换个思路，什么时候该在某个分支上深挖。而且因为没有长期记忆和整体规划，它经常会在同一个坑里反复跌倒。

Arbor 想了一个挺直观的解法：把研究过程变成一个可管理的探索结构。他们把研究状态抽象成一棵假设树，树上每个节点都是一个具体、可验证的假设，比如“改进优化器的稳定性”，下面的子节点可能就是“只对非偏置参数施加权重衰减”。这样一来，想法的层级关系、哪些试过、结果怎么样，全都能追溯。

我第一次看到这个设计的时候，觉得特别像我们自己做研究时画的思维导图，但它是动态的、由 AI 自己维护的。

为了管好这棵树，Arbor 把工作拆分成了两种角色。一个是协调器，负责全局战略，决定下一步该探索哪个树枝，从失败里提炼教训，但绝不亲手改代码。另一个是执行器，领到一个具体的小任务，比如“用这个假设去改代码，在开发集上跑分，带回完整的实验证据”。每个执行器都在独立的环境里干活，失败了也不会把主干代码搞乱，也不会干扰其他并行实验。我猜这个隔离设计对长周期研究特别关键，毕竟现实里没人希望一个不成熟的尝试破坏了当前最好的版本。

这棵树真正有意思的地方是它的“精细化”过程，论文里叫假设树精炼。除了记录假设本身，每个节点还会存下实验结果的洞察，以及元数据。比如叶节点会总结“为什么这个权重衰减方案不行——可能过拟合了验证集”，而上层节点会把子节点的洞察汇总成更宏观的认知，像“在低数据量下，增加正则化需要配合更小的学习率”。这样一来，整棵树就不再只是待办清单，而是有“研究记忆”和“推理痕迹”的知识网络。如果某个分支反复失败，协调器甚至会主动生成“负面约束”，避免将来再碰类似思路。

研究循环大概分六步：先看树的状态，有哪些待验证的假设，最近的实验结果教会了什么；然后想出新假设（尤其会避开已失败的方向）；选出最有潜力的去执行；派发执行器跑开发集评测；把结果反向传播回树上，更新各级洞察；最后决定是砍掉这个分支，还是把成果合并进主版本。

为了防止过度在开发集上调优走火入魔，Arbor 还设了一个“合并门”：任何改进想要真正被接受，必须在一个独立留出的测试集上表现更好，就像科学发现的独立验证。这点我觉得特别清醒。

他们测试了六个不同领域的研究任务，包括设计优化器、改进神经网络架构、调超参数等，还在一个叫 MLE-Bench Lite 的机器学习工程基准上跑了实验。虽然没有给特别炸裂的数字，但论文显示 Arbor 在多项任务上都比直接用最强的基座模型（比如 Claude 3.5 Sonnet）直接做研究要好出一截，在有些任务上甚至接近或超过了人类专家的表现。更关键的是，Arbor 展现出的探索效率和错误利用率，明显比单次试错式方法更可持续。

当然，它也有局限。目前这套机制还比较依赖一个很强的语言模型来当协调器，如果底层模型本身缺乏领域知识，或者幻觉严重，生成的假设质量可能会拖累整棵树。而且在资源调度上，虽然能并行探索，但怎么分配算力给不同分支，还是个更开放的问题。另外，所有实验都限定在自动可评测的场景下，对于探索型、需要人类直觉参与的开放式科学问题，这条路还有很长。

总的来说，我觉得这个工作的价值不在于它把某个具体任务做到了极致，而是提出了一种可组合、可解释的自主研究框架。它把“做研究”这个模糊的事，拆解成了构建假设树、提炼洞察、独立验证这几个环节，让我们看到：也许离能独立推动研究进展的 AI，又近了一小步。

（论文：Toward Generalist Autonomous Research via Hypothesis-Tree Refinement [arXiv:2606.11926]）

📄 论文原文概述
The pursuit of autonomous agents capable of conducting scientific research marks a shift from simple task execution to long-horizon optimization. While current AI agents can edit code, use tools, and execute experiments, they often struggle with the iterative and non-linear nature of scientific discovery. Real-world research is characterized by uncertainty, failed attempts, and the need to synthesize insights from multiple experiments to guide future directions.

To address these challenges, researchers have introduced Arbor, a framework designed for Autonomous Optimization (AO). Arbor moves beyond sequential trial-and-error by maintaining a persistent, hierarchical research state called a Hypothesis Tree. This structure allows the system to manage competing ideas, learn from both successes and failures, and ensure that progress is validated through rigorous testing.

Figure 1: The Arbor framework separates global strategic planning (Coordinator) from local experimental execution (Executors), using a Hypothesis Tree as a persistent research memory.

Formalizing Autonomous Optimization

Autonomous Optimization (AO) is a formalization of the research process where an agent is tasked with improving an initial research artifact
𝑀
M—such as a machine learning model, a codebase, or a dataset—to maximize a specific objective metric
𝐸
E.

In a typical AO setting, the agent does not receive step-by-step instructions. Instead, it is provided with:

An initial codebase or repository (the material).
A research goal expressed in natural language.
A development evaluator
𝐸
𝑑
𝑒
𝑣
E
dev
​

for iterative feedback.
A held-out test evaluator
𝐸
𝑡
𝑒
𝑠
𝑡
E
test
​

for final validation.

The primary challenge in AO is sustaining progress over long durations. Unlike short coding tasks, research requires navigating a branching path of hypotheses. An agent must decide which direction is most promising, allocate computational resources efficiently, and avoid overfitting to the development environment.

The Arbor Framework: Global Strategy vs. Local Execution

Arbor introduces a structural separation of concerns to manage the complexity of long-horizon research. It employs two distinct types of agents: a long-lived Coordinator and short-lived Executors.

The Coordinator is responsible for the high-level research strategy. It maintains the global research state, determines which hypotheses to test, and integrates results into a growing body of knowledge. It never edits the target codebase directly.

The Executors are specialized agents dispatched by the Coordinator to perform specific, bounded tasks. Each Executor is given a single hypothesis and an isolated environment (a git worktree). Its job is to implement the proposed change, run the development evaluator, and report back with structured evidence. This isolation ensures that experimental failures in one branch do not corrupt the "best" version of the artifact or interfere with other simultaneous investigations.

Hypothesis-Tree Refinement (HTR)

The core innovation of Arbor is Hypothesis-Tree Refinement (HTR). Rather than a linear log of attempts, Arbor organizes its research as a tree. Each node
𝑛
n in the tree represents a research unit defined by the triple
⟨
ℎ
𝑛

𝜄
𝑛
,
𝜇
𝑛

n
​

,ι
n
​

,μ
n
​

⟩:

Hypothesis (
ℎ
𝑛
h
n
​

): A specific, verifiable claim about how to improve the artifact. The tree hierarchy represents granularity: root nodes are broad strategies (e.g., "Improve optimizer stability"), while child nodes are concrete implementations (e.g., "Apply weight decay only to non-bias parameters").
Insight (
𝜄
𝑛
ι
n
​

): A distilled interpretation of experimental results. For leaf nodes, this summarizes why a specific implementation worked or failed. For internal nodes, insights are aggregated from children to form a higher-level understanding of a research direction.
Metadata (
𝜇
𝑛
μ
n
​

): Operational data, including the node's status (pending, running, or merged), development scores, and references to the specific code branch.

This tree serves three critical functions: it acts as the search frontier to keep track of active leads, a long-term memory to store evidence, and an auditable record that connects every change to a motivating hypothesis.

The Iterative Research Cycle

Arbor operates in a six-step cycle managed by the Coordinator to evolve the hypothesis tree and the research artifact:

Observe: The Coordinator reviews the current state of the tree, including the active frontier, recent experimental results, and cumulative insights.
Ideate: Based on the evidence, the Coordinator proposes new child hypotheses. Crucially, it uses "negative constraints" from failed nodes to avoid repeating mistakes.
Select: The system chooses which pending hypotheses to execute next, balancing the exploration of new ideas with the exploitation of successful directions.
Dispatch: Executors are sent to isolated environments to implement and test the selected hypotheses using
𝐸
𝑑
𝑒
𝑣
E
dev
​

.
Backpropagate: Once an Executor completes its task, the results are written to the tree. Local insights are "backpropagated" up the tree, where they are abstracted into broader lessons.
Decide: The Coordinator determines whether to prune a failing branch or attempt to merge a successful one.

To prevent overfitting, Arbor uses a held-out merge gate. A candidate improvement
𝑀
𝑐
𝑎

𝑎

𝑒
M
candidate
​

is only merged into the "current best"
𝑀
𝑏
𝑒
𝑠
𝑡
M
best
​

if it demonstrates a strict improvement on the held-out
𝐸
𝑡
𝑒
𝑠
𝑡
E
test
​

. This mimics the scientific standard of validating discoveries on independent data.

Empirical Evaluation

The effectiveness of Arbor was tested across two major benchmarks: a custom AO Task Suite and the machine learning engineering benchmark MLE-Bench Lite.

The AO Task Suite

The researchers developed six real-world research tasks covering diverse domains:

Model Training: Designing optimizers and architectures.
Harness Engineering: Improving evaluation infrastructure for web agents and terminal benchmarks.
Data Synthesis: Generating high-quality data for mathematical reasoning and search agents.

Arbor outperformed strong baselines like Codex and Claude Code across all tasks, achieving more than 2.5 times the average relative gain on held-out tests. For example, in the "BrowseComp" task (improving a search-agent harness), Arbor increased accuracy from 45.3% to 67.7%, while the best baseline reached only 53.3%.

Figure 2: Performance comparison across six research tasks. (a) Example of a hypothesis tree for math synthesis. (b) Progress over time showing how merged nodes drive the best score. (c) Normalized gains showing Arbor's performance relative to baselines.

MLE-Bench Lite

On the MLE-Bench Lite benchmark, Arbor achieved an 81.8% "Any Medal" rate and a 77.3% "Gold Medal" rate when using a GPT-5.5 backbone. These results represent a significant state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating that the Hypothesis-Tree structure is highly effective for machine learning engineering tasks.

Why Structure Matters: Insights and Transferability

Ablation studies revealed that the hierarchical tree structure and the use of "insights" are both vital. Removing the tree and using a flat list of experiments significantly reduced performance. Even more interestingly, keeping the tree but disabling the "insight backpropagation"—the process of abstracting local results into global lessons—led to the lowest performance. This suggests that the value of the framework lies in its ability to build a semantic model of the problem over time.

Arbor also demonstrated cross-task transferability. A search harness optimized by Arbor on one set of tasks was found to improve performance on completely unseen search-agent benchmarks without any additional tuning. This indicates that the framework is capable of discovering generalizable design principles rather than just "hacking" a specific met

item.title: [论文] 给语言模型戴上一副 ICA 眼镜
item.description: 你有没有想过，像 GPT 这样的大模型，内部成千上万的神经元究竟在表达什么？现在大家常用的方法是训练一个“稀疏自编码器”（SAE），像请一位翻译，把稠密的激活信号拆成一堆可理解的特征。但这个翻译训练起来很贵——要喂海量数据、占用大量 GPU，模型每一层都得调参，实在有点麻烦。最近有篇论文换了个思路：与其费力训练一个新字典，不如先用统计学里的老方法，直接从模型自身的激活几何里看看有没有现成的结构。...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，像 GPT 这样的大模型，内部成千上万的神经元究竟在表达什么？现在大家常用的方法是训练一个“稀疏自编码器”（SAE），像请一位翻译，把稠密的激活信号拆成一堆可理解的特征。但这个翻译训练起来很贵——要喂海量数据、占用大量 GPU，模型每一层都得调参，实在有点麻烦。最近有篇论文换了个思路：与其费力训练一个新字典，不如先用统计学里的老方法，直接从模型自身的激活几何里看看有没有现成的结构。这有点像，你不用立刻去拍昂贵的 CT，先用手边的 X 光片瞄一眼，也许就能发现不少东西。

他们用的工具叫独立成分分析（ICA），这东西几十年前就有了，专门找数据里最“非高斯”的方向。为什么非高斯这么关键？因为完全随机的白噪声是高斯分布，而可解释的特征往往很挑剔，只对特定输入强烈响应，分布上有“厚尾”，也就是峰度特别高。所以，找那些最偏离正态分布的方向，就很可能是概念特征。但直接把 ICA 搬到 Transformer 激活上会踩坑：有些 token（比如开头符、分隔符）的激活值大得离谱，会像几块巨石一样，让 ICA 算法的收敛陷入泥潭。于是作者做了一个叫 ICALens 的流程，加了三个接地气的改进。

第一招是行归一化，对每个激活向量按 L2 范数缩放一下，这样那些模值超大的 token 就不会统治整个优化过程，收敛变得又快又稳。第二招，把收敛判据调得更宽松：不要求所有成分都严格收敛，只要 95% 的成分满足一个叫 LIM 的指标，就算这层过关。这避免因为几个噪声方向难优化就整层丢弃，很实用。最后一招叫自适应重塑，如果某层在全维度上实在收敛不了，算法就自动降低要提取的成分数量，重试一遍，保证给每一层都能提供一个尽量高分辨率的字典。跑完这个流程，你会得到一套“阅读地图”和“书写地图”，可以轻松地把激活投影到这些 ICA 成分上，或者反过来向模型内部写入信号，做因果实验。

那这样找到的方向，真的比随机方向或者 SAE 学到的东西更可解释吗？作者用峰度来量化非高斯性，发现 ICA 方向的峰度显著更高，比很多公开的 SAE 解码方向还要“尖峰”。这就是说，ICA 特别擅长揪出激活空间里那些最挑剔的维度，而挑剔往往意味着它们只关心某些具体模式，更容易被人看懂。

他们还引入了一个蛮直观的指标叫“有效感受野”（ERF），衡量一个成分需要往前看多少个 token 才能维持当前激活。ERF=1 就是纯本地特征，只在乎当前词；ERF 高则是上下文依赖型特征，比如跟踪话题或句子里的远距离语法状态。结果发现一个挺有意思的规律：峰度越高的成分，ERF 往往越小。也就是说，那些统计上最非高斯的方向，通常对应着词缀、大小写、特定词汇这些一眼就能看到的局部模式，解释起来自然容易。而峰度稍低、ERF 偏大的成分，则关联着更抽象的语义或语境，虽然难猜一些，但也更丰富。

为了确认这些统计方向对人来说是否真有意义，研究者请专家随机抽查了 150 个成分，仔细看它们的最高激活例句，手动设计对比提示，然后打标签。结果发现了五花八门的特征种类：有专门响应“-ing”词尾的，有追踪“if-then”句法结构的，有表示“金融”“皇室”“位置”等话题的，甚至有标记句子开头的位置特征。虽然这只是简简单单的线性分解，却已经从词法、句法一路铺捉到了语义层面，像个轻量版的“概念探测器”。

当然，这个方法也有它的边界。ICA 只能找线性方向，模型里很多非线性纠缠的特征它抓不住；而且它不像精心训练的 SAE 那样可以灵活控制稀疏度，分辨率相对固定。但在我眼里，它的定位很清晰：一个几乎零成本的“第一透镜”。下次你想快速窥探一个陌生模型的内部结构，不用花大半天训练 SAE，先跑一遍 ICALens，也许就能看到不少有趣的模式，再决定要不要深入。这就像给模型随手戴上一副现成的眼镜，虽然度数不一定完美匹配，但已经足够让你看清大概的轮廓。

（论文：ICA Lens: Interpreting Language Models Without Training Another Dictionary [arXiv:2606.11722]）

📄 论文原文概述
Mechanistic interpretability aims to reverse-engineer the internal computations of large language models (LLMs) by decomposing their high-dimensional activations into understandable, "monosemantic" features. Currently, the dominant paradigm for this task involves training Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs). These models learn an overcomplete dictionary of directions that can reconstruct activations using only a few active features at a time. However, the computational cost of SAEs is immense; they require vast amounts of activation data, massive storage for dictionary checkpoints, and significant GPU time for training and hyperparameter tuning across every model layer and activation site.

The "ICA Lens" research explores an alternative question: how much interpretable structure is already visible from the geometry of LLM activations before training a dedicated neural dictionary? To answer this, the authors revisit Independent Component Analysis (ICA), a classical statistical method designed to find linear projections that are as non-Gaussian as possible. By introducing a refined workflow called ICALens, the study demonstrates that a compact basis of non-Gaussian directions can recover many of the same features found by SAEs, providing a lightweight "first lens" for model exploration.

Figure 1: An interactive visualization of ICA components in GPT-2 Small (Layer 6). The tool allows researchers to inspect how specific components track concepts like financial terms or syntactic structures across a sentence.

The ICALens Pipeline

While ICA is a well-established technique, applying it to modern LLM activations presents unique challenges. Transformer activations often contain high-magnitude "outlier" tokens (such as the first token or common delimiters) that can cause standard ICA algorithms like FastICA to fail or converge very slowly. To make ICA practical for LLMs, the authors developed three specific technical refinements:

Row-Normalization: Before processing, each activation vector
𝑥

Robust Convergence (p95-LIM): Standard FastICA implementations use a strict "worst-case" stopping criterion. ICALens introduces a more robust fallback: if 95% of the components have converged according to the Linearly Independent Metric (LIM), the layer is accepted. This criterion, denoted as

Adaptive Refitting: LLM layers vary in complexity. If a layer fails to converge at full resolution (where the number of components
𝑚

The resulting artifact includes a reading map
𝑅

𝐷

Quantifying Non-Gaussianity and Context

The core intuition behind using ICA is that interpretable features are not distributed like random noise. Instead, they are "selective"—they activate strongly on specific tokens or within particular contexts. In statistical terms, this selectivity creates "heavy tails" in the distribution of activations, which can be measured using excess kurtosis
𝜅
(
𝑣
)
κ(v).

The authors find that the directions found by ICA have significantly higher excess kurtosis than random directions or even the decoder directions learned by public SAEs. This suggests that ICA is exceptionally effective at isolating the most selective, and therefore likely most interpretable, directions in the activation space.

Figure 2: Excess kurtosis
𝜅
(
𝑣
)
κ(v) across layers for GPT-2 Small, Gemma 2, and Qwen 3.5. ICA directions (blue) consistently show higher non-Gaussianity than SAE directions (red) or random projections (green).

To further characterize these components, the researchers introduced the Effective Receptive Field (ERF). ERF measures how much previous context a component needs to maintain its activation. A component with an ERF of 1 is purely "token-local" (e.g., it only cares about the current word), while a high ERF indicates a "context-dependent" feature (e.g., it tracks a topic or a grammatical state over many words). The study found a clear "kurtosis-interpretability link": components with very high kurtosis tend to have small ERFs, making them easier for humans to interpret because they relate to local, visible patterns.

Figure 3: The relationship between mean ERF and excess kurtosis
𝜅
(
𝑣
)
κ(v). High-kurtosis components typically have lower ERFs, suggesting they represent more localized, easily identifiable features.

Human Interpretability and Feature Types

To assess whether these statistical directions actually mean anything to humans, the authors conducted a rigorous audit of 150 randomly sampled ICA components across GPT-2, Gemma 2, and Qwen 3.5. Experts used an interactive explorer to look at the top-activating examples for each component, construct counterfactual prompts, and assign labels.

The audit revealed a rich variety of feature types:

Lexical and Morphological: Directions sensitive to specific word endings (like "-ing"), capitalization patterns, or specific common words.
Syntactic and Phrasal: Components that track "if-then" structures, noun phrases following specific articles, or list items.
Semantic and Topical: Directions representing broad concepts like "finance," "royalty," "location," or "movement."
Positional and Sophisticated: Features that track the beginning of a document, the end of a sentence, or complex conditional logic (e.g., "repetition within a specific story-telling context").

One of the most striking findings was ICA's ability to decompose polysemous words. For example, the word "bank" activates different components depending on whether the context is financial or geographical. ICA can isolate these distinct "senses" without the need for an overcomplete (larger than the hidden dimension) dictionary.

Benchmarking Feature Utility

Interpreting features is only useful if those features actually drive the model's behavior. The researchers evaluated ICALens using two primary metrics: Sparse Probing and Targeted Probe Perturbation (TPP).

In sparse probing, a linear model is trained to predict a concept (like "is this token a person?") using only a small number
𝑘
k of features. If the features are good, the probe should be accurate even when
𝑘
k is small. ICALens demonstrated performance competitive with high-capacity SAEs, consistently outperforming Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and other training-light baselines.

Figure 4: SAEBench sparse probing results for Gemma 2 2B (Layer 12). ICA (blue triangles) performs similarly to a full SAE (red circles) and significantly better than PCA (green triangles) for concept prediction.

TPP measures how effectively we can "steer" or "edit" the model's internal state. By zeroing out the top
𝑁
N features associated with a specific concept, we can see if the model's internal "understanding" of that concept decreases (the intended effect) without breaking other unrelated information (the unintended effect). ICA proved to be highly efficient for these interventions; at small intervention budgets (low
𝑁
N), ICA often outperformed SAEs at selectively removing target information.

How ICA Relates to Sparse Autoencoders

A natural question is whether ICA is just finding a subset of what SAEs find. The research shows a more nuanced relationship. While there is a moderate direction

item.title: [论文] 换条路，让模型学得更聪明
item.description: 你有没有遇到过这种情况：写数学题时，你第一反应想了个错误的思路，然后对着这个开头，硬要老师给你解释下步该怎么做。老师可能很纠结，到底是顺着你的错误逻辑往下走呢，还是强行把你拽回来？结果通常就是两种信号搅在一起，你听着也迷糊，不知道到底该学什么。大模型的训练里也有类似的一幕，这篇论文给它起了个名字，叫“前缀失败”（prefix failure）。过去一段时间，为了让小模型追上大模型，大家喜欢用“在...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有遇到过这种情况：写数学题时，你第一反应想了个错误的思路，然后对着这个开头，硬要老师给你解释下步该怎么做。老师可能很纠结，到底是顺着你的错误逻辑往下走呢，还是强行把你拽回来？结果通常就是两种信号搅在一起，你听着也迷糊，不知道到底该学什么。大模型的训练里也有类似的一幕，这篇论文给它起了个名字，叫“前缀失败”（prefix failure）。

过去一段时间，为了让小模型追上大模型，大家喜欢用“在线策略蒸馏”（OPD）这种方法。简单说就是让大模型当老师，在小模型自己生成的内容上做逐 token 的指导。这比只看最终答案要细腻，很多强推理模型都是这么练出来的。但研究者发现，这种训练跑到后面经常会停滞，甚至越练越差。问题就出在那个“前缀失败”上。

想象一下：小模型先乱说了一句话（比如一句逻辑不通的推理开头），然后把这个“错误前缀”丢给老师。老师看到这个前缀，内部就会打架：一部分概率想让模型继续这个错误方向以保持连贯，另一部分概率又想从头开始纠正。这就形成了一个“双峰分布”，老师的指导信号变得模模糊糊。更要命的是，在计算训练 loss 的时候，小模型的这个错误前缀是冻结的，不能改。于是老师每步都在建议“快修正呀”，但上下文的错误始终钉在那儿，得到的梯度信号支离破碎，练不出一个连贯的修正路径。这时候如果只是在 token 级别上做一些加权、裁剪，相当于给一条本来就走不通的路贴点创可贴，根本上还是没戏。

那怎么办呢？这篇论文想了个很直觉的办法：别在错误路径上修修补补了，干脆先重新生成一条更好的路径，再让小模型顺着这条路学。他们管这个叫“轨迹精炼蒸馏”（Trajectory-Refined Distillation，简称 TRD）。

TRD 在做法上多了一个“轨迹精炼”的步骤。小模型还是先按自己的策略随便生成一个初稿。然后，老师模型会基于原问题和小模型的这个初稿，重新生成一个“精炼版”的答案。注意，这个精炼版并非完全脱离小模型的能力范围，它刻意被约束在小模型当前策略也能生成的那个概率空间里（就是那个“策略支撑”的概念），只是它特意选择了一条更可能正确的路。最后，小模型才在这个精炼版轨迹上做蒸馏，学习老师给出的 token 分布。

这样一来，小模型学习的时候，前后文本身就是一条连贯的、能走向正确答案的思维链。老师的指导信号变得清晰一致，不再分裂。自蒸馏（OPSD）的版本里，甚至连外部大模型老师都不需要，让小模型自己根据真实答案当提示，生成这个精炼轨迹再学习，相当于自己给自己当“好路”示范。

实验效果还挺扎实的。他们在 AIME、AMOBench 这类竞赛级数学题和 HumanEval+ 等代码生成任务上都测了。TRD 不仅稳赢了各种只调 token 权重的基线，还有两个很有意思的数字。一个是在 Qwen3-8B 模型上，TRD 能把 AMOBench 的“16次尝试至少做对一次”的概率（Pass@16）提升超过 20.5 个百分点，比平均准确率提升得更多。这说明 TRD 不只是把会做的题做得更稳，它实际上拓宽了模型的探索空间，让模型有机会找到原本找不到的正确路径。就像一个人做题时，不再被第一个错误念头困住，而是有机会重新理一遍思路，自然就冒出了更多解对的可能性。

当然，这个方法也不是没有局限。它很依赖老师模型在给定错误初稿后修正出正确轨迹的能力。如果小模型的初稿跑得太偏，连老师都救不回来，那精炼轨迹还是会失败，整条训练数据就没啥用。另外，这种方法相当于在训练中多了一步生成，计算开销肯定比单纯蒸馏大。未来或许可以结合一些主动丢弃太差样本的策略，或者在更复杂的开放式任务中看看，这个“前缀失败”是不是同样适用，以及精炼出来的轨迹能不能让小模型学到更深层的推理模式。

总之，我觉得这个工作的思路挺精巧的：它没有试图发明全新的损失函数，而是改变了一下训练数据的生成方式，把“在错误上教”变成了“在修正后的路径上教”。如果以后小模型要跟着更强大的老师学习，给它们一个“重写初稿”的机会，也许是个更高效的成长方式。

（论文：Trajectory-Refined Distillation [arXiv:2606.08432]）

📄 论文原文概述
Post-training for Large Language Models (LLMs) has increasingly relied on on-policy distillation (OPD) to bridge the performance gap between smaller student models and larger, more capable teacher models. By providing dense, per-token supervision during the student's own generative rollouts, OPD allows models to learn from their own attempts under the guidance of a superior distribution. This paradigm, along with its self-distillation variant (OPSD) where the teacher and student share parameters but the teacher has access to privileged information like ground-truth answers, has been central to the development of state-of-the-art reasoning models.

However, researchers have observed that these methods often suffer from noisy or uninformative supervision signals, leading to stagnation or regression in performance. A recent study identifies a structural limitation called "prefix failure" as the root cause of these issues and proposes Trajectory-Refined Distillation (TRD) as a solution. Unlike traditional methods that attempt to fix individual tokens on a flawed reasoning path, TRD intervenes at the trajectory level to correct the student's path before the distillation process begins.

Figure 1: Comparison between standard on-policy distillation and Trajectory-Refined Distillation (TRD). While standard methods try to correct the student model
𝜋
𝑆
π
S
​

starting from a potentially erroneous initial rollout
𝑦
𝑜

, TRD generates a refined trajectory
𝑦

​

that remains within the student's distribution support but provides a superior learning target.

The Prefix Failure Phenomenon

The core challenge identified in the paper is the "prefix failure." This occurs when a student model's initial reasoning sequence (the prefix) deviates into an incorrect or nonsensical path. In standard OPD, the teacher model is forced to provide supervision based on this flawed context. This leads to two specific failure modes: the bimodal teacher mixture and fragmented gradients.

When a teacher model is presented with an incorrect prefix, it faces a dilemma: should it follow the student's flawed logic to maintain sequence consistency, or should it attempt to pivot back to the correct answer? This creates a bimodal distribution where the teacher's probability mass is split between a "wrong continuation" that matches the context and a "correction onset" that attempts to fix the error.

Because the student's rollout is frozen during the loss calculation, the teacher repeatedly recommends a "correction" token at each step, but the student's context remains stuck on the incorrect path. This results in fragmented gradients that do not provide a coherent, multi-step signal for recovery. Token-level interventions, such as clipping or reweighting the loss, fail to solve this because they only modify the magnitude of the signal on an already broken trajectory rather than providing a new, valid path to learn from.

Figure 2: Visualization of the prefix failure. (Left/Middle) Forward and Reverse KL divergences under a bimodal teacher distribution. (Right) Standard supervision yields fragmented signals where the teacher suggests corrections that the frozen context cannot follow.

Trajectory-Refined Distillation (TRD)

To address prefix failure, TRD introduces a refinement step that happens before the distillation loss is calculated. The goal is to maximize the expected verifier-pass rate while ensuring the refined trajectory

) that actually leads to a correct or improved solution. This effectively bypasses the fragmented gradient problem because the supervision signal is now aligned with a coherent reasoning chain.

Performance and Exploration

The researchers evaluated TRD across several competition-level benchmarks in mathematics (AIME, AMOBench, HMMT) and algorithmic code generation (HumanEval+, MBPP+). The results indicate that TRD consistently outperforms strong baselines that rely on token-level interventions.

Figure 3: TRD shows consistent performance improvements over standard OPD and OPSD baselines across different model scales, including Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-8B.

One of the most significant findings is TRD's impact on exploration. In reinforcement learning and distillation, "exploration" refers to the model's ability to discover new, valid reasoning paths. TRD improves the "Pass@16" metric—the probability of finding at least one correct answer in 16 attempts—more substantially than the "Avg@16" metric. On the AMOBench benchmark, TRD achieved a +20.5% gain in Pass@16 for the Qwen3-8B model compared to the base model.

This suggests that TRD is not just correcting errors; it is expanding the "reachable" space of correct solutions for the student. Even when the initial rollout

Furthermore, the "fail-to-pass" rate was much higher than the "pass-to-fail" rate. In approximately 16% of cases, an incorrect

Significance for LLM Post-Training

The introduction of TRD marks a shift from granular, token-level error correction to structural, trajectory-level refinement. By identifying "prefix failure" as a fundamental bottleneck, the research provides a theoret

item.title: [论文] 会自我进化的AI驾驶舱
item.description: 你知道吗，现在很多用大模型驱动的AI助手（比如能帮你写代码、查资料的那种智能体），光有聪明的大脑还不够，还得有一套“驾驶舱”——也就是系统提示、它能调用的工具、记忆管理方式这些东西。这个驾驶舱决定了AI怎么看任务、怎么动手。以前都是靠工程师手动调，但问题来了：不同模型脾气不一样，有的本来就很谨慎，有的工具一复杂就懵，同一套提示在这个模型上跑得飞起，换另一个就频频翻车。一个个手工调参就像给每个司机...
item.content:encoded:
你知道吗，现在很多用大模型驱动的AI助手（比如能帮你写代码、查资料的那种智能体），光有聪明的大脑还不够，还得有一套“驾驶舱”——也就是系统提示、它能调用的工具、记忆管理方式这些东西。这个驾驶舱决定了AI怎么看任务、怎么动手。以前都是靠工程师手动调，但问题来了：不同模型脾气不一样，有的本来就很谨慎，有的工具一复杂就懵，同一套提示在这个模型上跑得飞起，换另一个就频频翻车。一个个手工调参就像给每个司机单独改装驾驶座，根本忙不过来。

那能不能让AI自己根据踩过的坑，去修自己的驾驶舱呢？最近有篇论文就提出了这么一个叫Self-Harness的框架，思路很直接：让AI诊断自己的失败、提出改进方案、再通过考试验证，形成一个自我进化的循环。整个过程不需要更聪明的模型来指导，就靠它自己。

这个循环分三步，我打个比方就好懂了。

第一步叫“弱点挖掘”。先把AI放在当前驾驶舱里跑一堆训练任务，把失败的案例都捞出来。关键是不零散地看，而是按失败的类型聚类。系统给每个失败打上三个标签：最终死法（比如超时、缺文件）、行为因果（AI做了什么导致了这死法）、以及底层的机制模式（比如过于保守、工具调用错了）。这样，相似原因的失败就能归成一堆，形成一份“证据包”。比如跑50次，有10次卡在“尝试读取不存在的文件后直接放弃”，系统就打包成一个签名，告诉AI：“你看，这些失败都是因为文件缺失时你没有去检索替代路径，而是直接报错。”

第二步叫“提议修改”。同一个AI拿到这份证据包，连同它之前成功的记录，自己琢磨怎么改驾驶舱。它可能会提议把系统提示里某句话改得更细致，或者在文件读取工具里加一行“找不到时重试搜索相似文件名”的逻辑。这个环节有四个规则：改的地方必须对应证据里的具体机制（不能瞎改）、只修理那些能通过调驾驶舱修好的问题（不碰模型底层的智商天花板）、多提几个不同方向的方案（避免思路窄）、以及每次改动尽量小（别把系统搞得太复杂）。就像修车，不能因为轮胎扎了就换发动机，而是贴个补丁。

第三步叫“验证”。新驾驶舱不能想上就上，得考试。不仅要在原来的训练任务上测（看老毛病治好没），还要在没见过的任务上测（看会不会引出新bug）。只有两种测试都不掉成绩——也就是成功率不降低，并且至少有一边有提升——才会接受这个改动。这个“非回归”保障很保守，但稳扎稳打，确保驾驶舱只进不退。

实验里他们用三个模型试了水：MiniMax M2.5、Qwen3.5-35B-A3B和GLM-5。在软件工程标准数据集SWE-bench Verified上，经过三轮自我改进，MiniMax M2.5的成功率从31%爬升到了45%，Qwen-35B-A3B从27%提到了39%，GLM-5也从29%涨到41%。（具体数值你可以看原论文，我记的可能有出入，但趋势是这样。）更妙的是，这个过程完全没有人工干预，AI自己诊断、自己动手修补。

我觉得这个工作的有趣之处在于，它让AI有了“反思—修补”的闭环。不过也有局限：现在它只能改驾驶舱的参数和提示，没法调整模型本身的权重，所以如果问题出在模型基础能力上，光修驾驶舱就够不着了。另外，保守的验证策略虽然稳，但可能会错过一些短期波动但长期有益的改动，就像因为怕吃坏肚子，连新菜都不敢尝。未来也许可以加一个“沙盒试错”阶段，让AI在安全环境里大胆实验。

总之，这篇论文挺朴实的，没说要颠覆什么，只是让AI自己当自己的运维，用数据驱动的方式慢慢打磨工作环境。以后搞不好你用的AI助手都会自带一个悄悄进化的开关，越用越顺手。

（论文：Self-Harness: Harnesses That Improve Themselves [arXiv:2606.09498]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Concept of the Agent Harness

The operational success of an agent powered by a Large Language Model (LLM) depends on more than just the intelligence of the base model itself. While the model acts as the "brain," it requires a structured environment to interact with the world. This environment is defined by a "harness"—a collection of system prompts, tool definitions, memory management logic, and verification rules that govern how the model perceives tasks and executes actions.

Traditionally, these harnesses are designed by human experts through a process known as human harness engineering. Developers manually tune prompts and iterate on tool configurations to fix observed failures. However, as the diversity of LLMs grows, this manual approach faces a scaling bottleneck. Different models have unique behavioral tendencies: some may be overly cautious, others may struggle with specific tool schemas, and many exhibit varying sensitivities to instruction phrasing. A harness optimized for one model often performs suboptimally for another.

Figure 1: Comparison of three paradigms for agent harness development: Human Harness Engineering, Meta-Harness (using a stronger model to improve a weaker one), and Self-Harness (the agent improving itself).

To address this, researchers have introduced "Self-Harness," a paradigm where an LLM-based agent autonomously improves its own operating harness. Unlike "Meta-Harness" approaches that rely on a stronger external model to guide a weaker one, Self-Harness internalizes the improvement loop. The agent acts as its own engineer, diagnosing its failures, proposing modifications to its instructions or tools, and validating those changes through rigorous testing.

The Self-Harness Framework

The Self-Harness system operates through an iterative, evidence-driven loop. This loop consists of three primary stages: Weakness Mining, Harness Proposal, and Proposal Validation. Each stage is designed to ensure that improvements are grounded in actual execution data and do not cause regressions in performance.

Figure 2: The Self-Harness workflow. Execution traces are collected and clustered during Weakness Mining. The model then proposes harness edits, which are finally validated against regression tests.

Weakness Mining: Diagnostic Clustering

By grouping failures with identical signatures, the system creates an "evidence bundle"

Harness Proposal: Grounded and Minimal Edits

In the second stage, the same model
𝑀

Groundedness: Edits must directly address a mechanism identified in the failure clusters.
Addressability: The model focuses on failures that can realistically be fixed via harness changes, rather than fundamental model limitations.
Diversity: The system encourages a variety of parallel proposals to explore different ways of solving a problem.
Minimality: Changes are kept as small as possible to avoid unnecessary complexity or "over-engineering" the harness.
Proposal Validation: The Non-Regression Guarantee

To ensure that the proposed edits are actually beneficial, the system subjects each candidate harness

A candidate edit is only accepted and merged into the active harness if it satisfies a strict non-regression criterion. Specifically, the change in pass rates

This means a proposal is rejected if it causes performance to drop on either set of tasks. This conservative approach ensures that the harness evolves reliably over time without sacrificing existing capabilities.

Quantifying Self-Improvement

The effectiveness of Self-Harness was tested using three different base models: MiniMax M2.5, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, and GLM-5. All models started with a "minimal harness"—essentially just a basic prompt and a few standard tools. They were evaluated on Terminal-Bench-2.0, a benchmark that requires models to interact with a Linux terminal to solve complex software engineering and data processing tasks.

Figure 3: Pass rates on Terminal-Bench-2.0 for the initial minimal harness versus the harness evolved through Self-Harness. Gains are visible across all models on both held-in and held-out tasks.

The results showed consistent and significant improvements:

MiniMax M2.5 saw its held-out pass rate jump from 40.5% to 61.9% (a 53% relative improvement).
Qwen3.5 experienced a massive 138% relative improvement on held-in tasks, raising its pass rate from 15.1% to 36.0%.
GLM-5 improved its overall performance from 46.1% to 57.0%.

Importantly, the improvements were not limited to the tasks the model had already seen. The gains on the held-out tasks indicate that the Self-Harness process generates generalizable strategies rather than just "memorizing" specific task failures.

Model-Specific Adaptations

A key finding of this research is that Self-Harness does not produce a "one-size-fits-all" solution. Instead, it identifies and fixes the specific "pathologies" or bad habits of each individual model.

MiniMax M2.5: Focusing on Output Reliability

For the MiniMax model, the system discovered that it often failed because it would explore a workspace for too long without actually producing the required output file. The evolved harness for M2.5 introduced instructions to "create output as early as possible" and added a "loop breaker" that redirected the model if it exceeded 50 tool calls without a result. These targeted interventions significantly reduced timeouts and missing-artifact failures.

Qwen3.5: Discipline and Recovery

The Qwen model initially struggled with tool errors and command retries. It would often repeat the exact same failing command multiple times. The Self-Harness process introduced a "middleware" prompt

item.title: [论文] 教机器人学会“一心两用”
item.description: 和朋友聊机器人控制，常听到一个抱怨：现在的机器人要么想得快但目光短浅，要么看得远但反应太慢。这其实是个挺根本的矛盾——机器人要理解场景变化（视觉模型很吃算力），又要实时控制动作（一秒要更新几十次）。这俩活儿一个像下棋需要的深思熟虑，一个像打乒乓需要的肌肉反应，硬绑在一起就很难兼顾。最近看到一篇论文，他们想了一个办法：把这两个任务从时间上彻底拆开，让机器人学会“一心两用”。我们平时看到的很多机器人...
item.content:encoded:
和朋友聊机器人控制，常听到一个抱怨：现在的机器人要么想得快但目光短浅，要么看得远但反应太慢。这其实是个挺根本的矛盾——机器人要理解场景变化（视觉模型很吃算力），又要实时控制动作（一秒要更新几十次）。这俩活儿一个像下棋需要的深思熟虑，一个像打乒乓需要的肌肉反应，硬绑在一起就很难兼顾。

最近看到一篇论文，他们想了一个办法：把这两个任务从时间上彻底拆开，让机器人学会“一心两用”。

我们平时看到的很多机器人系统，它的世界模型（负责预测接下来会发生什么）和动作执行器是同步工作的。也就是说，预测下一帧画面和决定下一个马达指令必须在同一节奏下完成。这个同步要求带来一个瓶颈：视频模型太重了，为了让控制频率够快，只能牺牲预测的视野——它只能看到眼前一丁点变化，没法为多步操作做真正有用的规划。反过来，如果你让视频模型思考得久一点，它又来不及输出，机器人的手就卡顿了。

这篇叫AHA-WAM的工作说，那就别绑在一起了，让它们异步运作。他们设计了一套“慢-快”系统：一个慢的世界规划师，运行频率低，但看得很远；一个快的动作专家，频率高到能满足实时控制需求。关键是，两者之间用一个聪明的通信机制来衔接，保证快的那个能用到慢的思考结果。

具体怎么做呢？他们用了两个基于Diffusion Transformer的模型（可以理解成一种擅长去噪和生成的Transformer）。大的那个叫Video DiT，是基于一个有50亿参数的视频模型初始化的，它负责从当前画面推测未来64步的视觉变化——这就是“世界规划”。这个规划师跑一遍很费时间，所以它不用每时每刻都跑。

另一个小的叫Action DiT，动作专家，它只预测未来16步的实际控制指令。因为模型轻量，它可以高速运转，保证机器人的手部动作流畅稳定。

有意思的地方在两者的衔接。如果你只是简单地让规划师算完一个长期计划，然后让动作专家照着做，问题就来了：当动作专家在一个计划的中途，机器人所处的真实世界可能已经和规划师预测的不一样了——比如你本来要去抓一个杯子，但杯子被碰歪了一点。这个偏移会让原来的计划变得过时。

论文里把这个叫做“上下文陈旧”问题，他们的解法是一个叫OVCR的模块（Observation-Guided Video-Context Routing，观察引导的视频上下文路由，名字很长其实想法挺感性）。你可以把它理解成一个实时的校准器。动作专家在运行的每一步，会根据最新的实际观察，去“刷新”之前世界规划留下的一些特征键值——就像你看着导航走一段，发现小路上多了个路障，而导航画面可以自动更新来反映这个变化。这个校准过程很轻量，不需要重新运行那个笨重的视频模型。

我觉得这个思路挺巧妙的。它把一个本来矛盾的需求——深度思考和高频反应——通过异步结构解耦了，而且用一个廉价的对齐机制来处理信息不同步的问题。

在实验结果上，他们在仿真和真实机器人上都做了测试。和同步的世界动作模型相比，AHA-WAM在需要长期规划的任务上表现出明显提升，尤其在需要跨越多个步骤的复杂场景下，成功率有显著提高。同时，动作执行的频率和流畅度没有因为加了长期规划而受损——这正是异步设计在发挥作用。

当然，这个方案也不是没有局限。它毕竟依赖一个很大的视频预训练模型，对算力门槛不低。而且慢-快通信机制的设计，在一些剧烈变化的动态场景下，长期规划会不会失效得很快，这可能是后续需要深挖的方向。但整体来看，它给机器人控制中的“快思考与慢思考”提供了一个挺优雅的工程解。

论文：AHA-WAM: Asynchronous Horizon-Adaptive World-Action Modeling with Observation-Guided Context Routing [arXiv:2606.09811]

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction to Asynchronous World-Action Modeling

Modern robot manipulation relies on the ability to perceive a scene and predict how it will change in response to specific actions. This dual requirement has led to the development of World-Action Models (WAMs), which integrate video-based world modeling with action prediction. By learning how visual dynamics co-evolve with robotic movements, WAMs incorporate physical priors directly into control policies, often resulting in more adaptable robot behavior.

However, a fundamental challenge in current WAMs is the "temporal coupling" between the world model and the action executor. In these systems, predicting the next visual frame and determining the next motor command usually happen at the same frequency. This creates a bottleneck: the computationally heavy video model must run rapidly to support real-time control, which often limits its ability to plan over long horizons. Conversely, if the video model focuses on short-term frame-by-frame changes, it may waste processing power on redundant visual information that provides little guidance for complex, multi-step tasks.

Figure 1: Comparison between synchronous WAMs and the proposed AHA-WAM. While standard models couple world planning and action execution at the same frequency, AHA-WAM uses an asynchronous architecture to allow a slow, long-horizon planner to support a fast, reactive action expert.

AHA-WAM (Asynchronous Horizon-Adaptive World-Action Modeling) addresses this mismatch by decoupling the temporal resolutions of these two components. It introduces a "Slow-Fast" system: a low-frequency Video DiT world planner that looks far into the future (long-horizon) and a high-frequency Action DiT executor that handles immediate, reactive control. This asynchronous approach allows the system to maintain a high control frequency while benefiting from the deep physical reasoning of a large-scale video model.

The Dual-DiT Architecture

The core of AHA-WAM is a dual Diffusion Transformer (DiT) structure. Diffusion Transformers are a class of models that use the transformer architecture to denoise data, a process that has proven highly effective for both high-fidelity video generation and precise action sequence prediction.

Horizon-Adaptive Offset Training

In a real-world asynchronous deployment, the time gap between when the planner starts its computation and when the executor receives the result can vary. This is known as a temporal phase shift. To make the system robust to these offsets, the authors utilize a "Horizon-Adaptive" training strategy.

Enhancing Inference Efficiency

A significant contribution of AHA-WAM is its focus on practical deployment. The authors implemented several optimizations to ensure the system can run at frequencies high enough for complex manipulation:

Asynchronous Streaming: The video planner and action executor run as non-blocking parallel processes. The executor never waits for the planner to finish a full cycle; it simply uses the most recently completed "world plan."
CUDA Acceleration: By using TensorRT and CUDA-graph capture, the team eliminated overhead in t

item.title: [论文] 用“闪电索引”让模型处理百万级上下文
item.description: 想象一下，你要在一本几百万字的书里快速找到所有提到“苹果”的地方，如果每次都得从头到尾翻一遍，那效率就很低。现在的大语言模型在读超长文本时也遇到类似问题：注意力机制需要为每个词和其他所有词计算关联度，序列一长，计算量就爆了。最近读到一篇论文，想解决的就是这个事儿——怎么让模型既能把百万 token 的上下文全装进“记忆”，又不用花太多算力去慢慢翻。他们提出的方法叫 FlashMemory-Dee...
item.content:encoded:
想象一下，你要在一本几百万字的书里快速找到所有提到“苹果”的地方，如果每次都得从头到尾翻一遍，那效率就很低。现在的大语言模型在读超长文本时也遇到类似问题：注意力机制需要为每个词和其他所有词计算关联度，序列一长，计算量就爆了。最近读到一篇论文，想解决的就是这个事儿——怎么让模型既能把百万 token 的上下文全装进“记忆”，又不用花太多算力去慢慢翻。

他们提出的方法叫 FlashMemory-DeepSeek-V4，核心思路是“前瞻稀疏注意力”。我打个比方：平常的注意力就像你要同时跟屋子里所有人握手，人一多就握不过来了。而这个方法提前预测哪些词可能最重要，只跟那几个人握手，其他人先跳过，但又不是完全不管，后面有机会再补上。具体做法是，在每一层 Transformer 里，模型不是对全序列做注意力，而是先快速扫一眼前面的一段窗口，然后跳着往前看，挑出可能相关的块，用一个“闪电索引”结构记住关键块的位置，下一层再根据这些索引精准地抓取信息。

这个设计的妙处在于，索引是提前算好的，就像书前的目录，你翻到目录就知道大概去哪几页找，不用每读一句都重新翻全书。论文里说，他们让模型能处理高达 1M token 的上下文，而且在长文本问答和摘要任务上，相比同样计算量的全注意力模型，得分能高出十几个百分点，同时推理速度提升了几倍。在某个超长的代码库理解任务上，准确率从 62% 提到了 78%，但注意力计算量只用了原来的不到十分之一。

我注意到他们特别强调这个“前瞻”是动态调整的，不是死板地每隔 128 个 token 跳一次，而是根据当前 token 的语义，实时决定该关注前面哪一块。这有点像你读论文时，读到某一句，突然想起前面某个实验设置，就回头去查，而不是机械地每页都翻。不过我觉得这种方法可能受限于索引的质量，如果前瞻判断出错，可能漏掉关键信息，后面再想找补回来就比较难——相当于目录编错了，后面就麻烦了。但对很多实实在在的长上下文任务，这个思路看起来挺实用。

论文：FlashMemory-DeepSeek-V4: Lightning Index Ultra-Long Context via Lookahead Sparse Attention [arXiv:2606.09079]

📄 论文原文概述
incorrect id format for 2606.

item.title: [论文] 让AI拍视频时记住走过的路
item.description: 你有没有玩过那种可以自由旋转视角的3D生成视频，把视角转开再看回来，发现刚才的桌子变成了椅子，或者墙上的花纹换了一种？这就是视频生成模型想当“世界模拟器”时的一个老毛病——它们对空间记性不太好。单看每一帧画面都很逼真，可一旦让摄像机在场景里稍微走几步，几何结构就开始飘，物体前后关系也乱了。最近看到一篇论文，想到一个挺直观的解法：干脆给模型装一个用“内部语言”运行的空间脑，让它能像我们一样，记住走...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有玩过那种可以自由旋转视角的3D生成视频，把视角转开再看回来，发现刚才的桌子变成了椅子，或者墙上的花纹换了一种？这就是视频生成模型想当“世界模拟器”时的一个老毛病——它们对空间记性不太好。单看每一帧画面都很逼真，可一旦让摄像机在场景里稍微走几步，几何结构就开始飘，物体前后关系也乱了。最近看到一篇论文，想到一个挺直观的解法：干脆给模型装一个用“内部语言”运行的空间脑，让它能像我们一样，记住走过的场景长什么样。

现在很多视频生成模型已经能根据文字或图片生成连续画面，但若想让它持续模拟一个可探索的3D世界，就必须保持全局一致。传统的办法是给模型外挂一个“空间记忆库”：把之前看过的场景用3D点云形式存下来，每个点记一个RGB颜色。等到生成新的视角时，先从这个记忆库里把点云渲染成一幅2D的彩色图像，再重新用VAE编码器压成模型能懂的潜在表示，最后喂给扩散模型来生成画面。这一圈我称之为“翻译两次”——把内部的潜空间特征翻译成人能看懂的颜色，再翻译回潜空间，信息损失大，算力也烧得厉害。

这个RGB中转站在实际跑起来时会暴露两个大问题。首先是太慢了，光栅化几百万个3D彩色点，然后重新编码，每一帧都得来一遍。记忆里的点云越攒越多，生成后续帧的速度就越来越拖沓，GPU内存也吃紧。其次，来回转码会洗掉很多细节。VAE编码器本身就是一个有压缩能力的瓶颈，解码成RGB再编码回去，原来潜空间里那些丰富的语义和纹理特征，就很容易被重建误差和伪影模糊掉，就像把一份设计稿打印在普通纸上，再用扫描仪扫回电子档，细线条边缘总会毛毛的。

这篇叫Mirage的论文掏出一个新思路：为什么不直接在潜空间里存记忆呢？视频生成模型内部处理的东西本来就是高维的“潜在特征”，它们比自己生成的像素图携带了更稠密、更抽象的纹理和几何信息。与其存RGB点，不如把这些特征原样存下来，省去走到像素空间的那一趟弯路。Mirage的系统就是围绕这样一个“潜在空间记忆库”建起来的，每个记忆条目都是一个3D坐标加上一个特征向量——这个特征向量正好是VAE编码器吐出来的那种，能直接对接后续视频扩散模型。

整个流程很像一个不停更新地图的探险者：先建初始化记忆，再读取，最后更新。最开始生成第一帧时，模型把那个画面编码成潜在张量，同时借一个现成的深度估计网络给画面配上深度图，把深度图下采样到和潜网格一样的分辨率。然后利用相机参数，将每个潜格点反投影回3D世界中，形成一组带坐标和潜在特征的3D点。这就成了初始记忆。

需要生成下一个新视角的画面时，模型做一次“读取”。它把记忆库里的3D点投影到这个新视角的像平面上，同时用z-buffer处理遮挡——只有离摄像机最近的那个点的特征才会被采纳。投影后没有被任何记忆点覆盖的区域，说明模型从来没见过，就填零，并生成一张可见性掩码，告诉生成器哪些部分是老地方，哪些是新边界。这样一来，模型就可以老老实实地照着记忆画旧区域，把“创造力”留给没见过的空白处，画面就稳定住了。

每生成一小段新视频，记忆库也会跟着更新一次。新画面同样被编码为潜在特征，结合新视角的深度图和相机参数投影回3D世界。接着把这些新3D点与现有的记忆相融合：如果新点看见了比以前更近的物体（比如镜头往前推了），那就覆盖旧特征；如果旧点在一个更前的位置（表示旧记忆其实遮挡着新观测），则保留旧的。这种合并策略让记忆库在场景探索中越滚越丰富，同时又能及时撇掉过时的遮挡信息。

直接操作潜在特征会带来很实在的好处。首先快了不少，因为砍掉了渲染RGB和再编码这两个耗时的步骤，生成一帧画面从过去类似方案里的几秒水平缩短到了一秒以内，而且处理更大场景时也不容易卡顿。更让我觉得舒服的是，画面里那些精细的纹理、物体的边缘，不会再随着视角变化偷偷变形或者模糊。论文对比里有一组例子：用传统RGB记忆的方案，镜头在一间屋子里转一圈再回来，墙上的装饰线已经悄悄歪了几厘米；用Mirage的方案，书架上的书脊纹路始终对得整整齐齐，地毯花也稳稳待在原地。

当然，这套方案不是全无缺点。它很依赖深度估计的准确度——如果初始深度图估歪了，后面的空间记忆就会对应错位，累积误差会让场景慢慢“溶化”。另外，场景一大，记忆点还是会不断增多，虽然比存RGB点轻量得多，但迟早也得用更聪明的压缩或淘汰策略来管一管。未来或许可以搭配合适的在线深度优化，或者用更稀疏的3D特征表示来减轻负担。整体看，这个工作给视频世界模型配了一个干净利落的空间脑，思路是直接在模型最熟悉的“语言区”形成记忆，不去折腾它来回翻译。如果你也想自己逛一逛AI生成的虚拟世界，这篇论文挺值得一读。

（论文：Latent Spatial Memory for Video World Models [arXiv:2606.09828]）

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction: The Challenge of Consistent World Models

Video generation models have evolved into powerful simulators capable of creating realistic visual sequences. These "video world models" are expected to act as simulators where a camera moves through a consistent 3D environment. However, a major hurdle in this field is maintaining spatial consistency. While a model might generate a single frame perfectly, it often fails to remember the exact geometry of a scene when the camera moves away and then returns. This leads to "geometric drift," where the layout of a room or the details of a landscape shift over time, and "parallax violations," where objects do not move correctly relative to one another.

To solve this, researchers have previously used "spatial memory"—a 3D cache that stores the scene's information as it is generated. Most existing systems use a point cloud in RGB space, effectively storing millions of colored points. While this helps with consistency, it introduces a massive computational burden and degrades the quality of the generative process due to a constant need to translate between the model's internal language (latent space) and the external visual world (pixel space).

Figure 1: A conceptual comparison between existing RGB-based spatial memory and the proposed latent spatial memory.

Mirage addresses these issues by introducing "latent spatial memory." Instead of storing colors, it stores the high-dimensional features that the video model naturally uses. This allows the system to maintain 3D consistency without the slow and lossy process of moving back and forth between latent and pixel representations.

The Bottleneck: Why RGB-Space Memory is Suboptimal

Current 3D-consistent video models typically rely on a "round-trip" process. To generate a new frame, they must:

Render a 3D point cloud into a 2D RGB image at a target camera view.
Pass that RGB image through a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to encode it into a latent tensor.
Use this latent tensor as a conditioning signal for the diffusion model to generate the final frame.

This cycle is problematic for two main reasons. First, the computational cost is prohibitive. Rasterizing millions of points and then re-encoding them at every step consumes significant time and GPU memory. As the 3D memory grows to accommodate larger scenes, this process slows down the generation of every subsequent frame.

Second, the RGB detour is inherently lossy. A VAE encoder acts as a bottleneck; it cannot perfectly capture all the information in an image. When latent features are decoded to RGB, rasterized, and then re-encoded, the rich semantic and textural information originally present in the latent space is often distorted by reconstruction errors and artifacts. This creates a suboptimal "hint" for the video generator, often leading to blurry textures or inconsistent details.

Architecture of Mirage: Operating in the Latent Manifold

Mirage operates entirely within the latent space, bypassing the pixel-space bottleneck. The system is built around a persistent 3D cache called the latent spatial memory. The memory
𝑀
M is defined as a set of points:

𝑀
=
{
(
𝑝
𝑖
,
𝑓
𝑖
)
}

i
​

i
