By balancing these signals, the authors provide the student with a stable distributional anchor (from the base teacher) while selectively incorporating the expertise of specialized models. This approach ensures that the student inherits the best qualities of multiple teachers without the risk of optimization failure.

Figure 3: Comparison of training stability between (a) using a single specialized teacher and (b) using the proposed step-wise multi-teacher guidance strategy.

Joint Distillation for Generation and Editing

Most few-step models are designed for either T2I generation or image editing, but rarely both. The researchers explored how to combine these tasks into a single student model. This requires "joint distillation," where the training data contains both standard T2I prompts and instruction-guided editing pairs (input image, instruction, and target image).

A key finding of the study is the importance of the task-mixture ratio. They tested ratios such as 9:1, 7:3, and 5:5 (T2I to Editing data). They discovered a "positive transfer" effect: incorporating editing tasks actually improved the model's performance on standard T2I generation. The 5:5 balanced mixture proved to be the most effective, as the dense supervision provided by editing tasks helped the model better understand the relationship between text instructions and visual changes, which translated back into higher-quality image generation.

Figure 4: Examples of Qwen-Image-Flash performing various editing tasks, including background change, text addition, and style transfer.

Evaluation and Benchmarking

To rigorously assess the distilled models, the authors developed two new benchmarks: T2I-Bench and Editing-Bench. These benchmarks cover a wide array of scenarios, from natural landscapes and human portraits to complex textual rendering and fine-grained semantic editing.

Traditional metrics like CLIP score or FID often fail to capture the nuanced differences in human preference or instruction following. Consequently, the researchers utilized advanced LLM-based evaluators (Gemini 3.1 Pro and GPT 5.5). These evaluators were provided with detailed rubrics to score images based on:

Perceptual Quality: Visual fidelity, lack of artifacts, and aesthetic appeal.
Instruction Following: How well the generated or edited image matches the prompt.
Source Preservation: (For editing) How well the model retains the identity and structure of the original image while applying requested changes.

The results showed that Qwen-Image-Flash, operating in 4 steps, consistently achieved higher preference scores than the 80-step teacher model across both benchmarks. This demonstrates that with a carefully designed training recipe, few-step distillation can not only match the speed of traditional methods but actually surpass the quality of the multi-step models from which they are derived.

Summary of Contributions

The development of Qwen-Image-Flash highlights several key principles for the future of visual foundation models:

Recipe Over Objective: While the loss function is important, the "how" and "what" of training—data splits and teacher selection—are primary drivers of success in few-step distillation.
Strategic Multi-Teacher Learning: Using a base teacher as a "safety net" while incorporating specialized te

item.title: [论文] AI考满分，为何上不了班？
item.description: 跟你说个事，你肯定也发现了，现在的大模型考试能力很强——MMLU、GPQA、HumanEval 这些基准动不动就接近满分，但真要让它去干一份金融分析师、机械工程师或者律师的活，好像又总差那么一口气。最近看到一篇论文，就是在认真琢磨这个“实用性鸿沟”：为什么 AI 在试卷上风生水起，可一进真实职场就有点施展不开？研究人员把这种现象叫“效用问题”。简单说就是，很多专业工作并不是回答几道选择题，甚至也...
item.content:encoded:
跟你说个事，你肯定也发现了，现在的大模型考试能力很强——MMLU、GPQA、HumanEval 这些基准动不动就接近满分，但真要让它去干一份金融分析师、机械工程师或者律师的活，好像又总差那么一口气。最近看到一篇论文，就是在认真琢磨这个“实用性鸿沟”：为什么 AI 在试卷上风生水起，可一进真实职场就有点施展不开？

研究人员把这种现象叫“效用问题”。简单说就是，很多专业工作并不是回答几道选择题，甚至也不是写几小段代码，而是持续好几小时甚至好几天的多步骤操作——在行业软件里建模、分析数据并生成报告、剪片子、做 CAD 设计。这些活儿往往没法用命令行搞定，全程得盯着图形界面，这儿点一下、那儿拖一拖。如果你想考考 AI 是不是真的能上班，就得给它一套足够接近真实工作的试卷。

于是就有了这篇论文里提出的 Agents’ Last Exam (ALE)，你可以把它理解成一场“AI 的终极职业资格考”。它要测的是智能体在专业场景里完成端到端的长线任务，并且产出的是实打实的经济价值，而不是一个答题正确率。名字里“Last Exam”有两层意思：一是想划出一条专业合格线，二是想找到目前最强模型也有心无力的那些难题。

这套考题的设计挺接地气的，遵循三条原则：代表性、复杂性、可验证性。代表性体现在必须用行业专家日常使用的真软件，比如机械工程用 SolidWorks，建筑用 Rhino，视频编辑用 DaVinci Resolve，而不是在一个简化模拟器里点点点。复杂性则要求输出的是一个完整交付物，比如拿到一份原始数据，完成清洗、分析，最后写出一份带图表的 PDF 报告——类似一个初级员工花一天才能做完的活。可验证性也很关键：因为输出可能是表格、CAD 图纸、音视频，评分不能靠人肉判断或者让另一个大模型凭感觉打分，而是预先设计好确定性的自动化检查，比如看是否能通过一个“安全门”（没有刀具路径碰撞）再算效率分，或者把任务拆成一串检查项，每个满足得一分，最后取平均分。这样一来，评分就客观了。

光有题还不行，得有个能做的“考生”。研究人员把这种智能体叫做通用计算机使用智能体（GCUA），并把它拆成了五个功能层，挺形象：大脑是大语言模型负责推理和规划；眼睛是视觉能力，能看懂屏幕上的按钮和菜单；身体是协调层，把大脑的决策手脚化；双手就是点击、打字、调用 API 这些具体动作；双脚是它运行的那台虚拟机环境。这样一来，智能体就不再只是一个对话机器人，而是能像人一样坐在电脑前，用图形界面做事。

出题过程也相当认真，超过 250 位领域专家参与，每人提交自己曾经做过的一个真实项目，经过编辑、同行评议、工程实现、质量检查等五道关，才变成一份可执行的评估任务。整套 ALE 覆盖了美国劳工部标准的 13 个大行业领域、55 个子领域，从生命科学到工程设计、法律到商业金融都有。总共有 1490 个任务实例，按难度分成了三个层次：近期可解、全谱代表、终极考试。

那结果怎么样呢？有点惨，但方向也特清晰。目前最强的配置，用 GPT-5.5 做大脑的智能体，整体平均通过率也就 26.2%。在那些被划为“终极考试”的最难任务里，分数就更低了，很多任务直接完不成，相当于交了白卷。这说明想让 AI 真正像模像样地上班，还有很长一段路要走。

我觉得这个工作有意思的地方，是把评估从“智能”的抽象维度拉回到了“能不能干活”的经济维度。但它的局限也很明显：构建这么一套题耗费巨大，需要大量专家和工程资源，而且任务虽然真实，毕竟还是在受控的虚拟机里跑，离现实中软件崩溃、需求变更、跨部门沟通的混乱感还差得远。另外，成本也是个问题，让智能体跑一遍长任务耗费的 token 和 CPU 时间都不少，短期内可能更适合用来帮人打下手，而不是完全替代。

未来也许需要让智能体学会更高效地探索界面、记忆之前学过的操作，或者直接跟人类协作完成这些考核。但至少，这类基准给我们一个更实在的标尺，帮我们看到那一天还有多远。

（论文：Agents' Last Exam [arXiv:2606.05405]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Utility Problem in Artificial Intelligence

Artificial Intelligence (AI) has reached a state of high performance on many academic and specialized benchmarks, such as MMLU (knowledge), GPQA (science), and HumanEval (coding). However, these successes often fail to translate into broad economic impact across professional industries like finance, engineering, or law. Researchers refer to this discrepancy as the "utility problem." While a model might be able to answer complex multiple-choice questions or write isolated code snippets, it often struggles to perform the sustained, multi-step, tool-intensive work that defines modern professional roles.

Existing benchmarks typically evaluate models in vacuum-like environments or simplified simulations. To address this, "Agents’ Last Exam" (ALE) was developed as a comprehensive evaluation framework designed to measure the ability of AI agents to perform economically valuable, long-horizon work in authentic professional settings. The "Last Exam" name signifies two goals: establishing a threshold for professional readiness and identifying a difficulty frontier that challenges even the most advanced current models.

A Framework Grounded in Professional Reality

ALE is built upon three core design principles to ensure its relevance to the actual labor market: representativeness, complexity, and verifiability.

Representativeness: Tasks must reflect actual professional practice. This means agents are required to use the same industry-standard software that human experts use, such as SolidWorks for mechanical engineering, Rhino for architecture, or DaVinci Resolve for video editing.
Complexity: Unlike simple command-line tasks, ALE focuses on end-to-end deliverables that would typically take a human expert hours or days to complete. A task might involve receiving a raw dataset, performing an analysis, and generating a formatted report or a 3D model.
Verifiability: Because professional work results in diverse outputs (spreadsheets, CAD files, media artifacts), the benchmark uses deterministic automated checking. This avoids the subjectivity of human graders or general "LLM-as-judge" prompts by using specific, evidence-anchored probes.

To ensure broad coverage, the benchmark adopts the O*NET/SOC 2018 occupational taxonomy, which is the standard federal classification for jobs in the United States. This grounding allows ALE to represent 55 distinct subdomains across 13 major industry domains, including Life Sciences, Engineering & Architecture, Legal, and Business & Finance.

The Anatomy of a Generalist Computer-Use Agent

Evaluating AI on these tasks requires a shift from simple chatbots to Generalist Computer-Use Agents (GCUAs). These agents are designed to interact with a computer much like a human does, integrating visual perception with action. The ALE framework conceptualizes these agents through a five-layer functional model:

Brain (LLM Reasoning): The core large language model that handles planning and decision-making.
Eyes (GUI Perception): The vision capabilities that allow the agent to "see" the screen, identifying buttons, menus, and visual artifacts.
Body (Orchestrator): The system that coordinates between the brain and the tools.
Hands (Tool Invocation): The mechanisms for clicking, typing, or calling software APIs.
Feet (Runtime Substrate): The underlying environment, typically a virtual machine (VM), where the work is performed.

ALE targets agents that can move beyond CLI-only interaction to use full Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs). This is critical because a significant portion of professional work—especially in design, medicine, and manufacturing—happens in software environments that do not have command-line alternatives.

Building and Verifying Complex Tasks

Creating a benchmark of this scale required a massive collaborative effort involving over 250 domain experts. The tasks were sourced through a rigorous five-gate protocol:

Expert Sourcing: Experts were recruited from various industries to submit real-world projects they had previously completed.
Task Submission and Editing: Using a dedicated portal, experts provided task descriptions, input assets (like raw data or initial designs), and target software requirements.
First-Pass Review: Submissions underwent a conference-style peer review to evaluate their difficulty and suitability.
Implementation: Engineers converted these specifications into executable code (a main.py file) and provisioned the necessary software containers or virtual machines.
Quality Control: A final committee verified the reference outputs and calibrated the scoring rubrics.

The evaluation itself is designed to handle heterogeneous outputs. Instead of a single "pass/fail" check, many tasks use a weighted rubric or a "gate-and-score" logic. For example, in a manufacturing task, the agent might first need to pass a "safety" gate (no toolpath collisions) before its efficiency score is even calculated. A common scoring pattern is binary checklist averaging, where the final score

Measuring Performance on the Difficulty Frontier

ALE divides its 1,490 task instances into three tiers based on difficulty and purpose:

Near-Term: Tasks that are partially solvable by today's leading agents, providing a metric for immediate progress.
Full-Spectrum: A balanced set that ensures representation across all 55 industry subdomains.
Last-Exam: The most challenging tasks, representing the long-term goal of the benchmark.

Initial testing shows that ALE is far from being "solved." Even the strongest configuration tested (using a GPT-5.5 backbone) achieved an overall pass rate of only
26.2
%
26.2%. On the "Last-Exam" tier, the average pass rate across most mainstream agents dropped to a mere
2.6
%
2.6%.

A deep dive into why agents fail reveals that the primary bottleneck is not just technical execution, but higher-level reasoning and domain knowledge. A failure taxonomy of one leading agent showed that
47
%
47% of failures were due to "Approach" errors (choosing the wrong strategy or abandoning the task) and
31
%
31% were due to "Understanding" errors (lacking the necessary domain knowledge or hallucinating facts).

One notable finding is the "Backbone vs. Harness" effect. Researchers found that varying the underlying LLM (the brain) resulted in an
18.0
18.0 percentage point spread in performance, while varying the agent's harness (the body/hands) only resulted in a
5.3
5.3 to
6.0
6.0 percentage point difference. This suggests that the current limit on agent performance is primarily the reasoning and knowledge capacity of the foundation models themselves.

Resource Efficiency and the Path Forward

The researchers also analyzed the relationship between cost, time, and performance. Interestingly, spending more money on API tokens or allowing the agent more time to work did not reliably lead to better results. Some efficient configurations achieved higher scores at a lower cost than their more expensive counterparts, emphasizing that the quality of tool use and context management is more important than sheer computational volume.

To maintain the integrity of the benchmark and prevent "contamination" (where models are trained on the test questions), ALE employs a public/private release strategy. Only
10
%
10% of the tasks are public, allowing developers to test their systems, while the remaining
90
%
90% are held in a private "vault" for official evaluation. Analysis has shown that performance on the public subset is highly representative of the full pool, with a Pearson correlation of
𝑟
=
0.89
r=0.89.

The significance of ALE lies in its potential to redirect AI research toward real-world utility. By

item.title: [论文] 教模型自己“爬山”变强
item.description: 你有没有想过，当我们刷到各种大模型榜单的时候，那些模型背后是怎么一步步变聪明的？很多时候我们看到的都是一个最终成品，但微软AI团队最近搞了个挺有意思的思路：他们不只是做了一个强模型，而是搭建了一整套让模型能自己“往上爬”的系统。这个项目叫MAI-Thinking-1，底层的想法很朴素——把模型改进当成一个持续优化的过程，就像爬山一样，每一步都在往更高的地方走。团队管这叫“hill-climbin...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，当我们刷到各种大模型榜单的时候，那些模型背后是怎么一步步变聪明的？很多时候我们看到的都是一个最终成品，但微软AI团队最近搞了个挺有意思的思路：他们不只是做了一个强模型，而是搭建了一整套让模型能自己“往上爬”的系统。

这个项目叫MAI-Thinking-1，底层的想法很朴素——把模型改进当成一个持续优化的过程，就像爬山一样，每一步都在往更高的地方走。团队管这叫“hill-climbing machine”，一套端到端的开发循环，把数据整理、架构设计、多阶段强化学习全串起来了。

有个做法我觉得挺特别的：他们坚持用干净的、人类生产的企业级数据来训练，从头开始训，不走“用大模型生成合成数据或者蒸馏”那条捷径。为什么这么选？他们的理由是，模型的智能如果建立在可验证的人类知识上，后面做对齐和推理的时候会更可控、更稳健。这个选择在现在合成数据满天飞的环境下，确实有点反直觉。

基础模型叫MAI-Base-1，是个MoE架构（混合专家模型），总参数量有1万亿，但每次推理只激活350亿参数。它的设计挺讲究的——把局部注意力和全局注意力交替排列，MoE层和普通前馈网络层穿插在一起。MoE层里用了叫LatentMoE的设计，每个token从512个“专家”里挑8个激活，在共享的降维投影之后做选择。这样既能把参数量做得很大，又能控制每次推理的计算量。

训这个基础模型用了30万亿token的数据。数据清洗流水线相当细致：针对不同源做HTML提取、用MinHash做多轮去重、再用嵌入模型做语义过滤。过程中还发现了一个有意思的现象，他们管它叫数据混合的“rank非不变性”——简单说就是，在小规模实验里表现最好的数据配比，到了大规模训练时不一定还是最优解。所以他们搞了“扩展阶梯”，用小规模训练来预测全量训练时的表现，然后再决定数据怎么配。

预训练分三个阶段，上下文窗口从16K慢慢扩展到256K。训完之后，这个基础模型在代码、STEM、数学这些任务上的损失值确实比同规模的模型要低。

但真正有意思的部分在后面。团队把模型推进了强化学习阶段，让它在没有见过任何“思维链”例子的情况下，纯靠奖励信号学会推理。用的算法是GRPO的一个改良版，加了两个技巧：自适应熵控制（防止模型太快变得过于确定），和外层比例裁剪（防止策略更新太猛导致梯度爆炸）。

RL阶段被拆成三条并行的“专精攀岩路线”：STEM攀岩专攻数学和编程竞赛题，奖励就是答案对不对；Agent攀岩让模型在沙盒环境里实操，可以跑命令、改文件、执行测试；还有一条是兼顾有用性和安全性的攀岩路线。

爬着爬着，团队观察到一些挺妙的质变。数学能力强的模型开始表现出“怀疑精神”，会回头检查自己的推理步骤、找不变量；而在做软件工程任务的模型学会了“证据考古”——自己去翻代码仓库的历史记录来解决问题。

最后要合并三条路线的成果，他们用了“痕迹蒸馏”的方法：把三个专精模型产生的优质推理痕迹收集起来，作为监督微调的数据喂回基础模型，再做一轮强化学习统一调优。这相当于让不同领域的专长互相教学，最后汇到一个模型里。

我觉得这套方法最吸引人的地方不是某个单点技术，而是它把模型开发想象成一个有机的成长过程。不过说实话，从头用纯人类数据训一个万亿参数的模型，数据和算力的门槛都相当高，这个思路能不能被更小的团队借鉴，可能还要看后续有没有更轻量的方案出来。

论文：MAI-Thinking-1: Building a Hill-Climbing Machine [arXiv:mai-thinking-1]

📄 论文原文概述
The Microsoft AI team has introduced MAI-Thinking-1, a large-scale Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model designed to compete with current frontier language models. Rather than focusing on a singular model release, the research emphasizes the creation of a "hill-climbing machine"—a systematic, end-to-end development loop that treats model improvement as a continuous optimization problem. This framework integrates data curation, architecture design, and multi-stage reinforcement learning (RL) to drive performance gains across STEM reasoning, coding, and general instruction following.

A core principle of this work is the reliance on clean, human-generated, enterprise-grade data. Unlike many contemporary approaches that utilize synthetic data or distillation from larger models to inherit capabilities, MAI-Thinking-1 was trained from scratch. This strategy aims to ensure that model intelligence is grounded in verifiable human knowledge, which the researchers argue leads to better steerability and robustness during subsequent alignment and reasoning stages.

Figure 1: Continuous performance improvement of MAI-Thinking-1 across thousands of reinforcement learning steps on AIME 2025, LiveCodeBench, and SWE-bench benchmarks.

The Hill-Climbing Architecture: MAI-Base-1

The foundation of this system is MAI-Base-1, a sparse MoE model featuring
35
B active 
/
1
T total
35B active /1T total parameters. The architecture is a decoder-only Transformer that utilizes several engineering optimizations for scale. It employs
𝑅
𝑀
𝑆
𝑁
𝑜
𝑟
𝑚
(
𝑥
0
)
RMSNorm(x
0
​

) and tied input/output embedding weights. To balance computational efficiency with modeling capacity, the design alternates five local attention layers with one global attention layer.

The model intersperses MoE layers with dense Feed-Forward Network (FFN) layers. In the MoE layers, a "LatentMoE" design is used where
𝑘
=
8
k=8 experts are activated out of
512
512 available experts per token. This activation occurs after a shared down-projection, which allows the model to maintain a high parameter count while keeping the active compute per token manageable.

Figure 2: Architectural layout of the 35B/1T MoE model, featuring periodic attention and interleaved Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and dense Feed-Forward Network (FFN) layers.

Training this base model required an enormous corpus of
30
T tokens
30T tokens. The data pipeline included source-specific HTML extraction, multi-stage deduplication using MinHash LSH, and semantic filtering via embedding models. A key scientific finding during this stage was the "rank non-invariance" of data mixtures: a data mixture that performs best at a small scale may not be the optimal choice as compute scales up. The team addressed this by using "scaling ladders"—small-scale training runs that predict the performance of data mixtures at full scale.

The pre-training process occurred in three phases with increasing context lengths. It began with the full
30
T tokens
30T tokens at a context window of
16
,
384
16,384, followed by mid-training phases at
64
K tokens
64K tokens and finally
256
K tokens
256K tokens. By the end of pre-training, MAI-Base-1 achieved a lower
NLL
NLL (Negative Log-Likelihood) and lower
BPB
BPB (bits-per-byte) on held-out code, STEM, and math tasks compared to other models with similar active parameter counts.

Teaching Reasoning Through Specialist Climbs

Once the base model was established, the "hill-climbing machine" shifted to reinforcement learning to develop advanced reasoning and agentic capabilities. Notably, the model was taught to reason without prior exposure to reasoning traces or "Chain-of-Thought" (CoT) examples. Instead, it learned through large-scale RL using an objective based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

To ensure the stability of these long RL runs, the researchers introduced two modifications to the standard GRPO objective:

Adaptive Entropy Control: This dynamically adjusts clipping bounds to maintain a target policy entropy (e.g.,
𝐻
∗
(
𝜋
𝜃
)
=
0.3
H
∗
(π
θ
​

)=0.3), preventing the model from becoming too deterministic too quickly.
Outer Ratio Clip: This prevents gradient-norm explosions that can occur when the policy shifts significantly between steps.

The RL stage was divided into three parallel "Specialist Climbs":

STEM Climb: Focused on mathematical problem-solving and competitive programming. The model was rewarded for producing correct, verifiable answers in subjects like physics and chemistry.
Agentic Climb: Focused on software engineering and multi-step tool use. The model interacted with a "Sandbox Execution Environment" (SEE) where it could run bash commands, edit files, and execute unit tests.
Helpfulness and Safety Climb: Balanced the model's ability to follow complex instructions with strict adherence to safety policies. This climb used a mixture of human preference reward models and rubric-guided AI judges.

As the models improved, the researchers observed a qualitative shift in reasoning. Stronger models began to exhibit "skepticism"—re-examining their own steps and identifying invariants—while agentic versions started performing "evidence archaeology" by searching through repository histories to solve coding issues.

Consolidation and Self-Distillation

To create the final MAI-Thinking-1 model, the insights and capabilities from the three specialist versions had to be merged. This was achieved through a process of trace distillation and consolidation. High-quality reasoning traces (the "chains of thought" produced during RL) were collected from the specialist models and used for a supervised finetuning (SFT) stage on the original mid-trained base model.

Figure 3: The consolidation process: integrating specialist teacher models into a unified model through trace distillation and a final reinforcement learning climb.

A technique called self-distillation was critical here. It allowed the team to transfer capabilities across model generations and recover from any training instabilities. By mixing approximately one million diverse reasoning traces with standard pre-training data, the model retained its broad general knowledge while adopting the deep reasoning patterns discovered during the specialist RL climbs.

Engineering Infrastructure: YOLO and Rocket

The scale of MAI-Thinking-1 required a bespoke infrastructure stack. The training was performed on
8
,
192
8,192 GB200 GPUs using a distributed framework called YOLO (You Only Launch Once). YOLO supports complex parallelism strategies (tensor, pipeline, and expert parallelism) and is optimized for "goodput"—the actual time spent on fruitful computation. The pre-training run achieved a
90.0
%
90.0% goodput rate, aided by asynchronous checkpointing and rapid fault recovery.

For the RL stages, the Rocket framework was developed. Rocket is an asynchronous distributed RL system that separates the "learner" (which updates model weights) from the "rollout workers" (which generate samples from the model). It ensures efficient weight transfer between these components and minimizes the "numerics gap" between training and inference environments by using
BF16
BF16 precision and consistent sampling masks.

When deployed on Microsoft's custom MAIA-200 hardware, MAI-Thinking-1 demonstrated over
40
%
40% higher token generation throughput compared to standard GPU deployments within the same power budget. This highlights the importance of hardware-software co-design in making frontier models economically viable.

Performance and Evaluation Results

MAI-Thinking-1 was evaluated against a wide array of public benchmarks and human side-by-side (SbS) tests. In STEM and reasoning, it achieved competitive scores of
97.0
%
97.0% on AIME 2025 and
84.9
%
84.9% on HMMT Feb 2026. In coding, it reached
73.5
%
73.5% on SWE-bench Verified, placing it in the same performance class as other frontier models like Claude 3.5 Sonnet.

The model's performance on general tasks

item.title: [论文] 让万亿模型长出百万个“小我”
item.description: 你有没有想过，为什么现在的 AI 聊天助手那么聪明，但总感觉像失忆了一样？每次对话都得重新自我介绍，或者重新交代偏好。它们的能力很强，但缺乏一个持续的“自我”——记不住你爱吃香菜，也记不住你喜欢代码注释里带点幽默。这篇论文就在认真琢磨这个问题：能不能给一个万亿参数的基础大模型，低成本地配上数百万个小小的“个性插件”，让每个用户都能拥有一个持续记忆、不断适应的 AI 分身？这个思路的源头挺有意思，...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，为什么现在的 AI 聊天助手那么聪明，但总感觉像失忆了一样？每次对话都得重新自我介绍，或者重新交代偏好。它们的能力很强，但缺乏一个持续的“自我”——记不住你爱吃香菜，也记不住你喜欢代码注释里带点幽默。这篇论文就在认真琢磨这个问题：能不能给一个万亿参数的基础大模型，低成本地配上数百万个小小的“个性插件”，让每个用户都能拥有一个持续记忆、不断适应的 AI 分身？

这个思路的源头挺有意思，是从人类基因组得到的启发。我们人和人之间共享超过 99% 的 DNA，真正让我们长得不一样、性格不同的，其实是那不到 1% 的差异。所以作者想，AI 也可以这么搞：用一个极其庞大的共享基座模型提供通用的推理能力和知识，再给每个用户挂一个极小的适配器——就像一小段个性化基因——用来存储那个用户的特定习惯、工具使用偏好、甚至一些需要长期记住的事情。这样一来，百万个性化模型的理论成本就能变得可行。

为了实现这个想法，他们沿着三个方向去探索，我管它们叫“往上攻”、“往下压”和“往外铺”。

往上攻，是说把基座模型做得更强，会让小小的适配器发挥出更大的威力。他们做了一个我觉得很敢想的实验：在万亿参数的混合专家模型（MoE）上，用 LoRA 这种极省参数的方法来做强化学习训练。常规的强化学习训这种规模的模型，运算量和通信代价高得离谱，但用了 LoRA 之后，他们把计算和通信占用量压缩到了大约全参数训练的 10%。不过马上遇到了新问题——在 MoE 架构里，训练时权重的微小扰动会让路由器把同一个 token 分到不同的专家模块里去，导致训练时计算路径和实际生成行为时的路径不一致，梯度就学歪了。他们给这种失效取了个很贴切的名字叫“训练-推理不匹配”。解决办法也挺直接：先在 rollout 阶段把路由决策记录下来，训练的时候原样重放，保证一致性。这个技巧叫 Router Replay R3，听着像录音机一样简单，但确实管用。

往下压，是去探一探适配器能有多小，小到极致了还能不能学、稳不稳定。他们试了从秩 r=1 到 r=256 的各种 LoRA 尺寸，发现虽然秩在 16 到 32 之间是性能与稳定性的甜点，但哪怕极端到 r=1，理论上也有可能学到跟高秩差不多的效果。不过 r=1 的适配器特别娇气，初始化稍微不对就崩溃。常规用 SVD（奇异值分解）去初始化 LoRA 因子的方法在强化学习里容易过早引入大幅度的策略变动，直接训塌。作者于是提出了一种叫 OLoRA-tail 的新初始化方式，利用预训练权重的“次要”奇异向量来起步，不加额外的缩放，这样相当于给 tiny 适配器一个几何上更温和的出发位，不轻易扰动基座模型原有的知识。同时他们还发现，LoRA 里的 alpha 缩放参数和秩之间如果遵循一个平方根关系（alpha ∝ sqrt(r)），超参数就能在不同的适配器尺寸之间复用，这让管理海量适配器省心不少，否则给每个尺寸调一遍参数简直不敢想。

另外在这个方向他们还塞了一个挺迷人的小模块，叫 δ-mem。它是一个带状态的在线记忆，不再只是静态的权重调整，而是像大脑里一个短期记忆缓存，在交互过程中根据预测残差不断写入状态，让模型能记住之前发生过的事情。这个记忆的参数量非常小，整体开销不到 0.5%，但在需要长期记忆的任务上效果提升很明显。我觉得这个设计很贴近我们平时对“记忆”的直觉——不只是记住固定知识，而是根据经历实时更新状态。

往外铺，就是把这些带着个性的适配器放到一个庞大的群体里，看它们互相作用会怎样。他们在 OASIS 模拟环境中跑了一个大规模社会仿真，对比了两类 AI 代理：一类只靠提示词描述人设，一类则带有一个持续学习过的个人 LoRA 适配器。粗略看，纯靠提示的代理在简单互动里也能应付，但一旦要求长期一致性、需要记住以前和谁说过什么话、维持特定的行为模式，那些有持久适配器的代理就稳多了。换句话说，个性不是说出来的人设，是“活”出来的记忆。

看完这个工作，我自己的感觉是，大规模个性化这条路正在变得很具体。但有些地方还有待进一步打磨。比如虽然单个人的适配器小到几百 KB 级别，但百万个适配器一起维护和更新，背后的存储、调度、隐私保护都不轻松。而且极低秩适配器的训练稳定性仍然是个坎，现在实际用大概率还是会选秩 16 以上。未来我挺好奇，会不会每个人都能在手机端训出属于自己的小模块，然后无缝挂到云端的巨型模型上，像一个永远不掉线的记忆容器。那该多方便。

论文：On the Scaling of PEFT: Towards Million Personal Models of Trillion Parameters [arXiv:2606.02437]

📄 论文原文概述
Current frontier artificial intelligence models are often characterized as "generic" or "amnesic." While they possess immense reasoning capabilities and knowledge, they typically lack a persistent individual identity or the ability to adapt to a specific user's habits, preferences, and long-term memory. Traditionally, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) have been viewed as cost-saving measures to approximate full fine-tuning. This research proposes a shift in perspective, investigating PEFT as a primary mechanism for creating and managing millions of persistent, personalized models layered on top of shared, trillion-parameter foundation models.

Figure 1: The conceptual framework of scaling Up, Down, and Out to enable a population of persistent personal models.

The core thesis is inspired by human biology: while humans share over 99% of their genome, the small remaining fraction accounts for the vast diversity of individual traits. The authors suggest that a similar architecture can be applied to AI, where a massive, shared base model provides general competence, while tiny, local adapters store instance-specific behavioral states—such as unique skills, tool-use habits, and personalized memory updates.

The Three-Axis Scaling Framework

To achieve the vision of "million personal models," the research investigates three interdependent scaling problems:

Scale Up: Investigating whether stronger, larger base models (at the trillion-parameter scale) enhance the effectiveness of small adapters.
Scale Down: Determining the minimum possible size for a local adaptive state while maintaining learning efficiency and expressiveness.
Scale Out: Exploring the possibilities that emerge when a large population of persistent, adapted instances coexists, such as in social simulations or collective intelligence tasks.
Scale Up: Strengthening the Shared Prior

The "Scale Up" axis focuses on the relationship between the base model and its adapters. The researchers demonstrate that a more capable base model offers a richer latent structure, making even minimal changes via an adapter more impactful.

A primary contribution in this section is the demonstration of LoRA-based reinforcement learning (RL) on trillion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, such as the Kimi K2. Conventional RL at this scale is prohibitively expensive, but by using LoRA, the researchers reduced the compute and communication footprint to approximately 10% of full-parameter training.

However, scaling to trillion-parameter MoE architectures introduces a specific failure mode called Training-Inference Mismatch (TIM). In MoE models, small perturbations in weights during training can cause the "router" to send tokens to different "experts" than those used during the initial rollout phase. This creates a semantic discrepancy where the gradients are calculated for a different computational path than the one that produced the behavior. To solve this, the authors introduced Router Replay R3, a technique that records routing decisions during the rollout phase and replays them during training to ensure consistency.

Scale Down: Minimizing the Adaptive State

For personalization to be practical for millions of users, the adaptive state must be as compact as possible. The "Scale Down" axis investigates the lower limits of adapter size and the stability of training at those limits.

LoRA Rank Regimes and OLoRA-tail

The researchers conducted an extensive sweep of LoRA ranks, from
𝑟
=
1
r=1 to
𝑟
=
256
r=256, using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). They found that while ranks 16 and 32 offer the best balance of performance and reliability, even an extreme rank of
𝑟
=
1
r=1 can theoretically match the performance of higher ranks. However, the reliability of
𝑟
=
1
r=1 adapters is highly sensitive to initialization and optimization.

Standard initialization methods for LoRA factors, like those based on Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), were found to cause training collapse in RL settings because they introduce large policy shifts too early. To address this, the authors proposed OLoRA-tail, an RL-native initialization method. OLoRA-tail initializes the adapter factors using the minor singular vectors of the pretrained weights without additional scaling. This provides a stable geometric starting point that allows tiny adapters to learn effectively without destabilizing the base model's prior.

Reusable Hyperparameters and Alpha Scaling

Managing a population of millions of adapters requires predictable training behavior. The research investigated how the "alpha" scaling parameter in LoRA affects the transferability of learning rates across different ranks. They established that a square-root scaling rule, where

𝛼
∝
𝑟
α∝
r
​


is more robust than constant or linear scaling, particularly for difficult reasoning tasks. This rule allows developers to reuse the same hyperparameters for different adapter sizes, simplifying the management of a large model population.

Stateful Adapters with
𝛿
δ-mem

Beyond static LoRA adapters, the paper introduces
𝛿
δ-mem, a compact, online associative-memory state. Unlike static weights,
𝛿
δ-mem is updated during interaction via a delta-rule learning mechanism that writes prediction residuals into a memory state
𝑆
𝑡
S
t
​

. This allows the model to maintain history-dependent steering with a parameter overhead of less than 0.5%, significantly improving performance on memory-intensive benchmarks.

Scale Out: Population-Scale Personalization

The "Scale Out" axis explores what happens when thousands or millions of these adapted models interact or are used together.

Social Simulation and Identity Persistence

In large-scale social simulations using the OASIS environment, the researchers compared "shared-base" agents (which rely only on prompts) against "per-user LoRA" agents (which have persistent, learned adapters). They found that prompt-based agents often suffer from "persona collapse," where they gradually lose their distinct traits and converge toward a generic average. In contrast, LoRA-adapted agents maintained higher identity-level heterogeneity, persistent stances, and formed more complex interaction communities. This suggests that structural changes in the model's policy are necessary for realistic long-term behavioral simulation.

Diversity as Collective Intelligence

The study also explored whether the diversity among cheaply produced adapters can be harnessed as a resource. By training many LoRA variants with minor variations in data and then aggregating their outputs via majority voting, the researchers observed a predictable improvement in task accuracy. This improvement followed a logarithmic scaling law:

Accuracy
≈
0.386
+
0.0172
ln
⁡
(
𝑘
)
Accuracy≈0.386+0.0172ln(k)

where
𝑘
k is the number of collaborating models. This "collaboration" outperformed simple repeated sampling from a single model, demonstrating that population-level diversity provides complementary policies that enhance overall reasoning.

Infrastructure for PEFT Populations: MinT

The final component of the research is MinT (Managed Infrastructure for Training and Serving Millions of LLMs). Realizing the vision of millions of personal models requires more than just algorithms; it requires a specialized systems architecture.

MinT manages the entire lifecycle of adapters, ensuring "adapter-only mobility." Instead of moving full, multi-gigabyte model checkpoints, the system only moves the lightweight adapter revisions. For a 30B parameter model, a rank-16 adapter is approximately 1.7 GB, compared to 61 GB for the full checkpoint.

The infrastructure handles:

Bounded Residency: Managing a massive catalog of up to
10
6
10
6
adapters while keeping only a small "working set" in the GPU's active batch slots.
Cold Loading and Hot-Reloading: Specialized "packed MoE LoRA loading" techniques were introduced to reduce the overhead of loading many fr

item.title: [论文] 一个模型搞定看、说、动，NVIDIA的“全模态大脑”
item.description: 你有没有觉得现在的AI模型分工太细了？想让它看懂图片，得调一个视觉语言模型；想生成一段视频，得换一个扩散模型；想控制机器人抓个杯子，还得再上另一个专门的动作模型。这些模型各干各的，互相之间不通气，就像你找了个翻译、一个画家、一个健身教练，但他们没法协同工作。NVIDIA最近提出的Cosmos 3，就是想把这些能力揉进同一个架构里，让AI既能理解文字、图像，又能生成视频、音频，甚至直接输出机器人的...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有觉得现在的AI模型分工太细了？想让它看懂图片，得调一个视觉语言模型；想生成一段视频，得换一个扩散模型；想控制机器人抓个杯子，还得再上另一个专门的动作模型。这些模型各干各的，互相之间不通气，就像你找了个翻译、一个画家、一个健身教练，但他们没法协同工作。NVIDIA最近提出的Cosmos 3，就是想把这些能力揉进同一个架构里，让AI既能理解文字、图像，又能生成视频、音频，甚至直接输出机器人的动作指令。这种“全模态世界模型”的思路，可能会让未来那些要在真实世界里干活的AI更协调一些。

Cosmos 3的核心设计挺有意思，它没有用一个大而全的Transformer把所有东西一锅炖，而是在每一层里分出了两条并行的“思考通道”。一条叫推理通道（Reasoner），专门负责理解，处理文字、图像的高层语义特征。它用的是自回归方式，就像你读一篇文章，一个字一个字往后看，慢慢构建出整段的含义。另一条叫生成通道（Generator），负责把理解好的东西变成高质量的视频、声音或动作。它不走自回归那套，而是用扩散的思路——先从一团噪声开始，然后学习怎么一步步把噪声“推”回干净的数据。具体来说，它用的是流匹配方法，让模型去预测一个固定的速度，让噪声点沿着直线滑到目标数据上，训练起来更稳定。两条通道在每个网络层里都能互相传递信息，比如生成视频的时候，可以随时参考推理通道对文字描述的解析，保证画面符合逻辑。这就好比大脑的语言区和视觉皮层能实时交流，你一边听“太阳落山”，一边就能在脑子里补完那个场景，而不会产生月亮挂在天上的奇怪画面。

为了让各种类型的输入都住进同一个“房子”，Cosmos 3给不同感官配了对应的编码器。视觉方面，用视觉Transformer提取语义特征给推理通道，同时用视频变分自编码器把一整段视频压缩成潜在空间里的令牌，方便生成通道干活。音频也用类似的VAE编码成令牌，这样生成视频时就能同步配上声音。最特别的是动作数据——论文里设计了一套统一的动作表示，无论是自动驾驶车的平移、旋转，还是机器人手臂的关节角度，甚至手指的抓取力度，都能用一串连续的向量来描述。这让同一个模型能适应不同形态的机器人，而不需要每个型号都从头训一个控制策略。更有趣的是，为了解决视频、音频、动作采样频率不同带来的对齐问题，他们用了一种3D多模态旋转位置编码，给每个令牌标上它在空间和时间里的绝对坐标，就像给一堆不同速度录制的视频打上统一的时间戳，保证所有线索都能在物理时间线上对齐。

数据规模是这个模型能否表现得像样的关键。Cosmos 3的训练分了三步走：先用几百万条图文对和7.67亿张图片、3.4亿段视频做预训练，让模型掌握基本的视觉和语言知识；中间阶段特意灌入了6.13万小时的机器人和车辆轨迹数据，以及很多带深度图、控制信号的视频转换任务，还请出了仿真生成的合成数据来弥补真实世界里罕见场景的不足（比如自动驾驶里危险的长尾案例）；最后再做专项微调，比如高分辨率视频生成或者特定机器人操作策略。这种渐进式课程，加上超大的数据量，让它能在不同任务之间分享知识，而不是孤立地学一招。

不过，这个工作目前更多是一个统一框架的展示，而不是某个单点性能的刷榜。论文里虽然展现了诸如物理级视频生成、同步音效和跨具身控制的例子，但具体在标准基准上的对照实验数量还不多。而且，要把这么庞大的模型塞进真实机器人里实时运行，计算开销是个不小的挑战。安全性验证也是一道坎——一个能自主生成动作的模型，如果出了离谱的预测，该怎么兜底？这可能是团队下一步要回答的问题。

（论文：Cosmos 3: Omnimodal World Models for Physical AI [arXiv:2606.02800]）

📄 论文原文概述
The development of intelligent systems capable of operating within physical environments—often called Physical AI—requires a confluence of perception, reasoning, and execution. Historically, these capabilities have been addressed by separate specialized models: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for understanding, diffusion models for high-fidelity video generation, and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic control. Cosmos 3, developed by NVIDIA, addresses this fragmentation by introducing a unified "omnimodal" world model. This model family is designed to jointly process and generate language, images, video, audio, and actions within a single architecture, providing a foundational backbone for systems that must simulate and interact with the physical world.

Figure 1: The Cosmos 3 framework unifies diverse capabilities—such as reasoning, video generation, and robotic control—into a single model backbone.

The Unified Mixture-of-Transformers Architecture

At the core of Cosmos 3 is a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture. While standard transformers use a single pathway for all tokens, the MoT design separates processing into two distinct but interacting "towers" within each transformer layer: a Reasoner pathway and a Generator pathway. This separation allows the model to handle the fundamentally different requirements of causal reasoning (which is typically autoregressive) and high-fidelity signal generation (which is typically diffusion-based).

The Reasoner pathway is designed for understanding and language-based reasoning. It processes discrete tokens, such as text and visual features from a Vision Transformer (ViT), using an autoregressive (AR) objective. In this mode, the model predicts the next token in a sequence, enabling tasks like visual question answering and complex scene description.

The Generator pathway is built for signal synthesis, including video, audio, and robotic actions. It utilizes a diffusion-based approach—specifically rectified flow matching. In this paradigm, the model learns to transform noise into clean data by predicting a velocity vector

By integrating these two pathways, Cosmos 3 can attend to reasoning tokens while generating signals. For instance, the Generator can conditioned its video synthesis on the Reasoner's internal representations of a text prompt or a previous image, ensuring that the generated physical world adheres to the logical constraints described in language.

Omnimodal Tokenization and Representation

To function as a single backbone, Cosmos 3 projects various modalities into a shared token space. This is achieved through modality-specific encoders and tokenizers that map raw data into a fixed hidden dimension
𝐷
D.

Vision: Visual inputs are handled by two separate systems. For reasoning, a ViT encoder provides high-level semantic features. For generation, a Video Variational Autoencoder (VAE) compresses video clips into a latent space. A video with dimensions
𝐿
×
𝐻
×
𝑊
×
𝐶
L×H×W×C (where
𝐿
L is the number of frames) is mapped to a latent grid of tokens.
Audio: Raw stereo audio is encoded using an audio VAE into a sequence of discrete tokens, allowing the model to generate sound that is temporally aligned with video.
Action: To support Physical AI across different embodiments, the researchers developed a unified action interface. This interface represents actions as continuous vectors encompassing ego-motion (3D position and 6D rotation), effector poses for robot arms, and grasp states for grippers.

Figure 2: The model uses a standardized action representation that can accommodate different embodiments, from autonomous vehicles to multi-fingered humanoid robots.

To maintain coherence across these modalities, Cosmos 3 employs a 3D Multimodal Rotary Position Embedding (MRoPE). This mechanism assigns coordinates to tokens based on their position in space and time. Because video, audio, and action data are sampled at different frequencies, the model uses absolute temporal indexing to ensure they align on a shared physical timeline.

Data Curation and Scaling

The utility of a world model is largely determined by the scale and diversity of its training data. Cosmos 3 was trained on a massive dataset comprising billions of images and videos, along with specialized Physical AI data. The training process follows a progressive curriculum:

Pre-training: The Reasoner is trained on millions of text and image-text pairs to establish a base of general knowledge. Simultaneously, the Generator is trained on 767 million images and over 340 million video clips to learn the visual appearance and basic dynamics of the world.
Mid-training: This stage introduces specialized data for Physical AI. It includes "Transfer" data (video-to-video tasks with control signals like depth maps) and "Action" data (61,300 hours of robot and vehicle trajectories). This phase also leverages Synthetic Data Generation (SDG) to fill gaps in real-world datasets, such as rare safety-critical scenarios or precise multi-view robot manipulation.
Post-training: The models are specialized for specific end-tasks, such as high-resolution text-to-video generation or specific robotic policies.

The researchers emphasize the importance of synthetic data in this pipeline. By using simulation tools to create diverse, labeled environments (e.g., SDG-DriveSim for autonomous driving or SDG-RobotSim for manipulation), they provide the model with a "ground truth" understanding of physics and causal relationships that are difficult to extract from raw web videos alone.

Figure 3: Synthetic data generation allows the model to learn from a wide variety of controlled scenarios, including warehouse logistics and human-robot interaction.

Infrastructure and Implementation

Scaling a model of this complexity requires a robust hardware and software stack. Cosmos 3 was developed using a custom infrastructure designed for high-throughput distributed training. The system utilizes Hybrid Sharded Data Parallelism (HSDP) and "Ulysses" Context Parallelism to split large models and long sequences across thousands of GPUs.

A key innovation in the training pipeline is the use of token-budgeted sequence packing. In standard training, fixed-length batches often lead to significant padding if data samples are shorter than the maximum length, wasting computation. Cosmos 3 uses a "JointDataLoader" that packs multiple samples of different modalities and resolutions into a single sequence, ensuring that the GPU remains fully utilized while respecting the model's maximum context window (up to 74,000 tokens).

For inference, the model is supported by a specialized serving framework called vLLM-Omni. This framework optimizes the sampling process for diffusion models, including techniques like "CFG-Parallel" (parallelizing classifier-free guidance) and "VAE-Patch-Parallel" (splitting image decoding across GPUs). These optimizations are critical for deploying the model in real-time or interactive applications.

Performance and Capabilities

Cosmos 3 was evaluated across dozens of benchmarks, demonstrating competitive or state-of-the-art performance in several domains.

Reasoning: In general vision-language tasks, Cosmos 3 variants (Edge 4B, Nano 16B, and Super 64B) show strong performance on benchmarks like MMMU and MMBench. In domain-specific reasoning for robotics and autonomous driving, the models of

item.title: [论文] 用写代码的方式画画
item.description: 你有没有遇到过这种情况：跟AI说“画五个苹果”，结果它给你画了六个、七个，或者苹果长在盘子上方飘着？这不是你的prompt写得不好，而是传统图像生成模型本质上是个“黑盒”——它用一步到位的方式，从文字直接跳到像素，中间发生了什么，谁也说不清。最近看到一篇论文，提出的GenClaw系统换了个思路。它不再让模型一次性“猜”出整张图，而是把画画这件事拆成了三个步骤：先想清楚要画什么，再画出结构草图，最...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有遇到过这种情况：跟AI说“画五个苹果”，结果它给你画了六个、七个，或者苹果长在盘子上方飘着？这不是你的prompt写得不好，而是传统图像生成模型本质上是个“黑盒”——它用一步到位的方式，从文字直接跳到像素，中间发生了什么，谁也说不清。

最近看到一篇论文，提出的GenClaw系统换了个思路。它不再让模型一次性“猜”出整张图，而是把画画这件事拆成了三个步骤：先想清楚要画什么，再画出结构草图，最后才上色渲染。我觉得这想法挺自然的，因为我们人类画画本来就是这么干的。

问题出在哪？

传统模型之所以数不对苹果，根子在于自然语言太模糊了。你说“五个苹果放在桌上”，模型可能抓住了“苹果”和“桌子”这两个概念，但“五”这个数字、苹果之间的空间关系，在从文字到像素的一步跳跃中很容易丢。于是你会看到苹果数量不对、位置乱飘、文字拼错——这些在学术上叫“幻觉”，其实就是模型没理解你的精确意图。

GenClaw的核心思路是：把精确的部分交给代码，把审美部分留给图像模型。

三层结构，各司其职

他们设计了一个三层框架，第一层叫“认知建构”。这层负责把用户的需求翻译成结构化的记录，有点像写一个详细的施工计划。如果用户说“设计一张2026世界杯的海报”，这层会主动去搜索实际的赛程日期、举办城市，确保事实准确。如果需要物理推理，比如画三个不同密度的球在水里的状态，它会直接计算每个球应该沉多深。

第二层是真正好玩的地方：“可执行画布”。这层用实际的编程语言来画草图。要画十三个甜甜圈？用SVG生成十三个独立节点，一个都不会少。要画一张文字密集的海报？用HTML/CSS来排，字体、对齐、拼写全给你保证对。要做物理模拟？用Three.js写代码，把阿基米德定律、胡克定律算清楚，球的沉浮位置是数学上决定的，不是模型猜的。

到了第三层，这个精确的结构草图才被送进高清图像生成模型，做的工作就只是“上色”——加纹理、打光、阴影，让画面真实好看。生成完之后还有个检查环节，用视觉模型对比原始计划和最终结果，哪里出了错都能追溯：是草图画错了，还是渲染阶段的问题。

数字说话

论文里的实验结果挺说明问题。在需要精确计数的任务上，GenClaw把物体数量的准确率从传统模型的33%拉到了90%以上。文本渲染任务中，拼写和布局的正确率从39%提升到95%以上。特别值得注意的是物理推理场景，比如画浮在水里的球，传统模型常常画得不合物理直觉，而GenClaw通过写代码解物理方程，能做到完全符合规律。

为什么我觉得这个思路有意思

一个让我觉得很舒服的设计是，它把“思考”和“执行”分开了。传统模型在生成过程中，结构和颜色是搅在一起的，所以改prompt其实是在碰运气——你强调“五个苹果”，它可能只是换个方式理解你这句话，但内部生成过程还是黑盒。GenClaw通过代码这个中间层，把结构先“锁死”再渲染，这就变得可调试、可追溯了。

当然，这个方法也有它自己的代价。比如对简单任务来说，走三层流程可能有点“杀鸡用牛刀”；而且代码生成的正确性本身也是个问题，虽然现在的大模型写SVG和HTML的能力已经很不错了。但我感觉这个方向是对的——让不同工具做它们擅长的事，代码做精确控制，扩散模型做审美表达。

未来可能的延伸也很有意思。既然草图是用代码写的，那可交互性就很高：用户可以手动改改SVG里的某个元素，再重新渲染。或者做动画时，代码能保证帧与帧之间的结构一致性。我觉得把“用代码思考”这个能力扩展到更多模态，比如3D内容生成，可能是水到渠成的事。

论文：GenClaw: Code-Driven Agentic Image Generation [arXiv:2605.30248]

📄 论文原文概述
Rethinking Image Generation: From Prompting to Planning

The landscape of artificial intelligence has transitioned rapidly from simple text-to-pixel translation to sophisticated agentic systems. Early diffusion models and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) functioned primarily as "black boxes"—users provided a text prompt, and the model generated an image in a single, end-to-end pass. While these models produce high-quality aesthetics, they often struggle with precision, such as exact object counts, complex spatial layouts, or specific typographical requirements.

GenClaw introduces a paradigm shift by treating image generation as a multi-staged, code-driven process. Instead of asking a model to "guess" the entire scene at once, GenClaw decomposes the task into conceptualization, sketching, and coloring. This approach mirrors the human artistic workflow: an artist first thinks about the subject, sketches the structure (the "skeleton"), and finally applies color and detail. By using executable code—such as SVG, HTML, and Three.js—as an intermediate representation, the system gains a "digital brush" that allows for deterministic control over the visual structure before a single pixel is rendered.

The Limitations of Black-Box Generation

Traditional Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) and text-to-image agents typically rely on iterative prompt refinement. If a model fails to generate exactly five apples, the agent might rewrite the prompt to emphasize the number five. This "trial-and-error" cycle is inherently stochastic; because the final synthesis is hidden within the model's weights, the agent acts more as an optimizer of words than a direct creator of visual structure.

Natural language is often too ambiguous for precise technical tasks. For example, describing the exact coordinates of a menu item or the physical trajectory of a water spray is difficult in prose. This ambiguity leads to "hallucinations" where models ignore constraints, miscount objects, or garble text. GenClaw addresses this by offloading structural planning to code, which is unambiguous and logically rigorous.

The Three-Layer Agentic Framework

The core of GenClaw is its three-layer architecture: Cognitive Structuring, Executable Canvas Construction, and Visual Generation and Review. This framework decouples the "thinking" from the "drawing," allowing for specialized tools to handle different aspects of the creative process.

Layer 1: Cognitive Structuring (Conceptualize)

The process begins with the agent parsing the user's intent. If the request involves real-world facts (e.g., "Design a poster for the 2026 World Cup"), the agent invokes search tools to retrieve current data, such as tournament dates and host cities. For tasks requiring reasoning, such as physics simulations or geometric problems, the agent performs explicit calculations. The output of this layer is a "Structured Record" (often in JSONL format), which prioritizes executability and logical consistency over descriptive flair.

Layer 2: Executable Canvas (Sketch)

This layer transforms the structured plan into a visual "sketch" using programming languages. By using code, the agent defines the deterministic structure of the image:

SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics): Used for compositional scenes where object counts (
𝑁
N) and spatial coordinates are critical. If the user wants thirteen donuts, the agent generates thirteen distinct SVG nodes.
HTML/CSS: Used for text-heavy tasks like posters or menus. This ensures that text is rendered with perfect spelling and alignment, offloading the difficult task of character generation from the image model.
Three.js/Python: Used for physical simulations. The agent can model physical laws, such as Archimedes' principle for buoyancy or Hooke's Law for springs, creating a "symbolic world model" that serves as the visual draft.
Layer 3: Visual Generation and Review (Color)

In the final stage, the executable sketch is passed to a high-fidelity image generation model. The model's role is no longer to plan the scene but to "color" it—adding textures, lighting, shadows, and photorealism. After generation, a review module (powered by a Vision-Language Model or user feedback) checks the output against the initial structured record. Because the process is staged, errors are traceable: if a count is wrong, the system can check if the error occurred in the SVG code (sketch) or the rendering stage (color).

Precise Control Through Structured Sketching

One of the most significant advantages of the code-driven approach is its ability to handle complex layouts that traditional models often fail. In tasks involving precise positioning, GenClaw uses the SVG backend to define exact bounding boxes and layer orders.

As shown in the evaluation, direct generators often suffer from attribute-binding issues—for instance, coloring the wrong object or placing items in the wrong relative positions. By generating a sketch first, GenClaw "locks in" these attributes. The image model then follows this structural guide, significantly reducing the likelihood of spatial hallucinations.

Bridging the Gap with Physical Reasoning

GenClaw extends the capabilities of image agents into the realm of physical simulation. Traditional generative models often produce physically implausible scenes because they lack an internal model of physics. GenClaw solves this by having the agent write code to solve the relevant physical equations before drawing.

For example, if asked to depict three balls with different densities in water, the agent identifies the relevant law (Archimedes' Principle) and calculates the submerged fraction for each ball. If a ball has density
𝜌
ρ, the submerged volume is proportional to the ratio of its density to the water's density. The agent then translates these calculations into a visual draft where the vertical position (the
𝑧
z-axis or
𝑦
y-coordinate) is mathematically determined.

Similarly, for a spring system, the agent uses Hooke's Law:

𝐹
=
𝑘
Δ
𝐿
F=kΔL

To find the elongation
Δ
𝐿
ΔL, it calculates
Δ
𝐿
=
𝑚
𝑔
/
𝑘
ΔL=mg/k, where
𝑚
m is the mass,
𝑔
g is the gravitational constant, and
𝑘
k is the spring constant. This ensures that the visual representation of the spring's length is physically consistent with the weight attached to it.

Layered Image Editing and Knowledge Grounding

Beyond generation from scratch, GenClaw demonstrates high performance in image editing. Most current editors modify the entire image, which can lead to unintended changes in unedited regions (measured by metrics like PSNR and SSIM). GenClaw adopts a "Layered Editing" approach: it decomposes the image into discrete layers using tools like Segment Anything (SAM). By targeting only the relevant layer in the code, the agent ensures that the rest of the canvas remains untouched.

For tasks requiring deep world knowledge, GenClaw uses a search-and-grounding workflow. When asked to draw a specific real-world location or a future event, the agent searches for reference images and facts, incorporating them into the structured plan.

Empirical Findings and Performance

The researchers evaluated GenClaw across several benchmarks, comparing it to both open-source models (like Qwen-Image) and proprietary systems (like GPT-Image-1.5).

Compositional Control: On the GenEval++ benchmark, which tests instruction following for complex layouts, GenClaw achieved a score of
0.878
0.878, outperforming Qwen-Image (
0.668
0.668) and GPT-Image-1.5 (
0.750
0.750). The deterministic nature of SVG code contributed significantly to its superior performance in object counting and spatial relations.
Text Rendering: On LongText-Bench, GenClaw achieved scores of
0.989
0.989 for English and
0.988
0.988 for Chinese. This highlights the effectiveness of using HTML/SVG text layers, as the model no longer needs to "draw" characters but simply renders the code.
Image Editing: In editing tasks, GenClaw maintained a high VLM-Score of
4.29
4.29 wh

item.title: [论文] 别只算token，算有用反馈
item.description: 你有没有这种感觉——现在聊AI代理系统，大家都在比谁调用的工具多、谁的上下文更长，但好像很少人关心这些消耗里到底有多少是“有用功”？最近我看到一篇论文，就在琢磨这件事。它想解决的问题很实在：我们在评估一个AI代理的时候，往往只看它总共花了多少token、跑了多少步，但这些粗糙的指标根本分辨不出有效探索与原地打转的区别。比如两个轨迹消耗的token数完全一样，一条路是每一步都有清晰反馈、稳扎稳打地...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有这种感觉——现在聊AI代理系统，大家都在比谁调用的工具多、谁的上下文更长，但好像很少人关心这些消耗里到底有多少是“有用功”？最近我看到一篇论文，就在琢磨这件事。它想解决的问题很实在：我们在评估一个AI代理的时候，往往只看它总共花了多少token、跑了多少步，但这些粗糙的指标根本分辨不出有效探索与原地打转的区别。比如两个轨迹消耗的token数完全一样，一条路是每一步都有清晰反馈、稳扎稳打地接近目标，另一条路却在一个错误上反复尝试、或者读了一大堆不相关的工具输出。如果只用原始计算量做参考，这两条路会被当成“算力消耗相同”，这显然不符合我们的直觉——后者的成功概率肯定更低。

于是研究者们提了个新指标，叫“有效反馈计算量”，英文缩写EFC。它的思路很巧妙：不看花了多少钱，而看花出去的钱买到了多少“真东西”。具体怎么做呢？他们把代理的每一步交互拆成一个个“反馈事件”，然后对每个事件打四个分，分值都在0到1之间。这四个维度分别是：信息量（这条反馈是否带来了任务相关的新线索）、有效性（它是不是被可靠的验证、比如一个可执行的测试结果）、非冗余相关性（它是不是刚好针对当前子目标，而且轨迹里还没出现过这个信息）、以及记忆更新（它是否真的改变了代理的计划或状态，影响了后续行动）。只有这四个维度同时得分，这个事件才算“有效”；只要有一项是0，就算你在这个事件上耗了很多token，它对EFC的贡献也是零。这很像我们平时说的“有效努力”——既要方向对，还要动作有根据，还不能是老调重弹。

他们给EFC定义了一个简单的乘法形式：每个事件的EFC等于一个缩放常数10，乘以那四个维度的乘积，然后再把整条轨迹的所有事件EFC加总。这样，轨迹的有效反馈就由一个标量给出来了。实验里他们在一些可控的合成任务上对比了不同缩放坐标的预测能力，结果很有意思：如果横轴用原始token数或墙钟时间，失败率基本看不出什么单调趋势，散点乱成一团；但换成EFC之后，失败率随EFC增加而规律下降，拟合度很高。也就是说，EFC这个坐标真的抓住了能推动任务前进的“养分”量。

更有意思的是，他们还往前走了一步，用两个概念把性能拆开来解释。一个是“套索效率”，也就是系统把原始计算预算（比如钱、token）转化成EFC的比例。好比两个代理花同样的钱，效率高的那个能榨出更多的有效反馈，自然更容易成功。他们发现很多时候性能提升的秘诀不是砸更多的原始算力，而是把转化效率搞上去——比如做好验证器、把路由器变聪明。另一个概念叫“任务需求”，不同问题需要的有效反馈量天生不同。他们用一个综合了步骤复杂度、工具选择难度、状态管理压力、环境噪声和验证器可用性的公式来量化任务难度，然后把EFC除以这个难度，得到了一个跨任务可比的归一化坐标。有了这个坐标，不同设计、不同难度的代理性能就可以放在同一张图上漂亮地预测出来了。

我觉得这个工作挺有启发的地方在于，它把我们设计代理系统时的模糊经验给量化了。我们之前总说“要加验证”“要少做重复动作”，但EFC给了这些设计原则一个统一的算力效率语言。当然，它目前的局限性也不少：四个维度在实际任务中是很难精确获得的，论文也只能用一些可观测特征去估计；乘性假设也许有点苛刻，有些“不那么有效但也不是完全无效”的信息可能被过分低估；任务需求的建模还比较简化，离真实世界的复杂任务有距离。但总体上，它打开了一个重新定义“代理算力”的口子——不是看用了多少电，而是用来做了多少有用功。以后也许可以自动从轨迹里学出这些维度，或者用它来指导代理的在线预算分配。

（论文：Scaling Laws for Agent Harnesses via Effective Feedback Compute [arXiv:2605.29682]）

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction to Agent Harness Scaling

Large language models (LLMs) have evolved from simple text predictors into components of interactive agent systems. These systems rely on an "agent harness"—a collection of software components that manage how the base model interacts with its environment. This includes handling tool calls, processing feedback, managing memory, and revising solutions based on errors. As these systems become more complex, understanding how their performance scales with computational resources is critical for design and deployment.

Traditional scaling laws in LLM research focus on pre-training, linking model performance to parameters, data volume, and training compute. Recent work has extended this to "test-time compute," showing that performance can improve by allowing a model more time to think, search, or revise during inference. However, these analyses typically use raw expenditure metrics—such as total tokens, wall-clock time, or the number of tool calls—as the scaling coordinate.

Figure 1: Comparison of various scaling coordinates on synthetic controllable tasks. Raw compute metrics (top row) show poor correlation with failure rates, while Effective Feedback Compute (EFC) and its demand-normalized variants (bottom row) provide a highly predictive scaling signal.

This research identifies a fundamental limitation in using raw compute as a scaling coordinate for agents: it fails to distinguish between useful interactions and redundant or unhelpful ones. Two agent trajectories might consume the same number of tokens, but one may contain critical, verified feedback that leads to success, while the other contains repetitive errors or uninformative tool outputs. To address this, the paper introduces Effective Feedback Compute (EFC), a trace-level scalar measure designed to quantify the volume of useful closed-loop feedback within an agent system.

Formalizing Effective Feedback Compute (EFC)

The core contribution of this work is the formalization of

The researchers developed several variants of this metric:

Oracle-EFC: Used in synthetic tasks where internal state and ground-truth progress are known.
Estimated-EFC: A version for real-world tasks where

Decomposing Performance: Harness Efficiency and Task Demand

The paper moves beyond just measuring

Experimental Results and Empirical Validation

The researchers validated the

Superiority of EFC over Raw Compute

The Role of Harness Quality

One of the most striking experiments involved "matched-budget interventions." The researchers compared two different harness configurations that consumed identical amounts of raw tokens and tool calls. Despite having the same "cost," the harness designed for higher feedback quality achieved a success rate of 0.90, compared to only 0.27 for the lower-quality version. Figure 4: Analysis of HumanEval tasks. The left panel shows that EFC-based coordinates have much higher predictive power (higher R^2) than raw tokens. The right panel illustrates the strong linear relationship between harness efficiency (

item.title: [论文] 反复打磨同一个思路
item.description: 你有没有试过从几张照片重建一个三维场景？拍几张不同角度的照片，然后拼出整个房间或建筑的样子——这个事在计算机视觉里叫多视角三维重建。传统方法是走一个迭代流程：先匹配不同图片中的特征点，再估算每张照片的拍摄位置，最后优化出三维点云。这个过程是循环往复、逐步求精的。但最近几年，深度学习领域的人开始用Transformer来干这个活。他们的思路是"一把梭"——把一堆图片扔进去，模型一口气输出三维几何。...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有试过从几张照片重建一个三维场景？拍几张不同角度的照片，然后拼出整个房间或建筑的样子——这个事在计算机视觉里叫多视角三维重建。传统方法是走一个迭代流程：先匹配不同图片中的特征点，再估算每张照片的拍摄位置，最后优化出三维点云。这个过程是循环往复、逐步求精的。

但最近几年，深度学习领域的人开始用Transformer来干这个活。他们的思路是"一把梭"——把一堆图片扔进去，模型一口气输出三维几何。为了应对重建任务的复杂度，这些模型被堆得越来越大，参数动辄超过十亿。可有趣的是，有研究分析发现，这些极深的Transformer其实隐含地做着类似迭代的事：不同层之间反复执行相似的计算，一点点打磨内部的表征。

这就引出了我们今天要聊的这篇论文。它提出的架构叫Déjà View，核心idea挺直觉的：既然深度模型内部已经在隐式迭代，那不如把这件事做明确。他们不堆几百层不同的Transformer，而是只用一个Transformer块，让它循环执行很多次。

我觉得这个思路有点像你反复推敲一句话怎么写。不是一口气写一百句不同的句子，而是一句话反复改，每次微调一点，最终打磨出满意的版本。Déjà View就是用同一个Transformer块反复做k次，每次喂给它上一步的输出，让它再优化一轮。

具体怎么做呢？整个流程分三步。首先，所有输入图片经过一个预训练的DINOv2编码器，变成一堆图像块token。模型还给这些token加了几种特殊token——寄存器token提供计算空间，相机token专门负责收集相机位姿的信息。

然后进入核心环节：那个被循环执行的Transformer块。每次执行包含两种注意力机制。帧内注意让同一张图里的token相互交流，关注单视角内的空间关系。全局注意则让所有图片的所有token跨视角沟通，在这里建立几何对应关系。做完一轮，输出喂给下一轮，同一套参数再次执行。

但这里有个问题：同一个块做这么多轮，它怎么知道自己在早期粗略对齐阶段还是后期精细调整阶段？于是作者引入了一个时间条件机制。他们用一个小MLP把当前的迭代进度（k/K）编码成一组缩放向量，去调制Transformer块里各个子层的输出。这意味着同一组参数在不同时间点可以表现出不同的行为——t=0.1时侧重粗对齐，t=0.9时侧重细节打磨。

一个物理层，通过时间调节模拟出了深度网络的效果，参数却少了一个数量级。

最后是解码阶段。做完K轮迭代后，模型用并行解码头预测三类东西：每个像素的射线（包含原点和方向）、深度图、以及每张图的相机参数。把射线和深度结合，就能算出最终的三维坐标。这套射线加深度的双重表示给了模型一定的冗余度，让它在室内、室外不同类型场景里都能保持稳定。

效果怎么样？参数效率相当亮眼。Déjà View以不到70M的参数，在两个主流多视角重建基准上跟那些800M甚至更大参数的模型打平或超过。更有意思的是，随着迭代次数k增加，重建精度确实持续提升，而且没有出现很快饱和的情况。这验证了循环迭代本身就是有效的。

研究者在分析内部状态演化时还发现一个现象：在早期迭代中，不同视角的信息迅速融合，状态变化剧烈；到了后期，变化幅度变小，模型在做局部微调。这种由粗到精的动态，恰恰是经典几何重建管线的节奏，只不过现在被一个可学习的循环块自发地涌现出来了。

局限的话，我觉得一个比较明显的地方是它目前还是在相对标准的场景设定下工作——输入图片数量固定、场景规模有限。真实应用中可能面临更乱的图片顺序、更大的场景范围，这时候循环迭代的鲁棒性怎么样还需要验证。另外，时间条件目前是个简单的时间比例信号，未来也许可以让模型自己决定什么时候停止迭代，实现自适应计算量。

论文：Déjà View: Looping Transformers for Multi-View 3D Reconstruction [arXiv:2605.30215]

📄 论文原文概述
Iterative Refinement in 3D Reconstruction

Multi-view 3D reconstruction is the process of recovering the three-dimensional geometry of a scene from a collection of two-dimensional images taken from different viewpoints. Traditionally, this was solved using Structure-from-Motion (SfM) pipelines. These classical systems are inherently iterative, alternating between establishing correspondences (feature matching), estimating camera positions (pose estimation), and optimizing the final 3D points (bundle adjustment).

In recent years, deep learning—specifically transformer-based architectures—has shifted the paradigm toward "one-shot" reconstruction. These models attempt to regress the entire 3D geometry from a set of images in a single forward pass. To handle the complexity of 3D scenes, researchers have scaled these models to massive proportions, often exceeding one billion parameters. However, recent analysis suggests that the depth of these models may be performing a form of "implicit iteration." Instead of performing a new operation in every layer, deep transformers often apply similar transformations repeatedly to refine an internal representation.

Figure 1: The DéjàView architecture uses a looping transformer block to iteratively refine 3D geometry and camera poses. As the number of iterations
𝑘
k increases, the accuracy of the reconstruction improves significantly.

DéjàView introduces an architecture that makes this iterative process explicit. Rather than using hundreds of unique transformer layers, it uses a single, shared transformer block applied recurrently. This approach, known as a Looping Transformer, achieves state-of-the-art results with a fraction of the parameters of typical large-scale models. By tying the weights across refinement steps, the model gains a stronger inductive bias for the reconstruction task, viewing the problem as a continuous refinement of an evolving state.

The DéjàView Architecture

The architecture of DéjàView is designed to be parameter-efficient while maintaining the representative power required for complex 3D scenes. It consists of three primary stages: feature encoding, iterative refinement through a looped block, and geometric decoding.

Patch Encoding and Tokenization

The process begins with
𝑉
V input images
{
𝐼
𝑖

𝑉

}

​

. Each image is passed through a shared, pre-trained DINOv2 (Vision Transformer-B) encoder. This encoder produces a grid of patch tokens that capture local and global semantic features. To these patch tokens, the model prepends several special tokens:

Register Tokens: A small set of learnable tokens (typically 4) that provide "workspace" for the transformer.
Camera Tokens: Learnable tokens specifically designated to aggregate information for camera pose estimation.

Each view is treated as a sequence of tokens, and 2D rotary position embeddings are used to encode spatial information within each frame. The concatenation of these tokens across all views forms the initial state
𝑧
0

​

The Looped Transformer Block

The core of the model is the shared transformer block applied for
𝐾
K steps. Each step consists of two distinct attention mechanisms:

Frame Attention: This sub-block allows tokens within the same image to interact. It focuses on spatial relationships and local geometry within a single viewpoint.
Global Attention: This sub-block operates across all tokens from all images simultaneously. It is here that the model performs cross-view matching and establishes the geometric correspondences necessary for 3D reconstruction.

Unlike standard transformers where every layer has different weights, DéjàView uses the exact same weights for every iteration
𝑘
k. To allow the model to adapt its behavior as the refinement progresses, the authors introduce time-conditioning.

Figure 2: The model workflow. Features from DINOv2 and special tokens are processed by a recurrent Looped Transformer Block. The resulting state is then passed to parallel heads for ray, depth, and camera parameter prediction.

Time-Conditioned Recurrence

To guide the shared block through the different stages of reconstruction (e.g., initial coarse alignment vs. fine detail refinement), the block is conditioned on a continuous time variable

Geometric Decoding

This dual representation (rays and depth) provides redundancy that helps the model stay robust across different scene types, from indoor rooms to outdoor landscapes.

Directional Refinement Dynamics

A key discovery in this work is how the internal state
𝑧
𝑘
z
k
​

evolves during the loop. In many recurrent systems, the state is expected to converge to a fixed point. However, in DéjàView, the authors observe "directional refinement."

As the iterations progress, the absolute magnitude (norm) of the feature vectors actually increases. However, the direction of the vectors stabilizes. This is evidenced by the cosine similarity between the state at step
𝑘
k and the final step
𝑧
𝐾

K
​

Figure 3: Metrics showing how the model converges. While the feature norm grows, the cosine similarity indicates the representation is pointing in an increasingly stable direction.

This suggests that the transformer is not trying to find a static representation, but rather a "refined direction" in high-dimensional space that the decoder heads can interpret as precise geometry. Furthermore, the global attention patterns show emergent behavior: in early iterations, a patch might attend to large areas of other views, but by the final iteration, the attention focuses sharply on the exact corresponding physical point in other images.

Figure 4: The evolution of task metrics (Pose AUC and Pointmap Rel. L2) over 16 iterations. Performance improves consistently, demonstrating the effectiveness of the explicit loop.

Results and Efficiency

DéjàView demonstrates that parameter count is not the only way to achieve high performance. With only 117M parameters, it competes with and often outperforms models with over 1.2 billion parameters.

On benchmarks like ScanNet++ (complex indoor scenes) and nuScenes (large-scale outdoor driving), DéjàView achieves superior camera pose accuracy and 3D pointcloud fidelity. For example, on ScanNet++, it achieves a Pose AUC@3
∘
∘
of 79.4, while much larger models like VGGT-Ω-1B (1144M parameters) achieve significantly lower scores under the same conditions.

Figure 5: Qualitative comparison of point clouds generated by DéjàView (117M parameters) versus much larger models. DéjàView produces d

item.title: [论文] 让一群AI自己组队做科研
item.description: 我最近看到一篇论文，想法挺朴素的：既然人类做研究都是分小组、随时调整方向、互相吐槽，那能不能让AI也这么干？现在大多数AI科研助手，要么是独自闷头一条道走到黑，要么是有一个“中央规划师”分配任务。这在小任务上还行，但碰到需要持续几天的实验探索，就容易卡壳——前期定错方向，后面就全废了。这篇论文提出的AUTOSCIENTISTS框架，就是想让一群AI智能体像人类实验室一样，没有固定领导，自己组队、...
item.content:encoded:
我最近看到一篇论文，想法挺朴素的：既然人类做研究都是分小组、随时调整方向、互相吐槽，那能不能让AI也这么干？现在大多数AI科研助手，要么是独自闷头一条道走到黑，要么是有一个“中央规划师”分配任务。这在小任务上还行，但碰到需要持续几天的实验探索，就容易卡壳——前期定错方向，后面就全废了。这篇论文提出的AUTOSCIENTISTS框架，就是想让一群AI智能体像人类实验室一样，没有固定领导，自己组队、自己争论、自己认栽，然后共享失败经验，连续跑好几周的实验。

核心思路其实挺直觉的：搞一个去中心化的多智能体团队，大家通过一个“共享白板”来协调。这个白板分成四块：一个“冠军位”，展示当前最好的模型和复现方法；一本“实验流水账”，记录每一次跑的实验代码和结果；一个“公共论坛”，大家在上面写自己的猜想、对别人方案的吐槽；还有各小组自己的任务队列和“死胡同登记表”。这样，任何人随时都能知道整个实验室的现状，避免重复劳动，还能立刻捡起别人发现的好线索。

团队里的角色分两种：分析师和实验员。分析师更像战略家，他们不亲自动手跑代码，而是盯着流水账和论坛看，找数据里的模式。比如，如果某个模型结构总是翻车，他们就在死胡同登记表上标一笔；如果某个优化器的小改动连续几次都带来提升，他们就提议沿着这个方向深挖。实验员是执行战术的，他们从任务队列里领当前最高优先级的提案，改代码、用GPU跑训练，然后把结果和一份“机理分析”扔回日志里——不光是报一个数字，还要用自己的理解解释为什么这次实验是这个结果。这套解释再被分析师拿去调整整体思路，形成一个循环。

最有意思的是他们如何自我组织。人类课题组不会永远只做一个方向，他们定期开组会讨论哪个方向更有戏。这里也给AI设计了类似的“讨论阶段”和“执行阶段”交替。当进展变慢，或者刚开始启时，所有智能体进入一个结构化辩论：大家各自提出候选研究方向，相互批评，但不搞“只选一个最优”的投票。系统允许同时形成多个小团队，分别盯不同研究轴，比如一个队专攻优化器，另一个队专攻数据增强。讨论什么时候结束也没有经理说了算，大家投票，一旦共识达成，最后那个按字母顺序排的智能体把提案整合成一个团队名单。整个过程，团队可以自然拆开、合并或者解散，完全由实验反馈驱动。

这样一个没有老板的体系，跑起来效果到底怎么样？论文里在几个基准上做了实验，把计算预算当做限制——说白了就是看花同样的钱、跑差不多的时间，哪组最后找到的模型更好。结果是，当实验允许跑得比较久（比如模拟几周的工作量），AUTOSCIENTISTS这种自组织团队找到的模型质量持续提升，而传统的中央规划式系统早早就碰到了天花板。研究者认为，关键就在于它能及时抛弃死胡同，把算力重新分配给更靠谱的方向，而且失败经验会被整个团队吸收，而不是淹没在单个智能体的记忆里。

当然，这个框架目前还是跑在代码优化这种干实验上，智能体们产出的是模型结构或超参数方案。未来能不能管湿实验室，比如自动安排生物实验并真的去操作机器人，还有不少距离。还有一个现实问题：这么多智能体不停地开会、读共享状态，本身的协调开销也不小，预算如果太紧反而可能划不来。但我觉得这个思路挺漂亮的，它没有试图造一个万能的“AI总师”，而是把科学发现看成一个社会化过程，用去中心化的方式让失败和知识都流动起来。也许以后我们真的可以放一支AI团队自己跑一个课题，隔段时间去看看论坛，发现它们已经自己吵过架、试过错、还找到了一个谁也没想到的方案。

论文：AutoScientists: Self-Organizing Agent Teams for Long-Running Scientific Experimentation [arXiv:2605.28655]

📄 论文原文概述
The Challenge of Sustained Scientific Discovery

Scientific research is rarely a linear path from hypothesis to discovery. Instead, it is a complex, iterative cycle involving the generation of multiple competing hypotheses, the design of experiments to test them, and the subsequent revision of plans based on empirical evidence. While Artificial Intelligence (AI) has made significant strides in automating specific parts of this cycle—such as literature review or code generation—most existing AI agent systems struggle with the "long-running" nature of science.

Current AI agents typically operate under two constraints. First, many are single-agent systems that follow a single trajectory, effectively "putting all their eggs in one basket." If the initial research direction is a dead end, the agent may struggle to pivot. Second, existing multi-agent systems often rely on a centralized "manager" or "planner." While this structure works for short-term tasks, it creates bottlenecks in complex research where the most promising directions only emerge after dozens of experiments. Centralized systems often assume that the search space can be partitioned into fixed tasks at the start, which is rarely true in open-ended scientific exploration.

Figure 1: The AUTOSCIENTISTS framework enables a decentralized team of agents to conduct parallel experiments, self-organize into teams, and coordinate via a shared state.

AUTOSCIENTISTS is a framework designed to overcome these limitations. It introduces a decentralized, self-organizing multi-agent team that mimics the collaborative nature of human research departments. Rather than following a fixed plan, agents in this system dynamically form teams around emerging ideas, critique each other's proposals, and share knowledge about what failed just as much as what succeeded.

A Decentralized Framework for Research

At the core of the framework is a team of
𝑛
n agents,
𝐴
=
{
𝑎

…
,
𝑎
𝑛
}

1

,…,a
n
​

}, working together to find an optimal program
𝑝
∗
p
∗
that maximizes a specific evaluation metric
ℓ
eval
(
𝑝

𝐷

ℓ

​

𝐷
D. The agents might start with an initial seed program
𝑝
0

​

and work to refine it over days or weeks of continuous experimentation.

The key distinction of this approach is its lack of a central orchestrator. Instead, coordination is achieved through a "Shared State" (
𝑆
S). This shared state acts as the laboratory's collective memory and is accessible to all agents through a standardized protocol. The shared state is composed of four distinct layers:

The Champion (
𝑝
∗
p
∗
): The current best-performing model or code, along with instructions on how to reproduce it.
The Experiment Log (
𝐿
L): A complete history of every experiment run by the team, including the code used, the results, and training diagnostics.
The Shared Forum (
𝐹
F): A space where agents post structured debate, hypothesize about why certain changes worked, and critique the proposals of others.
Team-Local State: This includes specific experiment queues (
𝑄
𝑘
Q
k
​

) and dead-end registries (
𝐷
𝑘
D
k
​

) for individual sub-teams.

By using this shared state, the system ensures that no two agents are redundantly performing the same work and that insights discovered by one team can be immediately leveraged by another.

The Architecture of Collaborative Agents

The system divides labor between two primary roles: Analyst Agents and Experiment Agents. These agents operate in a "heartbeat cycle," where they repeatedly read the shared state, decide on an action, and update the state with their findings.

Analyst Agents

Analysts are the "strategists" of the team. They do not run the experiments themselves; instead, they monitor the experiment log
𝐿
L and the shared forum
𝐹
F. Their job is to look for patterns in the data. If an analyst notices that a certain type of model architecture consistently fails, they will register that direction in the "Dead-End Registry." Conversely, if they see a small but consistent improvement from a specific hyperparameter change, they will propose new experiments to explore that direction further. They are also responsible for maintaining the team's long-term research hypotheses.

Experiment Agents

Experiment agents are the "tacticians." They claim the highest-priority proposals from the experiment queue
𝑄
𝑘
Q
k
​

and execute them. This involves modifying the codebase, running the training on a GPU, and reporting the results back to the log. Importantly, experiment agents do more than just report a single number; they provide a "mechanistic analysis" of why they think the experiment yielded a certain result, which then feeds back into the analyst's strategic planning.

Dynamic Self-Organization and Peer Review

One of the most characteristic features of this framework is how it handles the organization of these agents. Scientific priorities shift as results come in. To reflect this, the system alternates between a Discussion Phase and an Execution Phase.

When experimental progress stagnates, or at the very beginning of a run, the agents enter a structured discussion. They propose candidates for research directions and critique them. Unlike a voting system that might settle on a single "winner," AUTOSCIENTISTS allows for the formation of a team roster
𝑅
=
{
(
𝑇

}

k
​

,axis
k
​

,members
k
​

)}, where
𝑇
𝑘
T
k
​

represents a team focused on a specific "axis" of research (e.g., one team might focus on the optimizer while another focuses on data augmentation).

The discussion phase is decentralized; there is no manager to decide when it ends. Instead, agents vote to continue or finish. Once a consensus is reached, the last agent (alphabetically) consolidates the proposals into a roster. This allows for emergent coordination—teams can split, merge, or dissolve naturally as research axes are exhausted or shown to be highly promising.

Ensuring Robustness: Noise-Aware Gating

In scientific experimentation, "luck" (such as a particularly favorable random seed) can often look like a genuine improvement. This is a significant problem for AI agents, which might otherwise "promote" a noisy result as the new champion
𝑝
∗
p
∗
, leading the entire team down a suboptimal path.

To prevent this, the framework implements a noise-aware champion validation mechanism. When a new candidate program

If the improvement is large (

This rigorous gating ensures that the team's "champion" is a robust foundation for future iterations.

Empirical Evaluation Across Scientific Domains

The researchers evaluated AUTOSCIENTISTS across several challenging benchmarks to see if this decentralized approach actually translates to better scientific outcomes.

Biomedical Machine Learning (BioML-Bench)

On the BioML-Bench, which covers 24 tasks in areas like drug discovery and protein engineering, AUTOSCIENTISTS achieved a mean leaderboard percentile of 74.40%. This was a significant improvement over the 66.07% achieved by Autoresearch, a leading single-agent baseline. Notably, in the drug discovery tasks, the system improved the mean percentile from 46.16% to 64.52%.

Figure 2: Performance of AUTOSCIENTISTS across 24 biomedical tasks compared to other AI agent baselines.

Language Model Optimization

When applied to optimizing a small language model (GPT nanochat), the framework demonstrated a 1.9x acceleration. It reached the same performance target in 34 experiments that required 65 experiments for the single-agent baseline. Furthermore, when both systems started fr

item.title: [论文] 给你的注意力加一个“纠错”通道
item.description: 最近的语言模型越来越大，处理的文本也越来越长，但传统的注意力机制有点跟不上节奏了。它每处理一个新词，都得回头和所有旧词重新算一遍关系，计算量随文本长度平方级增长，特别吃内存带宽。其实有一类叫“局部线性注意力”的方法，理论上比我们现在用的 Softmax 注意力更聪明。你可以把 Softmax 想象成只看邻居的平均水平——它本质上是一个“局部常数估计器”。而局部线性注意力是“局部线性估计器”，能感...
item.content:encoded:
最近的语言模型越来越大，处理的文本也越来越长，但传统的注意力机制有点跟不上节奏了。它每处理一个新词，都得回头和所有旧词重新算一遍关系，计算量随文本长度平方级增长，特别吃内存带宽。

其实有一类叫“局部线性注意力”的方法，理论上比我们现在用的 Softmax 注意力更聪明。你可以把 Softmax 想象成只看邻居的平均水平——它本质上是一个“局部常数估计器”。而局部线性注意力是“局部线性估计器”，能感知到邻居之间的变化趋势，理论上记忆能力更强、偏差更小。但问题是它一直很难真正用于大规模训练，因为每次计算都需要解一个复杂的方程组，既慢又容易数值不稳定。

这篇论文想了个挺巧妙的办法来解决这个工程难题。他们设计的 Parallax 注意力，核心思路是：不是去“解”那个修正方向，而是让模型自己去“学”一个修正方向。

具体来说，Parallax 在原有 Softmax 输出结果的基础上，加了一个修正项。这个修正项做的事大致是：看一看当前查询词和之前所有键值对之间的统计协方差，然后判断“按 softmax 给的概率分布，是不是哪里不太对”。原先的方法需要为每个查询单独求解一个最优向量，Parallax 则直接训练了一个投影矩阵，把输入向量映射一下，就直接得到这个修正向量。这个过程就像你原本需要现场解一道微积分题来纠偏，现在变成了一个直觉式的快速反应——看到一个输入，就自动输出一个修正量。

有意思的还在后面。光有这个设计还不够，实验发现，用传统的 AdamW 优化器训练 Parallax，性能跟普通 Transformer 差不多，甚至略差。真正让它发挥出来的是配合一个叫 Muon 的优化器。Muon 是为矩阵参数特别设计的一种优化方法，能让权重矩阵“条件更好”——简单说就是参数矩阵的秩更高、信息更丰富。在这种训练条件下，Parallax 模型内部出现了明显的变化：深层网络开始更依赖修正项来调整注意力，而不再只依赖 vanilla softmax。这种“修正依赖度”的提升，直接转化成了更好的性能。

在计算效率上，他们也没有妥协。Parallax 设计成了单次遍历键值序列的流式算法，内存读写量和 FlashAttention 一样，但计算密度提高了一倍。这在现代 GPU 上是件好事——因为内存带宽往往是瓶颈，让计算更密集反而能更好地利用硬件。在 NVIDIA Hopper 架构的 GPU 上做推理测试，Parallax 相比同等计算配置的 Softmax 注意力提速了 54%。

在语言建模任务上，Muon 训练的 Parallax 模型在相同参数量下，比用 Muon 训练的普通 Transformer 损失更低、下游任务准确率更高。并且，随着模型参数规模从 1.5 亿扩大到 10 亿，这个优势保持稳定。

我觉得这个工作的有趣之处在于，它揭示了一个容易被忽视的点：很多时候模型的架构设计，和它所用的优化器之间是有深层互动的。一个在某种优化器下平平无奇的模型结构，换成另一种优化器可能就展现出完全不同的潜力。当然，目前这个研究主要在小到中等规模模型上验证，扩展到几百亿甚至更大参数时效果如何，还有待观察。但“让修正项可学习”这个简化思路，以及它和优化器之间微妙的配合关系，确实给我们理解注意力机制提供了新的视角。

论文：Parallax: Parameterized Local Linear Attention for Language Modeling [arXiv:2605.29157]

📄 论文原文概述
The Evolution of Attention Mechanisms

The standard Transformer architecture, which powers the majority of modern Large Language Models (LLMs), relies on Softmax Attention to capture relationships between tokens in a sequence. While highly effective, Softmax Attention suffers from two primary limitations: its computational complexity grows quadratically with the sequence length, and it incurs high input/output (I/O) costs. These bottlenecks have prompted extensive research into more efficient alternatives, such as Linear Attention and State Space Models (SSMs).

A theoretical refinement of the standard approach is Local Linear Attention (LLA). In mathematical terms, standard Softmax Attention can be viewed as a "local constant estimator" of the relationship between queries and keys. LLA upgrades this to a "local linear estimator," which theoretically offers a superior bias-variance tradeoff and a higher capacity for associative memory. However, LLA has historically been difficult to scale due to the need for iterative numerical solvers that add significant overhead and numerical instability.

Parallax, or Parameterized Local Linear Attention, is designed to resolve these practical hurdles. By replacing the complex iterative solver of LLA with a learnable linear projection, Parallax achieves the theoretical richness of a linear estimator while maintaining the scalability required for modern LLM pretraining.

This structure applies an additive covariance correction to the standard Softmax Attention output. This correction allows the model to refine its predictions by leveraging the second-order statistics of the key-value (KV) pairs, effectively "correcting" the local constant estimate provided by the softmax.

Efficiency and Hardware-Aware Implementation

One of the primary goals of Parallax is to provide these theoretical benefits without sacrificing hardware efficiency. Standard optimized attention mechanisms, like FlashAttention, focus on minimizing I/O overhead by keeping data in high-speed GPU memory (SRAM) for as long as possible. Parallax extends this streaming approach.

The Parallax algorithm computes the output in a single pass over the KV sequence. By expanding the covariance term, the computation can be reorganized into two parallel branches: a standard scoring branch and a correction branch. The combined output is calculated as:
