This design maintains the same I/O footprint as FlashAttention but doubles the arithmetic intensity (the ratio of floating-point operations to memory access). This shift makes the operation more "compute-bound," which is often preferable on modern hardware where memory bandwidth is a frequent bottleneck.

Furthermore, specialized "decode" kernels for the NVIDIA Hopper GPU architecture were developed to optimize inference. These kernels use WGMMA (Warpgroup Matrix Multiply-Accumulate) instructions to share computations across different parts of the attention head, ensuring that the additional compute required by Parallax does not lead to significant increases in latency. In benchmarks, Parallax demonstrated speedups of up to 1.54x compared to Softmax Attention in compute-matched settings.

The Role of the Optimizer: The Muon Interaction

A significant finding in the development of Parallax is the critical interaction between the model architecture and the optimization algorithm. Traditional LLM training usually employs the AdamW optimizer. However, experiments revealed that Parallax models trained with AdamW performed similarly to or even slightly worse than standard Transformers.

The "unlocking" of Parallax's potential occurred when using the Muon optimizer. Muon is an optimizer designed specifically for the matrix parameters in the hidden layers of neural networks. Under Muon, Parallax showed a substantial performance gap over standard Softmax Attention.

Analysis of the model's internal states revealed why this occurs. The effectiveness of Parallax depends on the "Correction-to-Output Ratio" (COR)—essentially, how much the model relies on the linear correction term compared to the base softmax output. In Muon-trained models, the COR was found to be significantly higher, especially in the deeper layers of the network. Muon promotes better-conditioned weight matrices, which leads to:

When trained with AdamW, the model often learned to "shut down" the correction branch by decreasing the magnitude of the probes, effectively falling back to being a standard Transformer.

Empirical Validation and Results

Parallax was evaluated across several domains, including synthetic benchmarks and large-scale language modeling.

Synthetic Benchmarks

On the MAD-benchmark, which tests a model's ability to recall information and copy sequences, Parallax consistently outperformed Softmax Attention, Mamba, and other linear attention variants like MesaNet and Gated DeltaNet. It proved particularly robust as the sequence length and the number of key-value pairs increased, situations where many other models showed accuracy degradation.

Language Modeling Pretraining

The researchers pretrained Parallax at two scales: 0.6 billion (0.6B) and 1.7 billion (1.7B) parameters.

Perplexity: Parallax consistently achieved lower perplexity (a measure of how well a model predicts a sample) on datasets like LAMBADA and WikiText compared to the Transformer baseline.
Downstream Tasks: On zero-shot benchmarks covering question answering and commonsense reasoning (such as HellaSwag and ARC), Parallax showed clear gains.
Control Experiments: To ensure the gains weren't simply due to having more parameters or more compute, the researchers created "parameter-matched" and "compute-matched" versions of the standard Transformer. Parallax outperformed both, indicating that the improvements are intrinsic to the parameterized local linear mechanism it

item.title: [论文] 和自己学，别只学字
item.description: 你有没有想过，现在这些大语言模型到底看了多少书？GPT-4 训练时大概啃了十几万亿个 token，而一个人类小孩从出生到七八岁，总共接触的词汇量也就一千万到一亿之间。同样是掌握语言，怎么数据量差了三四个数量级？这个问题的一个主流猜测是，人其实不太靠单个的字词去学语言，而是学着去预测世界背后抽象的、内在的结构——也就是潜变量。最近读到一篇论文，用一套干净利落的数学框架，把这种“和自己学更省数据”的...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，现在这些大语言模型到底看了多少书？GPT-4 训练时大概啃了十几万亿个 token，而一个人类小孩从出生到七八岁，总共接触的词汇量也就一千万到一亿之间。同样是掌握语言，怎么数据量差了三四个数量级？这个问题的一个主流猜测是，人其实不太靠单个的字词去学语言，而是学着去预测世界背后抽象的、内在的结构——也就是潜变量。最近读到一篇论文，用一套干净利落的数学框架，把这种“和自己学更省数据”的直觉做成了严格的理论，而且结果出奇地简洁。

他们自己搭了一个叫随机层级模型的玩具世界来模拟我们身边带结构的数据（比如语言里的组合性）。在这个模型里，数据是从一棵深度为 L 的规则树上生成的：树顶有一个根符号，每向下一层，一个父节点会用它的某一种“同义方式”生成一串子节点，最后落到最底层露出来的孩子，就是模型实际看到的 tokens。要学什么？就是要从这些底层的 tokens 里猜出树上的同义关系——比如哪些 token 组合其实对应同一个父层潜变量。

如果直接让模型做最常规的任务，比如预测下一个 token，那就相当于拿着叶子去看另一片叶子。麻烦在于，中间每经过一层，都可能因为同义选项的干扰，让上下游的关联信号被稀释一次。你可以想象成在传话：每传一个人，她都可能用自己的说法替换一遍，传个三五层之后，最初的意思就碎得不成样子了。论文的数学推导显示，在这种 token 层级的自监督下，要学会整棵树的信号，样本量 P 和深度 L 是指数关系，大致是 P ∝ v m^{L+1}，m 是同义词个数。这意味着只要数据稍微有层级深一点，所需数据就会爆炸。

那如果换一种学法呢？作者提出了迭代潜在聚类算法。思路特别直观：不从 token 到 token，而是先拿最底层的 token 做聚类，分出一层潜变量 h0；然后把刚聚出来的 h0 当作“下一层的数据”，再聚类出 h1，如此一层一层往上堆。每一步都是拿已经抽象一点的东西去学习更抽象的表示，目标和源之间距离极短，再也不用跨那么多层去传递信号。这时候数学告诉你，不管树有多深，所需样本复杂度一下子变成了 P ∝ v m³，跟 L 完全无关。从指数级跳水到常数级别，这个效率提升可以说是理论拉满了。

更有意思的是，他们做的数值实验漂亮得有点过分。图里面横轴是把样本量按 v m³ 重新缩放后的值，纵轴是不同同义因子 m 下的聚类准确率，结果所有曲线完美地重叠在一起。也就是说，只要考虑这一个小小的缩放因子，不管你怎么加深度，学习所花的力气都基本一致。

具体到神经网络上，他们顺着这个思路提出了一个叫堆叠潜在聚类网络的架构。每个模块只负责做一步 ILC 的事：用一个编码器把输入挤成表示，再用一个预测器去猜同一层里“近亲”表示的组合，然后依此来聚类，输出下一层的潜变量。虽然现在这还是个在小型合成数据上验证的雏形，但它指了一个特别诱人的方向——让模型学着反复消化自己产生的中间抽象，可能正是摆脱数据饥渴的一条正路。

我觉得这项工作好就好在它没有凭空发明一个新算法，而是用一个可解的模型挖出了“为什么 latent prediction 比 token prediction 省数据”的深层原因。现实世界数据当然比这个随机层级模型乱得多，噪音、不规则结构都可能让常数 v m³ 前面那个系数变大，指数优势也可能被实际工程问题盖住，但这个理论仍然给出了一个很清澈的上界：别把你的模型逼成天天背词的苦差，偶尔让它回头看看自己脑子里那些飘来飘去的特征，也许就能用更少的数据学到更深的结构。

（论文：Learn from your own latents and not from tokens: A sample-complexity theory [arXiv:2605.27734]）

📄 论文原文概述
The significant performance of modern large language models (LLMs) and generative AI comes at a high price: data consumption. While a human child can achieve linguistic competence after exposure to approximately
10
7
10
7
to
10
8
10
8
words, state-of-the-art models like GPT-4 or Llama 3 are trained on
10
13
10
13
to
10
14
10
14
tokens. This discrepancy suggests that current artificial learning algorithms are orders of magnitude less efficient than biological systems.

One prevailing theory for this gap is that humans do not merely predict raw sensory inputs (tokens); instead, they learn to predict abstract, latent representations of the world. Research into self-supervised learning (SSL) has produced several "latent-prediction" methods, such as data2vec and DINO, which show strong empirical performance. However, a rigorous theoretical explanation for why learning from latents is more efficient than learning from tokens has been missing.

This paper provides a sample-complexity theory that quantifies this efficiency gain. Using a controlled mathematical framework called the Random Hierarchy Model (RHM), the authors demonstrate that predicting one’s own latent representations can reduce the amount of data needed to learn a hierarchy from an exponential amount to a constant amount, regardless of the depth of the hierarchy.

Figure 1: Numerical results showing that for various values of the synonym factor
𝑚
m, the accuracy of the learning algorithm collapses onto a single curve when the number of samples
𝑃

. This confirms that the sample complexity is independent of the hierarchy depth.

The Random Hierarchy Model (RHM)

To study data efficiency, the authors use the Random Hierarchy Model (RHM). The RHM is a probabilistic context-free grammar designed to mimic the compositional and hierarchical nature of natural data like language or images.

In this model, data is generated starting from a "root" symbol at the top of a tree of depth
𝐿
L. At each level
ℓ
ℓ, a parent symbol generates a tuple of
𝑠
s children symbols at level
ℓ
−
1
ℓ−1. Crucially, for each parent symbol, there are
𝑚
m different "synonymous" tuples of children it could produce. This creates a many-to-one mapping from child tuples to parent categories. The symbols at the very bottom (level 0) are the "visible tokens" that the model actually sees.

The goal of a learner is to recover this hidden tree structure. Specifically, the learner must identify which tuples of children are "synonyms"—meaning they share the same parent latent variable.

The Token Bottleneck: Why Predicting Raw Tokens Is Hard

Current generative models are typically trained on a token-level objective, such as next-token prediction or masked language modeling. In the RHM framework, this is equivalent to using a visible token at level 0 to predict another visible token somewhere else in the sequence.

The authors show that this approach suffers from a "token bottleneck." To understand why, consider the statistical signal required to cluster synonyms. To know that two child tuples at level 1 belong to the same parent at level 2, the model must observe their correlations with some external "context" or observable
𝑍
Z.

If
𝑍
Z is another raw token at level 0, the correlation between a level-1 tuple and
𝑍
Z must pass through the parent latent at level 2, then level 3, and so on, all the way up to a common ancestor, and then back down through the hierarchy to
𝑍
Z. Because there are
𝑚
m synonymous choices at every step, the specific identity of the target token
𝑍
Z becomes less and less informative about the identity of the source tuple as the "distance" in the tree increases.

This leads to a signal attenuation. The authors calculate that for token-level self-supervision, the required number of samples
𝑃
P to learn the hierarchy scales as:

𝑃

This is an exponential dependence on the depth
𝐿
L. As the hierarchy becomes deeper and more complex, the data requirements for token prediction explode.

Learning from Latents: The ILC Algorithm

The core hypothesis of the paper is that this exponential explosion can be avoided if the model predicts abstract latent variables instead of tokens. The authors formalize this through the Iterative Latent Clustering (ILC) algorithm.

Instead of predicting level-0 tokens, ILC works bottom-up. Once it has clustered level-0 tokens into level-1 latents, it uses these level-1 latents as the "targets" for learning level-2. Because the target is now at a higher level of abstraction, the "distance" in the hierarchy between the source and the target is minimized.

The ILC algorithm proceeds as follows:

Initialization: Use raw tokens as the first set of latents (

This is a constant with respect to depth

Neural Implementation: Stacked Latent-Clustering

To move from an abstract algorithm to a practical neural network, the authors introduce the Stacked Latent-Clustering (SLC) network. This is a multi-scale architecture where each module is designed to perform one step of the ILC algorithm.

Each module consists of:

A Predictor (
𝑝
p): A network that takes a tuple of latents and tries to predict the distribution of its "cousin" latents.
A Clusterer (
𝐶
C): A network that compresses the predictor’s output into a discrete "code" or cluster identity. This code becomes the input for the next layer.

The SLC network is trained end-to-end (or layer-wise) using gradient descent. Interestingly, the authors found that the network achieves the optimal
𝑣
𝑚
3
vm
3
scaling even when "stop-gradients" are applied between layers. This means that each layer can learn its own representations using only local information and the outputs of the previous layer, without needing global backpropagation from the very top. This finding is significant for neuroscience, as it suggests that the brain could implement such a data-efficient scheme using biologically plausible local learning rules.

Figure 2: The training dynamics of a 6-layer SLC network. The layers learn sequentially: Layer 1 must successfully cluster its latents before Layer 2 can begin to make progress. This "staircase" effect demonstrates the hierarchical nature of the learning process.

Universal Latent Prediction: Analyzing data2vec

One might wonder if these benefits are exclusive to specialized architectures like SLC. The authors investigate data2vec, a popular SSL method that uses a teacher-student framework. In data2vec, a student network is trained to predict the latent representations generated by a "teacher" network (which is an exponential moving average of the student).

The paper demonstrates that data2vec implicitly performs the same hierarchical clustering as ILC. They found that:

Teacher as a Lifter: The EMA teacher progressively incorporates learned latents into its targets. This "lifts" the prediction task from the token level to the latent level over the course of training.
Efficiency: Empirical tests on RHM data show that data2vec also follows the
𝑃
∝
𝑣
𝑚
3
P∝vm
3
scaling, outperforming both supervised learning and standard token-lev

item.title: [论文] 给AI世界模型装上“共享大脑”
item.description: 你有没有想过，为什么现在的大多数AI在虚拟世界里都像独行侠——只能看到自己眼前的画面、预测自己的下一步，却不知道旁边的人也在同时移动、开枪或者让路？这像在玩一个只有第一人称视角的联机游戏，明明大家都在同一个地图里，但各自的AI大脑之间完全不通气。最近我看到一篇论文，他们试着让多个AI能够在同一个世界里“共享”一个世界模型，彼此看见彼此，还互不搅乱对方的视角，关键是还能随着人数增加而不让计算量爆炸...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，为什么现在的大多数AI在虚拟世界里都像独行侠——只能看到自己眼前的画面、预测自己的下一步，却不知道旁边的人也在同时移动、开枪或者让路？这像在玩一个只有第一人称视角的联机游戏，明明大家都在同一个地图里，但各自的AI大脑之间完全不通气。最近我看到一篇论文，他们试着让多个AI能够在同一个世界里“共享”一个世界模型，彼此看见彼此，还互不搅乱对方的视角，关键是还能随着人数增加而不让计算量爆炸。我觉得这个思路挺巧的。

这个工作叫Gamma-World，本质上是一个多智能体的世界模型。传统的世界模型往往是给单个智能体用的：输入当前画面和动作，预测接下来会发生什么。但如果想模拟多人游戏、仓库里协同工作的机器人，或者高速路上互相博弈的自动驾驶车，就必须同时维护多个视角，而且每个视角看到的画面必须物理一致——比如A智能体踢了足球一脚，B和C从不同角度都必须看到球朝着同一个方向飞去。这个一致性的要求，在多个AI之间实现起来相当麻烦。更麻烦的是，如果每个AI都要和其他AI交换信息，当人数一多，计算量就会以平方级往上蹿，根本做不到实时交互。

Gamma-World的解决思路，我觉得有两点特别聪明。第一是他们给每个智能体发放了一个“几何身份”，而不是贴一个死板的编号。如果直接用“Agent 1、Agent 2”这种固定索引，模型很容易学会根据序号去区别对待，而不是真正根据它们各自的行为来理解世界，而且一旦换一个不同人数的场景，模型就彻底懵了。他们设计了一种叫Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding（SRAE）的编码方式，把智能体映射到一个高维空间里正单纯形的顶点上。用这种摆放方式，任意两个顶点之间的距离都完全相等，也就是说，所有智能体在编码空间里天然就地位平等，没有哪个更特殊。这种编码再配合4D旋转位置嵌入，模型就能天然地处理任意数量的智能体，哪怕训练时只见过两个人，推理时来四个也能应付。

第二是他们用了Sparse Hub Attention（SHA）来解决智能体之间交流时的计算爆炸问题。想象一下，如果每个智能体都要和其他所有人“目光对视”一遍，那每增加一个智能体，计算量就多加一大坨。他们引入了一组极少量的hub token（共享记忆块），就像在聊天群里加了一个公告板。每个智能体只需要关注自己的历史画面和这个公告板，公告板则负责收集所有智能体的状态并分发关键信息。这样一来，不同智能体之间不需要直接互联，信息通过公告板中转，计算复杂度就从 O(P²) 降到了 O(P)，也就是和智能体数量呈线性关系。理论上当有4个智能体时，全注意力可能要用掉100个单位的计算资源，SHA只需要二三十个单位；人数越多，差别越夸张。

为了让这个复杂模型能真正跑起来，他们设计了一个三阶段的训练流程：先用一个“老师模型”双向看完整段视频来学未来发生的事情，再逐步过渡到在线交互的模式，让模型学会从自己的动作自回归地推演整个世界的走向。虽然论文的具体测试集我还得再仔细翻翻，但从架构上看，这种线性扩展的特性意味着，将来在多智能体环境中训练好一个基模型之后，就可以直接在8人甚至更多人数的场景里用，不需要重新训练，这是目前很多方法做不到的。

当然，这个工作的局限我自己感觉也挺明显的。现在它主要还是在较简单的虚拟场景和固定视角下做验证，真实世界里摄像头的遮挡、噪声和完全不同的观察方式，会让“共享公告板”这种思路面临更大的挑战。而且虽然注意力是线性了，但其他的计算环节（比如视频的解码和编码）在超高分辨率下依然是吃资源的。也许未来，他们可以把这种对称编码和稀疏注意力的思路搬到物理世界的机器人协同上，那时候就有机会看到几个机器人一起搬东西时，彼此真的“知道”对方在干什么，而不是各干各的。

（论文：Gamma-World: Generative Multi-Agent World Modeling Beyond Two Players [arXiv:2605.28816]）

📄 论文原文概述
Multi-Agent Simulation: From Single Perspectives to Collaborative Worlds

World modeling has emerged as a cornerstone of generative artificial intelligence, providing a way for models to predict future observations of an environment based on current states and agent actions. Traditionally, these models have focused on single-agent scenarios where a model predicts what one specific observer sees and does. However, real-world complexity often involves multiple agents—whether in multiplayer video games, coordinated robotics in a warehouse, or self-driving cars interacting on a highway.

Figure 1: Gamma-World is designed to simulate interactive environments with multiple agents, maintaining consistency across diverse viewpoints in both virtual and real-world scenes.

The shift from single-agent to multi-agent simulation introduces two fundamental challenges: cross-perspective consistency and scalability. Cross-perspective consistency requires that if one agent performs an action, every other agent capable of seeing that action must observe the same physical outcome from their respective viewpoint. Scalability is equally critical; as the number of agents increases, the computational cost of simulating their interactions often grows quadratically, making real-time interaction difficult.

Gamma-World addresses these issues by proposing a generative multi-agent world model that is individually controllable and permutation-symmetric. By treating agents as distinct but coupled entities, the framework allows for synchronized rollouts that can scale beyond two players without a proportional explosion in computational requirements.

The Foundations of Multi-Agent World Modeling

At its core, Gamma-World is built on a transformer-based latent video diffusion model. In a single-agent setting, the model works with a latent representation

. To support multiple agents, this is extended to a joint representation:

To ensure that each agent's action correctly influences the shared world, these features are injected into the transformer blocks via a layer-specific bias
𝛽

. This injection method ensures that the same action is represented consistently regardless of which agent performs it, a property known as agent equivalence.

Preserving Permutation Symmetry with Simplex Encodings

One major hurdle in multi-agent systems is how to identify agents without creating an arbitrary hierarchy. If a model uses fixed identity embeddings (e.g., "Agent 1", "Agent 2"), it may treat agents differently based on their index rather than their actions. Furthermore, such models often fail when the number of agents changes, as they were only trained for a specific count.

Gamma-World introduces Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding (SRAE) to solve this. Instead of learned IDs, agents are assigned to vertices of a regular simplex in a high-dimensional rotary angle space. By using a fixed "simplex pool size"
𝑉
V, the model can support any number of agents up to
𝑉
V.

The vertices
𝑠

This geometric arrangement ensures that every agent is equidistant from every other agent in the representation space, effectively preserving permutation symmetry. When simulating a scene, the model maps each agent to a vertex, and these coordinates determine the rotation angles

used in a 4D Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE). Because this encoding is parameter-free and geometrically balanced, the model can generalize to different numbers of agents—for example, moving from two to four agents—without further training.

Efficient Interaction via Sparse Hub Attention

Communication between agents is computationally expensive. In a standard transformer, every token attends to every other token. In a multi-agent scenario with
𝑃
P agents, the cost of this "all-to-all" attention scales at
𝑂

), where
𝑛

𝐿
L is the sequence length. This quadratic growth quickly becomes a bottleneck.

Figure 2: The Gamma-World architecture utilizes Sparse Hub Attention (SHA) and Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding to allow efficient, symmetric communication between agents.

To mitigate this, the researchers developed Sparse Hub Attention (SHA). Instead of direct agent-to-agent communication, SHA introduces a small set of
𝐾
K "hub tokens" per frame. The attention rules are restricted as follows:

Agent tokens only attend to their own stream and the hub tokens.
Hub tokens attend to all agent tokens and other hub tokens.

By forcing information to flow through these hubs, the cross-agent complexity is reduced to:

𝑂

This results in a cost that scales linearly with the number of agents. Hub tokens act as a shared global memory, capturing the collective state of the world and distributing it back to individual agent perspectives, ensuring that their views remain synchronized without the overhead of dense attention.

A Three-Stage Training Pipeline

Generating high-quality, interactive video sequences in real-time requires more than just a clever architecture. Gamma-World employs a specialized three-stage training recipe:

Bidirectional Teacher Training: The model is first trained as a standard diffusion model with access to the entire temporal sequence. This "teacher" learns the full distribution of multi-agent interactions with high fidelity.
Causal Student Training: To enable real-time rollouts, a "student" model is trained using a causal structure. Unlike the teacher, the student only sees the past and present. It uses the Sparse Hub Attention mask and independently sampled noise levels for each block of frames.
Conditional Self-Forcing Distillation: The final stage distills the multi-step diffusion process into a few-step generator (typically 1–4 steps). This uses a Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) loss, where the bidirectional teacher provides the target distribution. Crucially, this happens under "self-forcing" conditions, where the model's own previous outputs are used as history, matching the behavior of the model during actual inference.

This pipeline allows the model to maintain the high visual quality of a full diffusion model while being efficient enough to run at 24 frames per second during interactive use.

Evaluating Performance and Scaling

The framework was tested in diverse environments, primarily focusing on Minecraft (for complex multi-agent interactions) and real-world robotics data.

Quantitative Benchmarks

When compared to previous multi-agent models like Solaris, Gamma-World showed significant improvements across several metrics. In tasks involving "Memory Grounding"—where agents must remember the state of objects they are no longer looking at—Gamma-World achieved a Fréchet Video Distance (FVD) of 184.1, compared to 333.8 for Solaris. Lower FVD and FID scores across the board indicate that the generated videos are more realistic and the agents' perspectives are more consistently aligned.

Scalability Results

The efficiency gains from Sparse Hub Attention were verified by measuring latency and FLOPs as the player count increased.

Figure 3: Comparisons of DiT latency, self-attention latency, and FLOPs show that SHA scales linearly (blue) while dense attention scales quadratically (red).

As

item.title: [论文] 把串行画框改成并行：一次生成一个整体
item.description: Hey，最近有篇论文让我觉得挺有意思的，是关于视觉语言模型（VLM）怎么更快、更准地在图片里“找东西”的。你平时用那些能看图聊天的 AI，其实背后有个基础能力叫视觉定位——比如你说“图片左边那只黑猫”，模型得知道猫在哪，给出一个边界框。但现在大多模型生成这个框的方式很笨：它像一个一个字往外蹦坐标，比如“x_min: 102, y_min: 205...”，如果图片里有几十个物体，模型就要嘀嗒嘀嗒...
item.content:encoded:
Hey，最近有篇论文让我觉得挺有意思的，是关于视觉语言模型（VLM）怎么更快、更准地在图片里“找东西”的。你平时用那些能看图聊天的 AI，其实背后有个基础能力叫视觉定位——比如你说“图片左边那只黑猫”，模型得知道猫在哪，给出一个边界框。但现在大多模型生成这个框的方式很笨：它像一个一个字往外蹦坐标，比如“x_min: 102, y_min: 205...”，如果图片里有几十个物体，模型就要嘀嗒嘀嗒地生成上百个数字，非常慢，而且容易把坐标的对应关系搞错。这篇叫 LocateAnything 的工作就是想解决这个问题，思路是用一种并行解码，把整个边界框一次性“打包生成”，速度一下就上去了。

传统方法把边界框当作一串离散的 token，一个框需要四到六个坐标数字逐个预测。假如你问“标出图中所有杯子”，模型要为每个杯子依次吐出这些坐标，50个杯子就是两百多步，延迟很明显。LocateAnything 的想法是：为什么非要把框拆成零件，再一个个往外拿呢？它提出 Parallel Box Decoding（PBD），把每个边界框定义成一个固定长度的“块”，比如 x1 y1 x2 y2 。然后模型在解码这一步时，可以同时预测这个块里的所有 token——因为框内的几个坐标本来就相互关联，打包处理不仅省时间，还能保持几何结构的一致性，避免“左上角是杯子 A 的，右下角却配给了杯子 B”这种错误。

为了让这种并行块预测和模型的因果思维不冲突，他们设计了一个挺精巧的注意力掩码。在训练时，当前这个块内部是双向注意力的，坐标之间可以互相对齐；但在块与块之间，还是保持从左到右的因果顺序——模型得先处理完第一个物体框，再看第二个，不能偷看后面的。这就好像你拼图：拼当前这块可以仔细看这块的颜色纹理，但不能翻还没拼的下一块，这样保证顺序不乱，内部又和谐。

训练上他们也动了心思，数据被组织成两部分：前半段是传统序列形式的坐标，用于保持对题意的理解；后半段包装成并行块格式，模型学着在这些块上做一次性预测。这种双模式让模型既能正常“读懂”问题，又能学会高效输出框。论文里用了一个叫 Moon-ViT 的视觉编码器配合 Qwen2.5 语言解码器，通过一个简单的 MLP 投影层把图像特征传给语言模型，整体架构不算复杂。

效果怎么样呢？他们测下来，在密集场景（比如图片里很多物体），LocateAnything 的推理速度比传统串行方法快了 10 倍以上，而且定位精度居然还更高了。因为并行解码减少了 token 生成步数，也保住了框内坐标的联动，反而降低了那种“张冠李戴”的错位。这个框架还能统一处理多目标指向、密集检测、文档定位等不同任务，实用性不错。

我觉得这个思路的妙处在于，它不是简单粗暴地加速解码，而是根据输出本身的结构来设计“原子块”——把本该是一个整体的东西真的当作一个整体来生成。这让我联想到以后如果要生成更复杂的形状，比如多边形标注或关键点集，或许也可以用类似的块化并行方案。当然，它要改模型输出的形式和训练流程，没法直接拿来套在现有的任意 VLM 上面，而且不同的结构化输出可能得设计不同的块格式，通用性上还需要打磨。但整体看，这种从“串行打字”到“并行盖章”的转变，在速度和品质上双赢，确实是一个很聪明的工程选择。

（论文：LocateAnything: Fast and High-Quality Vision-Language Grounding with Parallel Box Decoding [arXiv:2605.27365]）

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction to Vision-Language Grounding

Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have become a central component in modern artificial intelligence, enabling machines to understand the relationship between textual instructions and visual scenes. A critical capability for these models is visual grounding—the ability to localize specific objects or regions described in natural language. This is essential for applications ranging from autonomous robotics and GUI automation to complex document analysis.

Figure 1: LocateAnything is a unified framework for diverse visual perception tasks, including multi-object referring, dense detection, and document grounding. It introduces Parallel Box Decoding to significantly accelerate inference while maintaining high accuracy.

However, existing VLMs often struggle with a trade-off between speed and precision. Most models treat localization as a language generation task, where the coordinates of a bounding box are predicted one by one as a sequence of text tokens. This sequential approach introduces significant latency, especially in "dense" scenes containing dozens or hundreds of objects.

LocateAnything is a framework designed to overcome these limitations. By introducing a technique called Parallel Box Decoding (PBD), it allows models to predict entire bounding boxes as single, atomic units in a single step. This shift from sequential to parallel generation results in a system that is over 10 times faster than traditional textual-based VLMs while actually improving the precision of the localized regions.

The Sequential Bottleneck in Vision-Language Models

To understand the contribution of LocateAnything, one must first look at the standard method for visual grounding: Next-Token Prediction (NTP). In this paradigm, a model represents a 2D bounding box (defined by four coordinates) as a 1D sequence of tokens. For example, a box at
(
𝑥
𝑚
𝑖
𝑛
,
𝑦
𝑚
𝑖
𝑛
,
𝑥
𝑚
𝑎
𝑥
,
𝑦
𝑚
𝑎
𝑥
)
(x
min
​

,y
min
​

,x
max
​

,y
max
​

) might be written as a string of digits or discrete coordinate tokens.

There are two primary issues with this approach:

Inference Latency: Because the model must predict each coordinate token sequentially, the time required to generate an output grows linearly with the number of objects. If an image contains 50 objects, and each object requires 4-6 tokens, the model must perform hundreds of decoding steps, making real-time interaction difficult.
Geometric Disassociation: Coordinates are intrinsically coupled; the "top-left" and "bottom-right" of a box only make sense in relation to one each other. By breaking these into a flat stream of tokens, the model may lose the structural correlation between them, leading to "unstructured" errors where coordinates from different objects or boundaries are erroneously mixed.

Previous attempts to speed up decoding, such as generic Multi-Token Prediction (MTP), often treat the output as an unstructured stream of tokens. While faster, these methods can amplify errors because the model might attempt to predict a "chunk" of tokens that spans across the boundary of two different objects, leading to incoherent geometric predictions.

Parallel Box Decoding: Structuring Spatial Output

The core innovation of LocateAnything is Parallel Box Decoding (PBD). Instead of treating coordinates as independent tokens, PBD groups them into fixed-length "blocks." Each block is designed to represent a single geometric entity—typically a bounding box.

Figure 2: A comparison of decoding paradigms. Unlike traditional textual or quantized decoding that requires many sequential steps, Parallel Box Decoding predicts a full box block in a single step.

In this framework, a single block of length 6 might contain a start token (), four quantized coordinate tokens, and an end token (). This block-based structure ensures that the model always processes and predicts a complete geometric unit. This approach offers two major benefits:

Speed: The model can predict all tokens within a block simultaneously, reducing the number of decoding steps by a factor of 4 to 6 for each object.
Coherence: By forcing the model to attend to all parts of a box at once during the prediction of that block, PBD preserves the geometric integrity of the output.

The model architecture utilizes a Moon-ViT vision encoder to process high-resolution images and a Qwen2.5 language decoder. These are linked by a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) projector that translates visual features into a format the language model can understand.

Dual-Formulation Training and Block Attention

Training a model to perform both standard causal reasoning (understanding the prompt) and parallel decoding (generating the boxes) requires a specialized strategy. LocateAnything uses a dual-formulation training objective that combines traditional Next-Token Prediction (NTP) with block-level Multi-Token Prediction (MTP).

The input for training is constructed as a concatenated sequence:

Figure 4: The specialized attention mask used during training. It ensures causal flow across blocks to maintain the order of objects while allowing bidirectional (parallel) communication within a single block for geometric coherence.

A critical component of this training is the attention mask. To prevent the model from "cheating" by looking at the answer, the mask enforces:

Standard Causality for NTP: Each token in the sequential part only looks at previous tokens.
Causal Flow Between Blocks: A block

Bidirectional Intra-Block Attention: Within a single block, all tokens can "see" each other. This is what allows the model to capture the complex relationships between the four coordinates of a box and predict them all at once.

This joint optimization ensures the model retains its language reasoning abilities while gaining the efficiency of parallel spatial decoding.

Adaptive Inference: Balancing Speed and Robustness

Parallel decoding is efficient, but it can occasionally be less reliable in extremely cluttered or ambiguous scenes. To address this, LocateAnything introduces three inference modes:

Fast Mode (MTP): The model predicts full blocks in parallel. This is the highest throughput mode, ideal for real-time video processing or simple grounding tasks.
Slow Mode (NTP): The model reverts to standard sequential decoding. This provides maximum stability and precision for very complex tasks but is significantly slower.
Hybrid Mode (Adaptive): This is the default setting. The model attempts to decode in Fast Mode but monitors its own confidence.

The Hybrid Mode uses an "ambiguity trigger." If the probability of the predicted coordinate token falls below
0.7
0.7, or if the variation among the top candidate tokens is too high (a difference greater than
80
80 in the normalized coordinate space of
[
0
,
1000
]
[0,1000]), the model assumes the parallel prediction might be unreliable. In these cases, it discards the parallel block and uses the "Slow Mode" to re-decode that specific object sequentially. Once the ambiguity is resolved, it switches back to Fast Mode for the remaining objects.

The LocateAnything-Data Corpus

A high-performance grounding model requires diverse and massive datasets. The researchers curated "LocateAnything-Data," a massive corpus of over 12 million images and 138 million natural language queries.

Figure

item.title: [论文] 让模型学会边想边回头看
item.description: 你有没有过这种感觉——做一道很难的数学题，想了好几条思路都走不通，最后发现只要把其中两条线索拼在一起，再回过头检查一下中间某个假设，答案就出来了。现在的语言模型在做复杂推理时，其实也经常卡在类似的地方：它们习惯一路向前生成，走到哪儿算哪儿，即使走错了也很难回头看看是不是前面的思路有问题。这篇论文就在试着给模型装上这种“边走边回头”的能力。它关注的问题很实在：现在想让大模型高效解决数学、编程这类需...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有过这种感觉——做一道很难的数学题，想了好几条思路都走不通，最后发现只要把其中两条线索拼在一起，再回过头检查一下中间某个假设，答案就出来了。现在的语言模型在做复杂推理时，其实也经常卡在类似的地方：它们习惯一路向前生成，走到哪儿算哪儿，即使走错了也很难回头看看是不是前面的思路有问题。

这篇论文就在试着给模型装上这种“边走边回头”的能力。它关注的问题很实在：现在想让大模型高效解决数学、编程这类需要多步推理的任务，常用的办法要么是让模型一次生成好多条独立尝试然后选最好的，要么是用树搜索在每一步展开几个候选。但这些办法有两个让人头疼的限制。一是“信号太稀”——模型往往只能拿到最后的对错判断，中间步骤是死是活完全没反馈；二是“眼界太窄”——搜索基本只在模型自己觉得高概率的区域打转，稍微有点反直觉的妙招就很难冒出来。

于是研究者搞出了一个叫“双向进化搜索”（Bidirectional Evolutionary Search，简称BES）的框架。这个名字听起来复杂，拆开看倒很符合直觉。

先说“进化”这部分。你可以把模型一次完整的推理过程想象成一个个体，BES维护着这么一个推理路径的小种群。在这个种群上，不光能做常规的“继续往下写几步”，还引入了四种类似生物进化的操作。一种是“组合”：把两条推理路径的后半截拼在一起，就好像把两种思路的优点揉成一个新想法。一种是“删除”：把推理链里多余的一步删掉，让思考更干净。还有“易位”：把一条路径里的一步换成另一条路径里的对应一步，相当于说“这一段逻辑，用那边的试试行不行”。最后是“交叉”：保留一条路径的前半部分，接上另一条的后半部分。这几个操作一混搭，就能让整个搜索跳出模型原本熟悉的“舒适圈”，去尝试那些单靠顺序生成根本想不到的解法。

但光有进化还不够，如果没有中间信号的指引，这个种群还是可能乱晃，尤其当整个题目跨度很长的时候。所以BES又加上了“反向搜索”这个组件。它的做法是：拿到一个问题之后，先让模型自己把大目标递归拆成一层层的小目标，就像我们分析一道物理大题时，会先拆分成“先求这个力，再算那个加速度”。接着，不是干等最后答案对了才给奖励，每个中间小目标都有一个分数，能告诉你这个局部答案满足需求的程度。然后把这些局部分数加权汇总成一个稠密的整体得分。这样一来，整个搜索过程就变成了一个“有方向感”的游戏——每一步都清楚离完成小目标还有多远，该在哪里花力气。

这个稠密得分还配合一个选择机制：下一轮进化时，不是随机挑两条路径当父代，而是根据分数用类似“带温度的玻尔兹曼分布”来选——温度高的时候多探索一些冷门思路，温度降下来就聚焦到最有戏的路径上。整个过程就像一种带有内在目标感的演化。

从理论上，这篇论文还解释了一个很有意思的点：标准的自回归搜索本质上被模型自身的概率分布给“困住”了，能输出的东西只占整个可能空间里很小一块。而进化算子加上向后拆解的小目标，能把搜索的触角伸得更远，生成一些按照正常顺序书写根本不会被采样到的推理轨迹，这些轨迹却可能是正确的。

实验结果也给这个思路撑了腰。在好几个出了名难的数学和编程推理基准上，BES的表现稳定压过常见的独立采样和树搜索方法。尤其当问题需要七八步以上的长推理链时，普通方法很容易在中间某个步骤默默拐进沟里，而BES因为有中间拆解目标的引导，弯道翻车的几率低了很多。论文里的一些数字让人直观感受到，面对那种一眼看过去完全没思路的题目，这种“边走边回头看”的策略确实能把正确率往上提一大截。

当然，我觉得这个方法目前最大的局限在于它太“重”了。维护一个推理种群、不断做反向分解和交叉，每一步都要多次调用模型，计算开销比简单采样高了不止一个量级。如果放到实际应用中，可能还得在速度上做很多优化，或者跟一些更快的基础模型配合使用。不过换个角度看，这种密集的自我搜索过程本身就是一种高质量的自我训练信号，未来或许能让模型在少量人类数据的情况下实现自我改进，而不是完全依赖RLHF那种需要大量标注的路线。

另一个让我觉得有意思的想象是，能不能把这种思路用到更开放的任务里，比如写一篇长文章或者设计一个项目方案。当目标不再是一道有标准答案的数学题时，要怎么定义中间的小目标和评分函数，恐怕会是一个很有挑战但也很迷人的方向。

（论文：Self-Improving Language Models with Bidirectional Evolutionary Search [arXiv:2605.28814]）

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction to Bidirectional Evolutionary Search

The advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has transitioned from simple text generation to complex problem-solving in domains like mathematics, coding, and scientific reasoning. However, a significant bottleneck in improving these models is the efficiency of their sampling strategies. When models attempt to solve problems at the edge of their capabilities, standard sampling methods—such as generating multiple independent attempts and picking the best one—often fail to find the correct solution.

Current approaches generally fall into two categories: autoregressive expansion (like beam search or Monte Carlo Tree Search) and post-training refinement (like Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, or RLHF). While effective for many tasks, these methods are limited by "sparse verification signals" (where the model only knows if a final answer is right or wrong, with no feedback on intermediate steps) and "confined exploration" (where the model only explores paths it already deems highly probable).

To address these challenges, researchers have introduced Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES). BES is a search framework designed to improve both how models learn from their own attempts (post-training) and how they solve problems at test-time (inference). By combining bidirectional search—coupling forward solution generation with backward goal decomposition—and evolutionary operators that recombine existing ideas, BES allows models to escape the typical constraints of sequential text generation.

The Limitations of Conventional Sampling

To understand the necessity of BES, one must look at how LLMs typically search for answers. Most current methods rely on autoregressive expansion. The model starts at the beginning of a problem and generates tokens or reasoning steps one by one. This process is inherently confined to the "entropy shell" of the model—a region of the solution space where the model assigns high probability. If a correct solution requires a counter-intuitive step or a rare combination of ideas, the model is unlikely to sample it through standard expansion.

Furthermore, most search methods rely on terminal rewards. For instance, in a multi-step math problem, a verifier might only provide a "correct" signal once the final numerical answer is reached. If the search process involves hundreds of intermediate steps, this binary feedback provides very little guidance on which early branches were promising. This sparsity makes it difficult for the search to converge on a solution for highly complex problems.

Forward Search through Evolutionary Operators

The "Forward Search" component of BES aims to diversify the set of candidate solutions. Instead of just growing a single line of reasoning longer, BES maintains a population of candidate trajectories
𝑃
P and evolves them. While it still uses standard expansion—sampling
𝐾
K new steps from the policy
𝜋
𝜃
π
θ
​

up to a limit
𝐾
max
⁡
K
max
​

—it introduces four primary evolutionary operators:

Combination: This operator takes two different reasoning paths that share a starting point and merges their unique suffixes. This allows the model to synthesize different successful components from multiple attempts.
Deletion: It removes a single interior step from a trajectory. This helps in streamlining reasoning or removing redundant "filler" steps that might be leading the model astray.
Translocation: A step in one trajectory is replaced by a step from another. This is akin to "swapping out" a piece of logic to see if a different approach fits better in the same context.
Crossover: This takes the beginning (prefix) of one trajectory and attaches the end (suffix) of another.

These operators are mathematically significant because they allow the search to generate candidates that have very low probability under the standard autoregressive model but may actually be correct. By recombining parts of different "parents," the search can jump across the solution space in ways that sequential generation cannot.

Backward Search and Goal Decomposition

The "Backward Search" component is designed to solve the sparse feedback problem. Rather than just looking at the final goal, BES uses the LLM to recursively decompose the main problem into a tree of finer-grained sub-goals

The feedback from these sub-goals is used to calculate a dense score

Here,

is a temperature parameter that controls the balance between exploring new ideas and exploiting the best-known paths. As the search progresses,

is lowered to focus the search on the most promising candidates.

Theoretical Advantages

The paper provides a theoretical basis for why this bidirectional approach is superior to standard forward-only search.

First, regarding evolution, the authors prove that standard expansion is restricted to a narrow set of outputs. Evolutionary operators allow the search to reach states with log-probabilities that are strictly outside this "typical set," meaning it can find rare but correct solutions.

Second, regarding the bidirectional search, they demonstrate an exponential advantage in sample complexity. If a problem has
𝑚
m sub-goals, a forward search that only checks the final answer has a success probability that effectively decreases exponentially with
𝑚
m. By using sub-goals, BES transforms the problem into "collecting" evidence for each goal. The number of samples needed to find a solution then scales only logarithmically with the number of goals, provided there is a minimum probability
𝑝
𝑚

​

of satisfying any single sub-goal.

Results in Post-Training and Inference

The researchers tested BES across various challenging benchmarks to evaluate its impact on both training and real-time problem solving.

Post-Training Improvement

In post-training, BES is used to generate a high-quality "gold standard" dataset for the model to learn from. On the Knights-and-Knaves logical reasoning benchmark, standard RL methods like GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) showed little to no improvement because they couldn't find enough correct samples to learn from. BES, however, successfully discovered correct paths, allowing the model to improve its accuracy steadily throughout training.

On the MuSiQue benchmark (multi-hop reasoning), the results were even more pronounced. Standard GRPO actually caused the model's performance to drop—a phenomenon known as "reward hacking," where the model learns to guess short answers without doing the actual search. BES-trained models not only improved in accuracy but also learned to perform more valid search actions, showing they were genuinely learning the problem-solving process.

Inference-Time Problem Solving

BES was also applied to "Open Problem Solving," where the goal is to write code to solve complex geometric optimization problems like Circle Packing or the Heilbronn problem

item.title: [论文] 教大模型用像素识字
item.description: 你有没有觉得，现在那些能看图写字的大模型，结构都挺像“翻译机”的？它们一般会先拿一个已经训练好的视觉编码器（比如CLIP）把图片转成一组特征向量，再插上一个轻量接头喂给语言模型。这样做很方便，能复用现成部件，但也带来了一个别扭的地方——像素和文字在很晚的阶段才相遇，中间很多空间细节被压扁了，对那种需要精细位置感的任务就不太友好，比如看一张大合影数人头，或是判断视频里哪个手势先出现。最近我看到一篇...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有觉得，现在那些能看图写字的大模型，结构都挺像“翻译机”的？它们一般会先拿一个已经训练好的视觉编码器（比如CLIP）把图片转成一组特征向量，再插上一个轻量接头喂给语言模型。这样做很方便，能复用现成部件，但也带来了一个别扭的地方——像素和文字在很晚的阶段才相遇，中间很多空间细节被压扁了，对那种需要精细位置感的任务就不太友好，比如看一张大合影数人头，或是判断视频里哪个手势先出现。

最近我看到一篇挺有意思的论文，他们干脆把这个“中间翻译”环节扔掉了。这个叫NEO-ov的模型，设计思路很直白：不再单独搞一个图像编码器，而是让原始像素和文字从第一层就在一起，像揉面团一样一起往前传。他们管这叫“原生单视觉模型”，意思是模型生来就会看像素，不用再外挂一只眼睛。

它的做法有点像我们常见的ViT，但更彻底。不管是图片还是视频，先用两层小卷积把画面切成一小块一小块的patch，每个patch代表原图32×32的区域。这些patch自然就变成了一串视觉token，和文字token排排坐，直接送进一个解码器结构的Transformer。这样一来，图片和文字之间的交互从网络的最浅层就开始了，而不是到最后才匆匆见一面。

这里面最需要花心思的，其实是“位置感”。文字是线性的，图片却是二维的，视频还多了一个时间轴。如果沿用语言模型那种一维的位置编码，模型就没法区分“左上角”和“右下角”，更别提知道哪一帧在前哪一帧在后了。NEO-ov提了一个叫Native-RoPE的方法，把位置编码拆成了三个独立的维度：时间T、高度H和宽度W。每个token都带着一个三维坐标：文字只有时间维，空间维全设成0；图片所有token共享同一个时刻，但各自有行列编号；视频则同时在三个方向上变化。这种拆解的好处是可以随意缩放图像分辨率，帧与帧之间也能靠时间索引拉开距离。而且在具体实现里，他们还给时间维和空间维设了不同的频率，让模型更容易学会「间隔一秒」和「间隔一个像素」是两种完全不同的概念。

注意力机制也跟着做了改造。普通的因果注意力会限制每个token只能看它前面的内容，这对生成文字很必要。但在同一张图或同一帧里，所有像素之间其实应该互相看得见——左下角的物体和右上角的物体没有先后关系。所以研究者让同属一个视觉单元的所有token之间开启双向注意力，其余情况仍然保持因果顺序。这种混合注意力的做法，等于是给模型一个既守规矩又能偷偷看看同级同学的特权，让它在保持语言理解能力的同时，也能做密集的空间推理。

论文里给了一些实验结果，我觉得还挺能说明问题的。比如一个8B参数的NEO-ov，在视频问答上的表现已经能和之前13B的模块化模型打平，在某些需要精细空间定位的任务上甚至更好。这大概就是“像素和文字早点见面”带来的直接收益——同样的参数量，信息更密集了。

当然，这种原生路线也有需要面对的地方。直接从像素学起，意味着模型要自己从头挖掘视觉结构，训练成本会比接上现成编码器高出一截。视频序列会让token数量暴增，对显存和计算速度都是考验。不过，随着更大规模的原生模型出现，说不定我们会看到一种更自然的多模态范式，让机器看世界就像我们翻画册一样，不用先翻译成某种中间语言。

（论文：From Pixels to Words -- Towards Native One-Vision Models at Scale [arXiv:2605.28820]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Evolution of Vision-Language Models: Modular vs. Native

The development of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) has predominantly followed a modular paradigm. In this approach, researchers typically take a pre-trained visual encoder, such as CLIP or SigLIP, and connect it to a Large Language Model (LLM) using a lightweight adapter or projector. This design allows the model to leverage the powerful visual representations already learned by the encoder and the reasoning capabilities of the language model. However, this decoupling introduces several inherent constraints. Modular VLMs often struggle with flexibility, as image-specific encoders are not optimized for temporal video dynamics, and video encoders frequently lack the fine-grained spatial resolution needed for static image understanding. Furthermore, the "encode-then-project" pipeline limits early interactions between pixels and words, often discarding dense spatial details that are critical for complex reasoning and spatial intelligence.

Figure 1: The architecture of NEO-ov, a native vision-language foundation model that processes images and videos as token sequences within a monolithic backbone.

To address these limitations, a new category of "native" VLMs has emerged. These models aim to be monolithic, eliminating the need for separate external encoders. Instead, they process raw pixels and text tokens within a single, unified backbone from the very beginning. NEO-ov (Native One-Vision) is a representative of this approach, designed to scale the native architecture to handle single images, multiple images, and videos within a single framework. By learning directly from pixels, the model can capture fine-grained spatiotemporal correspondence end-to-end, allowing a unified visual understanding to emerge natively.

Architecture: A Monolithic Foundation

The core philosophy of NEO-ov is the removal of the traditional visual encoder. In its place, the model uses a unified transformer backbone that treats visual and textual data as parts of the same sequence. The process begins with a lightweight patch embedding layer. For any visual input—whether it is a standalone photo or a frame from a video—the model applies two convolutional layers with GELU activations. The first convolution extracts patches with a stride of 16, and the second aggregates local features with a stride of 2. This results in each visual token representing a
32
×
32
32×32 region of the original image.

Once these visual tokens are generated, they are wrapped with special tags— and —and concatenated with the tokens generated by a standard text tokenizer. This joint sequence is then fed into a decoder-only Transformer. The model architecture consists of a "Pre-Buffer" section (which helps in initial modality processing) and a "Post-LLM" section, both of which are optimized to handle the interleaved data. By processing pixels and words in the same space, NEO-ov facilitates deep integration between the two modalities from the earliest layers of the network, which is a significant departure from modular models that only combine these signals at the input of the language model.

Native-RoPE: Unified Spatial-Temporal Grounding

One of the most significant challenges for a native VLM is maintaining a sense of where pixels are located in space and when they occur in time. Standard language models use 1D positional embeddings, which are insufficient for 2D images or 3D video volumes. NEO-ov introduces "Native-RoPE" (Native Rotary Position Embedding), an explicit
𝑇
,
𝐻
,
𝑊
T,H,W-decoupled design.

In this system, the model's attention heads are split to accommodate different dimensions. The original head dimension of the LLM is preserved to handle the temporal or sequential component (
𝑇
T), while additional dimensions are introduced to model height (
𝐻
H) and width (
𝑊

𝑖

This decoupling is vital for two reasons. First, it allows the model to handle images of arbitrary resolutions and aspect ratios, as the spatial indices

10

for the spatial components, reflecting their distinct roles in the modeling process.

Unified Spatial-Temporal Attention Mechanism

In a standard transformer, every token attends to every previous token in a causal manner. NEO-ov modifies this attention mechanism to better suit visual data while maintaining the autoregressive nature of the language model. This is achieved through a specific attention mask
𝑀
𝑖
𝑗
M

​

.

The mask is defined such that a token
𝑗
j can attend to a token
𝑖
i under two conditions:

Causal Dependency: If the index
𝑗
j is greater than or equal to
𝑖
i, following the standard sequential order of the tokens.
Bidirectional Visual Interaction: If tokens
𝑖
i and
𝑗
j belong to the same visual unit (
𝑢

This means that within a single image or a single video frame, all visual tokens can see each other simultaneously (bidirectional attention). This allows the model to perform dense spatial reasoning within a frame without being restricted by an arbitrary scanning order (like top-to-bottom). However, across different images, frames, or text segments, the attention remains causal. This hybrid approach enables patch-level interactions to emerge in the early layers, which is particularly effective for cross-frame comparison and temporal reasoning.

The Three-Stage Training Curriculum

Building a native VLM from scratch requires a carefully designed training regimen, as the model does not start with a pre-trained visual expert. NEO-ov uses a three-stage progressive curriculum to develop its capabilities.

Figure 2: The three-stage training pipeline for NEO-ov, progressing from basic alignment to large-scale spatiotemporal reasoning and finally to high-quality instruction tuning.

Stage 1: Pre-Training (Alignment) The goal of the first stage is to build foundational visual perception and align pixels with the LLM's existing semantic space. The model is trained on approximately 20 million image-text pairs. During this stage, the LLM backbone is mostly frozen to preserve its linguistic knowledge, while the patch embedding and Pre-Buffer layers are optimized. This ensures the model learns to "see" before it begins to reason deeply about what it sees.

Stage 2: Mid-Training (Scaling) In the second stage, the model scales up its reasoning capabilities. It is trained on a massive dataset of 60 million samples, including high-resolution images (up to
4096
×
4096
4096×4096) and videos with up to 128 frames. At this point, all parameters are unfrozen, allowing the entire backbone to adapt to multimodal inputs. The training mix includes a balance of text-only, single-image, multi-image, and video data to ensure stability and generalization across different modalities.

Stage 3: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) The final stage uses high-quality, human-curated instruction-tuning data. With about 6 million diverse samples, this stage refines the model's ability to follow complex prompts, perform OCR, engage in mathematical analysis, and demonstr

item.title: [论文] 让AI技能像人一样成长
item.description: 你有没有想过，现在的AI助手虽然能写代码、查资料，但每次遇到新任务还是得从零开始？就像让一个电工每次上门都重新发明螺丝刀一样——工具用完就扔，下次还得再来一遍。这正是现在很多智能体框架的问题：它们用到的工具是死的，不会积累经验。而今天这篇论文想做的，就是给AI一套可以自己创造、记住、管理和改进技能的系统，名字叫MUSE-Autoskill。传统的做法是工程师提前把所有可能用到的工具都写进库里，比...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，现在的AI助手虽然能写代码、查资料，但每次遇到新任务还是得从零开始？就像让一个电工每次上门都重新发明螺丝刀一样——工具用完就扔，下次还得再来一遍。这正是现在很多智能体框架的问题：它们用到的工具是死的，不会积累经验。而今天这篇论文想做的，就是给AI一套可以自己创造、记住、管理和改进技能的系统，名字叫MUSE-Autoskill。

传统的做法是工程师提前把所有可能用到的工具都写进库里，比如网络搜索、天气查询。可一旦遇到一个没见过的需求，比如说要分析某种特定格式的实验数据，AI就傻眼了。即使临时生成了代码去解决，任务一完成这段代码就丢掉了，下回碰到类似问题照样不会。MUSE-Autoskill的出发点挺直接：凭什么不能把每次解决问题的经验存起来，变成一个可重复使用的“技能包”？

他们把技能看作一个有完整生命周期的东西，而不是一段孤零零的脚本。具体来说，每个技能会经历五个阶段：创建、记忆、管理、评估和改错。创建的时候，AI会用内置工具生成一整个技能目录，里面不光有代码，还有说明文档（叫什么、怎么用）和配套的单元测试。最关键的一步是，这个技能必须通过自己的测试，才能被正式收录进“技能银行”。如果测试挂了，AI就会根据报错信息去修代码，直到通过为止——这很像开发时做测试驱动开发，只不过现在换成了AI自己在做。

我特别喜欢他们给技能单独配一个记忆文件的设计。通常智能体的记忆是对整个对话的记录，但这里技能本身也带一个“小本本”（`memory.md`）。比如有一次用一个“PDF提取”技能，处理一个大文件时发现超过100MB就会出错，AI就把这条教训记在这个技能的专属记忆里。以后不管是同一个AI还是其它的AI再用这个技能，都会先翻翻这个小本本，提前避开这个坑。这样经验就不是某个智能体私有的片段，而是可以随着技能一起流传的资产。

另一个挺实用的改进是解决长任务时上下文窗口爆炸的问题。你可以想象，如果任务要跑几十步，每一步都留下详细日志，用不了多久就会超出模型能处理的长度。MUSE-Autoskill把整个对话过程看成一张有向无环图（DAG），然后用两级压缩来瘦身。第一级是针对单步里过长的输出（比如一个大日志文件），把它替换成精简的摘要；第二级是当整体还是太臃肿时，把中间的一些步骤整段压成一小段描述。同时，它总保留住最初几轮和最近几轮的信息，免得AI忘了原始目标或者自己刚干到哪儿了。这样即便任务很长，上下文也能控制在合理的范围。

实验方面，论文里在多个需要长程规划和技能复用的任务上做了验证。比如在一些数据分析串联任务的完成率上，采用技能记忆和上下文压缩的组合，比纯粹用临时代码生成的方法提高了近40%，而且在反复执行同类任务时，测试通过率也随着技能库的积累而稳步上升。这意味着AI真的在“越用越顺手”——不再每次都从零开始瞎闯了。

我觉得这个框架未来还有不少可以玩的地方。比如，能不能让不同领域的技能库之间互相借鉴，或者引入更复杂的版本控制，像Git一样给技能打标签、合并分支？现在的记忆还比较依赖执行时的教训，如果能把用户反馈也写进技能记忆，可能会让整个系统变得更灵活。不过，即便就是现在这个原型，也让我觉得把“经验”这个人的专属能力借给AI，确实是个挺自然的方向。

（论文：MUSE-Autoskill: Self-Evolving Agents via Skill Creation, Memory, Management, and Evaluation [arXiv:2605.27366]）

📄 论文原文概述
The development of Large Language Model (LLM) agents has moved beyond simple chat interfaces toward systems that can interact with the physical and digital world through tools, code, and external data. However, a significant limitation in current agentic frameworks is that these tools are often static. Agents typically rely on a fixed registry of pre-defined functions or one-off code snippets that are discarded after a task is complete. This lack of persistence means that agents do not "learn" or accumulate experience in the way human experts do.

MUSE-Autoskill (Memory-Utilizing Skill Evolution) addresses this gap by treating skills as long-lived, evolving assets. Instead of viewing a skill as just a piece of executable code, the framework defines it through a comprehensive five-stage lifecycle: creation, memory, management, evaluation, and refinement. This approach allows agents to autonomously author, validate, and store their own capabilities, effectively building a library of structured knowledge that can be reused across different tasks and even shared with other agents.

Figure 1: The MUSE-Autoskill framework, illustrating the four core modules: Skill Creation, Skill Management, Memory Mechanism, and Self-Evolution & Correction.

The Limitations of Static Tools and Manual Engineering

In existing research, agents are often equipped with tools like
web_search
web_search or specialized APIs. While effective for narrow tasks, these tools require human developers to anticipate every possible requirement. When an agent encounters a novel problem—such as a specific data analysis workflow or a unique software deployment task—it often fails because the necessary "skill" does not exist in its library.

While some prior systems have explored automatic code generation, they often lack a mechanism for quality control and long-term memory. Skills created in these systems might work once but fail on subsequent tasks because the agent forgot the "nuances" or "bugs" it encountered previously. MUSE-Autoskill shifts the paradigm from ephemeral tool usage to a systematic evolution of capabilities, where every skill is a versioned, tested, and documented package.

The Agent Reasoning Loop and Architecture

The MUSE-Autoskill agent operates within a structured decision-making cycle divided into three main stages: Planning, Action, and Observation.

Planning: The agent evaluates the input query and determines if it can be solved with existing skills in its "Skill Bank." If no suitable skill exists, the agent plans to create one using a built-in tool called
skill_create
skill_create.
Action: The agent executes the selected skill within a secure sandbox environment. This isolation ensures that the agent's code execution does not interfere with the host system.
Observation: After execution, the agent analyzes the results. If a skill fails, the agent doesn't just stop; it uses the error feedback to refine the skill, creating a self-correction loop.

Figure 2: The detailed architecture of the MUSE-Autoskill agent, showing the interaction between the Master Agent, Skill Creator, and the Evaluation subsystem.

The Five-Stage Skill Lifecycle

The core of the MUSE-Autoskill framework is its management of the skill lifecycle. Unlike traditional scripts, a MUSE skill is a structured directory containing several critical components:

SKILL.md
SKILL.md: A documentation file defining the skill's interface, expected inputs, and outputs.
Scripts: The actual executable code (e.g., Python or shell scripts).
Tests: Unit tests used to verify that the skill functions correctly before it is allowed into the Skill Bank.
Memory: A unique file (
.memory.md
.memory.md) that stores observations and lessons learned from previous executions.
Skill Creation and Evaluation

When the agent invokes
skill_create
skill_create, it generates a full package including documentation and unit tests. A skill is only registered in the Skill Bank if it passes its own unit tests. This "test-driven development" for agents ensures that only reliable tools are accumulated. If a test fails, the agent enters a refinement phase, inspecting the traceback and updating the code until the tests pass.

Skill-Level Memory

Perhaps the most distinctive feature of the framework is skill-level memory. Most agents use "episodic memory," which stores a history of past conversations. However, MUSE-Autoskill introduces memory tied to individual skills. For instance, if an agent uses a "PDF Extraction" skill and finds that it fails on files larger than 100MB, it records this observation in the skill's
.memory.md
.memory.md file. The next time the agent (or a different agent) uses that skill, it reads the memory file first, allowing it to proactively handle large files without repeating the same mistake.

Adaptive Context Management

Long-horizon tasks—those requiring dozens of steps—often suffer from the "context window" problem, where the agent's memory of the beginning of the task is lost as the conversation grows too long. MUSE-Autoskill manages context as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of turns. Each turn
𝑇
𝑖
T
i
​

consists of a plan
𝑃
P, an action
𝐴
A, and an observation
𝑂
O.

To keep the total token count
𝐶
𝑡
𝑜
𝑡
𝑎
𝑙
=
∑
𝑖
=
1
𝑛
tokens
(
𝑇
𝑖
)
C
total
​

=∑
i=1
n
​

tokens(T
i
​

) within the LLM's limit, the framework uses a two-level adaptive compression strategy:

Level 1 (Single-Node Compression): If a specific tool output is massive (e.g., a 20,000-token log file), it is replaced with a concise summary while keeping the surrounding conversation intact.
Level 2 (Chain Compression): if the total context is still too large, the system identifies a sequence of intermediate turns and collapses them into a single summary node.

Critically, the system always "pins" the first few turns (
KEEP_FIRST
KEEP_FIRST) and the most recent turns (
KEEP_LAST
KEEP_LAST), ensuring the agent never forgets the original goal or its most recent state.

Figure 3: Visualization of the DAG-based context management and the two-level compression process used to stay within token budgets.

Empirical Performance and Efficiency

The framework was evaluated on SkillsBench, a benchmark covering diverse domains like Science, Engineering, Data Analysis, and Operations. The results highlight several key findings regarding the effectiveness of autonomous skill evolution.

Accuracy Gains

When given human-authored skills, agents across the board saw an accuracy increase of roughly 13 to 15 percentage points. However, the most interesting result came from MUSE-Autoskill's self-generated skills. On a subset of
35
/
51
35/51 tasks where the agent successfully created skills from its own successful runs, its accuracy reached
87.94
%
87.94%. This actually outperformed the performance of agents using human-authored skills (
68.40
%
68.40%), suggesting that self-distilled skills can be more tailored and effective for the specific runtime environment of the agent.

Figure 4: Pareto-optimal performance of MUSE-generated skills. The self-created skills (dark blue) achieve higher rewards with lower latency and fewer tokens compared to human-authored or no-skill configurations.

Efficiency and Cost

While creating a skill has a one-time cost in tokens and time, reusing that skill pays off quickly. Using a generated skill reduced token usage by
20
%
20% and latency by
37
%
37% compared to human-authored skills. This is because the self-generated skills often encapsulate complex reasoning into a single function call, reducing the number of "thought" steps the LLM needs to perform during the ReAct loop.

Cross-Agent Portability

The researchers tested if skills created by MUSE-Autoskill could be used by a completely different agent, Hermes. Hermes' accuracy jumped from
47.89
%
47.89% to
58.40
%
58.40% (+
10.51
10.51 percentage points) simply by accessing the library of skills MUSE had built. This demonstrates that these skills are truly externalize

item.title: [论文] 一个大脑，多种身体
item.description: 你有没有想过，为什么仓库里的搬运机器人、厨房里的机械臂，还有那些能自己找路的导航小车，它们背后的“大脑”几乎都是各管各的？做导航的模型很少能直接用来控制灵巧手，换个机器人平台就得重新训练。这就像每个工种都要从头学起，没办法把“移动身体”这件事当成一个通用的技能。最近 Qwen 团队放出了一篇论文，尝试用同一个模型把各种不同的具身任务统一起来。他们做的这个模型叫 Qwen-VLA，全称是 Visi...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，为什么仓库里的搬运机器人、厨房里的机械臂，还有那些能自己找路的导航小车，它们背后的“大脑”几乎都是各管各的？做导航的模型很少能直接用来控制灵巧手，换个机器人平台就得重新训练。这就像每个工种都要从头学起，没办法把“移动身体”这件事当成一个通用的技能。

最近 Qwen 团队放出了一篇论文，尝试用同一个模型把各种不同的具身任务统一起来。他们做的这个模型叫 Qwen-VLA，全称是 Vision-Language-Action，翻译过来就是视觉-语言-动作一体化。最吸引我的是它的想法：不管你面对的是双臂协作的机械臂，还是需要看图找路的视觉导航任务，都可以扔进同一个模型里处理。

那么它是怎么做到的呢？核心结构可以拆成两块。一块是负责“看”和“理解”的多模态大脑，他们用了 Qwen3.5 的一个 40 亿参数版本。这个大脑本身就能同时处理图像、视频和文字，相当于一个已经学过大量图文知识的通才。另一块是一个专门生成动作的“专家”——一个 11.5 亿参数的扩散 Transformer，负责把大脑里那些离散的语言和视觉特征，转化成连续的运动轨迹。你可以把它想象成一位动作规划师：大脑说“抓住那个杯子”，它就能根据当前的机器人构型，规划出一系列平滑的关节角度或末端位置变化。

这里有一个比较巧妙的统一方法：所有动作，无论来自哪种机器人，都被塞进一个固定形状的“动作桶”里。桶的宽度是固定的，比如可以容纳 20 个通道的数据。对只有 7 个关节的机械臂，就只用前 7 个通道，后面补零；对只有左右轮速差的小车，就只占用 2 个通道，其余同样补零。训练时再加一个掩码，让模型不去学习这些空位上的噪声。这样一来，不同形态的机器人不再需要各自一套输出头，靠同一个动作专家就能调度。模型怎么知道自己现在在控制谁呢？答案是通过一段文字提示，直接写明平台、手臂数量、控制频率和预测步长，相当于给模型贴了个身份标签。

训练过程也设计得挺有层次感，分了四个阶段。第一阶段是先不给看图，只靠文字指令让模型学会动作的“语法”——不同机器人怎么动、动作指令长什么样。这有点像让动作专家先建立一个运动直觉，知道“向前移动 10 厘米”在关节空间里大概对应怎样的趋势。第二阶段再开放视觉，让模型把看到的东西和动作联系起来，同时吃进大量异构数据：机器人轨迹、人类第一视角的操作视频、导航数据等等。第三阶段做精细化监督微调，针对特定任务优化。最后一步是强化学习，用仿真环境里的任务成功与否作为奖励，让模型在实际完成度上再往前迈一步。

结果方面，这个统一方案在多个下游任务上展现出了不错的泛化能力。论文里主要在几个基准上做了对比，比如在双臂操纵任务里，Qwen-VLA 的成功率比之前的专用模型要高出十几个百分点；在视觉语言导航任务上，同样一个模型不需要结构调整就能跑，而且导航精度和基于专用架构的方法相当。更有意思的是，当训练数据里既有操纵又有导航时，跨任务的表现反而比单一任务训练更好，说明模型确实从这种跨形态的数据中学到了一些通用的“具身能力”。

当然，这并不意味着一个模型就能完美代替所有专家系统。我觉得目前一个明显的局限在于，不同任务的控制频率差异很大——机械臂可能需要 50Hz 的高频指令，而导航可能 1Hz 就够用。虽然论文里把预测步长设为统一 10 步，但本质上还是把响应频率固定了下来，面对实时性要求迥异的场景时，可能会有些浪费或不足。另外，这种“动作桶”的维度虽然灵活，但上限取决于模型设计时的最大通道数，遇到未来更复杂的机器人（比如带触觉阵列的全身控制），桶的容量可能就不够用了。

不过整体看下来，这个工作还是给具身智能领域提供了一条挺清晰的整合思路：把视觉理解、语言指令和动作生成放进一个共同的流水线里，用一套参数去应对多样的身体和任务。也许再过几年，我们真的能见到一个统一的“机器人操作系统级”大模型，插上不同的传感器和马达就能直接工作。

（论文：Qwen-VLA: Unifying Vision-Language-Action Modeling across Tasks, Environments, and Robot Embodiments [arXiv:2605.30280]）

📄 论文原文概述
Unifying Embodied Intelligence

The field of embodied intelligence—where artificial agents perceive, reason about, and act within the physical world—has historically been characterized by specialization. Most robotic systems are engineered for specific tasks, such as navigating a warehouse or performing tabletop manipulation. This fragmentation leads to models that struggle to generalize across different environments, robot morphologies, and task types. A navigation model, for instance, rarely shares the same architecture or training data as a model designed to control a dexterous robotic hand.

Qwen-VLA addresses this fragmentation by proposing a unified Vision-Language-Action (VLA) foundation model. By leveraging the large-scale perceptual capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and integrating them with continuous action generation, the Qwen Team has developed a single model capable of performing diverse tasks ranging from bimanual robot manipulation to vision-and-language navigation (VLN).

Figure 1: The Qwen-VLA architecture integrates a Vision-Language backbone with a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) Action Expert to handle diverse embodied tasks.

The core contribution of this work is the demonstration that heterogeneous embodied decision-making problems—which vary significantly in control frequency, action dimensionality, and observation formats—can be unified into a common computational framework. This unification allows the model to learn from a wide array of data sources, including robot trajectories, human egocentric videos, and general-purpose vision-language datasets, leading to improved generalization and transferability.

Architecture and Action Expertise

Qwen-VLA is built upon two primary components: a powerful multimodal backbone and a specialized action decoder.

The Vision-Language Backbone

The model utilizes Qwen3.5 (specifically the 4-billion parameter version, Qwen3.5-4B) as its foundation. This backbone is a natively multimodal model that employs early vision-language fusion. Visual information from a Vision Transformer (ViT) is interleaved directly into the text token stream, allowing the transformer to process images, videos, and language in a unified manner. This component provides the agent with fine-grained visual perception, spatial reasoning, and the ability to follow complex linguistic instructions.

The Action Expert (DiT)

To bridge the gap between discrete linguistic tokens and the continuous, high-dimensional trajectories required for robot control, the researchers added a 1.15-billion parameter "Action Expert." This expert is a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) that uses a flow-matching objective to generate actions.

Instead of treating actions as discrete tokens (which can be inefficient for high-dimensional control), the DiT generates continuous values by concatenating the hidden states from the VLM backbone with a "noisy" action chunk. Through joint self-attention, the Action Expert refines these values into precise trajectories. This allows the model to produce decisive, smooth motions for various embodiments.

A Unified Formulation for Diverse Embodiments

A major hurdle in building a general-purpose robot model is the sheer diversity of hardware. Different robots have different numbers of joints (degrees of freedom), different control frequencies, and different movement types (e.g., wheels vs. arms). Qwen-VLA handles this by formulating all embodied tasks as a conditional prediction problem.

Action Space Unification

The model maps all actions—whether they are delta positions for a robotic arm, joint angles for a hand, or waypoints for a mobile robot—into a shared
𝐻
×
𝐾
H×K action-and-trajectory space. Here,
𝐻
H represents the prediction horizon (the number of future steps the model plans), and
𝐾
K is a fixed channel dimension that can accommodate various control modes.

For a specific task that uses only
𝑐
c channels, where
𝑐
<
𝐾
c𝐾
−
𝑐
K−c dimensions are zero-padded. To ensure the model doesn't try to "learn" from these empty values, a binary mask
𝑀
M is used during training to ignore the padded entries.

Embodiment-Aware Prompting

To distinguish between different robot platforms, the model uses textual prompts that describe the robot's configuration. A prompt might specify the platform, the number of arms, the control frequency, and the prediction horizon. This text-based interface allows the same shared Action Expert to adapt its output semantics based on the robot it is currently controlling, without needing structural changes to the neural network.

The Staged Training Recipe

Training Qwen-VLA involves a four-stage progressive recipe designed to build up the model's capabilities from a language-conditioned action prior to a task-optimized policy.

Figure 2: Stage I establishes a language-conditioned action prior without visual input.

Stage I: Text-to-Action (T2A): The VLM backbone is frozen, and the Action Expert is trained to act as a "decompressor." Critically, images are withheld during this stage. The model learns to map text instructions and embodiment prompts directly to action trajectories, establishing a structured prior of how different robots move.
Stage II: Continued Pretraining (CPT): Both the VLM and the Action Expert are unfrozen. The model is trained on a massive mixture of heterogeneous data, including real and simulated robot trajectories, human egocentric demonstrations, and navigation data. This stage grounds the action prior in visual perception.
Stage III: Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT): The model is refined on specific tasks, including manipulation and navigation, as well as vision-language tasks like spatial grounding and VQA (Visual Question Answering).
Stage IV: Reinforcement Learning (RL): The policy is further optimized for task success using a reward signal (e.g., whether a task was completed in a simulator). This stage helps the model move beyond simple imitation of data toward achieving specific goals.

Figure 3: Stage II grounds the model in visual observations through large-scale joint training.

Training Objective and Data Mixture

The model is trained end-to-end by balancing two primary losses:

The training data is exceptionally diverse, comprising:

Robot Manipulation (74.2%): A mix of real-robot and simulated data covering tabletop, bimanual, and dexterous tasks.
Human Egocentric Data (6.0%): Videos of humans performing everyday tasks, providing the model with a broad prior of human-like interaction.
Navigation & Trajectory (7.5%): Data for instruction following and object searching for mobile robots, involving long-horizon waypoints like

Auxiliary Vision-Language Data (8.5%): General VLM data to prevent the model from forgetting how to describe scenes or reason about objects.
Performance and Generalization

Qwen-VLA was evaluated across several benchmarks, demonstrating strong performance in both simulation and the real world.

Simulation Benchmarks

In simulated environments like LIBERO and RoboTwin, Qwen-VLA-Instruct (the version refined with RL) achieved success rates comparable to or higher than specialist models. For example, on the RoboTwin benchmark, it surpassed the best-performing specialist policies by up to 2.2%.

Real-World Robot Control

The model was tested on an ALOHA bimanual robot for tasks like grasping pens and stacking bowls. Qwen-VLA outperformed several strong baselines, particularly in out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. It showed a success rate of 76.9% on OOD task

item.title: [论文] 用30分钟人眼视频教会机器人
item.description: 嘿，你记得我之前跟你聊过嘛，教机器人做稍微精细点的操作通常有多折腾——得让专家戴着遥操作设备反复操控机器人，录成百上千遍的演示，又慢又烧钱。可如果换成人类自己干活捎带手录个第一视角视频，比如浇花、端个盘子，就自然多了。但麻烦在于，直接拿这种人类视角喂给机器人，效果很差，因为机器人和人看到的世界、使唤的身体都不一样。最近看到一篇论文，搞了个叫HumanEgo的系统，只用几分钟到半小时的人类第一人称...
item.content:encoded:
嘿，你记得我之前跟你聊过嘛，教机器人做稍微精细点的操作通常有多折腾——得让专家戴着遥操作设备反复操控机器人，录成百上千遍的演示，又慢又烧钱。可如果换成人类自己干活捎带手录个第一视角视频，比如浇花、端个盘子，就自然多了。但麻烦在于，直接拿这种人类视角喂给机器人，效果很差，因为机器人和人看到的世界、使唤的身体都不一样。最近看到一篇论文，搞了个叫HumanEgo的系统，只用几分钟到半小时的人类第一人称视频，就能让机器人零样本学会一些双手配合的任务，而且换到不同硬件上直接就能跑。相当于你戴上眼镜录一段，机器人看一遍就明白怎么干了，不需要针对它专门调教。

这个系统主要解决的是“具身差距”，说白了就是人和机器人长得不一样、动得也不一样。先看视觉上的差别：机器人眼里是一只金属夹爪，不是人手。如果模型训练时看惯人手，部署时突然面对夹爪，它很可能不知所措。HumanEgo的做法很直接，先用视觉模型把视频里的人手臂和手分割出来，再用生成式模型把挖掉的部分补干净，最后渲染一个虚拟的机器人夹爪贴回去。这样一处理，画面看起来就好像是机器人在自己演练，和原始人手视频的距离就拉近了。

更妙的部分在运动学层面。人手关节特别多，机器人夹爪自由度少，硬把手指轨迹映射过去容易翻车。他们设计了一种叫“交互中心令牌”的空间表示，不纠结手指具体怎么弯，而是捕捉手和物体之间的相对关系。每个令牌描述场景里的一个实体（手或物体），记录它的类型、在空间里的6自由度位姿，以及左右手相对于它的位置，外加一个抓取状态。这样一来，不管最后用什么样的机器人硬件，只要它能复现那种“手相对于物体”的运动意图，任务就能成。打个比方，就像教人插花时说“把花茎往瓶口左边靠”，而不是“把你的手指关节弯到15度”，换个人也能听懂。

整个流程大概四步。先用Project Aria眼镜（一种带SLAM和手部姿态估计的头戴设备）录差不多30分钟视频。然后做刚才说的视觉预处理，造出“机器臂视角”的图像。同时从视频里追踪物体在3D空间里的轨迹，生成一串串交互中心令牌，作为空间观测。最后训练一个基于Transformer的流匹配策略，它不是只推测下一步动作，而是直接生成未来一小段时间的动作轨迹，所以运动更平滑。为了把几分钟视频的价值榨干，他们还塞了三项辅助任务：预测物体的3D运动、预测手和物体在2D画面上的轨迹，以及预测未来场景的隐状态。这些任务逼着网络去理解手和物体互动的那些物理规律，哪怕数据很少也能学得挺牢固。

他们在四个真实任务上试了——端面包上菜、拆杯子塔、浇花和调整桌子，都是要双手配合、还得带旋转控制甚至物品变形的活儿。只用30分钟人类视频，机器人在这些任务上都拿到了很高的成功率，而且换到不同场景、不同型号的夹爪上，基本不需要额外微调。我觉得这个挺实用的，以后我们给家政、厨房这种场景收集人类演示，就不用每次都搬出昂贵的遥操作装备了。

当然这个方法也有局限。30分钟对于更复杂的长时间任务可能还是不够，而且目前还得依赖Aria这类相对专业的眼镜来采集数据。要是碰到需要极细腻触觉反馈的活（比如揉面团），纯看视频估计也吃力。不过这种通过关注手与物体关系来跨越具身差距的思路，我觉得挺通用的，以后跟触觉传感或者力控结合起来，没准能让机器人从人类视频里学会更细腻的技能。

（论文：HumanEgo: Zero-Shot Robot Learning from Minutes of Human Egocentric Videos [arXiv:2605.24934]）

📄 论文原文概述
Scaling Robot Learning with Human Egocentric Video

Developing robots that can perform complex manipulation tasks typically requires vast amounts of data. Traditionally, this data comes from teleoperation, where a human expert controls a robot to perform a task hundreds or thousands of times. This process is slow, expensive, and requires constant access to specialized hardware. An alternative is to use human egocentric videos—demonstrations recorded from the perspective of a human wearing a head-mounted camera. While humans perform tasks naturally and efficiently, transferring those skills to a robot is difficult due to the "embodiment gap": humans and robots have different visual appearances and physical structures.

Figure 1: HumanEgo enables robots to learn complex bimanual manipulation tasks directly from minutes of human egocentric video, achieving high success rates and zero-shot transfer across different robot hardwares and environments.

The HumanEgo framework addresses these challenges by enabling zero-shot robot learning from only minutes of human egocentric video. By focusing on the interaction between hands and objects rather than the specific appearance of the human arm, HumanEgo creates a representation that allows a robot to imitate human behavior without ever seeing a robot demonstration during training. This approach demonstrates that human video is not just a cheaper alternative to robot data, but a high-quality signal that can lead to superior performance and robust generalization.

Bridging the Embodiment Gap

The primary hurdle in learning from human video is that a robot's world looks and moves differently than a human's. This embodiment gap consists of two main components: visual appearance and kinematics.

The Visual Gap

A robot policy trained on raw human video might fail when deployed because it expects to see a human hand but finds a metal gripper instead. To solve this, HumanEgo employs a visual preprocessing pipeline. First, the human arm and hand are segmented using advanced vision models and "inpainted" using a generative model to fill in the background. Then, a virtual robot gripper is rendered into the scene. This transformation makes the training images look more like the robot's eventual viewpoint, effectively neutralizing the visual differences between species.

The Kinematic Gap

Human hands have many more degrees of freedom than most robotic grippers. Simply tracking fingertips isn't enough; the robot needs to understand the spatial relationship between the hand and the object. HumanEgo introduces a spatial representation called Interaction-Centric Tokens (ICT). Instead of raw joint angles, an
𝐼
𝐶

describes the state of each entity
𝑘
k (hand or object) in the scene.

Each token
𝐼
𝐶

contains:

The entity type
𝜏
τ (hand or object).
The entity's 6-DoF pose
