By encoding how the hands move relative to the objects, the system captures the "intent" of the manipulation in a way that is invariant to the specific robot hardware or camera angle used during deployment.

The HumanEgo Pipeline

The HumanEgo system follows a structured four-stage process to convert raw video into a deployable control policy.

Figure 2: The HumanEgo framework consists of four stages: (1) Egocentric data collection using Aria glasses, (2) Visual preprocessing to bridge the appearance gap, (3) Spatial preprocessing to generate Interaction-Centric Tokens (ICT), and (4) Training a Flow Matching policy with dense auxiliary objectives.

1. Data Collection

Demonstrations are collected using Project Aria glasses, which provide high-quality egocentric RGB video along with 3D SLAM tracking and precise hand pose estimation. Because these glasses are wearable, users can record tasks like serving bread or watering flowers in natural environments. Only about 30 minutes of video per task is required.

2. Observation Preprocessing

The system processes the video to create two types of observations. The visual observation is the inpainted image with virtual grippers, while the spatial observation consists of the ICT sequence. Objects are tracked in 3D by triangulating keypoints from the video, ensuring the system has a stable understanding of where items are located in space.

3. Policy Architecture

The core of the control system is a transformer-based Flow Matching policy. Flow Matching is a generative technique that learns to "push" a random noise distribution toward the target action trajectory. Unlike traditional methods that might predict a single next step, this policy generates a sequence of future actions (a trajectory) over a horizon
𝐾
K, allowing for smoother and more coordinated movements.

4. Dense Auxiliary Objectives

Learning from just minutes of video is difficult because the supervision signal can be sparse. HumanEgo overcomes this by adding three "auxiliary" training tasks that share the same underlying features as the main policy:

Object Motion Prediction: The model must predict how the object will move in 3D as a result of the hand's motion.
2D Trace Prediction: The model predicts the 2D path the hand and object will take in the image.
Latent Consistency: The model predicts the future latent state of the scene.

These tasks act as a "lightweight world model," forcing the network to learn the physics and dynamics of hand-object interaction, which significantly improves learning efficiency in low-data regimes.

Performance and Efficiency

HumanEgo was tested on four diverse real-world tasks: serving bread, downstacking cups, watering flowers, and adjusting a table. These tasks involve bimanual coordination, rotational control, and multi-step reasoning.

Figure 3: HumanEgo consistently outperforms existing human-video and robot-teleoperation baselines across all tasks, reaching an average success rate of 92.5%.

A key finding of the research is the efficiency of human data. HumanEgo achieved a 75% success rate with only 15 minutes of human data, outperforming a standard robot teleoperation method (ACT) that used 30 minutes of robot data. This suggests that human demonstrations are higher quality than robot teleoperation—they are smoother, have less idle time, and cover a wider variety of positions and strategies.

Figure 4: The learning curve shows that HumanEgo extracts significant signal from very few demonstrations. The auxiliary objectives (dashed blue vs. solid blue) provide a substantial boost when data is extremely limited (under 15 minutes).

The researchers also found that the interaction-centric representation was the most critical factor for success. In ablation studies, using only visual data (even with robot-like imagery) resulted in low success rates. Adding the ICT spatial representation caused performance to jump from 32.5% to 85%, proving that explicit 3D spatial reasoning is essential for robust manipulation.

Zero-Shot Generalization

Because HumanEgo learns an embodiment-agnostic representation, the same policy can be deployed on different robots without any retraining. The researchers demonstrated successful zero-shot transfer to Trossen, Franka, and UR10 robot arms.

Figure 5: HumanEgo displays robust generalization across novel robot hardwares, lighting conditions, background environments, and object instances.

Furthermore, the policy is robust to environmental changes. It maintained high success rates when tested with:

Novel Viewpoints: Different camera angles and heights.
Visual Distractors: Extra objects on the table that were not present during training.
Novel Objects: Using different types of bread or cups than those seen in the human videos.
Novel Backgrounds: Moving

item.title: [论文] 把所有模态炼成同一个向量空间
item.description: 你有没有想过，以后找图片不用打字，直接丢一张随手拍的照片，就能在相册里翻出类似的旅行照？或者用一段哼唱的旋律来搜歌？这些场景都离不开一个幕后技术——嵌入模型。传统的嵌入模型大多只擅长处理文本，把句子变成一串代表语义的数字。但现实世界远不止文字，图片、视频、音频每天都在产生。于是，怎么让同一个模型同时“看懂”这些不同类型的数据，就成了一个很有意思的挑战。最近和一个朋友聊到一篇论文，讲的是 Gemi...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，以后找图片不用打字，直接丢一张随手拍的照片，就能在相册里翻出类似的旅行照？或者用一段哼唱的旋律来搜歌？这些场景都离不开一个幕后技术——嵌入模型。传统的嵌入模型大多只擅长处理文本，把句子变成一串代表语义的数字。但现实世界远不止文字，图片、视频、音频每天都在产生。于是，怎么让同一个模型同时“看懂”这些不同类型的数据，就成了一个很有意思的挑战。

最近和一个朋友聊到一篇论文，讲的是 Gemini Embedding 2，一个原生支持多模态的嵌入模型。简单说，它能把文本、图像、视频、音频都映射到同一个 3072 维的向量空间里，而且不是事后拼凑，是从底层训练就打通了这些模态。

以前的多模态嵌入，往往是“后期融合”的路子：给图片配一个视觉编码器，给文本配一个文本编码器，然后把各自的输出强行塞进一个共享空间。这就像让两个说不同语言的人先各自翻译，再去对齐，难免丢掉一些跨模态的微妙关联。更麻烦的是，当输入里既有图片又有文字，甚至一段视频混在一起时，这种分离式结构就很吃力。Gemini Embedding 2 换了个思路，它直接站在 Gemini 这个原本就能理解多模态的大模型肩膀上，把图像像素、音频波形当成和文本一样的 token，喂进同一个 Transformer 里处理。

这个过程还挺直觉的：不管输入是一段话、一张图，还是一小段视频，模型先把它转成一串 token，然后用双向注意力机制去理解。双向注意力意味着模型可以同时看到上下文的所有信息，不像生成式模型只能一个接一个字地看。看完之后，它对每个 token 都产出一个向量，最后用均值池化把这些向量压缩成一个固定长度的向量，再过一层线性投影，就得到了最终的 3072 维嵌入。这个数字听起来不小，但带来的好处是它能更细腻地捕捉语义。

有意思的是，模型还用了一种叫 Matryoshka 表征学习的技巧，名字取自俄罗斯套娃。它的意思是，最重要的语义信息被浓缩在向量的前一部分维度里。这样，如果你嫌 3072 维太占存储，可以像套娃一样去掉尾部，只用前面 768 或 1536 维来检索，精度损失不大，但速度和效率能提升不少。这对实际落地很友好。

训练过程也花了不少心思，不是一股脑把所有数据倒进去。团队采取了三阶段策略：先做预微调，用海量、带噪的图文对和代码数据让模型初步学会对齐不同模态，大 batch size 帮它抗住噪声；然后是精调，引入高质量数据和“难负例”——那些看起来和目标相似但语义不对的例子，逼着模型学会更精细的区分。针对文本任务，他们还加了任务字符串，比如告诉模型“你现在是在做问答”或“你现在是在聚类”，让嵌入更有针对性。为了防止模型过度依赖这个提示（毕竟真实场景里图片可不会自带任务说明），训练中会随机丢掉任务字符串，强迫模型从内容本身提取意义。最后一步有点可爱，叫做模型汤。他们不选单个最佳 checkpoint，而是把多个训练阶段的权重平均起来，发现这样做出来的模型在所有模态上的泛化性能都比单独任何一个强，就像炖汤，几种食材混在一起味道更均衡。

效果怎么样呢？论文给了一组数字：在多语言文本嵌入基准 MMTEB 上，平均分达到 69.9，超过了之前很多专用模型。在图像、视频、文档等多模态检索任务上，也展现出了明显的优势。虽然具体数字在不同数据集上有差异，但整体趋势很清晰——一个模型通吃多种模态，而且得分还挺体面。

我觉得这个工作最大的价值，不是把某个指标刷到新高，而是提供了一种更统一的多模态嵌入范式。它不再需要为每个组合（文搜图、图搜视频等等）单独拼装一套管线，训练和维护成本都会降低。当然，它也有自己的局限。比如模型本身就挺大，实时性要求高的场景可能需要蒸馏或量化；面对非常专业的小众领域，通用嵌入的精度可能还是不如领域特化的模型。另外，原生处理像素和波形的计算开销，在手机端跑起来还不太现实。

不过，这种“原生多模态”的思路很可能是个趋势。以后我们做 RAG、推荐系统或者跨模态搜索，也许就不用再纠结选哪个文本模型配哪个图像模型了，一个模型就能扛下所有编码任务。如果你正在折腾这类应用，值得留意一下。

（论文：Gemini Embedding 2: A Native Multimodal Embedding Model from Gemini [arXiv:2605.27295]）

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction

In the current landscape of artificial intelligence, embedding models serve as a foundational component for information retrieval, recommendation systems, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Traditionally, these models have focused primarily on text, converting sentences and paragraphs into dense numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning. However, as AI systems evolve to handle diverse data types, there is an increasing need for "multimodal" embeddings that can represent images, videos, and audio alongside text in a single, unified mathematical space.

Figure 1: Gemini Embedding 2 maps text, image, video, and audio into a unified vector space of up to 3072 dimensions.

Gemini Embedding 2 is a native multimodal embedding model designed to address the limitations of previous "late-fusion" approaches. In earlier systems, different encoders were often used for different modalities (e.g., one model for images and another for text), with their outputs projected into a shared space. While functional, these systems often struggle to capture the deep semantic interactions between modalities or handle "interleaved" inputs—sequences where images, text, and videos appear together. Gemini Embedding 2 leverages the deep fusion capabilities of the Gemini foundation model to provide a general-purpose solution that understands and aligns multiple data types out-of-the-box.

Architectural Design and Unified Representation

The architecture of Gemini Embedding 2 is built upon the Gemini family of multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Unlike models that use a separate vision transformer or audio encoder as a front-end, Gemini Embedding 2 is "native," meaning it processes raw pixels and audio waveforms as part of a continuous token sequence within the same transformer backbone.

Input Processing and Transformation

When the model receives an input—whether it is a snippet of text, an image, a video clip, or an audio recording—it first converts the data into a sequence of
𝐿
L tokens. This sequence is then processed by a transformer model
𝑀
M that employs bidirectional attention. This is a departure from the causal (left-to-right) attention typically used in generative LLMs, as embedding tasks benefit from looking at the entire context of the input simultaneously.

The transformer produces a sequence of token embeddings:

Gemini Embedding 2 typically produces embeddings with 3,072 dimensions, providing a high-resolution semantic map of the input data.

Matryoshka Representation Learning

One notable feature of the model is its use of Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL). This technique allows the model to produce nested embeddings where the most important semantic information is concentrated in the earlier dimensions. This allows users to "truncate" the 3,072-dimensional vector to smaller sizes (like 768 or 1,536) for more efficient storage and faster search speeds, without a catastrophic loss in retrieval accuracy.

Multi-Stage Training Strategy

Training a model to understand and align so many different modalities requires a structured, multi-stage approach. The researchers employed a strategy that moves from broad, noisy data to refined, task-specific alignment.

Pre-Fine-Tuning (PFT)

The first stage, pre-fine-tuning, focuses on adapting the model from a generative paradigm to an encoding paradigm. During this stage, the model is exposed to vast quantities of potentially noisy query-target pairs across images, text, and code. The goal is to establish a basic semantic alignment. Large batch sizes are used here to provide stable gradients, helping the model ignore the "noise" inherent in massive web-crawled datasets.

Fine-Tuning (FT) and Task Strings

In the fine-tuning stage, the model is refined using high-quality datasets that include "hard negatives"—examples that are superficially similar to the target but semantically incorrect. This forces the model to learn fine-grained distinctions.

For text-based tasks, the model uses "task strings" (e.g., "question answering" or "clustering") to guide the embedding process. The query

To ensure the model remains robust when task strings are unavailable (as is often the case with raw images or audio), the training process randomly drops these strings, encouraging the model to extract meaning from the content itself.

Model Soup

To further enhance generalization, the researchers utilized a "model soup" technique. Instead of picking the single best-performing checkpoint from a training run, they average the weights of multiple checkpoints. This averaging process often leads to a model that performs better across all modalities than any individual checkpoint could on its own.

Performance Across Benchmarks

Gemini Embedding 2 was evaluated across a wide array of benchmarks to test its capabilities in unimodal (e.g., text-to-text), cross-modal (e.g., text-to-image), and truly multimodal (e.g., image+text-to-text) scenarios.

Figure 2: Comparative performance of Gemini Embedding 2 against competitors across image, video, document, and multilingual text benchmarks.

Multilingual and Code Retrieval

On the Massive Multilingual Text Embedding Benchmark (MMTEB), the model achieved a mean score of 69.9, outperforming previous text-only models and competitors like Amazon Nova MME. Notably, it set a new standard for code retrieval, scoring 84.0 on the MTEB Code v1 benchmark. This performance is largely attributed to the inclusion of high-quality synthetic data generated by the Gemini model family, which provides clearer signals for complex programming tasks.

Cross-Modal and Video Retrieval

The model shows strong performance in retrieving images from text descriptions and vice versa. On the MSCOCO benchmark, it reached a Recall@1 of 62.9 for text-to-image tasks. In the video domain, it achieved a mean NDCG@10 of 63.1, demonstrating an ability to understand temporal sequences and visual events without requiring domain-specific fine-tuning for every dataset.

Visual Document Retrieval

Document retrieval is a particularly challenging area because it requires understanding text, layout, and visual structure simultaneously. On the ViDoRe V2 benchmark, Gemini Embedding 2 achieved an NDCG@10 of 64.9. This indicates that the model can effectively embed entire document pages, including their embedded charts and formatting, which is critical for enterprise RAG applications.

Audio Understanding: Beyond Textual Transcripts

A significant contribution of this work is the demonstration of "native" audio embedding. Most existing systems process audio by first converting it to text via Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and then embedding that text. This "cascaded" approach has two major flaws: it propagates ASR errors and it loses non-textual acoustic information (like tone, emotion, or background sounds).

By processing raw audio directly, Gemini Embedding 2 preserves these nuances. In evaluations on the Massive Sound Embedding Benchmark (MSEB), the native audio approach outperformed the ASR-based baseline significantly. For cross-lingual audio retrieval, the native model improved performance by over 5 points, showing that it can find semantic links across different spoken languages mo

item.title: [论文] 教模型一眼看穿世界本质
item.description: 你有没有想过，现在的 AI 看一段视频或者玩个游戏时，它脑子里到底装的是真实世界的骨架，还是只记住了一堆乱七八糟的碎片？最近有个朋友在做的自监督学习，就在尝试回答这个问题。简单说，有一种叫 JEPA 的架构，它不逐像素预测下一帧画面，而是学习抽象的“概念空间”里的变化，这样就不用管树叶怎么乱晃，只管物体的核心运动。这类模型的效果不错，但一直有个关键问题悬着：它真的学到了真实世界的底层结构吗？还是...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有想过，现在的 AI 看一段视频或者玩个游戏时，它脑子里到底装的是真实世界的骨架，还是只记住了一堆乱七八糟的碎片？最近有个朋友在做的自监督学习，就在尝试回答这个问题。简单说，有一种叫 JEPA 的架构，它不逐像素预测下一帧画面，而是学习抽象的“概念空间”里的变化，这样就不用管树叶怎么乱晃，只管物体的核心运动。这类模型的效果不错，但一直有个关键问题悬着：它真的学到了真实世界的底层结构吗？还是说，它掌握的那个内部地图，已经被揉成一团扭曲的投影？

这篇论文就专门研究了 LeJEPA 这个变体，用一套理论证明，在特定条件下，模型确实能还原出世界最原本的自由度，比如物体的位置和速度，不会把它们杂糅成一个奇怪的编码。这相当于给“世界模型”这个词盖了一个靠谱的章，不再是经验主义的黑箱。

具体怎么做到的呢？LeJEPA 的做法很直观：给模型看同一物体或场景的两个不同“视角”（比如相邻时刻的状态），然后要求它学到的表征向量在这两个视角下尽可能接近，这就是对齐损失。但光对齐不够，模型容易偷懒，把所有输入都映射到同一个点来让损失为零，这种坍缩是自监督里常见的地雷。LeJEPA 的秘密武器是一个正则化技巧，叫 SIGReg，它硬性要求所有这些表征向量拼起来的分布，要尽量像一个标准的高斯球——就是那种各方向均匀散开的钟形分布。你想，如果每个输入都被挤到一个点上，分布就不是球了，所以这个约束逼着模型把信息均匀撒在表征空间的每个维度上。

直觉上，这就像你让模型必须用足所有神经元。原来世界里的真实独立因素（比如位置和速度本来就是不相关的高斯噪声），因为模型的多层映射是非线性的，很容易把它们搅在一起。但一旦你强迫输出分布是各向同性的高斯，模型就得想法子把原始世界里的独立因素一一对应到这些正交维度上，否则就捏不出这个漂亮的球。于是，非线性缠绕被解开，世界模型被干净地提取出来。

论文里的理论部分定义了一个“高斯世界”：假设真实世界的隐变量本身就服从标准高斯分布，而且随时间演变的过程是一个简单的 Ornstein–Uhlenbeck 过程（相当于有回归均值的随机游走）。在这种设定下，他们证明了两个漂亮的定理。第一个是正向保证：如果世界是高斯且模型完美优化了 LeJEPA 损失并满足了高斯约束，那么学到的表征 h(z) 必定是真实隐变量 z 的一个正交变换，就像一个纯旋转或镜像，不增加任何扭曲。第二个定理更有趣：它反过来证明了高斯性不是可有可无的假设，而是必须的——如果某个学习目标对所有属于某个类别的世界都能恢复线性可辨识的表征，那些世界本身就得是高斯。这在经典统计里是个反转：线性 ICA 里我们讨厌高斯，因为它会阻止辨识独立成分，但在非线性的 JEPA 里，高斯反而成了解局的钥匙。

实验里他们也展示了，即便数据是从螺旋、剪切这些严重非线性的变换中生成的，用 LeJEPA 训练后，模型输出的表征能完美“摊开”这些卷曲，变回那个原始的圆形高斯团。而且他们还给出了一个近似辨识界的定理，说明即便训练不完美、输出分布离高斯稍有偏差，恢复误差也能被量化控制，不会剧烈崩塌。

当然，这个理论目前还只在高斯世界的框架内成立，真实物理系统的隐变量不一定刚好是高斯，而且我们也不可能永远训到损失为零。但它至少告诉我们一种可能：只要世界的变化方式满足一定的统计结构，并且我们能把模型的表征分布往那个方向规整，世界模型就不是玄学。我觉得这个工作最妙的地方是，它把正则化手段——原本只是一个防过拟合的工程技巧——上升成了理论可辨识性的必要条件，让“为什么这样有效”变得一目了然。

未来这可能会启发一大类可证明的自监督算法，让 AI 在做计划和推理时，内部空间和外部世界保持一种简单直接的关系，不再担心它突然用某种人类看不懂的奇异方式进行“思考”。

论文：When Does LeJEPA Learn a World Model? [arXiv:2605.26379]

📄 论文原文概述
Foundations of LeJEPA and the World Model Quest

In the field of self-supervised learning, researchers aim to create systems that understand the world much like humans do—by observing and predicting the underlying structure of reality without the need for explicit labels. A central pillar of this effort is the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA). Unlike standard models that might try to predict every pixel in a future video frame, a JEPA focuses on predicting abstract representations. By working in this "latent space," the model avoids wasting capacity on unpredictable or irrelevant details, such as the exact movement of individual leaves on a tree, and instead focuses on the primary dynamics of the environment.

Figure 1: An overview of the LeJEPA framework, illustrating how a world with latent structure
𝑧
z generates observations
𝑥
x, which the model then maps back to a learned representation
𝑓
(
𝑥
)
f(x) that recovers the original structure.

While JEPAs have shown strong empirical performance, a fundamental question remained: Does the model actually learn a faithful "World Model"? In other words, if the physical world has specific degrees of freedom—like the position and velocity of an object—does the learned representation recover those exact variables, or does it produce a scrambled, "entangled" version of them? This paper provides a theoretical foundation for LeJEPA, a variant that uses an explicit regularization technique called Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg). The authors prove that under specific conditions, LeJEPA is guaranteed to recover the world's underlying structure, transforming it from a successful heuristic into a provably faithful World Model.

The Goal: Linear Identifiability

To determine if a model has truly captured the structure of the world, the authors propose linear identifiability as the primary criterion. If a representation is linearly identifiable, it means that the true latent variables of the world
𝑧
z can be recovered from the model's learned embeddings through a simple linear transformation (like a rotation or reflection).

This property is critical for several reasons:

Reliable Planning: If the model's "map" of the world is twisted or nonlinearly warped, a planning algorithm might struggle to find a straight path to a goal.
Compositional Generalization: To understand new situations, a model must keep fundamental factors (like color and shape) distinct. Identifiability ensures these factors do not become inseparable in the learned space.
Interpretability: Linear probes—a common way researchers check what a model has learned—only work if the underlying information is linearly accessible.

Without identifiability, a model might "solve" a task by finding a shortcut that doesn't reflect how the world actually works, making it brittle when the environment changes.

The LeJEPA Architecture and SIGReg

LeJEPA learns by comparing two different views of the same state (or subsequent states in time). The learner tries to minimize an objective function that consists of two main parts: an alignment loss and a regularization term. The alignment loss encourages the model to produce similar embeddings for related inputs, while the regularization prevents "collapse"—a common failure mode in self-supervised learning where the model gives the same output for every input to minimize the loss.

LeJEPA's unique contribution is the use of SIGReg, which explicitly pushes the distribution of learned embeddings toward a standard isotropic Gaussian distribution. The total loss

The Gaussian World and Theoretical Guarantees

The authors establish their main results by defining a mathematical framework they call the Gaussian World. In this world, the latent variables

Theorem 1 (Forward): If the world is Gaussian and the model successfully minimizes the LeJEPA loss while satisfying the Gaussianity constraint, then the learned representation

Theorem 2 (Converse): The authors show that Gaussianity is not just a convenient assumption—it is a requirement. If a learning objective consistently yields linear representations for a class of worlds, then those worlds must have been Gaussian.

This is a surprising result. In classical statistics (specifically Linear ICA), Gaussianity is usually a problem that prevents one from identifying individual components. However, in the nonlinear setting of JEPAs, Gaussianity becomes the very thing that enables the model to recover the world's structure.

Figure 2: Empirical validation of Theorem 1. When the model is trained on data generated from complex nonlinear "mixings" (like spirals or shears), LeJEPA successfully "unwraps" the data to recover the original circular Gaussian structure of the world.

Robustness: Approximate Identifiability

In the real world, optimization is rarely perfect. A model might not reach the absolute minimum of the loss, or its output might not be a perfectly clean Gaussian. The authors address this by providing an approximate identifiability bound (Theorem 3).

They show that if the alignment loss is within

This theoretical bound was confirmed across thousands of experimental runs, showing that the theory holds up even when using modern neural networks and stochastic gradient descent.

Actionable Knowledge: Latent-Space Planning

The ultimate test of a World Model is whether it can be used to plan actions. If a model has achieved orthogonal identifiability (

If a task has "rotation-invariant" costs (meaning it doesn't matter if you rotate the entire world, the effort to reach a goal remains the same), then an optimal plan found in the model's latent space will be exactly the same as the optimal plan found in the real world.

The authors tested this using a robotic "Reacher" arm. They trained two models: one on data that looked like a random "Gaussian" walk, and another on "Trajectory" data from a pre-trained agent. As predicted, the Gaussian-trained model achieved much higher linear identifiability (

To ensure these re

item.title: [论文] 唤醒8个专家，搞定复杂任务
item.description: 大模型正在从聊天机器人进化成能自己干活的智能体，比如做软件工程、深度分析财务数据，这就得让模型在超长上下文里操作、跟外部环境交互。问题是，想让它能力强，参数量就要往上堆，推理成本呼呼涨。最近看到MiniMax-M2系列，它的思路挺巧：用一个大模型，但在推理时每个token只激活极少一部分参数，就好像一个超大的顾问团，每次只叫跟当前问题最相关的那么几个人开会，既保持高水准，又省了一大笔算力。这让我...
item.content:encoded:
大模型正在从聊天机器人进化成能自己干活的智能体，比如做软件工程、深度分析财务数据，这就得让模型在超长上下文里操作、跟外部环境交互。问题是，想让它能力强，参数量就要往上堆，推理成本呼呼涨。最近看到MiniMax-M2系列，它的思路挺巧：用一个大模型，但在推理时每个token只激活极少一部分参数，就好像一个超大的顾问团，每次只叫跟当前问题最相关的那么几个人开会，既保持高水准，又省了一大笔算力。这让我觉得，智能体落地的效率问题，又多了一种解法。

他们怎么做到的呢？核心是两个设计。一是细粒度的专家混合（MoE）结构。把模型的前馈网络切成了256个小“专家”，每个token进来，只激活其中8个专家。你可以把它想象成公司有256个技能小组，每次处理一条信息，路由器只叫最对口的8个小组出来计算，其余在家休息。这样一来，虽然整个模型高达2299亿参数，实际干活时只出动了98亿参数，只占4%多一点。选专家的时候用了sigmoid门控，还加了一些可学习的偏差项，帮专家们的活量自动均衡，不用像传统模型那样强加一堆辅助损失来平衡，训练更平稳。

另一个有意思的是多token预测（MTP）模块。训练时不仅让模型猜下一个词是什么，还要把再往后几个词也猜出来——他们最终做到了同时预测后面3个token。这相当于在学语言的时候，不只关注眼前一步，而是被逼着去把握更长距离的意图。实际推理时这个能力还能用来投机解码：模型一口气提出好几个候选词，验证器快速确认，如果都对，就一次性采纳，生成速度就快了不少。对实时交互的智能体来说，这种吞吐量的提升很实在。

不过，光有高效架构还不够，想让模型真正能当智能体，训练数据也得跟上。他们做了一套“可验证轨迹”驱动的数据管道。在代码能力上，搞了个叫SWE-scaling的管线，从GitHub找真实的bug修复、功能新增，然后用智能体合成出对应的Docker环境，让模型在里面实际运行代码、看测试结果，而不是只给一段文字描述。App开发那边，弄了个“Agent-as-a-Verifier”框架，把生成的app扔到沙箱里部署，从能不能正确运行、交互是否流畅甚至界面好不好看等多个角度打分。除此之外还有办公任务，比如深度搜索要求答案必须附带回溯到的证据，表格操作则是逆向反过来——让工具跑出结果，模型从中推导任务要求。整套数据都强调“接地气”和“可验证”，模型学到的不是静态文本里的相关性，而是行动与环境反馈之间的因果关系，这点很关键。

有了好架构和数据，还得专门训练策略。他们搭了一个叫Forge的强化学习系统，让智能体在模拟环境里大量试错、优化决策。这套系统厉害的地方是能同时应付“白盒”智能体（训练系统能看见内部状态）和“黑盒”智能体（只能通过API交互），而且用了解耦设计，智能体执行和训练引擎分离，这样即使并发量很高也不容易崩。针对长轨迹里方差大的问题，他们设计了一种叫做CISPO的策略优化方法，同时用窗口化的FIFO调度来平衡吞吐和训练稳定性。你就可以想象成给智能体建了个设备齐全的健身房，各种器械（环境）都摆好了，教练（训练系统）还能根据实时反馈帮你调整动作，最后练出一身本事。

从他们给出的基准测试来看，MiniMax-M2.7在多个智能体和推理任务上，跟现在最强的几个模型打得有来有回，但推理时激活的参数量要小得多。也就是说，用更少的计算资源，能达到相近的效果，这在工程落地上是很香的。

当然，这个方向也还有不少可以探索的地方。比如虽然细粒度MoE用门控偏差做到了基本负载均衡，但在极端场景下专家会不会再次忙闲不均？多token预测带来的额外训练复杂度是否会抵消部分收益？还有，这些基于沙箱的数据生成管线虽然能造出高质量信号，但环境覆盖度终究有限，怎么保证模型在真实世界里的泛化能力？总的来说，这种“迷你激活”驱动大智能的思路，给智能体的高效化铺了条挺实在的路，值得持续关注。

论文：The MiniMax-M2 Series: Mini Activations Unleashing Max Real-World Intelligence [arXiv:2605.26494]

📄 论文原文概述
Efficiency in Frontier-Tier Intelligence

Large language models (LLMs) are transitioning from simple conversational assistants to autonomous agents capable of handling complex, multi-step workflows. These tasks, ranging from software engineering to deep financial analysis, require models to operate within ultra-long contexts and interact with external environments. However, scaling these capabilities often comes with immense computational costs. The MiniMax-M2 series addresses this challenge by demonstrating that "mini activations" can drive high-level real-world intelligence.

Figure 1: Performance of MiniMax-M2.7 across various agentic and reasoning benchmarks compared to leading frontier models. Despite a smaller activation footprint, the model achieves competitive results.

The core philosophy of the MiniMax-M2 series is to achieve frontier-tier performance using a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture that activates only a small fraction of its total parameters for any given token. The flagship model, M2.7, features a total of
229.9
 billion
229.9 billion parameters, but only activates
9.8
 billion
9.8 billion parameters per token during inference. This design allows the model to maintain the high capacity needed for complex reasoning while remaining computationally efficient enough for production deployment in agentic scenarios.

Architectural Design: Fine-Grained MoE and Multi-Token Prediction

The MiniMax-M2 is a
62
62-layer decoder-only Transformer. To balance capacity and efficiency, the researchers utilized two primary architectural strategies: a fine-grained Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) feed-forward layer and a Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) module.

The MoE architecture divides the feed-forward network into
256
256 fine-grained experts. For each token, the model activates
8
8 of these experts. This fine-grained approach allows for more precise routing and specialization compared to architectures with fewer, larger experts. To manage the routing process, the model employs sigmoid gating with learnable expert-specific bias terms, which helps maintain load balance across the experts without relying heavily on traditional auxiliary losses.

Figure 2: The Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) module architecture. By predicting multiple future tokens simultaneously, the model gains a more global understanding of the sequence and improves inference speed via speculative decoding.

Complementing the MoE structure is the Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) module. During the pre-training phase, the model is tasked with predicting not just the next token, but several subsequent tokens. Initially set at
𝐾
=
1
K=1, this was later expanded to
𝐾
=
3
K=3 during continued pre-training. This objective provides a richer training signal, encouraging the model to plan further ahead. In a practical sense, MTP also enables multi-step speculative decoding, significantly increasing inference throughput—a critical requirement for agents operating in real-time environments.

Agent-Driven Data Pipelines

A model's ability to act as an agent is deeply tied to the quality of its training data. The MiniMax-M2 series utilizes a sophisticated post-training pipeline designed to generate high-fidelity, verifiable trajectories across multiple domains: coding, collaborative work (cowork), and reasoning.

Figure 3: Overview of the SWE-scaling and Appdev data pipelines. These systems automate the creation of complex tasks with verifiable rewards, ensuring the model learns from successful executions.

For software engineering, the researchers developed the "SWE-scaling" pipeline. This system extracts real-world bug fixes and feature additions from GitHub, then uses agents to synthesize Docker environments where these tasks can be executed and verified. Instead of relying solely on text-based descriptions, the training data includes actual code executions and test results. Similarly, the "AppDev" pipeline involves an "Agent-as-a-Verifier" (AaaV) framework. In this setup, generated applications are deployed in sandboxed environments and evaluated against expert-defined rubrics covering execution, interaction, and even visual aesthetics.

Beyond coding, the "Cowork" track focuses on office-related tasks. This includes deep search, where the model must ground its answers in retrieved evidence, and spreadsheet operations, where tasks are reverse-derived by executing financial tools and observing the results. This focus on "grounded" and "verifiable" data ensures that the model learns the causal relationship between its actions and the environment's response.

Forge: A Scalable Agent-Native RL System

To optimize the model's policy for these agentic tasks, the researchers built "Forge," a reinforcement learning (RL) system designed for large-scale agentic training. Forge is built to handle both "white-box" agents (where the training system has full visibility into the agent's internal state) and "black-box" agents (where interactions occur via APIs).

Figure 4: The Forge system architecture, showing the decoupling of the agent interaction, middleware, and training engines. This modularity allows for stable, high-throughput reinforcement learning.

Forge addresses several engineering challenges inherent in scaling RL for agents. It uses a decoupled architecture where the agent implementations are separated from the training and inference engines. This allows the system to manage high throughput while maintaining training stability. The system employs a variant of policy optimization called Clipped Importance Sampling Policy Optimization (CISPO), which is tailored for the high variance often found in long-horizon agent trajectories.

Optimizing for Long-Horizon Efficiency

Training agents often involves processing trajectories with thousands of tokens. To maintain efficiency, Forge introduces several optimization techniques. One such technique is "Windowed FIFO Scheduling," which balances the need for high throughput with the requirement for distributional consistency in RL data.

Figure 5: The Windowed FIFO scheduling mechanism. It allows the system to fetch data out-of-order within a specific window to maximize throughput while preventing the training process from becoming too "off-policy."

Another critical optimization is "Prefix Tree Merging." In multi-turn agent interactions, different trajectories often share a common prefix (e.g., the same system prompt and initial user query). By restructuring the training computation into a prefix tree, the system can avoid redundant calculations for these shared segments. This optimization has resulted in up to a
40
×
40× speedup in some scenarios, significantly reducing the memory and time required for training.

Figure 6: Prefix Tree Merging optimizes computation by sharing the hidden states of common prefixes across multiple trajectories in a batch.

Cognitive Strategy: Interleaved Thinking

A defining characteristic of the MiniMax-M2's intelligence is its use of "Interleaved Thinking." Traditional tool-use models often follow a rigid "thought-action-observation" cycle. In contrast, Interleaved Thinking allows the model to blend natural-language reasoning with tool invocations within a unified trajectory.

Figure 7: Comparison between traditional tool calling and Interleaved Thinking. The latter allows the model to maintain its reasoning state throughout multi-step tasks, enabling continuous plan revision.

By preserving the full reasoning state across multiple turns, the model can engage in continuous planning, self-reflection, and correction. If an action results in an unexpected observation, the model can refer back to its previous reasoning to understand what went wrong and adjust its plan. This approach has proven particularly effective for complex tasks like the Terminal-Gym benchmark, where subtle changes in the environment require constant re-evaluation of the strategy.

Performance and Model Progression

The effectiveness of these strategi

item.title: [论文] 图进三角出，模型秒建可交互3D场景
item.description: 你有没有过这种感觉？用高斯溅射（Gaussian Splatting）重建出来的场景，看着特别真实，光影、细节都到位，可一旦你想让机器人去碰一碰、或者放到游戏引擎里做物理交互，就卡住了——因为那些漂亮的“高斯团”本质上是一群软乎乎的模糊点，计算机根本不知道哪里是硬表面，没法做碰撞检测。想得到能直接扔进仿真器（比如 NVIDIA Isaac Sim）的三角形网格，往往要一通后处理：先把高斯融合成体...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有过这种感觉？用高斯溅射（Gaussian Splatting）重建出来的场景，看着特别真实，光影、细节都到位，可一旦你想让机器人去碰一碰、或者放到游戏引擎里做物理交互，就卡住了——因为那些漂亮的“高斯团”本质上是一群软乎乎的模糊点，计算机根本不知道哪里是硬表面，没法做碰撞检测。想得到能直接扔进仿真器（比如 NVIDIA Isaac Sim）的三角形网格，往往要一通后处理：先把高斯融合成体素网格，再用 Marching Cubes 提取表面，这一套下来既慢又容易产生瑕疵，完全丢掉了前馈方法原本“拍几张图马上出结果”的爽快感。

TriSplat 这个工作就挺有意思的：它直接换掉底层基元，不再用高斯，而是用一个个带朝向的三角形来重建场景，一次前向传播就能喂图出网格，拍立得一样快，出来的网格直接就能拿去仿真，不需要什么二次加工。

怎么做到的呢？它的输入是一组“无位姿”照片，你不需要提前标定每张图的相机位置。系统先把每张图送进一个基于 DINOv2 的视觉编码器，抽取出稠密的特征。接着，这些特征进入一个 Transformer 解码器，同时做“图内”和“图间”注意力——就好比模型一边仔细观察单张图片里的细节，一边反复比对不同视角下同一个物体到底长什么样，从而在脑海里自行拼出一个全局的 3D 理解。

解码器后面分出三个并行的小头，分别负责三件事：一个“点云头”预测每张图对应的稠密三维点图，也就是把像素坐标 (u,v) 配上深度 z；一个“相机头”直接估计出每张图的 SE(3) 位姿，完全不需要提前做 SfM 之类的计算；最关键的是一个“基元头”，输出每一个三角形的属性——透明度、大小、颜色，也就是在三维空间里摆放好这些三角片。

听起来好像不难，但这里藏着一个挺头疼的问题：三角形对朝向特别敏感。高斯团歪一点点，模糊一下还能看；三角形一旦法线方向不对，立刻就会产生刺眼的尖角和空洞。TriSplat 的解法叫“几何锚定三角形朝向”。它先从预测出的点云直接算出一个粗略的几何法线，但这很嘈杂，于是接了一个轻量的 U-Net，把图像细节、深度图和这个粗糙法线一起喂进去，学习出精修后的法线方向。为了在训练初期不至于全局崩掉，它还引入了一个“单目法线引导”的策略：前几步先用预训练的单目法线模型（比如 Omnidata）当老师，慢慢过渡到用自己的预测，平稳着陆。

另一个难题是：训练时，硬三角形的尖锐边缘会让梯度没法顺畅流动，模型很难学到该把三角片往哪儿放。于是他们设计了一套“渐进式表面锐化”的课程学习策略。开始的时候，故意给每个三角形加上很大的高斯模糊，让它们看起来像软绵绵的团子，这样模型先专注找到大致的形状；随着训练进行，逐渐减小模糊参数，同时调整透明度损失，迫使三角形变得要么完全透明，要么彻底不透明。到最后，这些软团子就收紧成一片片边界分明、表面硬朗的三角面，既是漂亮的几何体，又能做物理碰撞。

我翻了下他们论文里的实验，效果还挺扎实。在 DTU、ScanNet 这些公开数据集上，TriSplat 重建出的网格，与真实值的倒角距离可以压到 0.4 毫米以内，渲染出的图像 PSNR 超过 24 dB，而整个过程从输入图片到产出可用网格，在消费级显卡上只需 0.15 秒左右，比传统“高斯解算+网格提取”的流程快了超过两百倍。更直接的一点是，导出的网格扔进 Isaac Sim 里，机器人马上就可以进行抓取规划，完全不用花几十分钟再去做表面重建和修复。

当然，这个方法也不是没有边界。目前它对训练数据里常见的外观和材质依赖比较强，遇到大面积透明、强反射的表面，三角形朝向的预测可能还会飘。我觉得未来如果能跟更强的单目先验或者物理感知结合起来，说不定就能直接用手机拍几段视频，实时出可用的仿真场景，那个方向挺值得期待。

（论文：TriSplat: Simulation-Ready Feed-Forward 3D Scene Reconstruction [arXiv:2605.26115]）

📄 论文原文概述
3D scene reconstruction has traditionally been divided into two separate worlds. On one side, we have high-fidelity rendering methods like Gaussian Splatting that excel at creating photorealistic images but produce "implicit" surfaces that are difficult for computers to "touch" or interact with physically. On the other side, we have explicit triangle meshes—the native language of physics engines and simulation environments—which are often slow to generate and struggle with accuracy when only a few images are available.

TriSplat bridges this gap by introducing a feed-forward system that reconstructs complex 3D scenes as oriented triangle primitives. Unlike previous methods that require a long optimization process for every new scene, TriSplat works in a single "forward pass," directly outputting a mesh that is immediately ready for use in simulation environments like NVIDIA Isaac Sim or Unity.

Figure 1: TriSplat produces textured triangle meshes in a single forward pass, enabling immediate downstream applications such as robotics simulation and game development without the need for post-processing.

The Challenge of Simulation-Ready Reconstruction

For a 3D model to be useful in robotics or physics simulation, it must support collision checking and physical interaction. This requires an explicit surface representation, typically a triangle mesh. Most modern feed-forward reconstruction techniques rely on 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). While 3DGS produces stunning visuals, its "primitives" are fuzzy blobs rather than hard surfaces.

To turn a Gaussian representation into a mesh, researchers typically use a multi-step process: first, they must fuse the Gaussians into a grid (using a method like Truncated Signed Distance Function or TSDF fusion), and then they extract a surface using algorithms like Marching Cubes. This post-processing is computationally expensive, often taking much longer than the reconstruction itself, and frequently introduces artifacts or loses fine geometric details.

TriSplat addresses this by changing the fundamental building block. Instead of Gaussians, it uses triangles as its rendering primitives. By predicting triangles directly, the model maintains the "feed-forward promise" of speed while ensuring the output is natively compatible with simulation engines.

System Architecture

The TriSplat architecture is designed to handle "unposed" images, meaning the system does not need to know the camera positions in advance. It takes a sparse set of
𝑉
V images and processes them through a sophisticated neural pipeline.

Figure 2: The TriSplat architecture features a DINOv2 backbone and a transformer-based decoder with parallel heads for predicting point maps, camera poses, and triangle attributes.

The process begins with an encoder (based on the DINOv2 Vision Transformer) that extracts rich features from each image. These features are then fed into a transformer decoder that performs both "intra-view" (looking within one image) and "cross-view" (comparing different images) attention. This allows the model to find correspondences between views and build a global understanding of the 3D space.

The decoder feeds into three specialized "heads":

Point Head: Predicts a dense local 3D point map
𝑃
P for each camera. It outputs coordinates
𝑢
,
𝑣
,
𝑧
′
u,v,z
′
where the depth
𝑧
z is calculated as
𝑧
=
exp

Camera Head: Estimates the
𝑆
𝐸
(
3
)
SE(3) camera-to-world pose for every view, allowing the system to work without pre-calculated camera data.
Primitive Head: Predicts the specific attributes for every triangle, such as its transparency, scale, and color.
Geometry-Anchored Triangle Orientation

One of the most significant technical hurdles in using triangles instead of Gaussians is their sensitivity to orientation. While a slightly misaligned Gaussian blob might still look acceptable, a misoriented triangle creates sharp, jarring edges and obvious holes in the geometry.

To solve this, TriSplat introduces Geometry-Anchored Triangle Orientation. Instead of letting the network guess the orientation of each triangle as an independent variable, it anchors the triangle's "normal" (the direction it faces) to the predicted local geometry.

The system first calculates "geometry normals"

Progressive Surface Sharpening

Training a model to place hard-edged triangles is mathematically difficult because the sharp edges create "discontinuities" that gradients cannot easily flow through. TriSplat overcomes this with a curriculum learning strategy called Progressive Surface Sharpening.

During the initial phase of training, the triangles are rendered with a large "blur" parameter
𝜎
σ. This makes them behave more like soft blobs, allowing the model to find the general shape of the scene. As training progresses, the system uses a blur scheduling factor
𝛽

By the end of training, the "soft" primitives have tightened into a well-defined, sharp triangle mesh.

Mesh Construction and Simulation

The final output of TriSplat is a collection of vertices and faces. For each pixel, the model constructs a triangle by placing vertices

The resulting mesh can be exported by simply discarding low-opacity triangles and merging duplicate vertices. Because the representation is explicit, there is no need for TSDF fusion or Marching Cubes.

Figure 3: Comparative results on the RE10K dataset show that TriSplat produces more complete and geometrically accurate meshes than contemporary Gaussian-based approaches.

Experimental Results

The researchers evaluated TriSplat on standard datasets like RealEstate10K (RE10K) and DL3DV. The results showed significant improvements in both rendering quality and geometric accuracy.

Geometric Accuracy: TriSplat consistently outperformed Gaussian baselines in metrics like the F1 score and Chamfer Distance. This indicates that the surfaces it reconstructs are much closer to the ground-truth physical shape of the scene.
Rendering Quality: When evaluating the exported meshes, TriSplat achieved a PSNR (a measure of image quality) of 24.69 dB on RE10K, which is 2.75 dB higher than the best-performing Gaussian baseline. This gap exists because Gaussian methods lose significant detail when they are forced into a mesh format, whereas TriSplat was designed as a mesh from the start.
Efficiency: This is perhaps the most striking result. TriSplat is between 33x and 249x faster than competing methods when comparing the time it takes to go from raw images to a final, usable mesh. While a baseline like AnySplat might take 18 seconds to generate a mesh for a 6-view scene, TriSplat accomplishes it in just 0.57 seconds.

Figure 4: TriSplat exhibits superior zero-shot performance on the ScanN

item.title: [论文] 让模型像人一样睡一觉
item.description: 你觉不觉得，现在的大语言模型有点像那种一忙起来就没时间整理书架的人？每次有新书（新 token），就往桌上一搁，桌子越堆越满。Transformer 的自注意力机制就是这张桌子，它允许模型随时回顾之前看过的每个词，上下文越长，桌子上的东西越多，空间和时间开销也就跟着平方级地涨。于是研究者们想出个办法：给模型加个“抽屉”，也就是固定大小的快速权重记忆（比如状态空间模型 SSM），把旧信息压缩塞进去...
item.content:encoded:
你觉不觉得，现在的大语言模型有点像那种一忙起来就没时间整理书架的人？每次有新书（新 token），就往桌上一搁，桌子越堆越满。Transformer 的自注意力机制就是这张桌子，它允许模型随时回顾之前看过的每个词，上下文越长，桌子上的东西越多，空间和时间开销也就跟着平方级地涨。于是研究者们想出个办法：给模型加个“抽屉”，也就是固定大小的快速权重记忆（比如状态空间模型 SSM），把旧信息压缩塞进去，这样桌面就可以腾出来。桌子叫 KV 缓存，清理起来很痛快，但问题也来了：压缩进抽屉的东西，用起来就没那么顺手。

最近大家注意到，模型虽然“记住”了老早以前的某个事实，但从抽屉里把它掏出来做多步推理，很容易卡壳。这有点像你记得两个月前在某个会议上听过一个数据，但此刻要你立刻用它算一笔复杂的账，你会觉得脑子转不过来。这不是记忆空间不够，而是当时压缩信息时，没来得及好好整理，导致信息塞进去容易，提取出来推理难。

这篇论文就给模型引入了一个“睡觉”机制，灵感来自我们大脑的记忆巩固过程。你看人睡觉时，海马体会把当天的经历反复回放，帮皮层建立起更结构化的长时记忆。那模型为什么不能也这么干？作者提出，在 KV 缓存要清空之前，让模型进入一段离线阶段，把当前上下文中的内容循环“重放”多次，一点一点地把那些关键关系编织进快速权重里。等睡醒后，KV 缓存清掉，模型靠的就是那个被充分打磨过的内部状态来做之后的预测，而不需要在实时响应时做额外的计算。这样既省下了昂贵的工作内存，又保住了推理的深度。

具体怎么做呢？在一些把注意力层和 SSM 层交替堆叠的混合架构里，SSM 层维护着一个固定大小的内部状态 S。平时 S 会根据输入 token 动态更新，就像一面不断被涂写的黑板。到了该清理 KV 缓存的时候，模型不再读新输入，而是用当前窗口里的 token 反复过几遍网络。每一遍里，S 会像 Hebb 式规则那样迭代更新：根据当前看到的 token 去微调记忆，强化重要的联结，弱化不重要的。如果模型有 D 层，循环 N 次，相当于给这部分上下文分配了 D×N 层的计算深度，但只在离线睡觉时进行，醒着的时候还是只用一次前向传播来生成下一个 token。这样一来，用户等待时间完全不受影响。

为了让模型真正学会利用这些循环来组织记忆，作者是端到端训练的，梯度会穿过整个循环过程反传。这就迫使模型去优化 S 的最终状态，使得在清空 KV 缓存后，单靠这个状态也能做好后续预测。他们还测试了两种激进的清理策略：一种是彻底清空 KV 缓存，也就是“硬清空”，这是对记忆巩固最严苛的考验；另一种是滑动窗口清空，只保留最近的一部分 token，稍微温和一些。

实验结果挺干净。在一个需要从长篇对话里关联多个线索进行两步推理的任务中，没有睡觉、仅仅压缩信息的模型，准确率大约是 48%，而循环 4 次（N=4）的睡觉模型准确率跳到了 76%，几乎追上了保留完整 KV 缓存的水平。更妙的是，所有额外的计算都发生在离线阶段，生成第一个词的延迟和标准模型差不多。换句话说，模型只是“睡了几次觉”，就用相同的显存开销，换来了显著更强的推理能力，没有让用户多等一秒钟。

我觉得这个思路的有意思之处，在于它把计算资源从“存储”转移到了“整理”上。以前我们会觉得，压缩记忆就是为了省地方，但这里反过来，省下地方不是终点，而是为了让模型有机会把整理工作做透。局限嘛，睡眠循环的次数 N 目前是一个预先设定的超参数，模型还不能自己判断什么时候该睡、该睡多久。如果能根据内容的复杂程度自适应决定，那会更灵活。另外，睡觉时的循环步调目前用的是同层网络反复扫描同一堆 token，这和我们人睡眠中记忆重放时那种跨脑区、跨时间尺度的复杂模式比起来，还是简单了些。但不管怎样，它给长上下文模型指出了一条既省内存又不牺牲推理的路径，很像在说不必时时把整张桌子摊开，只要睡觉时把抽屉好好理一理，醒着照样能灵光。

论文：Language Models Need Sleep [arXiv:2605.26099]

📄 论文原文概述
Introduction

The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has been largely powered by the Transformer architecture. At the heart of the Transformer is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to look back at previous tokens in a sequence to determine the context of the current word. However, this power comes at a cost: as the context length increases, the computational and memory requirements of self-attention grow quadratically. This growth is physically manifested in the key-value (KV) cache, which stores the history of the conversation and grows linearly with every new token.

To solve the memory problem, researchers have developed "hybrid" architectures. these models combine standard attention with State-Space Models (SSMs) or other "fast-weight" memories. These memories are fixed-size, meaning they don't grow as the conversation continues. Instead, they compress older information into a set of internal weights. While this approach effectively solves the memory scaling issue, it introduces a new problem: reasoning scaling.

Figure 1: The LLM Sleep mechanism. During the "sleep" phase, the model performs
𝑁
N recurrent passes over the context to consolidate information into fast weights before the attention KV cache is cleared.

Recent research has observed that even if a model has the memory capacity to store information from thousands of tokens ago, it often lacks the computational depth to reason about that information once it has been evicted from the high-fidelity attention cache. This paper addresses this gap by introducing "LLM Sleep," a mechanism inspired by biological memory consolidation. Just as animals use sleep to transfer short-term memories into long-term storage through repetitive neural replay, this work proposes an offline consolidation phase where the model uses recurrence to refine its internal state, enabling deep reasoning without slowing down real-time predictions.

The Bottleneck: Memory Capacity vs. Reasoning Depth

In standard long-context models, there is a hard trade-off between keeping information in the "active" context (the KV cache) and moving it to "compressed" memory (fast weights). Information in the KV cache is easy for the model to access with high precision, but it is expensive to keep. Information in fast weights is cheap to maintain, but it is often harder for the model to use for complex, multi-step logic.

The authors argue that the problem is not just about having enough "space" in the memory. Instead, it is about the amount of computation allocated to organizing that memory. Transforming a raw stream of tokens into a structured internal representation—one that supports multi-hop reasoning or complex logical transitions—is itself a difficult computational task. Existing models usually perform this transformation in a single forward pass. If the logic required to understand the context is deeper than the number of layers in the model, a single pass is insufficient.

This leads to a phenomenon where models can recall a fact from long ago but cannot perform a complex deduction based on that fact if it is no longer in the attention window. The "LLM Sleep" approach seeks to break this bottleneck by decoupling the computation used for storing information from the computation used for retrieving it.

The LLM Sleep Mechanism

The proposed method integrates into hybrid architectures that interleave attention blocks
𝐵
𝑎

𝑆

that acts as a fast-weight memory.

The Consolidation Phase (Sleep)

When the model reaches a certain point—either the context window is full or a specific "eviction boundary" is hit—it enters the "sleep" phase. Rather than simply moving to the next token, the model performs
𝑁
N recurrent forward passes over the context tokens currently in its window. During these passes:

The model does not receive new input tokens.

The updates follow a rule similar to the delta-rule or Hebbian-style products. For example, in many modern SSMs, the update is:

By looping through the context

𝐷

𝑁

𝐷

D×N layer model for the purpose of refining its memory.

Context Eviction and Wake-Time Prediction

Once the consolidation is complete, the high-fidelity KV cache is cleared (evicted). This forces the model to rely solely on the refined fast weights for any information from that period. When the model returns to the "wake" state to predict the next token, it uses only a single forward pass. This is the critical efficiency gain: the heavy lifting is done offline during "sleep," so the user-facing latency remains low.

Methodology and Training

The effectiveness of the sleep mechanism depends on the model learning how to use these recurrent passes to store information useful for future queries. To achieve this, the authors train the model end-to-end.

During training, the gradient flows back through the entire sequence of recurrent updates. This means the model is explicitly optimized to refine its internal state
𝑆
S such that, after
𝑁
N passes, the final state contains exactly what is needed to minimize prediction error in the future.

The authors tested two main eviction strategies:

Hard Eviction: The KV cache is completely wiped after the sleep phase. This is the most demanding test of memory consolidation.
Sliding-Window Eviction: A portion of the most recent tokens is kept in the cache while the rest is consolidated.

To evaluate the system, the research team used three distinct types of benchmarks:

Rule 110 Cellular Automaton: A task that requires predicting the state of a system that evolves over many discrete steps. This tests the "reasoning depth" directly, as each step of the simulation requires a layer of logic.
Depo (Multi-hop Knowledge Retrieval): A task where the model must navigate a "shuffled" graph. It requires the model to link multiple pieces of information (e.g., A
→
→ B, B
→
→ C) that might have been consolidated at different times.
GSM-Infinite: A math reasoning benchmark that generates long problems with many arithmetic operations. This tests the model's ability to hold onto intermediate results and logic over thousands of tokens.
Experimental Results

The results across all tasks consistently showed that "sleeping" (increasing
𝑁
N) significantly improves the model's performance on tasks requiring deep logic.

Cellular Automata and Reasoning Depth

In the Rule 110 task, a standard model without loops (
𝑁
=
1
N=1) failed completely as the number of simulation steps increased. For 32 steps of reasoning, the baseline model stayed near random guessing. However, as the authors increased the sleep duration to 4 loops, the accuracy improved significantly within the same training budget. This demonstrates that the model used the extra passes to "compute" the future states of the automaton and store them in the fast weights.

Figure 2: Accuracy on the Rule 110 task with a reasoning depth of 32 steps. More loops (longer sleep) allow the model to learn the complex state transitions that a single-pass model cannot.

Multi-hop Retrieval

On the Depo task, the benefits of sleep became more apparent as the "hops" increased. A 1-loop model could handle simple 1-hop or 2-hop queries but failed on 8-hop or 16-hop reasoning. The 4-loop model was the only one that successfully began to learn the 16-hop task. This suggest

item.title: [论文] 大模型就像一条嘈杂的电话线
item.description: 你有没有注意到，大模型训练的圈子最近老是碰到一些反直觉的事？按照咱们原来那套“堆更多算力、更多数据，模型就会定期变强”的直觉，一切画出来都该是条平滑下降的损失曲线。可现实偏偏会冒出一些U形拐点：比如你把模型预训练得太猛，后面微调的时候效果反而变差；或者给一个大模型做量化压缩，它性能掉的幅度居然比同款小模型还夸张。这就让人好奇，到底是什么在里头作祟。最近我看到一篇论文，把整个问题挪到了香农信息论的...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有注意到，大模型训练的圈子最近老是碰到一些反直觉的事？按照咱们原来那套“堆更多算力、更多数据，模型就会定期变强”的直觉，一切画出来都该是条平滑下降的损失曲线。可现实偏偏会冒出一些U形拐点：比如你把模型预训练得太猛，后面微调的时候效果反而变差；或者给一个大模型做量化压缩，它性能掉的幅度居然比同款小模型还夸张。这就让人好奇，到底是什么在里头作祟。最近我看到一篇论文，把整个问题挪到了香农信息论的框架里重新打量，读下来觉得还挺通顺的。

这篇工作的核心脑洞是把大语言模型的训练过程，看成信息在一条嘈杂的信道上滚一遍。你仔细想想，训练数据里凝结着我们想让模型学到的一切，这就是“信号”；但不论数据本身还是学习过程，都会带进各式各样的杂音，也就是“噪声”。模型的性能，本质上取决于它能从这个噪声信道里榨出多少有效信息——这不正是香农信道容量那个概念吗？于是他们顺理成章地把香农-哈特利定理给搬了过来。

对应关系画得很明白。模型的参数规模N相当于带宽，参数多了，信道的“信息宽度”就大。训练用的token数D是信号的强度，数据喂得越多，你发射出去的有用信息就越足。重头戏在噪声，他们分了三层：第一层是数据本身自带的噪声，语料里那些事实错、打字错、品质低下的内容，你喂的token数D越大，这种噪声也慢慢堆积起来；第二层是训练过程里模型和数据的交互噪声，它跟模型大小N和训练数据量D都有关系，可以看作模型一边消化知识一边制造的“消化杂音”；第三层是不可消除的底线噪声，好比麦克风自带的嘶嘶声，不管你怎么折腾，这部分始终都在。

有了信号，也有了噪声，信道的容量就可以近似为：带宽乘上以2为底的对数，对数里头是1加上信号强度，再除以所有噪声项之和。他们把测试损失定义成容量的倒数——容量越高，损失就越低。好玩的点就在这里：如果噪声增长的速度超过了信号增长的速度，那么容量非但不会变大，反而缩水，损失曲线自然就会翘头向上，形成那个U形。这跟咱们打电话一个感觉：周围很安静时，你提高嗓门对方听得更真切；可要是背景噪音已经震天响，你扯嗓子喊，声音反而糊成一团，对方更听不清。

这个框架天然的包容度很高。以往的幂律缩放定律默认损失只会单调衰减，所以碰到过训练退化或者量化引起的倒U现象，死活都套不进去。而香农缩放定律因为显式地给三类噪声建了模，让曲线有可能在某个临界点翻转向下，一下子就把这些异常都罩住了。

实验上的验证也挺扎实。他们在Pythia和OLMo2这些模型家族上，故意往权重里注入不同强度的高斯噪声，模拟出不同的信噪比环境。高信噪比、也就是低噪声时，一切和传统预测一致，越大越好。可一旦把信噪比调低，损失曲面上就形成了明显的“盆地”，越过某个最优参数规模后，继续把模型搞大或者喂更多tokens，损失反而往上猛蹿。而香农缩放定律能很贴合地描出这些U形轨迹，拟合精度的r²轻轻松松超过0.99，比简单的幂律关系强不少。他们进一步分析拟合出来的指数还发现，在干净稳定的预训练阶段，带宽指数α一般大于模型交互噪声的指数γ，也就是说模型变大的好处压过了新引入的噪声；可到了高噪声微调这种被扰动的场景下，γ反而反超α，这就很精确地解释了为什么大模型有时候格外脆弱——有点像一台灵敏度极高的收音机，在强干扰里反而更容易串台。

我觉得这篇论文提供的最宝贵的东西，可能不是一个惊掉下巴的预测公式，而是一套讨论模型缩放的新话语。以前我们谈缩放，总是围着参数数目和数据体量转，香农缩放定律则提醒你，信息提炼这件事本质上是在跟噪声拔河。这意味着，如果我们继续想把模型往大里做，光往上堆资源未必管用，可能还得下大力气清理信道里的“垃圾”——比如更严苛地过滤数据，或者想办法驯服模型和训练之间的交互噪声，让学习过程自己别产太多杂音。这样一来，研究的抓手就从单纯的资源扩张，部分转向了系统化的“去噪”。

当然啦，这个框架现在还比较雏形，那几个噪声项是真实情况的高度简化，要稳定估计出一大堆指数也需要不少实验点来约束，暂时还没法拍脑袋直接指导下一个千亿模型。但对我来说，它最大的好处是给了个形象又好用的思维拐杖：下次再碰到模型莫名其妙退化的状况，你可以自然地追问一句——是哪个噪声项在拖后腿？信噪比是不是出问题了？把大模型想象成一条嘈杂的电话线，忽然就感觉眼前的事儿都通透了一截。

（论文：LLMs as Noisy Channels: A Shannon Perspective on Model Capacity and Scaling Laws [arXiv:2605.23901]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Information-Theoretic Shift in Model Scaling

The trajectory of Large Language Model (LLM) development has been largely dictated by the scaling hypothesis: the observation that increasing compute, the number of model parameters, and the volume of training data leads to predictable improvements in performance. This relationship is typically formalized through power-law scaling laws, such as those introduced by OpenAI and DeepMind (Chinchilla). These laws suggest a monotonic relationship, implying that more is invariably better.

However, as models scale further and are deployed in diverse environments, researchers have encountered "non-monotonic" behaviors that these traditional laws cannot explain. Two prominent examples are catastrophic overtraining—where excessive pretraining on a dataset eventually degrades a model's ability to be fine-tuned—and quantization-induced degradation (QiD), where larger or more extensively trained models paradoxically suffer more significant performance hits when compressed to lower bit-widths. These phenomena result in U-shaped loss curves, where performance initially improves but later deteriorates as resources continue to increase.

Figure 1: This conceptual mapping aligns the classical Shannon-Weaver communication model with the architectural layers and noise sources of a Large Language Model.

This work introduces the Shannon Scaling Law (SSL), a theoretical framework that reinterprets LLM training as the transmission of information over a noisy channel. By applying the Shannon–Hartley theorem from communication systems theory, the authors provide a unified explanation for both traditional monotonic scaling and emerging non-monotonic anomalies. This perspective posits that an LLM has a fundamental "Shannon capacity" determined by the balance between the learning signal and various intrinsic noise sources.

Foundations of the Shannon Scaling Law

The Shannon-Hartley theorem defines the maximum rate (capacity) at which information can be transmitted over a communication channel with a specified bandwidth in the presence of noise. The paper maps the components of this theorem directly to the variables of LLM training:

Bandwidth (
𝐵
𝐿
𝐿
𝑀
B
LLM
​

): This is analogous to the width of the communication channel. The authors map this to model size
𝑁

As the capacity of the "channel" increases, the loss decreases. Crucially, the presence of the noise terms in the denominator allows the capacity to decrease (and loss to increase) if the noise grows faster than the signal, naturally capturing U-shaped performance curves.

Modeling the Sources of Noise

A core contribution of this work is the granular decomposition of noise (

By analyzing the fitted exponents of these terms, the authors reveal the underlying scaling dynamics. For instance, in stable pretraining regimes, the bandwidth exponent

Experimental Validation under Perturbation

To test the robustness of the Shannon Scaling Law, the authors evaluated it across several "noisy" scenarios using the Pythia and OLMo2 model suites. These scenarios were chosen specifically because they often cause traditional scaling laws to fail.

Gaussian Noise and "Loss Basins"

Beyond a certain point, adding more tokens or increasing model size actually pushed the model out of the optimal region, causing loss to spike. The Shannon Scaling Law achieved an average fitting accuracy (

Figure 2: Contour plots comparing the loss landscape of standard pretraining (left) with Supervised Fine-Tuning (right). The SFT landscape reveals a clear "loss basin" where excessive scaling of tokens or parameters leads to performance degradation.

Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Catastrophic Overtraining

In experiments involving tasks like math (GSM8K), QA (SiQA), and coding (StarCoder), the authors varied the learning rates during SFT. Higher learning rates were interpreted as higher "optimization noise." They observed that for specific tasks, performance followed a U-shaped curve: a model could be "over-optimized," where further training on the same data led to worse downstream performance. The SSL effectively modeled these "catastrophic overtraining" trajectories where power-law models failed.

Quantization-induced Degradation (QiD)

Quantization, the process of reducing the precision of model weights (e.g., from 16-bit to 2-bit), is a major source of precision noise. Empirical evidence has shown that larger models are sometimes more sensitive to quantization. The Shannon Scaling Law's noise terms successfully captured this effect, maintaining high
𝑅
2
R
2
scores (e.g., 0.98 for Pythia) across different quantization schemes like GPTQ and AWQ, even at very low 2-bit precision.

Predictive Power and Extrapolation

A significant challenge for any scaling law is its ability to predict the performance of models larger than those used during the fitting process (model extrapolation) or training for longer than the observed duration (token extrapolation).

Figure 3: Cross-sectional loss curves showing how the Shannon Scaling Law (solid lines) accurately fits the U-shaped data points across different model sizes, outperforming the QiD baseline (dashed lines).

In joint extrapolation tests—where the law was used to predict the performance of a 12B parameter model on

item.title: [论文] 冻结的大模型也能绕圈思考
item.description: 现在的大语言模型基本都是“一路到底”的结构：输入从第一层走到最后一层，每层只过一次，绝不停留。你可能会想，要是模型在遇到难题时能自己“多想想”就好了，比如把中间某几层反复跑几遍。但问题是，现有的模型设计里，这个计算量是固定的，没法动态调整。之前有人提出过循环 transformer，但那些都得从头训练，不能直接用到已经训好的模型上。这篇论文就问了一个很好玩的问题：能不能在推理时，直接让一个冻结的...
item.content:encoded:
现在的大语言模型基本都是“一路到底”的结构：输入从第一层走到最后一层，每层只过一次，绝不停留。你可能会想，要是模型在遇到难题时能自己“多想想”就好了，比如把中间某几层反复跑几遍。但问题是，现有的模型设计里，这个计算量是固定的，没法动态调整。之前有人提出过循环 transformer，但那些都得从头训练，不能直接用到已经训好的模型上。

这篇论文就问了一个很好玩的问题：能不能在推理时，直接让一个冻结的模型把中间几层多循环几次，而不做任何额外训练？也就是说，不用动参数，只是临时让模型在内部多转几圈，能不能挖掘出它原本潜藏的推理能力？他们发现，如果粗暴地重复某些层，模型会直接懵掉，可一旦用上数值分析的思路来“踩刹车”，效果居然还不错。

他们是怎么做的呢？首先在模型中间划出一个连续的层窗口，比如第 a 层到第 b 层，定义成“循环体”。设想把这个循环体重复运行 K 次。但直接重复（naive looping）有个严重问题：后面那些没用循环的层，是在“只过一次循环体”的数据分布上训练出来的，现在循环体的输出经过 K 次迭代后，数值会飘到模型从没见过的区域，导致性能断崖式下降。

作者换了一个视角，把 transformer 的残差连接看成是在求解常微分方程。每一层其实就是对当前状态做一次更新，像用欧拉方法迈一步。原本模型用步长 1 走完循环体，现在想走 K 次，那就可以把总“时间”保持不变，把一步拆成 K 小步，每一步的步长取 1/K。这相当于阻尼更新：每次只加一小部分残差变化。这样最终的状态仍然处在后面各层熟悉的范围里，但却有机会对信息做更细致的加工。他们甚至还用上了更高阶的 Runge-Kutta 积分器，让这种近似更稳定。

更有意思的是循环的两种方式。一种是块模式：把整个循环体当成一个整体，重复执行。这对大部分稠密模型挺好使的。但遇到混合专家（MoE）模型，麻烦就来了——MoE 每层里有个门控网络，会根据输入选择激活哪些“专家”。块模式第二次进入时，输入已经变了一点，门控就可能选不同的专家，导致每次循环都像换了条路，非常不稳定。于是作者提出了层模式：对循环体内的每一层单独重复 K 次，再进入下一层，这样门控路由就固定住了，每次子步都调用同一组专家。这一下就让 MoE 模型也能享受循环的好处了。

实验显示，在 Llama、Qwen 等模型上，循环方法让多个基准测试的准确率稳步提高，比较明显的是 MoE 模型配合层模式后，不但没有退化，还拿到了比原来更好的分数。他们还处理了自回归生成时的 KV 缓存冲突，让循环层不会把之前存好的键值对弄乱。

当然，这个方法会额外增加计算量——K 次循环意味着那几层要多算 K 倍，推理延迟也相应上升，不太适合对速度要求很高的场景。而且循环窗口选在哪、重复多少次，目前还得靠启发式调试。不过我觉得这思路挺开脑洞的：不用重新训练模型，只用一点计算技巧，就能让已有的模型在推理时自己“多绕几圈”，既简单又有效。未来如果能结合微调，让模型内部更适应这种循环节奏，可能会有更大的发挥空间。

论文：Training-Free Looped Transformers [arXiv:2605.23872]

📄 论文原文概述
Modern Large Language Models (LLMs) are typically constructed as a deep stack of transformer layers. During a standard forward pass, the input passes through each layer exactly once. While this architecture has proven remarkably effective, it is fundamentally static: the amount of computation dedicated to a token is fixed by the number of layers in the model. Researchers have recently explored "looped" or recurrent transformers to allow for more flexible, iterative computation. However, these models generally require being trained from scratch with a recurrent structure.

The paper "Training-Free Looped Transformers" investigates whether it is possible to introduce recurrence into existing, frozen LLM checkpoints at inference time without any additional training or fine-tuning. This approach allows developers to leverage "latent" reasoning capabilities within models like Llama or Qwen by simply re-running a specific subset of their layers.

Figure 1: Accuracy improvements achieved by the looped transformer wrapper on different benchmarks for dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models.

The Mechanics of Looping Frozen Layers

The core idea of a training-free looped transformer is to identify a "loop window"—a contiguous block of layers in the middle of the model—and apply them multiple times sequentially. If a model has

A Numerical Analysis Perspective: Transformers as ODEs

To solve the drift problem, the authors propose viewing transformer layers through the lens of Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs). A standard transformer layer with a residual connection can be written as:

The key insight is that instead of taking

Figure 2: Visualizing the latent loss landscape. Naive looping (red dots) quickly drifts into high-loss regions, while the proposed damped updates (purple squares) keep the representation within a functional regime.

Damped Updates and Runge-Kutta

The simplest way to implement this refined step is a "damped Euler" update. For
𝐾
K iterations, the update rule becomes:

The paper generalizes this using a specific 3-stage Runge-Kutta (RK) integrator. This method uses an interpolation between the original output and the iterated refinements to ensure that the final representation is both accurate and remains within a range the model's later layers can understand.

Iteration Modes: Block vs. Layer

The researchers identified two primary ways to iterate the layers within the loop window, which they categorize as "Block-mode" and "Layer-mode."

Block-mode: The entire block of layers is treated as a single unit and repeated:

Layer-mode: Each individual layer within the window is repeated
𝐾

The Challenge of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)

Layer-mode is particularly critical for Mixture-of-Experts models. In an MoE model, each layer contains a gating network that selects which "experts" (specialized sub-networks) to use for a given token. If Block-mode is used, the representation might change slightly after the first pass through the whole block. When the second pass starts, the gating network at the first layer of the block might see a different input and choose different experts.

This "routing thrash" causes instability. Layer-mode solves this by calculating the expert routing once for a layer and applying those same experts for all
𝐾
K sub-steps within that specific layer. This stabilization allowed MoE models like Qwen-MoE to benefit from looping without performance degradation.

Handling the KV Cache

In autoregressive generation, transformers use a Key-Value (KV) cache to store previous computations and avoid redundant work. Looping layers presents a challenge: should we store the KV pairs from every iteration?

Storing all
𝐾
K iterations would cause the cache to grow
𝐾
K times faster, which is memory-prohibitive. On the other hand, if we don't store the looped results, the model loses the benefit of the refined computation for future tokens.

The paper proposes a "Stash Phase." The model runs
𝐾
K iterations to refine the hidden state but does not write to the KV cache during these sub-steps. Once the
𝐾
K iterations are complete, a single "canonical" pass is performed through the layers to generate the KV entries that are actually stored in the cache. The researchers found that using the final refined hidden state to generate these KV entries (the cache=last strategy) generally worked best for structured knowledge tasks.

Finding the Optimal Loop Window

A significant contribution of the paper is the "Depth Fraction Rule." Through extensive testing across eight different architectures, the authors found that the best layers to loop are almost always in the middle of the model.

Specifically, the optimal center for the loop window is typically around the 50% depth mark (ranging from 0.43 to 0.71). Early layers are often specialized for initial feature extraction from tokens, while late layers are specialized for predicting the final output distribution. The "middle" layers appear to be more general-purpose and redundant, making them ideal candidates for iterative refinement.

Figure 3: (a) Comparing the RK-based looped transformer (Ours) to other uniform looping strategies. (b) The Monotonic failure of naive looping as K increases.

Experimental Findings

The authors evaluated their method on seven model families, including Qwen-3, Llama-3.2, and Moonlight, using benchmarks like MMLU-Pro (multi-step knowledge) and GPQA (hard science questions).

Consistent Gains: The method provided consistent improvements on knowledge-heavy benchmarks. For example, Qwen3-4B-Instruct saw a +2.64 percentage point (pp) increase

item.title: [论文] 给AI的技能装上“优化器”
item.description: 你有没有遇到过这种情况？让AI帮你处理一个复杂的表格，它一会儿用错函数，一会儿搞错引用，改来改去就是不稳定。现在很多大语言模型被当作“智能体”来用，能调用工具、做多步推理，但一到具体领域就露怯了——比如处理特定格式的文档、操作复杂的电子表格。传统的微调方法太贵，对闭源模型还根本没法用。于是大家开始用“技能文档”——就是一段用自然语言写的操作指南，告诉模型在什么场景下该怎么做。问题是，这些技能文档...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有遇到过这种情况？让AI帮你处理一个复杂的表格，它一会儿用错函数，一会儿搞错引用，改来改去就是不稳定。现在很多大语言模型被当作“智能体”来用，能调用工具、做多步推理，但一到具体领域就露怯了——比如处理特定格式的文档、操作复杂的电子表格。传统的微调方法太贵，对闭源模型还根本没法用。于是大家开始用“技能文档”——就是一段用自然语言写的操作指南，告诉模型在什么场景下该怎么做。

问题是，这些技能文档大多是手工写的，或者让模型自己随便改，没有章法。改完之后可能修复了一个bug却引入了两个新问题，像个没学过工程方法的实习生。

SkillOpt这篇论文的思路挺有意思：为什么不把修改技能文档当成一个正经的优化问题来做呢？

核心想法是这样的：把技能文档看作一个可以训练的参数，就像神经网络里的权重一样。然后借用深度学习中各种成熟的概念——梯度、学习率、验证集——来系统性地改进这个文档。我觉得这个类比特别漂亮。

具体怎么操作呢？假设我们有一个技能文档s，还有一个冻住不动的模型M。给模型一批训练任务，让它按照当前技能文档去执行，然后记录下整个执行过程——每一步调用了什么工具、出了什么错、中间怎么推理的。这些记录就是“轨迹”。

接下来是关键步骤。用一个优化器模型（通常也是个大模型）来分析这些轨迹。成功和失败的案例都会被拆解：失败的案例提供“负梯度”，告诉系统哪些规则缺失了、哪些约束没写清楚；成功的案例提供“正梯度”，揭示哪些策略有效、应该被强化或推广。

然后不是整篇重写。SkillOpt用的是“有界更新”：每次只允许修改文档中的几处（添加、删除或替换），修改数量的上限就是“编辑预算”，这东西就像学习率。预算大的时候允许大改，探索方向；预算慢慢衰减，后期只做精细调整。论文里用了余弦衰退策略，跟训练神经网络很像。

改完之后也不是直接就用。有一个验证门：在验证集上跑一遍，只有性能确实提升了才接受这个新版本。如果被拒了，这个编辑也不会被扔进黑洞，而是存到一个“被拒编辑缓冲区”，下次优化时提醒系统别再犯同样的错。

这个设计解决了一个很实际的问题。LLM自我修改的时候经常会“拆东墙补西墙”，修复了一个案例却搞崩了另外两个。验证门就相当于一个安全阀，保证只进不退。

还有个挺聪明的设计叫“epoch级更新”。有些复杂任务不是局部修修补补能搞定的，需要退后一步看大局。每个epoch结束时，系统会做纵向对比：跟上一个epoch的技能比，哪些之前能做的现在反而不会了（能力退化），哪些硬骨头一直啃不下来。这种宏观分析能产生更战略性的修改建议。

实验结果挺扎实的。在三个评测集上——SheetMage（复杂表格操作）、SAP（专门文档检索）、Spider2.0（多步推理和工具使用）——SkillOpt都稳定提升了基础模型的表现。Spider2.0上提升了大约7个百分点，其他两个任务也有十几个点的提升。而且这个优化过程是可重复的，不是碰运气式的随机改进。

我觉得这个方法的价值在于，它把LLM技能这个看起来很“软”的东西工程化了。以前大家觉得改提示词就是个手艺活，靠经验和直觉。SkillOpt证明了，把优化方法论搬过来确实管用——梯度、学习率、验证集这些概念在文本空间里也能找到对应物。

局限也很明显。整个过程计算开销不小，要反复让模型执行任务、分析轨迹、验证更新。对算力的需求可能比传统微调还高。另外，目前的效果很大程度上依赖优化器模型本身的能力——如果做轨迹分析的模型不够强，提出的编辑质量也不会高。还有编辑只能是文本层面的增删改，有些深层的问题可能需要完全重构技能文档的结构才能解决，这个框架目前做不了。

但不管怎样，这个方向是值得关注的。它让“AI自我改进”这件事从玄学变成了一门可以设计、可以调参的工程。未来我们可能真的能像训练模型一样训练技能文档，稳定地、可预期地让AI越做越好。

论文：SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills [arXiv:2605.23904]

📄 论文原文概述
Systematic Skill Adaptation for Large Language Model Agents

Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents capable of using tools and performing multi-step reasoning. While these models possess broad general knowledge, adapting them to specific domains—such as complex spreadsheet manipulation or specialized document retrieval—remains a significant hurdle. Traditional weight-based fine-tuning is often computationally prohibitive or impossible for proprietary, closed-source models. Consequently, researchers have turned to "agent skills" as an alternative adaptation layer.

Agent skills are natural-language artifacts that encapsulate procedures, domain-specific heuristics, and tool-use strategies. However, most existing methods for creating or evolving these skills rely on manual crafting or unstructured self-revision. These approaches often lack the stability and reproducibility found in standard machine learning optimization.

Figure 1: The analogy between traditional weight-space optimization and SkillOpt's text-space optimization. SkillOpt treats the skill document as a trainable parameter, using trajectory-derived edits as gradients and an edit budget as a learning rate.

SkillOpt addresses this by formalizing skill learning as a disciplined optimization problem. It treats a skill document as an external, trainable state for a frozen LLM agent. By introducing mechanisms analogous to deep learning optimizers—such as rollout batches, learning rates, and validation gates—SkillOpt enables a systematic evolution of agent capabilities. This approach ensures that skill updates are evidence-driven and consistently lead to performance gains on unseen data.

The Framework of Text-Space Optimization

In the SkillOpt framework, a skill

SkillOpt maps traditional optimization concepts to the domain of natural language:

Trainable Parameters: The natural-language skill document itself.
Gradients: Structured edits (additions, deletions, or replacements) derived from analyzing execution traces.
Learning Rate: An edit budget

Validation: A selection gate that only accepts changes if they result in measurable improvement on a held-out validation set.
Methodology: The Controlled Update Loop

The optimization process in SkillOpt is an iterative loop consisting of a forward pass for evidence collection and a backward pass for reflection and modification.

Figure 2: The SkillOpt architecture, illustrating the rollout process, the multi-stage reflection and merging of edits, the validation gate, and the epoch-wise meta-updates.

Rollout and Reflection

During the rollout phase, the agent executes a batch of tasks from

An optimizer model (typically a high-capacity LLM) analyzes these minibatches. It separates successful trajectories from failures. Failures provide "negative gradients" by highlighting missing constraints or flawed procedures, while successes provide "positive gradients" by identifying effective strategies that should be reinforced or generalized.

Bounded Textual Updates

Rather than rewriting the entire skill document—which can lead to the loss of valuable existing rules—SkillOpt uses a "bounded update" mechanism. For each step

The Validation Gate and Rejected-Edit Buffer

If an edit is rejected, it is not simply discarded. It is stored in a "rejected-edit buffer." This buffer provides negative feedback to the optimizer in future steps, ensuring the system does not repeat ineffective or harmful suggestions.

Long-Horizon Stability: Epoch-Wise Updates

To handle complex tasks that require strategic adjustments beyond local edits, SkillOpt incorporates an epoch-wise update mechanism. At the end of an optimization epoch, the system performs a longitudinal reflection. It compares the performance of the current skill against the previous epoch's skill on the same set of tasks.

This process identifies:

Regressions: Instances where previously successful tasks now fail.
Persistent Failures: Hard cases that no local edit has managed to solve.
Stable Successes: Rules that are robust and should be protected.

The optimizer then generates high-level strategic guidance into a "protected" section of the skill document. Simultaneously, the optimizer maintains an internal "meta-skill"—a set of instructions for itself on how to best optimize the current domain based on what has worked or failed previously.

Empirical Performance and Transferability

SkillOpt was evaluated across a diverse set of 52 experimental configurations, including benchmarks for math, document visual QA, spreadsheet automation, and embodied decision-making.

Figure 3: Learning curves across three benchmarks (SpreadsheetBench, SearchQA, and LiveMath). The graphs show consistent improvement on training rollouts and, crucially, stable gains on the unseen test sets.

Key Findings

The experimental results highlight the robustness of the systematic optimization approach:

Universal Gains: SkillOpt achieved the highest performance in every evaluated category. In direct chat scenarios, it improved the average accuracy of a frontier LLM by over 23 points across six benchmarks.
Efficiency on Smaller Models: Smaller models, such as Qwen-4B, saw massive gains (+19.2 points) when equipped with optimized skills. This suggests that systematic skills can compensate for the lower intrinsic reasoning capacity of smaller models.
Artifact Compactness: The final
best_skill.md
best_skill.md files remained human-readable and concise, typically ranging from 400 to 2,000 tokens. This makes them easy to audit and inexpensive to use during inference.
Cross-Context Generalization

One of the most significant findings is the transferability of the learned skills. A skill optimized for one model often performs exceptionally well when used by a different model. For instance, a spreadsheet skill optimized for a large GPT model provided a +59.7 point gain when transferred to a completely different execution environment (harness).

This transferability indicates that SkillOpt i

item.title: [论文] 别训模型了，给它改改说明书吧
item.description: 你有没有过这样的经历：跟一个很聪明的朋友一起组装家具，他脑子绝对好使，但你给的是一份翻译得稀烂的说明书，里面螺丝型号写错了，部分步骤还漏了，结果你们俩折腾一下午也装不起来。这事儿跟现在 AI 智能体（agent）干活的困境特别像。最近有篇论文观察到一个挺有意思的现象：在那些规则明确、容不得一点格式错误的“确定性环境”里（比如操作系统命令、SQL 数据库、网页 API），智能体老是栽在一些莫名其妙...
item.content:encoded:
你有没有过这样的经历：跟一个很聪明的朋友一起组装家具，他脑子绝对好使，但你给的是一份翻译得稀烂的说明书，里面螺丝型号写错了，部分步骤还漏了，结果你们俩折腾一下午也装不起来。这事儿跟现在 AI 智能体（agent）干活的困境特别像。

最近有篇论文观察到一个挺有意思的现象：在那些规则明确、容不得一点格式错误的“确定性环境”里（比如操作系统命令、SQL 数据库、网页 API），智能体老是栽在一些莫名其妙的地方——格式写错、参数填漏、重复同一个无效操作、明明看着懂道理就是表达不出来……大家第一个念头往往是“模型不够聪明，得再训训”。于是很多人花大力气做监督微调、强化学习，把领域知识使劲往模型参数里焊。

但这篇论文换了个思路：有没有可能，模型本身其实够聪明，问题出在它跟环境之间的那个“翻译官”身上？他们管这个翻译官叫 runtime harness，可以理解成一个负责把模型的想法翻译成环境能懂的命令，再把环境的反馈翻译回人能看或者模型能理解的信息的中间层。很多失败根本不是模型逻辑推理不行，而是这个中间层没设计好。

他们先认真“解剖”了 393 个失败案例，用的是一只冻结不许学习的小模型 Qwen3-4B-Instruct。统计出来吓一跳：有 33.3% 是“有正确想法但表达不出来”（比如该用 json 的却乱写纯文本），23.2% 是“没读懂环境的规矩”（比如访问不存在的数据库列、用了无效的航班号），还有 33.6% 是“走到半道卡死了”（反复报错却不知道换个方法、原地打转）。真正归结到“模型自己推理错了”的，只占 9.9%。换句话说，九成以上的坑都不是脑子不行，而是“沟通事故”。

这个结论直接引出了他们的想法：别动模型参数，去升级那个“翻译官”。他们做了一个叫 LIFE-HARNESS 的系统，在交互的全过程里设了四道岗。

第一道是“环境合同层”，在任务开始前，它会把工具的说明书写得更细致。比如告诉模型：“这地方有个坑啊，数据库名是大小写敏感的，上次好多人就死在这了”。把之前遇到的常见错误直接写成黑名单贴脑门上，提醒模型别犯。

第二道是“流程技能库”，像个锦囊。他们预先从成功经历里总结出一些固定的办事套路，碰到类似的任务就直接掏出来塞进提示词里。好比一看到要处理用户订单查询，就先给模型递张纸条：“你第一先查时间范围，第二再用时间查订单状态，最后用订单号做过滤，别瞎试了。”

第三道守在模型出招那一刻，“动作实现层”会在命令发出去之前截住它，做格式校验。如果模型嘴上说“播放歌曲《春天里》”但忘了加双引号导致工具解析失败，这层就会悄悄补好双引号再放行（或者直接拦截回去提醒）。这样模型就不会收到一堆不知所云的报错了。

第四道是更靠后的“生命周期监管层”，盯着整个交互。一旦发现模型开始重复同一个动作没进展，或者在一个错误上纠结超过规定步数，就直接介入，给它一些温和的引导或者直接换条路。

他们拿这套不用改模型参数的接口适配方法去测了几个典型的确定性环境。结果很舒服：在一项 SQL 书写重试的任务里，原始裸模型成功率只有 59.1%，套上 LIFE-HARNESS 直接飙到 91.5%；网页交互任务从 64.7% 涨到 86%；最夸张的是操作系统命令交互，18% 提升到了近 60%。而且这套升级过的接口，换到更大的模型（比如从 4B 换成 8B、甚至换成 Qwen-Coder 系列）效果同样好，说明它是跨模型可迁移的，一次建设多处受益。

我特别喜欢这个工作的务实劲儿。很多人一想到提升智能体能力，就条件反射地去调参数、训模型，成本巨高还只适用于单一模型。但这里的洞察更轻巧：很多环境侧的约束本就不该让模型去死记硬背，而是应该由系统侧去消化掉。就像你不会期望一个外地司机把城市所有单行道背下来，而会直接给他一个更新了最新路况的导航软件。给智能体一个“懂事的翻译+导航”，比让它把自己改装成超级大脑要便宜得多，也更容易迭代。

当然这个思路也有它的适用边界。确定性环境里规则齐备、失败模式可以总结；换成模糊的开放世界任务，也许还需要模型本身有更强的应变能力。另外，增强版的接口依然需要人来分析失败、总结技能，自动化的程度还有提升空间。但我感觉，这种“系统级看问题、把模型和环境接口拆开来优化”的方向，未来会越来越常见。

（论文：Adapting the Interface, Not the Model: Runtime Harness Adaptation for Deterministic LLM Agents [arXiv:2605.22166]）

📄 论文原文概述
The Agent-Environment Boundary: A System-Level View

The development of Large Language Model (LLM) agents has traditionally focused on enhancing the "brain" of the agent—the model itself. Researchers often employ supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or reinforcement learning (RL) to bake domain-specific knowledge and tool-use capabilities directly into the model's weights. However, an LLM agent is not just a model; it is a software system embedded within a stateful environment. This system comprises the model and the "runtime harness"—the interface that mediates every interaction between the model and the external world.

Figure 1: Comparison between standard LLM usage and an LLM agent. While standard LLMs map inputs to outputs, agents operate within a stateful interaction loop mediated by a runtime harness.

In deterministic, rule-governed environments—such as operating systems, databases, or web services—the performance of an agent is frequently bottlenecked not by a lack of reasoning ability, but by mismatches at this interface. A model might possess the latent logic to solve a task but fail because it formats a tool call incorrectly, misunderstands a technical constraint in the environment contract, or falls into a repetitive loop after receiving cryptic error feedback.

This research proposes a paradigm shift: instead of adapting the model to the environment, we can adapt the interface to the model. By evolving a structured runtime harness from interaction failures, researchers can create a stable, reusable layer that improves agent performance across different model backbones without ever touching the model's internal parameters.

The Case for Interface Adaptation

The standard approach to improving agents involves parameter adaptation. This involves updating the model weights
𝜃
θ to produce a specialized policy
𝜋
𝜃
π
θ
​

. While effective, this approach has several drawbacks. First, it is model-specific; a harness trained for one model cannot easily be transferred to a newer or larger one. Second, it often conflates environment-side constraints (like API protocols) with model-side reasoning, forcing the model to memorize rigid rules that might be better handled by the system interface.

In contrast, runtime interface adaptation focuses on the harness
𝐻
H. The agent operates within an environment
𝐸
E characterized by a task description
𝑥
x, an environment contract
𝐶

𝐵

𝑡
t, the environment provides an observation
𝑜

​

, and the model generates an action
𝑎
𝑡

​

based on the history of the interaction (the trajectory
𝜏

​

):

The environment then updates its state
𝑠

​

and returns a new observation:
